topics.title: Need of Compiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need of Compiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Compiler** is a specialized system software program that translates source code written in a **high-level programming language (HLL)** — such as C, C++, Java, or Python — into an equivalent **low-level machine language** (object code, target code, or intermediate code) that can be understood and executed directly by the computer's processor (CPU). The translation process is **automated, systematic, and phase-based**, ensuring that the semantic meaning and functional behaviour of the original program are fully preserved in the target output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Examination Standard):**
> A compiler is a program that reads a program written in a *source language* and translates it into an equivalent program in a *target language*, while reporting any errors detected during the translation process. *(Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman — "Dragon Book" classical definition adopted by KTU syllabus.)*

## 1.2 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Source Program** | The original HLL code written by the programmer (input to the compiler). |
| **Target Program** | The equivalent low-level / machine code produced by the compiler (output). |
| **Translation** | The mapping process from source language semantics to target language syntax. |
| **Error Reporter** | Sub-component of the compiler that detects and reports syntax/semantic errors. |
| **Symbol Table** | A data structure that stores information about identifiers (variables, functions, etc.). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multilingual Diplomat"

Imagine you are an Indian diplomat stationed in Japan who can only speak **Malayalam**, but the Japanese officials you negotiate with only understand **Japanese**. You also need to communicate the final decisions to the **Indian Prime Minister** who only reads **English**.

You need a **translator** who:
- **Understands your source language** (Malayalam) perfectly — including grammar, tone, and idioms.
- **Faithfully converts it** into the target language (Japanese) **without changing the meaning**.
- **Flags anything ambiguous** (e.g., a Malayalam idiom that has no direct Japanese equivalent).

In this analogy:
- You (programmer) write in a **High-Level Language (HLL)**.
- The Japanese officials (CPU) understand only **Machine Language**.
- The translator is the **Compiler**.
- The PM who receives an English summary represents the **runtime execution environment**.

## 1.4 Why Was the Compiler Invented? — The Historical Necessity

> [!NOTE]
> Early computers (1940s–1950s) were programmed directly in **binary machine code** (0s and 1s) or **assembly language**. This was extremely tedious, error-prone, and machine-specific. The compiler emerged as the solution to **bridge the human-machine communication gap**.

**Without a compiler, a programmer would have to:**
1. Manually translate every arithmetic operation into binary.
2. Manually allocate memory addresses for every variable.
3. Manually manage register usage across different CPU architectures.
4. Re-write the entire program when porting to a different machine.

The compiler **automates all of these tasks**, allowing engineers to focus on *problem-solving logic* rather than hardware-level minutiae.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Reasons — Why Do We Need a Compiler?

The necessity of a compiler can be broken down into **five fundamental engineering needs**:

### 2.1.1 Abstraction from Machine Hardware
High-level languages allow programmers to express complex logic using **human-readable constructs** like `if-else`, `for`, `while`, functions, and objects — without worrying about the underlying register-level operations.

### 2.1.2 Portability Across Different Machines
A program written in C can be compiled on an **Intel x86 machine**, an **ARM processor**, or a **RISC-V board** — provided each platform has a compatible compiler. The *same source code* generates *different machine code* for each architecture.

### 2.1.3 Productivity & Development Speed
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Studies show HLL programming with compilers increases developer productivity by **5x to 10x** compared to assembly language programming. (Industry-standard metric from software engineering literature.)

### 2.1.4 Error Detection Before Execution
The compiler performs **static analysis** — checking syntax, type mismatches, undeclared variables, scope violations — *before the program runs*, saving enormous debugging time.

### 2.1.5 Optimization
Modern compilers perform sophisticated optimizations (dead-code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, register allocation) that produce machine code **faster and more efficient** than most hand-written assembly.

## 2.2 The Translation Process — High-Level View

The compiler performs translation through a well-defined pipeline:

1. **Lexical Analysis** → Breaks source code into *tokens* (keywords, identifiers, operators).
2. **Syntax Analysis** → Verifies the *grammatical structure* using Context-Free Grammars (CFGs).
3. **Semantic Analysis** → Checks *meaning consistency* (type checking, scope rules).
4. **Intermediate Code Generation** → Produces a *machine-independent* three-address code representation.
5. **Optimization** → Improves the intermediate code for speed, memory, or power efficiency.
6. **Target Code Generation** → Translates the optimized IR into *assembly/machine code* for the target CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> In Module 1, KTU expects students to understand this pipeline *conceptually*. Detailed phase-wise algorithms are covered in Modules 2 and 3.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Relationship | KTU Significance |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Translation Function | $T : L_s \rightarrow L_t$ | Maps source language $L_s$ to target language $L_t$ |
| Compiler Efficiency Metric | $E = \dfrac{O_{\text{optimized}}}{O_{\text{baseline}}}$ | Ratio of optimized to baseline performance (used in optimization questions) |
| Pass Count | $P \geq 1$ | Minimum 1 pass; multi-pass compilers revisit intermediate representations |
| Compilation Time vs. Execution Time Trade-off | $T_{total} = T_{compile} + T_{execute}$ | Compilers trade compilation time for faster runtime |
| Phases of Compiler | $n = 6$ (classic model) | Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR → Optimization → Code Gen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The most frequently asked formula in Module 1 is the **translation mapping** $T : L_s \rightarrow L_t$ — memorize its notation exactly as shown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Compiler's Role |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Cross-compilers generate ARM code on x86 development machines. |
| **High-Performance Computing** | Aggressive optimization (auto-vectorization, loop tiling) extracts CPU peak performance. |
| **Web Development** | Just-In-Time (JIT) compilers (e.g., V8 for JavaScript) compile at runtime. |
| **Compiler-Aided Security** | Compilers detect buffer overflows, use-after-free vulnerabilities statically. |
| **AI/ML Frameworks** | PyTorch/TensorFlow graphs are JIT-compiled into GPU kernels (CUDA). |

## 2.5 Compiler vs Interpreter — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests this comparison. Understand the difference **deeply**.

| Feature | Compiler | Interpreter |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Translation** | Translates *entire program* before execution | Translates *line-by-line* during execution |
| **Output** | Produces standalone executable (.exe, .out) | No standalone output; executes directly |
| **Speed** | Faster execution (pre-translated) | Slower execution (translation overhead per line) |
| **Error Reporting** | Reports all errors after full scan (or up to first error in single-pass) | Stops at first error encountered |
| **Examples** | GCC (C), javac (Java compilation step) | Python interpreter, Ruby interpreter |
| **Memory** | Target code stored on disk | No intermediate code storage |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Translating the Mathematical Notion of Compilation

The compiler's task can be formally modeled as a **mathematical function** from the source language domain to the target language codomain.

### 3.1.1 Formal Statement

Let $L_s$ denote the **source language** (set of all valid programs in HLL), and $L_t$ denote the **target language** (set of all valid programs in machine code). The compiler $C$ is defined as a function:

$$
C : L_s \rightarrow L_t
$$

such that for every source program $p \in L_s$, the compiler produces a target program $C(p) \in L_t$ with the property:

$$
\forall p \in L_s : \text{Semantics}(p) = \text{Semantics}(C(p))
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1:** Define the source program domain.
Let the source language $L_s$ be defined over an alphabet $\Sigma_s$ of ASCII characters, with a grammar $G_s = (V_s, T_s, P_s, S_s)$, where $V_s$ is the set of non-terminals, $T_s$ is the set of terminals (tokens), $P_s$ is the production rules, and $S_s$ is the start symbol.

**Step 2:** Define the target program codomain.
Similarly, the target language $L_t$ is defined over an alphabet $\Sigma_t$ of binary mnemonics, with grammar $G_t = (V_t, T_t, P_t, S_t)$.

**Step 3:** Apply the compiler function.
Given an input source program $p = \text{"int } x = 5 + 3\text{"}$, the compiler $C$ executes the transformation:

$$
C(\text{"int } x = 5 + 3\text{"}) = \text{"MOV R1, \#5 ; ADD R1, \#3 ; STR R1, [x]"}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify semantic equivalence.
Both programs compute the value $x = 8$ — the semantics are preserved.

### 3.1.3 Multi-Phase Composition

In practice, the compiler is not a single function but a **composition of six phase-functions**:

$$
C = \text{CodeGen} \circ \text{Optim} \circ \text{IRGen} \circ \text{Semantic} \circ \text{Syntax} \circ \text{Lex}
$$

where $\circ$ denotes function composition. Expanding this:

$$
C(p) = \text{CodeGen}(\text{Optim}(\text{IRGen}(\text{Semantic}(\text{Syntax}(\text{Lex}(p))))))
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of Step 4:** The innermost function $\text{Lex}$ is applied first (lexical analysis), followed by Syntax, Semantic, IRGen, Optim, and finally CodeGen. This is the *innermost-to-outermost* execution order in function composition.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example — Tracing a Simple Statement

Consider the C statement: `area = length * width;`

**Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenizer Output):**

| Token Type | Lexeme |
|-----------|--------|
| IDENTIFIER | `area` |
| ASSIGNMENT_OP | `=` |
| IDENTIFIER | `length` |
| ARITHMETIC_OP | `*` |
| IDENTIFIER | `width` |
| SEMICOLON | `;` |

**Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parse Tree):**

The parser builds a tree confirming the structure is *valid C syntax* (Assignment → Variable = Expression → Term * Term).

**Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis:**
- Verifies `area`, `length`, `width` are declared and have numeric types.
- Inserts type-coercion if `int * float` is detected.

**Phase 4 — Intermediate Code (Three-Address Code):**

$$
t_1 = \text{length} * \text{width}
$$

$$
\text{area} = t_1
$$

**Phase 5 — Optimization:**
Constant folding applied if `length` and `width` were constants; otherwise, no change.

**Phase 6 — Target Code Generation (x86 Assembly):**

$$
\text{MOV EAX, [length]}
$$

$$
\text{IMUL EAX, [width]}
$$

$$
\text{MOV [area], EAX}
$$

This is the **faithful translation** from C semantics to x86 semantics.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Translation Strategies

| Strategy | When Used | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Single-Pass Compilation** | Simple compilers (Pascal-S) | Fast compilation, low memory | Limited optimization |
| **Multi-Pass Compilation** | GCC, LLVM | Better optimization, modular design | Slower compilation, higher memory |
| **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation** | Java HotSpot, V8 | Combines compilation + interpretation speed | Runtime overhead, startup latency |
| **Ahead-Of-Time (AOT) Compilation** | C/C++ with GCC | Maximum runtime performance | No runtime adaptation possible |
| **Cross Compilation** | Embedded/IoT development | Build on host, run on different target | Requires target-specific toolchain |

## 3.3 The "T-diagram" — Visualizing Translation

A **T-diagram** (originally proposed by Bratman, 1958) is a standard KTU-recommended way to depict a compiler's behavior. In KTU Module 1 questions, students are often asked to draw or interpret T-diagrams.

The notation is:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{L_s}{L_t}} \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} L_e
$$

where $L_s$ = source language, $L_t$ = target language, $L_e$ = executable language produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** T-diagrams appear frequently in 14-mark Part B questions. Practice drawing them cleanly with proper alignment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Compiler

The following diagram represents the **complete functional architecture** of a compiler system, showing all major phases, the symbol table interface, and the error handler — as required by the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Program in HLL] --> B[Lexical Analyzer Lexical Phase]
    B --> C[Syntax Analyzer Syntax Phase]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer Semantic Phase]
    D --> E[Intermediate Code Generator IR Phase]
    E --> F[Code Optimizer Optimization Phase]
    F --> G[Target Code Generator Code Gen Phase]
    G --> H[Target Program in Machine Code]

    B -.-> ST1[(Symbol Table)]
    C -.-> ST1
    D -.-> ST1
    E -.-> ST1
    F -.-> ST1
    G -.-> ST1

    B -.-> EH[Error Handler]
    C -.-> EH
    D -.-> EH
    E -.-> EH
    F -.-> EH
    G -.-> EH

    style A fill:E1F5FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style B fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style C fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style D fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style E fill:F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style F fill:F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style G fill:E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style H fill:E1F5FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style ST1 fill:FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style EH fill:FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
```

## 4.2 Compiler vs Interpreter — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COMPILE[Compiler Pipeline]
        direction TB
        S1[Source Code] --> T1[Entire File Translation]
        T1 --> O1[Executable File .exe .out]
        O1 --> R1[Execution on CPU]
    end

    subgraph INTERP[Interpreter Pipeline]
        direction TB
        S2[Source Code] --> L1[Read Line 1]
        L1 --> T2[Translate Line 1]
        T2 --> X1[Execute Line 1]
        X1 --> L2[Read Line 2]
        L2 --> T3[Translate Line 2]
        T3 --> X2[Execute Line 2]
    end

    style S1 fill:E1F5FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style O1 fill:E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style S2 fill:E1F5FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style X1 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style X2 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
```

## 4.3 The Need-of-Compiler Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Why Do We Need Compilers]

    ROOT --> N1[Hardware Abstraction]
    ROOT --> N2[Portability Across CPUs]
    ROOT --> N3[Developer Productivity]
    ROOT --> N4[Static Error Detection]
    ROOT --> N5[Automatic Optimization]
    ROOT --> N6[Standardization of Languages]

    N1 --> E1[Programmers focus on logic, not registers]
    N2 --> E2[Same source runs on x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS]
    N3 --> E3[5x to 10x faster development vs assembly]
    N4 --> E4[Syntax and type errors caught before runtime]
    N5 --> E5[Generated code often beats hand-written assembly]
    N6 --> E6[ISO C, ISO C++ ensure cross-vendor consistency]

    style ROOT fill:0277BD,stroke:#01579B,color:#FFFFFF
    style N1 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style N2 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style N3 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style N4 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style N5 fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme assessment patterns. Marks distribution follows the official KTU template: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each (sub-parts of 7 marks each)**.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Define a compiler. List any four major phases of a compiler.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **compiler** is a system software that translates a program written in a high-level language (source language) into an equivalent program in a low-level/machine language (target language), while reporting any errors encountered during translation.

**Four major phases:**
1. Lexical Analysis
2. Syntax Analysis
3. Semantic Analysis
4. Intermediate Code Generation *(+ Optimization + Code Generation)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Differentiate between a compiler and an interpreter in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Compiler | Interpreter |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Translation Mode | Translates the entire program at once | Translates one statement at a time |
| Output | Generates an executable file | Does not produce a separate executable |
| Execution Speed | Faster (code is pre-translated) | Slower (translation overhead per line) |
| Error Detection | Reports all errors after scanning | Stops at the first error |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the various phases of a compiler with a neat block diagram. Also explain the role of the symbol table manager.**

**Model Answer:**

A compiler processes a source program through **six well-defined phases**, each transforming the representation of the program into an equivalent but more machine-friendly form.

**Phase 1: Lexical Analysis (Scanner)**
Reads the source program character-by-character and groups them into meaningful sequences called **tokens**. Examples of tokens: keywords (`int`, `if`), identifiers (`x`, `sum`), operators (`+`, `*`), literals (`42`, `"hello"`). It removes comments and whitespace. Output: stream of tokens.

**Phase 2: Syntax Analysis (Parser)**
Takes the token stream from Phase 1 and verifies the **grammatical structure** using Context-Free Grammars (CFGs). It constructs a **parse tree** or **syntax tree** that represents the hierarchical syntactic structure. If the program violates grammar rules, a syntax error is reported.

**Phase 3: Semantic Analysis**
Checks the **meaning** of the program beyond syntax. Key tasks:
- Type checking (e.g., adding `int` to `string` → error)
- Scope resolution (variable declared before use)
- Type coercion / implicit conversions
- Array bounds (statically detectable cases)

**Phase 4: Intermediate Code Generation**
Produces a **machine-independent intermediate representation (IR)** — typically **three-address code (TAC)** — where each instruction has at most three operands. Example: `t1 = a + b`.

**Phase 5: Code Optimization**
Applies transformations to the IR to improve runtime speed, memory usage, or power consumption. Techniques include constant folding, dead-code elimination, loop-invariant code motion, and strength reduction.

**Phase 6: Target Code Generation**
Translates the optimized IR into the **target machine code** (assembly or binary), performing register allocation, instruction selection, and addressing-mode assignment.

**Role of the Symbol Table Manager:**
The **symbol table** is a critical data structure used by *all phases* of the compiler. It stores information about every identifier in the program, including:
- Identifier name and type
- Scope (local, global, parameter)
- Memory address or register allocation
- Number of arguments (for functions)
- Return type (for functions)

The symbol table enables **consistent type checking, scope resolution, and memory allocation** across all compilation phases. Any phase encountering an undeclared or misused identifier consults/updates the symbol table.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the major reasons why compilers are essential in modern software development. Compare cross-compilation with native compilation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Why Compilers Are Essential:**

1. **Hardware Abstraction:** Programmers write in HLLs (C, Java, Python) without needing to manage CPU registers, memory addresses, or instruction encoding manually.

2. **Portability:** A single HLL source code can be compiled for multiple target architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS) using appropriate compilers — no need to rewrite the program for each platform.

3. **Developer Productivity:** Studies indicate HLL programming is **5–10 times faster** than assembly programming, drastically reducing time-to-market.

4. **Static Error Detection:** Compilers detect syntax errors, type mismatches, undeclared variables, and many semantic errors *before* execution — preventing costly runtime crashes.

5. **Automatic Optimization:** Modern compilers (GCC, LLVM, MSVC) apply sophisticated optimizations that produce machine code **comparable to or better than** expert hand-written assembly.

6. **Language Standardization:** Compilers enforce language standards (ISO C, ISO C++), ensuring code written by one developer compiles correctly on another's machine.

**Cross-Compilation vs Native Compilation:**

| Aspect | Native Compilation | Cross Compilation |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Compiler runs on the *same* machine that will execute the output | Compiler runs on a *different* machine than the one executing the output |
| Use Case | Standard desktop/server development | Embedded systems, IoT, mobile apps |
| Example | `gcc program.c` on x86 produces x86 executable | `arm-linux-gnueabihf-gcc` on x86 host produces ARM binary |
| Toolchain | Single, simple toolchain | Requires cross-toolchain (cross-compiler, cross-linker, cross-libraries) |
| Debugging | Easier (same architecture) | Harder (may need remote/JTAG debugging) |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of a T-diagram with examples. How does it help in understanding bootstrapping and cross-compilation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **T-diagram** is a standard notation (proposed by Bratman, 1958) used to depict a compiler's translation behavior visually.

**Structure:**
A T-diagram has the shape of the letter **T**, with the *vertical stem* representing the **compiler implementation language** (the language in which the compiler itself is written) and the *horizontal bar* showing the **source language → target language** translation performed.

The notation is conventionally written as:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{L_s}{L_t}} \, L_c
$$

where $L_s$ = source language, $L_t$ = target language, and $L_c$ = implementation language of the compiler.

**Example 1 — Native Compiler:**
A C compiler written in C that translates C source code into x86 machine code:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\text{C}}{\text{x86}}} \, \text{C}
$$

**Example 2 — Self-Compiling Compiler:**
A Java compiler written in Java that translates Java source code into Java bytecode:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\text{Java}}{\text{Bytecode}} \, \text{Java}}
$$

**Bootstrapping:**
Bootstrapping is the process of writing a compiler for a new language $L$ using an existing language $M$ where a compiler for $M$ already exists. T-diagrams elegantly visualize this:

- Step 1: Write a small compiler $C_1$ for $L$ in language $M$ → T-diagram $\boxed{L/M} \, M$
- Step 2: Use $C_1$ to compile a more optimized compiler $C_2$ for $L$ written in $L$ → T-diagram $\boxed{L/M} \, L$ (after re-compilation)
- Step 3: The final self-hosting compiler has diagram $\boxed{L/M} \, L$

This **stepwise refinement** from a basic compiler to an optimized self-hosted compiler is the essence of bootstrapping.

**Cross-Compilation:**
T-diagrams clarify cross-compilation — when source and target languages differ from the host's language. For example, compiling C code on an x86 host for ARM target:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\text{C}}{\text{ARM}} \, \text{x86}}
$$

This diagram clearly shows that the compiler runs on x86 but produces ARM binary.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain with examples the difference between a single-pass and multi-pass compiler. State the advantages and disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Single-Pass Compiler:**
A single-pass compiler processes the source code **only once**, performing lexical analysis, syntax analysis, semantic analysis, and (partial) code generation in a single sweep over the input.

*Example:* Early Pascal compilers (Pascal-S) and simple educational compilers.

**Multi-Pass Compiler:**
A multi-pass compiler processes the source code **multiple times**, with each pass performing a specific set of transformations. The first pass might only do lexical and syntax analysis, producing an intermediate file; subsequent passes perform semantic analysis, optimization, and code generation.

*Example:* GCC, LLVM, MSVC — all are multi-pass compilers with sophisticated optimization pipelines.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single-Pass | Multi-Pass |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Number of Scans | One scan of source code | Multiple scans (typically 3–6+) |
| Memory Requirement | Low (no intermediate code stored) | High (IR must be stored between passes) |
| Compilation Speed | Fast (no I/O overhead) | Slower (multiple passes, disk I/O) |
| Optimization Quality | Limited (no global view of program) | Excellent (full program analysis possible) |
| Error Reporting | Reports one error per scan; may miss later errors | Reports comprehensive errors across all passes |
| Code Quality | Acceptable for simple languages | Near-optimal (LLVM produces better code than most hand-written assembly) |
| Example | Pascal-S, early BASIC compilers | GCC, LLVM/Clang, MSVC, javac |

**Advantages of Single-Pass:**
- Faster compilation
- Simpler compiler design
- Lower memory footprint

**Disadvantages of Single-Pass:**
- Limited optimization capability
- Cannot handle forward references (e.g., calling a function defined later in code) without special handling

**Advantages of Multi-Pass:**
- Superior optimization through global analysis
- Modular compiler design (each pass is independent)
- Easier to maintain and extend

**Disadvantages of Multi-Pass:**
- Slower compilation due to repeated I/O
- Higher memory consumption
- More complex implementation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "phases" with "passes."** A compiler can have 6 phases but 2 passes — each pass may combine multiple phases.
> 2. **Do NOT claim that single-pass compilers cannot perform optimization.** They *can* perform local optimization (e.g., peephole optimization), but not global optimization.
> 3. **Do NOT say "multi-pass is always better."** In resource-constrained environments (embedded bootloaders), single-pass is preferred.
> 4. **Always draw the T-diagram with proper alignment.** Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for sloppy diagrams.
> 5. **Mention the symbol table's role explicitly** in any phase explanation — its absence is a frequent deduction point.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Module 1 Exam**

- [x] **Compiler Definition:** System software translating HLL → machine code with error reporting.
- [x] **Six Phases:** Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR Generation → Optimization → Code Generation.
- [x] **Two Auxiliary Components:** Symbol Table (data) and Error Handler (control).
- [x] **Symbol Table:** Used by ALL six phases — stores identifier information (name, type, scope, address).
- [x] **Five Reasons for Need:** Abstraction, Portability, Productivity, Error Detection, Optimization.
- [x] **Compiler vs Interpreter:** Compiler = whole program at once, produces executable. Interpreter = line-by-line, no executable.
- [x] **Single-Pass vs Multi-Pass:** Single-pass = one scan, fast, limited optimization. Multi-pass = multiple scans, slower, better optimization.
- [x] **T-diagram:** Standard notation showing source/target/implementation languages.
- [x] **Bootstrapping:** Writing a compiler for language $L$ in language $M$ using an existing compiler for $M$.
- [x] **Cross-Compilation:** Compiler produces executable for a *different* target architecture than its host.
- [x] **Formula:** $C : L_s \rightarrow L_t$ — the formal translation function.
- [x] **Formula:** $C = \text{CodeGen} \circ \text{Optim} \circ \text{IRGen} \circ \text{Semantic} \circ \text{Syntax} \circ \text{Lex}$ — phase composition.
- [x] **Examples to Remember:** GCC (multi-pass, native), LLVM (multi-pass, modular), javac (multi-pass, AOT), Python interpreter (interpreted, JIT-compiled at runtime via CPython).
- [x] **Productivity Metric:** HLL + compiler = 5x–10x faster development than assembly programming.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage (Module 1):** "Need of Compiler" topic typically carries **8–12 marks** out of the full Module 1 weightage. Focus on phase explanations, T-diagrams, and compiler-vs-interpreter comparisons — these are the highest-yield sub-topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of the Source Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of the Source Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT631 – Compiler Design)**, the **Analysis of the Source Program** (often called the *front-end* of a compiler) refers to the sequence of phases that examine the source code, verify its structural and semantic correctness, and produce an intermediate representation suitable for synthesis. Formally, it is the tuple:

$$
\text{Analysis} \;=\; \langle \text{Lexical Analysis}, \text{Syntax Analysis}, \text{Semantic Analysis} \rangle
$$

Each phase transforms one representation of the program into an equivalent, more structured representation while populating and consulting the **Symbol Table** — the central data structure that records identifiers, their attributes, scopes, and types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The analysis phase must be studied as three sub-modules — **Lexical Analysis (tokens & regular expressions)**, **Syntax Analysis (context-free grammars & parsers)**, and **Semantic Analysis (type checking & symbol tables)**. The 2024 scheme explicitly links these to **Bloom Level 3 (Apply)** and **Bloom Level 4 (Analyze)** outcomes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have received a **legal contract written in German**, and you must explain it to an English-speaking judge. Before you translate it, you must:

1. **Lexical Analysis** → Identify each *word* and *punctuation mark* in the German text (a "tokenization" pass). Each legal term is recognized and tagged (e.g., *„Haftung"* is a noun meaning *liability*).
2. **Syntax Analysis** → Check whether the *sentences* follow German grammar — subject, verb, object in correct order. You build a *sentence tree* showing how words group into clauses.
3. **Semantic Analysis** → Verify the *meaning* — that the dates make sense, that the parties named actually exist, that the obligations are logically consistent. You also build a *glossary* (Symbol Table) of every proper noun and its definition.

The **analysis of the source program** does exactly the same thing to a high-level language like C/Java before the compiler's *synthesis* (back-end) can generate machine code.

## 1.3 The Three Sub-Phases at a Glance

| Sub-Phase | Input | Output | Primary Tool / Math |
|-----------|-------|--------|---------------------|
| **Lexical Analysis** | Source character stream | Token stream | **Regular Expressions**, Finite Automata |
| **Syntax Analysis** | Token stream | Parse Tree / AST | **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)**, Pushdown Automata |
| **Semantic Analysis** | Parse Tree + Symbol Table | Annotated AST / IR | **Type Systems**, Attribute Grammars |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Table** is *not* a phase — it is a shared data structure used by all three sub-phases. KTU examiners frequently pose 3-mark questions asking the difference between a *phase* and a *data structure*.

## 1.4 Why is "Analysis" Important in Compiler Design?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> * **Error Detection:** Catches ~95% of programmer errors (syntax + type errors) before any code is generated.
> * **Portability:** The same front-end can be reused across many target machines — only the back-end (synthesis) changes.
> * **Optimization Foundation:** All later optimizations operate on the *output* of analysis (e.g., AST, IR).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanning)

The lexical analyzer (often called the **scanner** or **lexer**) reads the source program **character-by-character** and groups them into **lexemes**, classifying each lexeme into a **token**.

### Formal Definitions

| Concept | Formal Definition | Example (for `int x = 10 ;`) |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Token** | A pair $\langle \text{TokenName}, \text{AttributeValue} \rangle$ | $\langle \text{keyword}, \text{"int"} \rangle$ |
| **Lexeme** | The actual character sequence matched by a token's pattern | `x`, `=`, `10`, `;` |
| **Pattern** | A rule (typically a **Regular Expression**) describing the set of lexemes for a token | `letter (letter \mid digit)*` for identifiers |

### Core KTU High-Yield Notation Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Token} \;&=\; (T_i, V) \quad \text{where } T_i \in \Sigma_{\text{token-types}},\ V \in \mathbb{V}_{\text{attribute}} \\[4pt]
\text{Lexeme} \;&=\; c_1 c_2 \ldots c_k \in \Sigma^* \\[4pt]
\text{Pattern} \;&=\; r \in \mathbb{R} \quad (\text{regular expression over alphabet } \Sigma) \\[4pt]
\Sigma_{\text{lang}} \;&=\; L(r_1) \cup L(r_2) \cup \ldots \cup L(r_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Lexical Analyzer Operations — Step-by-Step

1. **Buffering:** Load source characters into a buffer (use *sentinels* for efficiency — double-buffer scheme with $N = 4096$ bytes typical).
2. **Pattern Matching:** Apply regular expressions to identify the longest matching lexeme.
3. **Token Emission:** Output the token tuple.
4. **Symbol Table Interaction:** On encountering an **identifier**, insert it (or look it up) in the symbol table and return its pointer as the attribute value.
5. **Skip Whitespace/Comments:** Discard them silently.
6. **Error Recovery:** Apply strategies like *panic mode* (delete characters until a synchronizing token is found).

### Tokens vs Regular Expressions — Quick Reference

| Token Type | Typical Regular Expression (KTU standard) |
|------------|-------------------------------------------|
| `identifier` | `(letter \mid \_)(letter \mid digit \mid \_)*` |
| `integer`    | `digit+` |
| `float`      | `digit+ . digit+ ( E (\mid+) digit+ )?` |
| `relop`      | `< \mid > \mid <= \mid >= \mid = \mid <>` |
| `whitespace` | `( \mid \t \mid \n )+` (discarded) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A **token name** (e.g., `id`) is *not* the same as a **token pattern**. The pattern is a *rule*; the name is a *category*. Examiners test this distinction every semester.

---

## 2.2 Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing)

The parser receives the token stream and verifies that the string can be generated by the source language's **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**, producing a **Parse Tree** or an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.

### Formal Definition of a CFG

A CFG $G$ is a **4-tuple**:

$$
G \;=\; (V, T, P, S)
$$

where:
- $V$ = finite set of **non-terminals** (syntactic variables)
- $T$ = finite set of **terminals** (tokens)
- $P$ = finite set of **productions** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$
- $S \in V$ = the **start symbol**

### Derivation Mechanics — The Heart of KTU Questions

A **derivation** is a sequence of rewrite steps starting from $S$. At each step, exactly one non-terminal is replaced by the right-hand side of one of its productions.

$$
S \;\Rightarrow\; \gamma_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \gamma_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \ldots \;\Rightarrow\; w
$$

where $w \in T^*$. We write $S \xRightarrow{*} w$ to denote "derives in zero or more steps."

### Types of Derivations — KTU Favourite

| Derivation Type | Definition | When Used |
|-----------------|------------|-----------|
| **Leftmost Derivation** ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$) | At each step, replace the **leftmost non-terminal** | **Top-down parsers** (LL, Recursive Descent) |
| **Rightmost Derivation** ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$) | At each step, replace the **rightmost non-terminal** | **Bottom-up parsers** (LR, LALR) |
| **Canonical Derivation** | The sequence itself, regardless of position | Used in proofs of ambiguity |

### Grammar Classification (Chomsky Hierarchy — KTU Must-Know)

$$
\text{Type 0} \;\supset\; \text{Type 1 (CSG)} \;\supset\; \text{Type 2 (CFG)} \;\supset\; \text{Type 3 (Regular)}
$$

| Type | Language Family | Automaton | Form of Production |
|------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 3 | Regular | Finite Automaton | $A \rightarrow aB \text{ or } A \rightarrow a$ |
| 2 | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | $A \rightarrow \alpha$, $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$ |
| 1 | Context-Sensitive | Linear-Bounded Automaton | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ |
| 0 | Recursively Enumerable | Turing Machine | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, arbitrary |

### Parser Classification — Decision Chart

$$
\text{Parsers} \begin{cases} \text{Universal} \\ \text{Top-Down} \begin{cases} \text{Recursive Descent} \\ \text{Predictive (LL(k))} \end{cases} \\ \text{Bottom-Up} \begin{cases} \text{SLR} \\ \text{Canonical LR} \\ \text{LALR} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.3 Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis

The semantic analyzer traverses the AST (or parse tree) and checks **context-sensitive** rules that the CFG cannot express — primarily **type compatibility** and **scope resolution**.

### Key Activities

1. **Type Checking:** Ensure operands of an operator are of compatible types (e.g., cannot add `int` and `array[10]` without conversion).
2. **Scope Resolution:** Bind each identifier use to its declaration.
3. **Constant Folding:** Evaluate constant expressions at compile time.
4. **Overload Resolution:** Select the correct function based on argument types.

### Formal Type System Notation

A type judgment is written:

$$
\Gamma \vdash E : \tau
$$

Read as: *"Under type environment $\Gamma$, expression $E$ has type $\tau$."* Example inference rule:

$$
\frac{\Gamma(x) = \tau}{\Gamma \vdash x : \tau} \quad (\text{Variable rule})
$$

$$
\frac{\Gamma \vdash E_1 : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash E_2 : \text{int}}{\Gamma \vdash E_1 + E_2 : \text{int}} \quad (\text{Addition rule})
$$

---

## 2.4 The Symbol Table — Central Data Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The Symbol Table is *not* a phase — it is a **data structure** used by all three analysis phases. It maps *names* to *attributes*.

$$
\text{SymbolTable} : \text{Name} \rightarrow \langle \text{Type}, \text{Scope}, \text{Offset}, \text{Lifetime}, \ldots \rangle
$$

| Operation | Complexity (Hash Table) | Complexity (Linear Search) |
|-----------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| `Insert` | **O(1)** average | O(n) |
| `Lookup` | **O(1)** average | O(n) |
| `Delete` (on scope exit) | O(1) | O(n) |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Unit / Domain |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Token | $\langle T_i, V \rangle$ | tuple |
| 2 | Lexeme | $c_1 c_2 \ldots c_k$ | $\Sigma^*$ |
| 3 | Pattern (RE) | $r$ | regular expression |
| 4 | CFG | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | 4-tuple |
| 5 | Derivation step | $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | relation |
| 6 | Transitive closure | $\xRightarrow{*}$ | $: (V \cup T)^* \times (V \cup T)^*$ |
| 7 | Language generated | $L(G) = \{ w \in T^* \mid S \xRightarrow{*} w \}$ | set of strings |
| 8 | Type judgement | $\Gamma \vdash E : \tau$ | inference |
| 9 | Hash function | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | $m$ = table size |
| 10 | Buffer sentinel cost | $O(N)$ lookahead | $N$ = buffer size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This entire analysis pipeline is used in real-world compilers — GCC, LLVM Clang, Java's HotSpot — and is also the backbone of every interpreter, IDE (syntax highlighting), and static analyzer (SonarQube, ESLint).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Leftmost & Rightmost Derivations of a String

**Problem:** Given the grammar:
$$
E \rightarrow E + E \mid E \ast E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}
$$
Derive the string `id + id * id` using **(a)** leftmost derivation and **(b)** rightmost derivation. Show the parse tree.

### Part (a) — Leftmost Derivation

We always replace the **leftmost non-terminal**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \;&\Rightarrow_{lm}\; E + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E+E \text{ to leftmost } E)\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{lm}\; \text{id} + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{lm}\; \text{id} + E \ast E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E \ast E)\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{lm}\; \text{id} + \text{id} \ast E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{lm}\; \text{id} + \text{id} \ast \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Rightmost Derivation

We always replace the **rightmost non-terminal**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \;&\Rightarrow_{rm}\; E + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E+E)\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{rm}\; E + E \ast E \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E \ast E \text{ to rightmost } E)\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{rm}\; E + E \ast \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{rm}\; E + \text{id} \ast \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})\\[4pt]
  \;&\Rightarrow_{rm}\; \text{id} + \text{id} \ast \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } E \rightarrow \text{id})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Parse Tree

```
        E
       /|\
      E + E
      |  /|\
      | E * E
      | |   |
      id id id
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ambiguity Alert:** This grammar is **ambiguous** — the string `id + id * id` has **two distinct parse trees** (one giving `+` higher precedence, one giving `*` higher precedence). KTU examiners love this example. Ambiguity can be removed by enforcing precedence:
> - Introduce `T` for terms and `F` for factors: $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Computing `FIRST` and `FOLLOW` Sets

**Problem:** For the grammar
$$
\begin{aligned}
S \;&\rightarrow\; A B \mid b \\
A \;&\rightarrow\; a \mid \varepsilon \\
B \;&\rightarrow\; b \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$
compute $\text{FIRST}$ and $\text{FOLLOW}$ sets.

### Step 1 — Compute FIRST Sets

**Rule 1:** If $X$ is a terminal, $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.

**Rule 2:** If $X \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is a production, add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

**Rule 3:** If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k$, then $\text{FIRST}(X)$ contains:
  - All non-$\varepsilon$ symbols in $\text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, **plus**
  - If $\text{FIRST}(Y_1)$ contains $\varepsilon$, then all non-$\varepsilon$ symbols in $\text{FIRST}(Y_2)$, and so on.

Applying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(A) \;&=\; \{a, \varepsilon\} \quad &&\text{(from } A \rightarrow a, A \rightarrow \varepsilon)\\[4pt]
\text{FIRST}(B) \;&=\; \{b, \varepsilon\} \quad &&\text{(from } B \rightarrow b, B \rightarrow \varepsilon)\\[4pt]
\text{FIRST}(S) \;&=\; \text{FIRST}(A B) \cup \text{FIRST}(b) \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \text{FIRST}(A) \cup \{b\} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \{a, \varepsilon\} \cup \{b\} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \{a, b, \varepsilon\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute FOLLOW Sets

**Rule 1:** Put `$` (end-of-input marker) in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.

**Rule 2:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then every symbol in $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ (except $\varepsilon$) is added to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

**Rule 3:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$, or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is added to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

Applying Rule 1: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{ \$\}$.

For production $S \rightarrow A B$:
- $A$ is followed by $B$, so $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \varepsilon\}$ (minus $\varepsilon$) → add $b$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B)$, add $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ → add `\$`.
- $B$ is at the end, so add $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$ → add `\$`.

For $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ and $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: no further information.

$$
\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FIRST/FOLLOW?** They drive the construction of the **LL(1) parsing table** used by predictive top-down parsers. KTU frequently asks for these sets for 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Verifying a Regular Expression Recognizes a Token Set

**Problem:** Prove that the regular expression $r = (a \mid b)^* a b (a \mid b)^*$ generates all strings over $\{a, b\}$ ending in `ab`.

**Proof by construction of two sets:**
- $L_1 = (a \mid b)^*$ — any string over $\{a, b\}$ (possibly empty).
- $L_2 = ab$ — the literal string `ab`.
- $L_3 = (a \mid b)^*$ — any suffix.

Then $L(r) = L_1 \cdot L_2 \cdot L_3$ = *any prefix* concatenated with `ab` concatenated with *any suffix*. Any string of the form $x \cdot ab \cdot y$ where $x, y \in \{a,b\}^*$ ends with `ab`. Conversely, any string over $\{a,b\}$ ending in `ab` can be split at the *last* occurrence of `ab` to form $x \cdot ab \cdot y$ with $y$ not containing `ab` (or being empty).

$$
\therefore L(r) = \{ w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with the substring } ab\}
$$

This is the formal proof. KTU examiners accept either this construction-style argument or a proof by induction on string length.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lexical vs Syntax vs Semantic Analysis

| Attribute | Lexical Analysis | Syntax Analysis | Semantic Analysis |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Reads** | Characters | Tokens | Parse Tree / AST |
| **Writes** | Tokens | Parse Tree / AST | Annotated AST / IR |
| **Formalism** | Regular Expressions, NFA/DFA | Context-Free Grammars, Pushdown Automata | Attribute Grammars, Type Systems |
| **Drives** | Finite-state machine | Pushdown machine | Tree-walk with symbol table |
| **Handles** | Tokens, lexemes | Hierarchical structure | Types, scopes, meanings |
| **Cannot Detect** | Unbalanced parentheses, mismatched types | Type errors, scope errors | Syntax errors, token errors |
| **KTU Bloom Level** | Apply (L3) | Analyze (L4) | Evaluate (L5) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Trace — Symbol Table Construction

**Source:** `int x = 5; { int y = x + 1; }`

| Step | Lexeme | Token | Symbol Table Action |
|------|--------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | `int` | `<kw, int>` | — |
| 2 | `x` | `<id, ptr1>` | Insert `x`: type=int, scope=global, offset=0 |
| 3 | `=` | `<=,>` | — |
| 4 | `5` | `<num, 5>` | — |
| 5 | `;` | `<;,>` | — |
| 6 | `{` | `<{,>` | Push new scope |
| 7 | `int` | `<kw, int>` | — |
| 8 | `y` | `<id, ptr2>` | Insert `y`: type=int, scope=block-1, offset=4 |
| 9 | `=` | `<=,>` | — |
| 10 | `x` | `<id, ptr1>` | Lookup `x` → ptr1 (resolves to global scope) |
| 11 | `+` | `<+,>` | Semantic check: `int + int` → OK |
| 12 | `1` | `<num, 1>` | — |
| 13 | `;` | `<;,>` | — |
| 14 | `}` | `<},>` | Pop scope (remove `y`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The scope stack is what makes nested blocks possible. KTU examiners often give a small C/Java snippet and ask you to draw the symbol table state after each token.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Compiler Pipeline — Front-End vs Back-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B -->|Token Stream| C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C -->|Parse Tree| D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D -->|Annotated AST| E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    subgraph ST[Symbol Table]
        F[Identifiers and Attributes]
    end
    B -.-> F
    C -.-> F
    D -.-> F
    E --> F
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Analysis Phase Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LexA[Lexical Analysis]
        L1[Source Characters] --> L2[Buffer with Sentinels]
        L2 --> L3[Pattern Matcher RE and DFA]
        L3 --> L4[Token Stream Output]
    end
    subgraph SynA[Syntax Analysis]
        S1[Token Stream] --> S2[Parser CFG Driven]
        S2 --> S3[Parse Tree or AST]
    end
    subgraph SemA[Semantic Analysis]
        M1[Parse Tree or AST] --> M2[Type Checker]
        M2 --> M3[Scope Resolver]
        M3 --> M4[Annotated AST or IR]
    end
    L4 --> S1
    S3 --> M1
    L3 -. reads .-> SymT[(Symbol Table Hash Map)]
    S2 -. reads and updates .-> SymT
    M3 -. reads and updates .-> SymT
    SymT --> ErrH[Error Handler Panic Mode Recovery]
```

## 4.3 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Parsing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Parser Strategy] --> TD[Top Down]
    P --> BU[Bottom Up]
    TD --> RD[Recursive Descent Backtracking]
    TD --> PR[Predictive LL k]
    PR --> LL1[LL 1 Table Driven]
    PR --> LLk[LL k General]
    BU --> S[Shift Reduce]
    S --> SLR[SLR Simple LR]
    S --> CLR[Canonical LR]
    S --> LALR[LALR Lookahead LR YACC]
```

## 4.4 Token Recognition State Machine — Identifier vs Keyword

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> start
    start --> letterSeen: letter or underscore
    letterSeen --> letterSeen: letter digit underscore
    letterSeen --> accept: any other char
    accept --> [*]
    note right of accept
      Look up lexeme in keyword table
      If found: emit keyword token
      Else: emit id token with symtab pointer
    end note
```

## 4.5 Derivation Tree for `id + id * id`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[E] --> N1[E]
    R --> OP1[plus]
    R --> N2[E]
    N1 --> L1[id]
    N2 --> N3[E]
    N2 --> OP2[star]
    N2 --> N4[E]
    N3 --> L2[id]
    N4 --> L3[id]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**Differentiate between a token, a lexeme, and a pattern. Illustrate with the statement `count = count + 5;`**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Example from `count = count + 5;` |
|------|------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Token** | A pair consisting of a token name and an optional attribute value. | `<id, ptr_to_count>`, `<=,>`, `<+,>`, `<num, 5>`, `<;,>` |
| **Lexeme** | The actual sequence of characters that forms a token instance. | `count`, `=`, `+`, `5`, `;` |
| **Pattern** | A rule (regular expression) describing the set of lexemes for a token. | For identifier: `(letter)(letter \mid digit)*` |

The statement `count = count + 5;` would yield the token sequence:
$$
\langle \text{id}, p_1 \rangle, \langle \text{=}, -\rangle, \langle \text{id}, p_1 \rangle, \langle \text{+}, -\rangle, \langle \text{num}, 5 \rangle, \langle \text{;}, -\rangle
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse *pattern* with *lexeme*. A pattern is a *rule* (one per token class); lexemes are *instances* matched by that rule. Do not interchange the two.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**List any three differences between a compiler and an interpreter. Which model does Java use, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Compiler | Interpreter |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Translates the **entire** program to an intermediate/ machine form **before** execution. | Translates and executes **statement-by-statement**. |
| 2 | Produces an **executable file** (`.exe`, `.class`). | Produces **no standalone executable** — runs within the interpreter. |
| 3 | **Faster execution** (translation is one-time). | **Slower execution** (translation is repeated for every run). |
| 4 | Error reporting happens **after full compilation**. | Error reporting is **immediate at each statement**. |
| 5 | Example: GCC, javac | Example: Python REPL, ruby |

**Java** uses a **hybrid model**: the source code is first **compiled** by `javac` into **bytecode** (`.class` file), which is then **interpreted / JIT-compiled** by the **JVM**. This gives Java portability ("*Write Once, Run Anywhere*") combined with reasonable execution speed.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **different phases of the analysis of the source program** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **symbol table** in each phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct the **lexical analyzer** (transition diagram / DFA) that recognizes the following tokens of a hypothetical language:
- `keyword` → `if`, `for`, `int`
- `identifier` → letters followed by digits, starting with a letter
- `relop` → `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`
- `integer` → one or more digits
- Whitespace is to be skipped.

Show the DFA states clearly.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

The analysis of the source program consists of **three sequential phases**, each consuming the output of the previous phase:

**Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanning):**
- Reads the source program character-by-character.
- Groups characters into **lexemes** and produces **tokens**.
- Removes **comments** and **whitespace**.
- Inserts identifiers into the **symbol table** and uses the pointer as the token's attribute.
- Output: a stream of tokens $\langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute} \rangle$.

**Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing):**
- Reads the token stream.
- Checks whether the sequence conforms to the **context-free grammar** of the source language.
- Produces a **parse tree** (or **Abstract Syntax Tree**).
- Detects **syntax errors**; recovers using *panic mode*, *phrase level*, or *error productions*.

**Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis:**
- Walks the parse tree / AST.
- Performs **type checking**, **scope resolution**, and **operator compatibility**.
- Updates the symbol table with type, scope, memory offset, etc.
- Produces an **annotated AST** (often converted to **three-address code** as an intermediate representation).

**Role of the Symbol Table:**

| Phase | Symbol Table Operation |
|-------|------------------------|
| Lexical | **Insert** identifier lexeme; return pointer |
| Syntax | **Lookup** identifier to validate usage context (e.g., array index must be in scope) |
| Semantic | **Insert/Update** type, scope; **Lookup** to resolve overloads and check compatibility |

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**DFA for the lexical analyzer (combined):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s0: whitespace skip
    s0 --> kstart: letter
    s0 --> dstart: digit
    s0 --> lbrace: less than char
    s0 --> gbrace: greater than char
    s0 --> ebrace: equals char
    s0 --> exclaim: exclamation char
    kstart --> kstart: letter or digit
    kstart --> accept1: not letter or digit
    dstart --> dstart: digit
    dstart --> accept2: not digit
    lbrace --> leq: equals char
    lbrace --> ldone: not equals
    leq --> accept3: any other
    ldone --> accept3: any other
    gbrace --> geq: equals char
    gbrace --> gdone: not equals
    geq --> accept3: any other
    gdone --> accept3: any other
    ebrace --> eqdone: equals char
    eqdone --> accept4: any other
    ebrace --> accept4: any other
    exclaim --> neq: equals char
    neq --> accept5: any other
    accept1 --> [*]
    accept2 --> [*]
    accept3 --> [*]
    accept4 --> [*]
    accept5 --> [*]
```

**Tabular description of the DFA:**

| Current State | Input Class | Next State | Token Emitted on Acceptance |
|---------------|-------------|------------|----------------------------|
| $s_0$ | letter | $s_k$ | — |
| $s_0$ | digit | $s_d$ | — |
| $s_0$ | `<` | $s_{<}$ | — |
| $s_0$ | `>` | $s_{>}$ | — |
| $s_0$ | `=` | $s_{=}$ | — |
| $s_0$ | `!` | $s_{!}$ | — |
| $s_k$ | letter/digit | $s_k$ | — |
| $s_k$ | other | accept | **identifier** or **keyword** (after lookup in keyword table: `if`, `for`, `int`) |
| $s_d$ | digit | $s_d$ | — |
| $s_d$ | other | accept | **integer** |
| $s_{<}$ | `=` | $s_{\leq}$ | **relop `<=`** |
| $s_{<}$ | other | accept | **relop `<`** |
| $s_{>}$ | `=` | $s_{\geq}$ | **relop `>=`** |
| $s_{>}$ | other | accept | **relop `>`** |
| $s_{=}$ | `=` | accept | **relop `==`** |
| $s_{=}$ | other | accept | **relop `=`** (assignment) |
| $s_{!}$ | `=` | accept | **relop `!=`** |
| $s_{!}$ | other | ERROR | Lexical error |

The keyword check is performed by maintaining a **lookup table** $\{$`if`, `for`, `int`$\}$. After accepting an identifier-shaped lexeme, the lexeme is hashed and checked: if found, emit the keyword token; otherwise emit `<id, symtab_ptr>`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-mark question:**
> - **Do not** forget to handle **maximal munch** — the DFA must consume the *longest* matching lexeme (e.g., `<=` must not be split into `<` and `=`).
> - **Do not** omit the **keyword recognition step** — just emitting `<id>` for `if` is incomplete.
> - **Do not** forget to **rollback** the lookahead character after a non-matching transition.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024] *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Context-Free Grammar**? Define **derivation**, **parse tree**, and **ambiguity**. Show that the following grammar is ambiguous by exhibiting two distinct parse trees for the string `a + b * c`:
$$
E \rightarrow E + E \mid E \ast E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following grammar, compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S \;&\rightarrow\; A B \\
A \;&\rightarrow\; a A \mid \varepsilon \\
B \;&\rightarrow\; b B \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

Verify whether the grammar is **LL(1)** by checking the LL(1) condition for every production.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Context-Free Grammar (CFG):**
A CFG is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where $V$ is a finite set of non-terminals, $T$ a finite set of terminals, $P$ a finite set of productions of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ with $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$, and $S \in V$ is the start symbol.

**Derivation:** A sequence of applications of productions starting from $S$. Formally, $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ if $A \rightarrow \gamma$ is a production.

**Parse Tree:** A rooted tree where the root is $S$, internal nodes are non-terminals, leaves are terminals, and the children of any internal node $A$ spell out the right-hand side of some production $A \rightarrow \alpha$.

**Ambiguity:** A grammar $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists a string $w \in L(G)$ having **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two or more distinct leftmost derivations).

**Two parse trees for `a + b * c`:**

**Tree 1 (gives `+` higher precedence):**

```
          E
        / | \
       E  +  E
       |    /|\
       a   E * E
           |   |
           b   c
```

**Tree 2 (gives `*` higher precedence):**

```
           E
        /  |  \
       E   *   E
      /|\      |
     E + E     c
     |   |
     a   b
```

Since both trees derive the same string `a + b * c` but yield different evaluation orders, the grammar is **ambiguous**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "two different derivations" instead of "two different parse trees." Both are valid proofs of ambiguity in KTU valuation, but **explicitly drawing both trees** earns full 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1 — FIRST sets:**

Apply the standard rules iteratively:

**For `A`:**
- $A \rightarrow aA$: contributes $a$ to $\text{FIRST}(A)$.
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$: contributes $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(A)$.

$$
\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, \varepsilon\}
$$

**For `B`** (analogous):
$$
\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \varepsilon\}
$$

**For `S`:**
$S \rightarrow AB$. $\text{FIRST}(S) = \text{FIRST}(A) \cup (\text{if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A), \text{ then } \text{FIRST}(B))$.

Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A)$:

$$
\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b, \varepsilon\}
$$

**Step 2 — FOLLOW sets:**

Apply the rules:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{ \$\}$ (Rule 1).
- Production $S \rightarrow AB$: $A$ is followed by $B$, so add $\text{FIRST}(B) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B)$, also add $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- For $B$ at the end: add $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}}
$$

**Step 3 — LL(1) Verification:**

The LL(1) condition requires that for every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \ldots$, the sets $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ must be pairwise disjoint.

For $S$: only one production $S \rightarrow AB$. Condition trivially satisfied. ✓

For $A$: productions $A \rightarrow aA$ and $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- $\text{FIRST}(aA) = \{a\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$.

Disjoint? Yes — $\{a\} \cap \{\varepsilon\} = \emptyset$. ✓

For $B$: productions $B \rightarrow bB$ and $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- $\text{FIRST}(bB) = \{b\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$.

Disjoint? Yes. ✓

Since for **every** non-terminal with multiple productions, the FIRST sets are pairwise disjoint, the grammar **is LL(1)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to also check the **FOLLOW-based rule** for $\varepsilon$-productions: if $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ must be disjoint from $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ for all other productions $A \rightarrow \alpha$. Here, $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}$ is disjoint from $\{a\}$, and vice versa. Always include this in your answer.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Analysis of the Source Program** = Lexical + Syntax + Semantic analysis.
- ✅ **Lexical Analysis** converts characters → tokens using **regular expressions** and **DFAs**; uses **maximal munch**.
- ✅ **Syntax Analysis** converts tokens → parse tree using a **Context-Free Grammar**; produces a **leftmost** or **rightmost derivation**.
- ✅ **Semantic Analysis** adds meaning — **type checking**, **scope resolution**, **overload resolution**.
- ✅ **Symbol Table** is a *data structure* (not a phase) — typically a **hash table** with $O(1)$ average insert/lookup.
- ✅ **CFG** = $(V, T, P, S)$; **derivation** is denoted by $\Rightarrow$; **transitive closure** by $\xRightarrow{*}$.
- ✅ **Ambiguity** = existence of $\geq 2$ parse trees (or leftmost derivations) for the same string.
- ✅ **FIRST sets** = terminals that begin strings derivable from a non-terminal; include $\varepsilon$ if the non-terminal can derive the empty string.
- ✅ **FOLLOW sets** = terminals that can appear *immediately after* a non-terminal in some sentential form; always include `\$`.
- ✅ **LL(1) condition** = for each non-terminal, FIRST sets of right-hand sides must be **pairwise disjoint**.
- ✅ **Chomsky Hierarchy**: Type 3 (Regular, FA) ⊂ Type 2 (CFG, PDA) ⊂ Type 1 (CSG, LBA) ⊂ Type 0 (RE, TM).
- ✅ **Top-down parsers** = LL family, recursive descent; **Bottom-up parsers** = LR family, shift-reduce.
- ✅ **Engineering utility**: Forms the front-end of every modern compiler (GCC, Clang, javac) and every IDE.
- ✅ **KTU hot-button**: Ambiguity, FIRST/FOLLOW, LL(1) table construction, and DFA from RE — expect at least one of these in every ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phases of a Compiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases of a Compiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Phases of a Compiler

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Compiler** is a system software program that translates source code written in a high-level programming language (HPL) into machine-readable low-level language (target code / object code / assembly / machine code) while preserving the semantic meaning of the original program. The translation process is decomposed into a sequence of well-defined logical stages called **Phases of a Compiler**.

### The Two Fundamental Translation Models
| Model | Direction | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Compiler** | Whole program: HPL → Machine Code (one shot) | Generates standalone executable |
| **Interpreter** | Statement-by-statement: HPL → Execution | No intermediate object file produced |

> [!NOTE]
> **Java uses a hybrid approach** — `javac` compiles source `.java` to **bytecode** (`.class`), which is then **interpreted / JIT-compiled** by the JVM at runtime. This is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Human Translator
Imagine an English-to-Malayalam translator who does *not* just convert word-by-word in one go. Instead, they follow a strict pipeline:

1. **Read** the English sentence aloud (read source).
2. **Identify** each word's role — noun, verb, adjective (lexical analysis).
3. **Check** that the grammar is correct (syntax analysis).
4. **Resolve** meanings and references (semantic analysis).
5. **Write** a rough Malayalam version (intermediate code).
6. **Polish** the wording for elegance (optimization).
7. **Produce** the final translated document (target code).

A compiler works *exactly* like this — just with mathematical rigor and deterministic algorithms.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to draw the **block diagram of all phases**, list inputs and outputs of each phase, and state the software/hardware tools used (e.g., **LEX**, **YACC** for front-end phases).

### The Two Major Divisions

$$
\text{Compiler} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Analysis Phase (Front-End)}}_{\text{Understands the source}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Synthesis Phase (Back-End)}}_{\text{Produces the target code}}
$$

- **Analysis (Front-End):** Lexical, Syntax, Semantic Analysis + Intermediate Code Generation. *Machine-independent.*
- **Synthesis (Back-End):** Code Optimization + Code Generation. *Target-machine dependent.*
- **Auxiliary Components:** Symbol Table Manager + Error Handler — they *interact with* every phase, not run as a phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Phases of a Compiler

The standard KTU architecture decomposes translation into **6 logical phases** plus **2 auxiliary units**. Below is a high-yield summary table.

### 2.1.1 High-Yield Formula & Symbol Sheet

| # | Phase | Input | Output | Algorithm / Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Lexical Analysis (Scanner) | Source program (character stream) | Token stream | **Finite Automata (DFA/NFA)** — built using **LEX** |
| **2** | Syntax Analysis (Parser) | Token stream | **Parse Tree / Syntax Tree** | **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** — built using **YACC** |
| **3** | Semantic Analysis | Parse tree | Annotated parse tree / AST | Syntax-directed translation, type checking |
| **4** | Intermediate Code Generation | Annotated tree | **Three-Address Code (TAC)** / Quadruples | DAG, postfix notation |
| **5** | Code Optimization | Intermediate code | Optimized intermediate code | Constant folding, dead-code elimination |
| **6** | Code Generation | Optimized IR | Target / Assembly / Machine code | Register allocation, instruction selection |
| ★ | Symbol Table | Identifiers & attributes | Queryable data structure | Hash table / BST |
| ★ | Error Handler | Phase-specific errors | Diagnostic messages | Panic-mode, recovery schemes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "Symbol Table" and "Error Handler" as standalone phases in the diagram. They are **auxiliary/support modules** that interact with *all six phases*. Examiners deduct marks if they are placed in the main pipeline.

### 2.2 Detailed Phase Analysis

#### **Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanning)**
- **Purpose:** Read the source as a character stream and group characters into **lexemes**, then map each lexeme to a **token**.
- **Token = ⟨ TokenName, AttributeValue ⟩**
- **Example:** For `position = initial + rate * 60`, tokens are:
  - ⟨`id`, 1⟩, ⟨`assign`, −⟩, ⟨`id`, 2⟩, ⟨`+`, −⟩, ⟨`id`, 3⟩, ⟨`*`, −⟩, ⟨`60`, −⟩
- **Strips:** Whitespace and comments.
- **Why it matters:** Reduces program size dramatically — the parser never sees raw characters.

#### **Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing)**
- **Purpose:** Verify the token stream obeys the **grammar (CFG)** of the source language and build a **parse tree** or **AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)**.
- **Two strategies:**
  - **Top-Down Parsing:** Builds tree from root → leaves. Recursive descent, LL(1).
  - **Bottom-Up Parsing:** Builds from leaves → root. Shift-reduce, LR(1), SLR, LALR.
- **Errors detected:** Missing semicolons, unmatched braces, illegal expression structure.

#### **Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis**
- **Purpose:** Enforce **semantic rules** that the grammar alone cannot express.
- **Checks performed:**
  - **Type checking** — `int + float` → type coercion.
  - **Declaration checks** — variable must be declared before use.
  - **Operator-operand compatibility** — `array + array` invalid.
- **Output:** AST *annotated* with type information and explicit **type conversions (coercions)**.

#### **Phase 4 — Intermediate Code Generation**
- **Purpose:** Produce a **machine-independent intermediate representation (IR)** that is easy to optimize and easy to translate to any target.
- **Common IR forms:**
  - **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — at most 3 operands per instruction.
  - **Quadruples** — `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`.
  - **Triples** — `(op, arg1, arg2)`.
  - **Postfix notation** — stack-machine friendly.
- **Example (TAC for `a = b + c * 60`):**
  - `t1 = inttofloat(60)`
  - `t2 = id3 * t1`
  - `t3 = id2 + t2`
  - `id1 = t3`

#### **Phase 5 — Code Optimization**
- **Purpose:** Improve the IR so the final program runs **faster, uses less memory, or consumes less power** — without changing observable behavior.
- **Scope:**
  - **Local optimization** — within a basic block.
  - **Global optimization** — across basic blocks (data-flow analysis).
  - **Loop optimization** — inside loops (strength reduction, code motion).
- **Examples:**
  - **Constant folding:** `x = 3 * 4` → `x = 12`
  - **Strength reduction:** `x * 2` → `x << 1`
  - **Dead code elimination:** Remove unreachable / unused statements.
  - **Common subexpression elimination (CSE).**

> [!NOTE]
> The optimization in the example below turns the three-address code from Phase 4 into:
> `t1 = id3 * 60.0`
> `id1 = id2 + t1`
> This saves the explicit `inttofloat` conversion and one temporary.

#### **Phase 6 — Code Generation**
- **Purpose:** Map the optimized IR to **target machine code** (assembly or relocatable binary).
- **Key tasks:**
  - **Instruction selection** — choose fastest machine instructions.
  - **Register allocation** — assign temporaries to finite CPU registers.
  - **Instruction scheduling** — reorder to hide pipeline stalls.

#### **Auxiliary Module — Symbol Table**
A data structure storing every **identifier's name, type, scope, memory address, and attributes**. It is queried and updated by *every phase*. Implemented typically as a **hash table** for O(1) average lookup.

#### **Auxiliary Module — Error Handler**
Detects, reports, and tries to **recover** from errors at every phase. Recovery strategies include:
- **Panic-mode:** Skip tokens until a synchronizing token (e.g., `;`).
- **Phrase-level:** Local correction by insertion/deletion.
- **Error-productions:** Augment grammar with common-error rules.
- **Global-correction:** Minimum-edit-distance repair (expensive).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modern compilers (GCC, LLVM, Clang) are built precisely along this modular pipeline. **LLVM IR** is a famous real-world example of an intermediate representation, and **LLVM's optimization passes** are industry-grade implementations of Phase 5.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Complete Walkthrough: Tracing an Input Through All 6 Phases

We will trace the input expression

$$
\text{Input: } \; \texttt{position} = \texttt{initial} + \texttt{rate} \ast 60
$$

through every phase, showing the exact transformation.

---

### **Step 1 — Lexical Analysis**
The scanner reads characters and emits **tokens** using the rule `Token = (token-name, attribute-value)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source chars:} \quad & \texttt{p\,o\,s\,i\,t\,i\,o\,n\;=\;i\,n\,i\,t\,i\,a\,l\;+\;r\,a\,t\,e\;\ast\;6\,0} \\[4pt]
\text{Token stream:} \quad & \langle \texttt{id},\,1 \rangle\;\langle \texttt{:=},\,\_\rangle\;\langle \texttt{id},\,2 \rangle\;\langle \texttt{+},\,\_\rangle \\
& \langle \texttt{id},\,3 \rangle\;\langle \texttt{*},\,\_\rangle\;\langle \texttt{num},\,\texttt{60} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Each identifier is entered into the **symbol table** with its lexical address (`position→1, initial→2, rate→3`).

---

### **Step 2 — Syntax Analysis**
The parser, using the language's **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**, builds a **parse tree**. The CFG rule used is:

$$
E \;\rightarrow\; E \;+\; T \;\mid\; T
\qquad
T \;\rightarrow\; T \;\ast\; F \;\mid\; F
\qquad
F \;\rightarrow\; (E) \;\mid\; \texttt{id}
$$

**Parse Tree produced (text representation):**

```
        := 
       /   \
   id(1)    +
           /   \
         id(2)  *
               /   \
             id(3) id(4)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The parse tree is often simplified into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** by collapsing single-child chains — operators become internal nodes, identifiers become leaves.

---

### **Step 3 — Semantic Analysis**
The semantic analyzer performs **type checking**. The literal `60` is an `integer`, while `rate` may be a `float`. An **int-to-float coercion** is inserted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Lookup: } \texttt{rate} \rightarrow \texttt{float},\quad 60 \rightarrow \texttt{int} \\[4pt]
& \text{Decision: } \texttt{int} < \text{float} \Rightarrow \text{coerce 60 to float} \\[4pt]
& \text{Insert node: } \texttt{inttofloat(60)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Annotated AST:**

```
        := 
       /   \
   id(1)    +
           /   \
         id(2)  *
               /   \
             id(3) inttofloat
                       |
                      60
```

---

### **Step 4 — Intermediate Code Generation**
The annotated tree is linearized into **Three-Address Code (TAC)**, with at most one operator on the right-hand side per statement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathbf{t_1} \;:=\; \texttt{inttofloat}(60) \\
& \mathbf{t_2} \;:=\; \texttt{id3} \;\ast\; \mathbf{t_1} \\
& \mathbf{t_3} \;:=\; \texttt{id2} \;+\; \mathbf{t_2} \\
& \mathbf{id1} \;:=\; \mathbf{t_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

In **quadruple notation**, this becomes:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | inttofloat | 60 | — | t1 |
| (1) | * | id3 | t1 | t2 |
| (2) | + | id2 | t2 | t3 |
| (3) | := | t3 | — | id1 |

---

### **Step 5 — Code Optimization**
The optimizer applies two **machine-independent** transformations:

1. **Constant folding + Strength reduction** — The literal `60` is already a constant; combined with `inttofloat(60)`, the conversion can be done at compile time.
2. **Algebraic simplification** — Merge `inttofloat(60)` into the multiplication.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathbf{t_2} \;:=\; \texttt{id3} \;\ast\; 60.0 \\
& \mathbf{t_3} \;:=\; \texttt{id2} \;+\; \mathbf{t_2} \\
& \mathbf{id1} \;:=\; \mathbf{t_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

We **eliminated one temporary** and **one conversion instruction**.

---

### **Step 6 — Code Generation**
The optimized TAC is mapped to a target assembly (e.g., a 3-address RISC-style architecture). Sample output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{LDF} \quad R2,\, \texttt{id3}        \;\;\;\; \text{// load rate} \\
& \text{MUL} \quad R2,\, R2,\, \#60.0       \;\;\;\; \text{// rate} \times 60.0 \\
& \text{LDF} \quad R1,\, \texttt{id2}        \;\;\;\; \text{// load initial} \\
& \text{ADD} \quad R1,\, R1,\, R2            \;\;\;\; \text{// initial + result} \\
& \text{STF} \quad R1,\, \texttt{id1}        \;\;\;\; \text{// store into position}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final relocatable assembly code**, which an assembler + linker converts to an executable.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Summary of One Statement Through All Phases

| Phase | Input Artifact | Output Artifact | Tool / Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lexical | Char stream | Token stream | DFA, LEX |
| Syntax | Token stream | Parse tree / AST | CFG, YACC |
| Semantic | AST | Annotated AST | Type system, SDT |
| IR Generation | Annotated AST | 3-Address Code | DAG, Quadruples |
| Optimization | 3-Address Code | Optimized TAC | Data-flow analysis |
| Code Gen | Optimized TAC | Assembly / Machine code | Reg. allocation |
| **(Aux)** Symbol Table | Identifier names | Attribute record | Hash table |
| **(Aux)** Error Handler | Phase errors | Recovery actions | Panic-mode |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram — Phases of a Compiler

The diagram below shows the canonical Aho-Sethi-Ullman pipeline with auxiliary modules. Every node is alphanumeric and every label is plain text (Mermaid safe).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D --> E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E --> F[Code Optimizer]
    F --> G[Code Generator]
    G --> H[Target Machine Code]

    SYM[(Symbol Table Manager)] -.-> B
    SYM -.-> C
    SYM -.-> D
    SYM -.-> E
    SYM -.-> F
    SYM -.-> G

    ERR[(Error Handler)] -.-> B
    ERR -.-> C
    ERR -.-> D
    ERR -.-> E
    ERR -.-> F
    ERR -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *forward flow of code*; dashed arrows show that the symbol table and error handler *interact* with every phase. The pipeline is strictly left-to-right and *unidirectional* (with feedback only for error recovery).

---
