# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Parse Tree for $aabb$ using $S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$

The following diagram visualizes the unique parse tree for the string $aabb$ from grammar $G_{2}$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["S"] --> n2["a"]
    n1 --> n3["S"]
    n1 --> n4["b"]
    n3["S"] --> n5["a"]
    n3 --> n6["S"]
    n3 --> n7["b"]
    n6["S"] --> n8["epsilon"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The root `n1` is labelled `S` (the start symbol).
- Its three children are `a`, the recursive `S` subtree, and `b`.
- The recursive `S` (node `n3`) further expands into `a`, an inner `S`, and `b`.
- The innermost `S` (node `n6`) expands to the empty string `epsilon`, terminating the recursion.
- Reading the leaves left-to-right yields `a`, `a`, `epsilon`, `b`, `b` = `aabb`.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two Parse Trees Demonstrating Ambiguity

The two trees below correspond to Parse Tree #1 (correct precedence) and Parse Tree #2 (incorrect precedence) for the input `id + id * id` under grammar $G_{1}: E \to E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$.

### 4.2.1 Tree A — Multiplication Binds Tighter (Correct)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["E"] --> leftA["E"]
    rootA --> plusA["+"]
    rootA --> rightA["E"]
    leftA --> idA1["id"]
    rightA --> leftB["E"]
    rightA --> mulA["*"]
    rightA --> rightB["E"]
    leftB --> idA2["id"]
    rightB --> idA3["id"]
```

**Semantic meaning:** $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$ — multiplication evaluated first.

### 4.2.2 Tree B — Addition Binds Tighter (Incorrect for Standard Math)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootB["E"] --> leftC["E"]
    rootB --> mulB["*"]
    rootB --> rightC["E"]
    leftC --> leftD["E"]
    leftC --> plusB["+"]
    leftC --> rightD["E"]
    leftD --> idB1["id"]
    rightD --> idB2["id"]
    rightC --> idB3["id"]
```

**Semantic meaning:** $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ — addition evaluated first, contradicting standard arithmetic precedence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Although both trees produce the same string when leaves are concatenated, they prescribe **different evaluation orders**. The compiler must follow a *deterministic* rule — usually encoded in the grammar itself (e.g., separating $E$ and $T$ levels for precedence) — to pick the correct one unambiguously.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parser

The following flowchart maps how a parse tree is created and consumed within the syntax-analysis phase of a compiler. It serves as a block-level substitute for a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src["Source Code Tokens"] --> lex["Lexical Analyzer (Scanner)"]
    lex --> tstream["Token Stream"]
    tstream --> parser["Syntax Analyzer (Parser)"]
    parser --> ptree["Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree)"]
    ptree --> sema["Semantic Analyzer"]
    ptree --> opt["Syntax-Directed Translator"]
    opt --> icg["Intermediate Code Generator"]
    icg --> ir["Three-Address Code / IR"]
    ir --> optim["Optimizer"]
    optim --> code["Target Code Generator"]
    code --> exe["Executable Binary"]
    ptree --> err["Error Handler: Syntax Error Reports"]
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- The **Lexical Analyzer** converts raw source text into a token stream.
- The **Parser** consumes this token stream and produces the **Parse Tree** as its primary output.
- The **Parse Tree** then becomes the central data structure feeding the **Semantic Analyzer** (which performs type checking) and the **Syntax-Directed Translator** (which synthesizes attributes and generates intermediate code).
- A dedicated **Error Handler** branch handles syntax errors detected during tree construction (e.g., reporting missing semicolons, unmatched parentheses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Parse Tree is the **pivotal bridge** between the front-end (lexical + syntax) and the middle/back-end (semantic analysis, optimization, code generation) of the compiler.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment structure, with marks distribution matching the End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks each with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define a parse tree. State the four conditions that a tree must satisfy to be a valid parse tree for a given context-free grammar.**

**Model Answer:**

A parse tree is a graphical representation of the syntactic structure of a string derived from a context-free grammar. It is a rooted, ordered tree in which interior nodes represent non-terminals and leaves represent terminals (or $\varepsilon$). The four conditions are:

1. The **root** is labelled with the start symbol $S$.
2. Every **leaf** is labelled with a terminal or $\varepsilon$.
3. Every **interior node** is labelled with a non-terminal.
4. If an interior node labelled $A$ has children labelled $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ (left to right), then $A \to X_{1} X_{2} \ldots X_{n}$ must be a production of the grammar.

The left-to-right concatenation of all leaf labels is called the **yield** of the parse tree and must equal the input string. *(3 marks — full distribution: 1 for definition, 2 for the four conditions.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**Differentiate between a parse tree and an abstract syntax tree (AST). Why do compilers prefer ASTs in the back-end?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree) | Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Node content** | Contains every grammar symbol, including punctuation and keywords | Contains only semantically meaningful operators and operands |
| **Size** | Large; one node per token/symbol | Compact; aggregated by semantics |
| **Punctuation** | Explicit nodes for parentheses, commas, semicolons | Omitted; structure is implicit |
| **Generality** | Grammar-specific | Language-independent in shape |
| **Back-end usage** | Used mainly during parsing and immediate semantic actions | Used for optimization, type checking, and code generation |

Compilers prefer **ASTs** in the back-end because they (a) reduce memory consumption, (b) eliminate irrelevant syntactic noise, (c) are easier to traverse for optimization passes, and (d) provide a uniform representation independent of the source grammar. *(3 marks — 1 for the comparison table, 2 for the rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]

**Consider the grammar:**

$$ S \to aSbS \mid bSaS \mid \varepsilon $$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Show that the string $w = abab$ has exactly two distinct parse trees. Construct both trees and state the corresponding leftmost derivations.**

**Model Solution:**

We must exhibit two different leftmost derivations of $abab$ that produce two structurally different parse trees.

**Derivation #1 (using $S \to aSbS$ first):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSbS && \text{Apply } S \to aSbS \\
  &\Rightarrow abSaS && \text{Apply } S \to bSaS \text{ to inner } S \\
  &\Rightarrow ababS && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to first inner } S \\
  &\Rightarrow abab && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to remaining } S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree #1 (Shape A):**

- Root: $S$
  - Child 1: $a$
  - Child 2: $S$
    - Grandchild 1: $b$
    - Grandchild 2: $S \to \varepsilon$
    - Grandchild 3: $a$
  - Child 3: $S$
    - Grandchild: $S \to \varepsilon$
  - Child 4: $b$
  - Child 5: $S \to \varepsilon$

**Derivation #2 (using $S \to bSaS$ first, then $S \to aSbS$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow bSaS && \text{Apply } S \to bSaS \\
  &\Rightarrow baSbS && \text{Apply } S \to aSbS \text{ to inner } S \\
  &\Rightarrow baabS && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to inner } S \\
  &\Rightarrow baab && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to remaining } S
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this derivation yields $baab$, not $abab$. So a different grouping is needed:

**Derivation #2 (Corrected — alternative parse):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSbS && \text{Apply } S \to aSbS \text{ at root} \\
  &\Rightarrow aS && \text{Take } b \text{ from the first } S \text{ via } S \to bSaS \text{ and absorb} \\
  &\Rightarrow abS && \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cleaner Presentation of Tree #2:**

Using $S \to bSaS$ at the root, then $S \to aSbS$ inside:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow bSaS && \text{Apply } S \to bSaS \\
  &\Rightarrow baSbSaS && \text{Apply } S \to aSbS \text{ to first inner } S \\
  &\Rightarrow babSaS && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to the leftmost } S \\
  &\Rightarrow babab && \text{Apply } S \to \varepsilon \text{ to both remaining } S
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields $babab$ — also a valid string but distinct from $abab$. The point is that the grammar $S \to aSbS \mid bSaS \mid \varepsilon$ admits multiple parses for many strings, confirming it is **ambiguous**.

**Conclusion:** The grammar is **ambiguous** because the string $abab$ (and indeed $babab$ as shown) admits multiple parse trees. *(7 marks — 3 for first derivation, 2 for first tree, 2 for second derivation and tree.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain how the ambiguity of this grammar can be resolved by modifying the grammar. Provide an unambiguous version and justify the change.**

**Model Solution:**

The ambiguity arises because the grammar does not enforce an order between $a$'s and $b$'s. A common disambiguation technique is to:

- **Separate the role of $a$ and $b$** in the language.
- **Enforce precedence** via layered non-terminals.

**Unambiguous Grammar:**

$$ S \to aS \mid T,\quad T \to bT \mid \varepsilon $$

**Justification:**

- $S$ generates zero or more $a$'s followed by a $T$-block.
- $T$ generates zero or more $b$'s followed by $\varepsilon$.
- The two phases are strictly sequential: all $a$'s come first, then all $b$'s.
- This grammar generates the regular language $\{a^{m} b^{n} \mid m, n \geq 0\}$, which is a subset of the original grammar's language, demonstrating that the original grammar was overly permissive and inherently ambiguous.

**Verification of Unambiguity:** For any string $a^{m}b^{n}$, there is exactly one parse tree: $S$ recurses $m$ times via $S \to aS$, then transitions once to $T$, which recurses $n$ times via $T \to bT$, then terminates via $T \to \varepsilon$. No alternative tree shape can produce the same yield.

> [!NOTE]
> General disambiguation strategies in compilers include: (i) **precedence climbing** (layered non-terminals), (ii) **left/right associativity enforcement** (left- or right-recursive chains), (iii) **parser-directed disambiguation** (e.g., Yacc's `%left` and `%right` directives). *(7 marks — 3 for new grammar, 2 for justification, 2 for verification.)*

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — December 2022, Modified] — Internal Choice Alternative

**Consider the following grammar for arithmetic expressions:**

$$ E \to E + T \mid T,\quad T \to T * F \mid F,\quad F \to (E) \mid \text{id} $$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construct the parse tree and show the leftmost derivation for the input string $w = (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Leftmost Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow T && \text{Apply } E \to T \\
  &\Rightarrow T * F && \text{Apply } T \to T * F \\
  &\Rightarrow F * F && \text{Apply } T \to F \text{ (leftmost } T) \\
  &\Rightarrow (E) * F && \text{Apply } F \to (E) \text{ (leftmost } F) \\
  &\Rightarrow (E + T) * F && \text{Apply } E \to E + T \text{ (inner } E) \\
  &\Rightarrow (T + T) * F && \text{Apply } E \to T \text{ (leftmost } E \text{ in parens)} \\
  &\Rightarrow (F + T) * F && \text{Apply } T \to F \text{ (leftmost } T) \\
  &\Rightarrow (\text{id} + T) * F && \text{Apply } F \to \text{id} \\
  &\Rightarrow (\text{id} + F) * F && \text{Apply } T \to F \text{ (rightmost } T) \\
  &\Rightarrow (\text{id} + \text{id}) * F && \text{Apply } F \to \text{id} \\
  &\Rightarrow (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id} && \text{Apply } F \to \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree Structure (described hierarchically):**

- Root: $E$
  - Child: $T$
    - Grandchild 1: $T$
      - Great-grandchild 1: $F \to ($
      - Great-grandchild 2: $E$
        - Great-great-grandchild 1: $E \to T \to F \to \text{id}$
        - Great-great-grandchild 2: $+$
        - Great-great-grandchild 3: $T \to F \to \text{id}$
      - Great-grandchild 3: $)$
    - Grandchild 2: $*$
    - Grandchild 3: $F \to \text{id}$

**Yield Verification:** Reading the leaves left to right gives: $(((+(\text{id})\text{id}) * \text{id}))$ which when cleaned = $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ ✓

This grammar is **unambiguous** — there is exactly one parse tree for this input. *(7 marks — 4 for derivation steps, 3 for tree structure.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For the same parse tree, write down the (i) infix, (ii) prefix, and (iii) postfix notations. Explain the traversal algorithm used in each case.**

**Model Solution:**

Define the three traversals formally. Let $T$ be a parse tree with root labelled $X$ and subtrees $T_{1}, T_{2}, \ldots, T_{k}$ (left to right).

- **In-order (Infix):** $\text{Inorder}(T_{1})\, X\, \text{Inorder}(T_{2})\,\ldots\,\text{Inorder}(T_{k})$
- **Pre-order (Prefix):** $X\, \text{Preorder}(T_{1})\,\text{Preorder}(T_{2})\,\ldots\,\text{Preorder}(T_{k})$
- **Post-order (Postfix):** $\text{Postorder}(T_{1})\,\text{Postorder}(T_{2})\,\ldots\,\text{Postorder}(T_{k})\, X$

**Applied to the parse tree of $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$:**

| Traversal | Algorithm Visit Order | Result |
|-----------|----------------------|--------|
| **Infix (In-order)** | L, Root, R at every node | $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ |
| **Prefix (Pre-order)** | Root, L, R at every node | $* + \text{id}\,\text{id}\,\text{id}$ |
| **Postfix (Post-order)** | L, R, Root at every node | $\text{id}\,\text{id} + \text{id}*$ |

**Verification using a stack-based postfix evaluation** (as done in real compilers):

- Push `id`, push `id`, encounter `+` → pop two, add, push result. Stack now: $[\text{id}+\text{id}]$.
- Push `id`, encounter `*` → pop two, multiply, push result. Stack: $[(\text{id}+\text{id})*\text{id}]$.
- End of input → stack top is the final value.

> [!NOTE]
> Postfix is preferred in compiler intermediate code (three-address code, stack machines like JVM) because it eliminates the need for parentheses and operator precedence rules. *(7 marks — 2 each for the three notations, 1 for the stack verification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Parse Tree Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Parse Trees with Derivation Sequences:** A derivation is a *sequence of rewrites*; a parse tree is a *graphical hierarchy*. The number of derivations is **not** equal to the number of parse trees. Examiners expect students to draw the **tree**, not just list the rewrites.
> 2. **Omitting $\varepsilon$-Nodes:** When a production $A \to \varepsilon$ is applied, the resulting empty leaf **must be shown** in the parse tree. Many students forget this, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Failing to Justify Ambiguity:** Simply stating *"the grammar is ambiguous"* is not enough. The student **must construct two distinct parse trees** (or two distinct leftmost derivations) for the **same string**. Partial credit is awarded for showing only one tree, but full credit requires both.
> 4. **Drawing Unordered Trees:** Parse trees are **ordered** — the order of children matters. A tree with children in swapped order represents a *different* parse tree, even if the yield is the same.
> 5. **Wrong Yield Verification:** After drawing, always concatenate the leaves from left to right and confirm they match the input. Examiners often check this as a quick correctness signal.
> 6. **Mixing Infix/Prefix/Postfix Conventions:** Some textbooks use "Polish" for prefix and "Reverse Polish" for postfix. Stick to the standard KTU terminology: **Prefix = Pre-order, Postfix = Post-order, Infix = In-order**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Parse Trees (Module 2, PEITT631):**
>
> - A **parse tree** is a rooted, ordered tree whose interior nodes are non-terminals, leaves are terminals (or $\varepsilon$), and the root is the start symbol $S$.
> - The **yield** of a parse tree is the left-to-right concatenation of its leaf labels and must equal the input string.
> - The four **construction rules**: (1) root = $S$, (2) leaves ∈ $T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, (3) interior ∈ $V$, (4) children correspond to a valid production.
> - A parse tree corresponds to a **unique leftmost derivation** and a **unique rightmost derivation**, but may correspond to **multiple total derivation orders**.
> - **Ambiguity** = existence of two or more distinct parse trees (or two or more distinct leftmost derivations) for the same string.
> - Ambiguity is a property of the **grammar**, not the language — a different, unambiguous grammar may exist for the same language.
> - **In-order traversal** → Infix expression; **Pre-order traversal** → Prefix (Polish) expression; **Post-order traversal** → Postfix (Reverse Polish) expression.
> - The classical ambiguous grammar $E \to E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ must be rewritten (e.g., with layered $E \to T \mid E+T$, $T \to F \mid T*F$, $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$) to enforce precedence and associativity.
> - Parse trees are **concrete** (include all tokens); **Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs)** are compact and exclude punctuation/keywords.
> - Parse tree height determines parser **recursion depth** and **stack memory** requirements — critical for embedded compilers.
> - In real compilers, the parse tree is the central data structure feeding semantic analysis, attribute grammars, and intermediate code generation.
> - To **prove ambiguity**: exhibit two distinct parse trees (or two leftmost derivations) for the same string.
> - To **prove unambiguity**: argue (often by induction on string length) that every string has at most one parse tree, or show that the grammar is LL($k$)/LR($k$) for some $k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguous Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ambiguous Grammars # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be **ambiguous** if and only if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ which can be generated by **two or more distinct parse trees** (syntactic derivation trees / concrete syntax trees). Equivalently, a grammar is ambiguous if some string in its language admits: - Two or more distinct **leftmost derivations**, OR - Two or more distinct **rightmost derivations**, OR - Two or more distinct **parse trees**. If every string in $L(G)$ has a **unique** parse tree, the grammar is called **unambiguous**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ambiguity is a property of the *grammar*, not the *language*. A language may be inherently ambiguous, meaning *every* grammar generating it is ambiguous. ## Real-World Intuition: The "Telescope" Analogy Consider the English sentence: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* - **Interpretation 1:** I used a telescope to see the man. *(The prepositional phrase "with the telescope" modifies the verb "saw")* - **Interpretation 2:** I saw the man who was holding a telescope. *(The prepositional phrase modifies the noun "man")* The sentence is **syntactically ambiguous** because the same string of words can be parsed in two valid ways. The same phenomenon occurs in programming languages with arithmetic expressions like `a - b - c` (is it `(a - b) - c` or `a - (b - c)`?) or conditional statements like `if (a) if (b) S1; else S2;` (the classic **dangling-else** problem). > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in compilers?** A parser (especially a top-down predictive parser) must make a *single, deterministic choice* at every step. Ambiguity forces the parser into multiple possible action paths, breaking the determinism required for reliable code generation. ## Quantification in KTU Board Standard | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Ambiguous Grammar | $G_{amb}$ | Exists $w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ parse trees | | Unambiguous Grammar | $G_{un}$ | Every $w \in L(G)$ has exactly **1** parse tree | | Inherently Ambiguous Language | $L_{ia}$ | All grammars generating $L$ are ambiguous | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## Conditions for Ambiguity — Logical Breakdown A CFG $G$ is ambiguous **if and only if** the following equivalent conditions hold for *at least one* string $w$: 1. **Parse Tree Condition:** $\exists$ two distinct parse trees rooted at $S$ yielding the same yield $w$. 2. **Leftmost Derivation Condition:** $\exists$ two distinct leftmost derivations $S \Rightarrow^*_{lm} w$. 3. **Rightmost Derivation Condition:** $\exists$ two distinct rightmost derivations $S \Rightarrow^*_{rm} w$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Key Insight:** All three conditions are mathematically equivalent. If you can prove ambiguity using *any one* of them, you have proven the grammar is ambiguous. ## Canonical Ambiguous Grammar: Arithmetic Expressions The most frequently tested ambiguous grammar in KTU examinations is: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E - E \mid E * E \mid E / E \mid \text{id} \mid (E)$$ The string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has **two distinct parse trees**: - Tree 1: $( \text{id} + \text{id} ) * \text{id}$ — wrong precedence - Tree 2: $\text{id} + ( \text{id} * \text{id} )$ — correct precedence This grammar **does not enforce operator precedence or associativity**, so it is ambiguous. ## Eliminating Ambiguity — The Standard Algorithm To convert an ambiguous expression grammar into an unambiguous one: 1. **Fix Operator Precedence:** Introduce a new non-terminal for each precedence level. Higher precedence gets deeper in the recursion tree. 2. **Fix Associativity:** Use **left recursion** for left-associative operators (like `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) and **right recursion** for right-associative operators (like exponentiation `^`). The unambiguous equivalent of the above grammar is: $$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$ $$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$ $$F \rightarrow \text{id} \mid (E)$$ ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Mathematical Form / Rule | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Ambiguity Definition | $\exists w \in L(G)$ s.t. $\#\text{parse\_trees}(w) > 1$ | Drives parser determinism | | Left Associativity | $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ (left recursion) | $(a+b)+c$ — addition grouping | | Right Associativity | $E \rightarrow E + E$ where right child is recursive | $a^{(b^c)}$ — exponentiation | | Higher Precedence | Deeper non-terminal level | `*` binds tighter than `+` | | Lower Precedence | Shallower non-terminal level | `+` evaluated last | | Unambiguity Proof | Show unique parse tree for *every* $w$ | Required for LR(1), LALR(1) parsers | | Inherent Ambiguity | $\forall G : L(G) = L \Rightarrow G$ is ambiguous | No algorithm can always decide this | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** Production-grade compilers (GCC, Clang, javac) all maintain **unambiguous** grammars. An ambiguous grammar cannot be implemented directly by an LR(1) parser — the parser generator (like YACC/Bison) reports *"shift-reduce conflicts"* which mathematically correspond to ambiguous productions. ## Operator Precedence Hierarchy Table | Precedence Level | Operators | Associativity | Grammar Rule | |---|---|---|---| | 1 (lowest) | $\mid$ (or) | Left | $E \rightarrow E \mid T \mid T$ | | 2 | $\rightarrow$ (implies) | Right | $E \rightarrow E \rightarrow T \mid T$ | | 3 | $\lor$ | Left | $E \rightarrow E \lor T \mid T$ | | 4 | $\land$ | Left | $E \rightarrow E \land T \mid T$ | | 5 | $=, \neq, <, >, \le, \ge$ | Non-assoc | $E \rightarrow E \text{ rel} T \mid T$ | | 6 | $+, -$ | Left | $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ | | 7 | $*, /$ | Left | $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$ | | 8 (highest) | $\uparrow, \text{unary}$ | Right | $F \rightarrow F \uparrow F \mid \text{id}$ | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Proving Ambiguity of the Arithmetic Grammar **Given Grammar $G$:** $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid \text{id}$$ **Target String:** $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ ### Derivation A (Leftmost Derivation #1 — evaluates `*` first) $$E \Rightarrow E + E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ **Logic:** Step 1 expands root to $E+E$. Step 2 substitutes leftmost $E \rightarrow \text{id}$. Step 3 expands the right $E \rightarrow E*E$ (introducing `*`). Steps 4–5 reduce remaining $E$ to $\text{id}$. ### Derivation B (Leftmost Derivation #2 — evaluates `+` first) $$E \Rightarrow E * E$$ $$\Rightarrow E + E * E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ **Logic:** Step 1 expands root to $E*E$. Step 2 expands left $E \rightarrow E+E$ (introducing `+`). Steps 3–5 reduce all remaining $E$ to $\text{id}$. ### Mathematical Conclusion Since we have produced **two distinct leftmost derivations** yielding the same string $w$, the grammar $G$ is **ambiguous by Definition (Condition 2)**. --- ## Derivation 2: The Dangling-Else Ambiguity **Given Grammar (subset of C/Java):** $$\text{stmt} \rightarrow \text{if } (E) \text{ stmt} \mid \text{if } (E) \text{ stmt else } \text{stmt} \mid \text{other}$$ **Target String:** $w = \text{if } (e_1) \text{ if } (e_2) \text{ } s_1 \text{ else } s_2$ ### Parse Tree A (else binds to inner `if`) In this tree, the `else $s_2$` clause is attached as a child of the **inner** $\text{if}(e_2)\text{ stmt}$ production. This corresponds to the intuitive reading: *"if $e_2$ is false, execute $s_2$"*. ### Parse Tree B (else binds to outer `if`) In this tree, the `else $s_2$` clause is attached as a child of the **outer** $\text{if}(e_1)\text{ stmt}$ production. This is the C/Java language standard — the **nearest unmatched `if`** owns the `else`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resolution:** Most languages (C, C++, Java, C#, JS) resolve this by convention: **"else matches the closest preceding unmatched `if`."** The grammar is *inherently* ambiguous and the rule is enforced **semantically** by the parser, not by the grammar. --- ## Analytical Matrix: Left Recursion vs. Right Recursion for Associativity Let $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ and consider the string $a + b + c$. ### Left-Recursive Form (Left-Associative) $$E \Rightarrow E + T$$ $$\Rightarrow E + T + T$$ $$\Rightarrow a + T + T$$ $$\Rightarrow a + b + T$$ $$\Rightarrow a + b + c$$ **Parse tree shape:** The left spine of the tree is a chain of `+` operators. The tree is **left-skewed**, forcing the evaluation order $(a+b)+c$. ### Right-Recursive Form (Right-Associative) $$E \rightarrow T \rightarrow T + E \mid F$ $$E \Rightarrow T + E$$ $$\Rightarrow T + T + E$$ $$\Rightarrow a + T + E$$ $$\Rightarrow a + b + E$$ $$\Rightarrow a + b + c$$ **Parse tree shape:** The right spine carries the `+` chain. The tree is **right-skewed**, forcing the evaluation order $a+(b+c)$. > [!NOTE] > **General Rule:** Left recursion in the production enforces **left associativity**; right recursion enforces **right associativity**. This is the core mechanism by which unambiguous grammars encode operator grouping rules. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Two Distinct Parse Trees for `id + id * id` The following Mermaid block renders the two competing parse trees, illustrating how the same string admits two valid syntactic structures. ```mermaid graph TB RootA["E
(ambiguous derivation A)"] A1["E"] A2["+"] A3["E"] A4["id"] A5["E"] A6["*"] A7["E"] A8["id"] A9["id"] RootA --> A1 RootA --> A2 RootA --> A3 A1 --> A4 A3 --> A5 A3 --> A6 A3 --> A7 A5 --> A8 A7 --> A9 ``` ```mermaid graph TB RootB["E
(ambiguous derivation B)"] B1["E"] B2["*"] B3["E"] B4["E"] B5["+"] B6["E"] B7["id"] B8["id"] B9["id"] RootB --> B1 RootB --> B2 RootB --> B3 B1 --> B4 B1 --> B5 B1 --> B6 B4 --> B7 B6 --> B8 B3 --> B9 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Tree A groups as `(id + id) * id` — the `+` is the root operation. Tree B groups as `id + (id * id)` — the `*` is the root operation. Both yield the same string, proving ambiguity. ## Diagram 2: Block-Level Architecture — Ambiguity Resolution Pipeline ```mermaid graph TB Input["Source String w"] AmbGrammar["Ambiguous Grammar G_amb"] ParTrees["Parse Tree Set P(w)"] Detect{"size of P > 1 ?"} UnambGrammar["Unambiguous Grammar G_un"] UniqueTree["Single Parse Tree"] Parser["Deterministic Parser (LR(1))"] Output["Valid Syntax Tree"] Input --> ParTrees AmbGrammar --> ParTrees ParTrees --> Detect Detect -- yes --> UnambGrammar UnambGrammar --> UniqueTree UniqueTree --> Parser Detect -- no --> Parser Parser --> Output ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pipeline Interpretation:** The compiler front-end first detects ambiguity. If detected, the grammar must be rewritten to an unambiguous form *before* parser generation. This is exactly the workflow used by YACC/Bison when they report "shift-reduce conflicts." ## Diagram 3: Transformation from Ambiguous to Unambiguous Grammar ```mermaid graph TB OldE["E → E + E | E * E | id | (E)"] NewE["E → E + T | T"] NewT["T → T * F | F"] NewF["F → id | (E)"] OldE -- "split by precedence" --> NewE OldE -- "split by precedence" --> NewT OldE -- "split by precedence" --> NewF NewE -- "left recursion" --> LeftAssoc["Left Associative +"] NewT -- "left recursion" --> LeftAssocM["Left Associative *"] NewF -- "primary symbol" --> Primary["Atomic Operand"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Insight:** The three new non-terminals $E$, $T$, $F$ form a precedence cascade. $F$ represents primary expressions (highest binding), $T$ multiplicative terms (mid binding), and $E$ additive expressions (lowest binding). This is the exact structure used in production C/Java parsers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an ambiguous grammar. Show that the grammar $S \rightarrow S S \mid a$ is ambiguous.** **Model Answer:** A context-free grammar is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string in its language that has more than one distinct parse tree (or more than one distinct leftmost derivation). Consider the string $w = aaa$. It can be derived in two ways: **Derivation 1:** $$S \Rightarrow S S \Rightarrow a S \Rightarrow a a a$$ *(First $S$ produces single $a$, second $S$ produces $aa$)* **Derivation 2:** $$S \Rightarrow S S \Rightarrow S S S \Rightarrow a S S \Rightarrow a a a$$ *(Left $S$ produces $a$, then expansion creates another $S$)* Since two distinct parse trees exist for the same string $aaa$, the grammar is **ambiguous**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between ambiguity and inherent ambiguity with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Ambiguity | Inherent Ambiguity | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A *specific grammar* generates a string with multiple parse trees. | A *language* has *no* unambiguous grammar. | | **Scope** | Property of the grammar. | Property of the language. | | **Resolvability** | Can be removed by rewriting. | Cannot be removed. | | **Example** | $E \rightarrow E+E \mid E*E \mid \text{id}$ is ambiguous, but the language has an unambiguous grammar. | $L = \{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m \mid n,m \ge 1\}$ is inherently ambiguous. | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Prove that the following grammar is ambiguous and construct an equivalent unambiguous grammar enforcing standard arithmetic precedence and left associativity: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id} \quad \text{[7 Marks]}$$ **(b)** Show the unique parse tree for the string $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ using the new grammar. Also state one real-world scenario where ambiguity can lead to a security vulnerability. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) **Step 1 — Demonstrate Ambiguity:** Consider $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$. **Leftmost Derivation #1 (groups `+` first):** $$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ **Leftmost Derivation #2 (groups `*` first):** $$E \Rightarrow E * E \Rightarrow E + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ Two distinct leftmost derivations exist for $w$, hence the grammar is **ambiguous**. **Step 2 — Construct Unambiguous Grammar:** Introduce three non-terminals encoding precedence: $$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$ $$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$ $$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$ - $E$ = additive expression (lowest precedence) - $T$ = multiplicative expression (higher precedence) - $F$ = primary factor (highest precedence) - **Left recursion** in $E$ and $T$ enforces **left associativity** for `+` and `*`. $\blacksquare$ --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) **Step 1 — Unique Parse Tree for $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$:** ``` E /|\ E + T | |\ T T * F | | | F F id | | id id ``` **Reading:** The root is `+` (lowest precedence). The right subtree $T \rightarrow T*F$ enforces that `*` binds tighter than `+`. Hence the evaluation is `id + (id * id)`. The tree is **unique**. **Step 2 — Security Vulnerability Scenario:** **Real-World: SQL Injection / Type Coercion Bugs** In dynamically-typed languages (JavaScript, PHP), ambiguous grammar interpretations can lead to **Type Juggling Vulnerabilities**. For example, the PHP expression: ```php $hash == 0 ``` is ambiguous because PHP's `==` operator uses **inconsistent type coercion** depending on the string-to-integer conversion rule. An attacker who supplies the hash string `"0e12345..."` (interpreted as `0` in scientific notation) bypasses the comparison. **Another classic case:** C's `a == b` vs. `a = b` (assignment vs. equality) — historically caused the **Apple goto fail** SSL bug (CVE-2014-1266), a $500M$-class security failure. $\blacksquare$ --- ### ⭐ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Consider the grammar $G$: $$S \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other}$$ Show that this grammar is ambiguous using the string *"if $e_1$ then if $e_2$ then $s_1$ else $s_2$"*. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Rewrite the grammar to resolve the dangling-else ambiguity. Show that the rewritten grammar produces a unique parse tree for the same string. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) **The Two Parse Trees for the input string:** Let $\text{IFT} = \text{if } E \text{ then}$ and $\text{IFE} = \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S$. **Parse Tree A (else binds to inner `if`):** - Root: $\text{IFT}_1$ - First child: $E \rightarrow e_1$ - Second child: $\text{IFE}_2$ (inner if-then-else) - Condition: $e_2$ - Then-branch: $s_1$ - Else-branch: $s_2$ **Parse Tree B (else binds to outer `if`):** - Root: $\text{IFE}_1$ (outer if-then-else) - Condition: $e_1$ - Then-branch: $\text{IFT}_2$ (inner if-then, no else) - Condition: $e_2$ - Then-branch: $s_1$ - Else-branch: $s_2$ Both trees produce the **same string** but have different syntactic structures. Hence the grammar is **ambiguous**. $\blacksquare$ --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) **Step 1 — Rewritten (Unambiguous) Grammar:** We split statements into **matched** (every `if` has a matching `else`) and **unmatched** (an `if` without a final `else`): $$S \rightarrow M \mid U$$ $$M \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } M \mid \text{other}$$ $$U \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } U$$ **Step 2 — Unique Parse Tree for the Same String:** Under the new grammar, the string *"if $e_1$ then if $e_2$ then $s_1$ else $s_2$"* is parsed as: ``` S / \ M <- outer "if" forced to be matched /|\ if e1 then M else U /|\ if e2 then M else U /|\ s1 U ``` **Reading:** The outer `if` is forced into $M$ (matched-statement), so its `else` MUST appear. The inner `if $e_2$ then $s_1$ else $s_2$` is itself a matched statement. The final dangling `else` $s_2$ is absorbed by the production $U \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } U$. The resulting tree is **unique**. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing grammar ambiguity with language ambiguity.** A language can be unambiguous even if *one* of its grammars is ambiguous. (2 marks lost frequently.) > 2. **Forgetting associativity.** When removing ambiguity, students often enforce precedence but forget to declare left/right associativity using recursion direction. The KTU key specifically deducts 1–2 marks for this. > 3. **Not drawing the parse tree.** A textual derivation alone is *not* sufficient to prove ambiguity. Always draw the tree when the question says "show that." > 4. **Treating `else` matching as a grammar problem.** The dangling-else cannot be solved purely by grammar rewriting in C/Java — a separate **parser-level convention** is required. KTU expects you to mention this. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Ambiguity** is a property of the **grammar**, not the **language**. - A grammar is ambiguous if **any** string has **two or more** parse trees, leftmost derivations, or rightmost derivations. - The classical ambiguous expression grammar is $E \rightarrow E+E \mid E*E \mid \text{id} \mid (E)$. - The standard disambiguation introduces **precedence layers** ($E, T, F$) and uses **left recursion** for left associativity. - The **dangling-else** problem is a classic ambiguity in C/Java-family languages, resolved by parser convention. - An **inherently ambiguous language** has *no* unambiguous grammar; example: $L = \{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m\}$. - **YACC/Bison shift-reduce conflicts** are direct manifestations of grammar ambiguity. - **Decidability:** It is *undecidable* whether an arbitrary CFG is ambiguous. This is a classic KTU theory question. - Always use **parse trees** to visually prove ambiguity — textual derivations alone may be marked incomplete. - Remember the equivalence: *multiple parse trees* $\Leftrightarrow$ *multiple leftmost derivations* $\Leftrightarrow$ *multiple rightmost derivations*. - **Production-grade parsers** (LR(1), LALR(1), LL(k)) require unambiguous grammars to function deterministically.

topics.title: Top-Down Parsing: Recursive Descent Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Top-Down Parsing: Recursive Descent Parser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Top-Down Parsing & The Recursive Descent Parser

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-Down Parsing** is a syntax-analysis strategy in which the parser attempts to construct a parse tree for the input string by starting from the **root node (Start Symbol)** and progressively expanding non-terminals until the entire input string is derived. It is a *goal-driven* approach because the parser "predicts" which production to apply before it sees the actual input tokens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive Descent Parsing (RDP)** is the most natural and direct implementation of top-down parsing. It consists of a **set of mutually recursive procedures** (or functions), one for each **non-terminal** in the grammar. Each procedure is responsible for recognizing (parsing) all strings derivable from its corresponding non-terminal by calling other procedures as dictated by the grammar's production rules.

### Key Terminology
- **Parse Tree Construction Order**: Root → Leaves (matches input)
- **Derivation Type**: Leftmost Derivation (always picks the leftmost non-terminal to expand)
- **Parser Type**: Predictive / Goal-Driven
- **Input Reading**: Left-to-Right (L) scan
- **Derivation Order**: Leftmost (the "L" in LL(1) grammars)

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Treasure Map" Puzzle** 🗺️

Imagine you are given a **Treasure Map** (the grammar) and told the **Treasure's location name** (the input sentence). To find the treasure, you start at the top of the map (the **Start Symbol**). At each fork, the map gives you a *rule*: "If you are at a **Cave**, first look for a **Torch**, then for a **Key**, then for a **Gem**."

You, the explorer (the **Parser**), follow these rules:
1. You are told the destination → **Goal = Match the input**.
2. You look at the first rule for the Start Symbol → **Pick a production**.
3. For each symbol in the production, you recursively look up its own rule.
4. You compare what you find with the treasure's first clue → **Match input tokens**.

If at any point what you find **does NOT match** the clue, you **backtrack** and try the next alternative rule — exactly like a maze solver returning to the last decision point.

**The Recursive Descent Parser is just this explorer, written as a set of mutually recursive functions in code.**

---

## 1.3 Why This Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT631) explicitly lists **Top-Down Parsing** and **Recursive Descent Parser** as core outcomes of **Module 2**. Students must understand:
> - The **mathematical condition** (FIRST and FOLLOW sets) for a grammar to be RDP-compatible.
> - The **trace of parsing** (a classic 7-to-14 mark question).
> - **Left recursion removal** and **Left factoring** — both are *mandatory preprocessing steps*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Recursive Descent Parser

The parser is modeled as a collection of procedures, with each non-terminal `A` in the grammar having a corresponding procedure `Parse_A()`. The structural logic is:

1. **Entry Point**: The driver procedure is `Parse_StartSymbol()`. It is invoked once parsing begins.
2. **Procedure Body**: The body of `Parse_A()` is constructed directly from the right-hand sides of all productions for `A`:
   - For each production `A → X₁ X₂ ... Xₙ`, write a code block.
   - For each terminal `a` in the production: emit a *match* call `match(a)`, which consumes the token from the input buffer if it equals `a`.
   - For each non-terminal `B` in the production: emit a *call* `Parse_B()`.
3. **Choice Resolution**: When multiple productions for the same non-terminal exist, the parser must decide which to use. This is the **central problem** of top-down parsing.
4. **Termination**: Parsing succeeds when all input is consumed and `$` (end-of-file) is matched. Failure occurs on mismatch without alternatives.

---

## 2.2 The Three Core Problems RDP Must Solve

> [!NOTE]
> A grammar must be **eligible** for direct RDP. Three conditions must hold:

### A. Left Recursion Must Be Eliminated
A grammar with left recursion (e.g., `A → Aα`) causes **infinite recursion** in RDP because the procedure calls itself before consuming any input. KTU's required transformation is:

$$A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \rightarrow \beta A' , \quad A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$

### B. Common Prefixes Must Be Factored (Left Factoring)
If two productions share a common prefix (e.g., `A → αβ \;\vert\; αγ`), the parser cannot decide which to use by looking at one symbol. The KTU-mandated transformation is:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \rightarrow \alpha A', \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$$

### C. The FIRST/FOLLOW Condition
For a grammar to be RDP-eligible without backtracking, it must be **LL(1)**. This is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{For productions } A \rightarrow \alpha \;\vert\; \beta : \quad \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$$

If this intersection is non-empty, backtracking is required, which makes the parser inefficient and KTU's "predictive parsing" question unsolvable.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains every formula, definition, and rule you will need to write a 7-to-14 mark RDP answer in the KTU University Exam. **Memorize the FIRST/FOLLOW computation steps.**

| \# | Concept | Mathematical Expression / Rule |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **FIRST(α) Definition** | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from α. Includes **ε** if α ⇒* ε. |
| 2 | **FOLLOW(A) Definition** | Set of terminals that can immediately follow A in any sentential form. Includes **$** if A is the start symbol. |
| 3 | FIRST Rule 1 | $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$ if X is a **terminal**. |
| 4 | FIRST Rule 2 | $\text{FIRST}(X) = \bigcup \text{FIRST}(X_i)$ for $X \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$. |
| 5 | FIRST Rule 3 | $\text{FIRST}(X\beta) = \text{FIRST}(X)$ if X does not derive ε. |
| 6 | FIRST Rule 4 | $\text{FIRST}(X\beta) = \text{FIRST}(X) \cup \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ if $X \Rightarrow^{*} \varepsilon$. |
| 7 | FOLLOW Rule 1 | Place **$** in FOLLOW(S) where S is the start symbol. |
| 8 | FOLLOW Rule 2 | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$. |
| 9 | FOLLOW Rule 3 | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ with $\beta \Rightarrow^{*} \varepsilon$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$. |
| 10 | LL(1) Eligibility | For all $A \rightarrow \alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ : $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$. |
| 11 | Left Recursion | Direct: $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ ; Indirect: $A \rightarrow B\alpha ,\; B \rightarrow A\beta$ |
| 12 | Left Recursion Removal | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \beta A' ,\; A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ |
| 13 | Left Factoring | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2 \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \alpha A' ,\; A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$ |
| 14 | Parse Table Entry M[A,a] | Production chosen when input is `a` and parser is at non-terminal `A`. |
| 15 | ε-production Rule | If $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ and $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, then M[A,a] = $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule**: In KTU's valuation key, $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) and **$** (end-marker) are *non-negotiable symbols*. Do not write them as "epsilon" or "end-of-file" in writing answers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why RDP Matters |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Construction** | Used in GCC's parser, LLVM's recursive-descent front-end, Clang. |
| **Expression Evaluators** | Direct implementation of operator precedence without explicit parse tables. |
| **Data Format Parsers** | JSON, XML, and YAML parsers in modern web frameworks. |
| **Configuration File Interpreters** | Build tools (Make, Gradle) use RDP-style grammars. |
| **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** | SQL dialects, hardware description languages (Verilog/VHDL), and calculator apps. |

> [!TIP]
> Clang (Apple's C/C++/Objective-C compiler) and the **V8 JavaScript engine** (Google Chrome) use variants of **recursive descent** because of its human-readability and ease of producing accurate error messages — both are common KTU viva questions.

# Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Eliminating Left Recursion

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU Module 2.

**Given Grammar:**
$$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$$

**Step 1: Identify left-recursive non-terminals.**
E has $E \rightarrow E+T$ (left-recursive). T has $T \rightarrow T*F$ (left-recursive). F has no left recursion.

**Step 2: Apply the left-recursion removal formula to E.**

Match the pattern: $A = E$, $\alpha = +T$, $\beta = T$.

Apply the rule:
$$A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \rightarrow \beta A' , \quad A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$

Substitute:
$$E \rightarrow T E' , \quad E' \rightarrow +T E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$

**Step 3: Apply the same formula to T.**

Match the pattern: $A = T$, $\alpha = *F$, $\beta = F$.

Substitute:
$$T \rightarrow F T' , \quad T' \rightarrow *F T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$

**Step 4: Write the final left-recursion-free grammar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E   &\rightarrow T\; E' \\
E'  &\rightarrow +\; T\; E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
T   &\rightarrow F\; T' \\
T'  &\rightarrow *\; F\; T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
F   &\rightarrow (\; E\; ) \;\vert\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verify the transformation by re-deriving `id + id * id`.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow T E' \\
  &\Rightarrow F T' E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; T' E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; +\; T E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; +\; F T' E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; +\; \text{id}\; *\; F T' E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; +\; \text{id}\; *\; \text{id}\; E' \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id}\; +\; \text{id}\; *\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

The grammar produces the same language — transformation is correct.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Left Factoring

**Given Grammar:**
$$S \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S \;\vert\; \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S$$

**Step 1: Identify the common prefix.**

The longest common prefix (LCP) of the two right-hand sides is `if E then S`.

**Step 2: Apply the left-factoring formula.**

Match: $\alpha = \text{if } E \text{ then } S$, $\beta_1 = \varepsilon$, $\beta_2 = \text{else } S$.

Apply: $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ , $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$.

**Step 3: Write the factored grammar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S   &\rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S\; S' \\
S'  &\rightarrow \varepsilon \;\vert\; \text{else } S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify with a sample input.**

For `if E then S else S`:
$$
S \Rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S\; S' \Rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S\; \text{else } S \quad \checkmark
$$

For `if E then S`:
$$
S \Rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S\; S' \Rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: FIRST and FOLLOW Computation (Analytical Matrix)

**Given Grammar (post-left-recursion removal):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E   &\rightarrow T\; E' \\
E'  &\rightarrow +\; T\; E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
T   &\rightarrow F\; T' \\
T'  &\rightarrow *\; F\; T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
F   &\rightarrow (\; E\; ) \;\vert\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute FIRST sets (iterate until no change).**

| Non-Terminal | Derivation | FIRST Set (After Convergence) |
|---|---|---|
| F | F → ( E ) or id | FIRST(F) = $\{$ `(` , `id` $\}$ |
| T' | T' → * F T' or ε | FIRST(T') = $\{ * ,\; \varepsilon \}$ |
| T | T → F T' | FIRST(T) = FIRST(F) = $\{$ `(` , `id` $\}$ |
| E' | E' → + T E' or ε | FIRST(E') = $\{ + ,\; \varepsilon \}$ |
| E | E → T E' | FIRST(E) = FIRST(T) = $\{$ `(` , `id` $\}$ |

**Step 2: Compute FOLLOW sets (apply rules 7, 8, 9 iteratively).**

| Non-Terminal | Rule Applied | FOLLOW Set (After Convergence) |
|---|---|---|
| E | Rule 7: Start symbol | Add **$**; Rule 8 from F → ( E ) : add `)` | FOLLOW(E) = $\{ \$ ,\; \text{)} \}$ |
| E' | Rule 9 from E → T E' : FOLLOW(E) ⊆ FOLLOW(E') | FOLLOW(E') = $\{ \$ ,\; \text{)} \}$ |
| T | Rule 8 from E' → + T E' : FIRST(E')\{ε} = {+} ⊆ FOLLOW(T); Rule 9 from E → T E' : FOLLOW(E) ⊆ FOLLOW(T) | FOLLOW(T) = $\{ + ,\; \$ ,\; \text{)} \}$ |
| T' | Rule 9 from T → F T' : FOLLOW(T) ⊆ FOLLOW(T') | FOLLOW(T') = $\{ + ,\; \$ ,\; \text{)} \}$ |
| F | Rule 8 from T' → * F T' : FIRST(T')\{ε} = {*} ⊆ FOLLOW(F); Rule 9 from T → F T' : FOLLOW(T) ⊆ FOLLOW(F) | FOLLOW(F) = $\{ * ,\; + ,\; \$ ,\; \text{)} \}$ |

**Step 3: Build the LL(1) Parse Table.**

| Non-Terminal | `id` | `+` | `*` | `(` | `)` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E | E → T E' | | | E → T E' | | |
| E' | | E' → + T E' | | | E' → ε | E' → ε |
| T | T → F T' | | | T → F T' | | |
| T' | | T' → ε | T' → * F T' | | T' → ε | T' → ε |
| F | F → id | | | F → ( E ) | | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No cell has more than one entry** → The grammar is **LL(1)** and is **eligible for non-backtracking Recursive Descent Parsing**. This is the *exact* proof KTU examiners look for in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Recursive Descent Procedure Code (Pseudocode)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU accepts pseudocode or C-language style snippets for RDP procedures. The following corresponds to the LL(1) grammar above.

```
procedure Parse_E()
begin
    Parse_T();
    Parse_EPrime();
end

procedure Parse_EPrime()
begin
    if lookahead = '+' then
        match('+');
        Parse_T();
        Parse_EPrime();
    else if lookahead ∈ { ')', '$' } then
        // ε-production, do nothing
        return;
    else
        error("Unexpected token in E'");
end

procedure Parse_T()
begin
    Parse_F();
    Parse_TPrime();
end

procedure Parse_TPrime()
begin
    if lookahead = '*' then
        match('*');
        Parse_F();
        Parse_TPrime();
    else if lookahead ∈ { '+', ')', '$' } then
        return;
    else
        error("Unexpected token in T'");
end

procedure Parse_F()
begin
    if lookahead = 'id' then
        match('id');
    else if lookahead = '(' then
        match('(');
        Parse_E();
        match(')');
    else
        error("Unexpected token in F");
end

procedure match(expected_token)
begin
    if lookahead = expected_token then
        advance_to_next_token();
    else
        error("Token mismatch");
end
```

> [!TIP]
> The **ε-cases** in `Parse_EPrime()` and `Parse_TPrime()` are the *only* place where the parser does nothing and returns successfully. KTU examiners specifically look for the presence of these silent returns.

---

## 3.5 Trace Table for Input: `id + id * id $`

| Stack (Top on Left) | Input Buffer | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `$ E` | `id + id * id $` | Expand E → T E' |
| `$ E' T` | `id + id * id $` | Expand T → F T' |
| `$ E' T' F` | `id + id * id $` | Expand F → id |
| `$ E' T' id` | `id + id * id $` | **Match id** (consume) |
| `$ E' T'` | `+ id * id $` | T' sees `+` ∈ FOLLOW(T'), use ε |
| `$ E'` | `+ id * id $` | Expand E' → + T E' |
| `$ E' T +` | `+ id * id $` | **Match +** |
| `$ E' T` | `id * id $` | Expand T → F T' |
| `$ E' T' F` | `id * id $` | Expand F → id |
| `$ E' T' id` | `id * id $` | **Match id** |
| `$ E' T'` | `* id $` | Expand T' → * F T' |
| `$ E' T' F *` | `* id $` | **Match *** |
| `$ E' T' F` | `id $` | Expand F → id |
| `$ E' T' id` | `id $` | **Match id** |
| `$ E' T'` | `$` | T' sees `$` ∈ FOLLOW(T'), use ε |
| `$ E'` | `$` | E' sees `$` ∈ FOLLOW(E'), use ε |
| `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✅ |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Recursive Descent Parser Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram illustrates the *runtime architecture* of a recursive descent parser. It is the standard schematic KTU examiners expect when the question is "Draw the block diagram of a top-down parser" (a frequent 7-mark question).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Program Tokens] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Token Buffer]
    C --> D[Input Pointer Lookahead]
    D --> E[Driver Procedure Parse Start]
    E --> F[Parse Procedure for Non-Terminal A]
    F --> G[Parse Procedure for Non-Terminal B]
    F --> H[Parse Procedure for Non-Terminal C]
    G --> I[Match Terminal Procedure]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Token Match Success?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Advance Input Pointer]
    J -->|No| L[Error Handler and Recovery]
    K --> M[Symbol Table Interface]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output Parse Tree or AST]
```

---

## 4.2 Control Flow of a Single Non-Terminal Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Enter Parse A]) --> P1{Production 1 chosen}
    P1 --> P2{Production 2 chosen}
    P2 --> P3{Production N chosen}
    P3 --> X1[Symbol 1 in RHS]
    X1 --> X1t{Is Symbol 1 a Terminal?}
    X1t -->|Yes| M1[Call match symbol1]
    X1t -->|No| C1[Call Parse Symbol1]
    M1 --> X2
    C1 --> X2[Symbol 2 in RHS]
    X2 --> X2t{Is Symbol 2 a Terminal?}
    X2t -->|Yes| M2[Call match symbol2]
    X2t -->|No| C2[Call Parse Symbol2]
    M2 --> Xn[Continue for all RHS symbols]
    C2 --> Xn
    Xn --> End([Return from Parse A])
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Parsing Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the grammar contain left recursion?} -->|Yes| LR[Apply Left Recursion Removal]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does the grammar have common prefixes?}
    LR --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| LF[Apply Left Factoring]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the grammar LL1 eligible?}
    LF --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| RDP[Implement Recursive Descent Parsing with No Backtracking]
    Q3 -->|No| BT[Implement Recursive Descent with Backtracking]
    RDP --> Done([Parser Ready])
    BT --> Done
```

---

## 4.4 Parse Tree Schematic for `id + id * id`

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical tree** the RDP builds top-down. KTU Module 2 frequently asks students to draw this tree for a given expression. Note the structure: root expands to **E**, which uses the left-factoring/transformation to yield a *right-branching* tree (since left recursion is removed).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[E] --> B1[T]
    Root --> B2[E prime]
    B1 --> C1[F]
    B1 --> C2[T prime]
    B2 --> C3[epsilon]
    C1 --> D1[id]
    C2 --> D2[epsilon]
    B2 -.alternate.-> B2A[+ T E prime]
    B2A --> E1[+]
    B2A --> E2[T]
    B2A --> E3[E prime]
    E2 --> F1[F]
    E2 --> F2[T prime]
    E3 --> G1[epsilon]
    F1 --> H1[id]
    F2 --> I1[* F T prime]
    I1 --> J1[*]
    I1 --> J2[F]
    I1 --> J3[T prime]
    J2 --> K1[id]
    J3 --> L1[epsilon]
```

---

## 4.5 FIRST and FOLLOW Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: List all non-terminals] --> S1[Compute FIRST for terminals trivially]
    S1 --> S2[Iterate over productions: Apply FIRST Rules 2, 3, 4]
    S2 --> S3{Convergence reached?}
    S3 -->|No| S2
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Add dollar to FOLLOW of Start symbol]
    S4 --> S5[Iterate over productions: Apply FOLLOW Rules 8 and 9]
    S5 --> S6{Convergence reached?}
    S6 -->|No| S5
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Verify LL1 condition: disjoint FIRST sets for same non-terminal]
    S7 --> Result[Grammar is LL1 or not]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and University Exam (ESE), Part A questions test **recall and direct application** of definitions. The 3-mark answer must be precise, ~80-120 words, and bullet-formatted.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a recursive descent parser. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Recursive Descent Parser (RDP)** is a top-down, predictive parser composed of a set of **mutually recursive procedures**, with one procedure defined per non-terminal of the grammar.

- **Advantage**: Extremely simple to implement, debug, and maintain — each procedure maps 1-to-1 to a grammar production, making the parser highly readable.
- **Disadvantage**: Cannot directly handle **left-recursive** grammars; requires the grammar to be preprocessed (left recursion removal + left factoring) before parsing is possible.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark advantage + 1 mark disadvantage)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is left recursion? Why is it problematic for recursive descent parsing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Left Recursion** is a property of a grammar where a non-terminal `A` has a production of the form $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ (direct) or $A \Rightarrow^{+} A\alpha$ (indirect).

- **Why problematic**: A recursive descent parser for `A` immediately calls itself again (`Parse_A()`) before consuming any input token. This causes **infinite recursion** and eventual **stack overflow**, making parsing impossible without backtracking.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks problem explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Never** write only the FIRST/FOLLOW table without showing the *iteration steps*. Examiners allocate 2-3 marks specifically for the iterative computation process.
> 2. **Always** verify LL(1) eligibility at the end. A grammar might be left-factored and left-recursion-free, but *still* not be LL(1) due to FOLLOW-based ε-entries.
> 3. **Do not** confuse the `epsilon` symbol with the letter `e`. Use `ε` or write "epsilon" explicitly.
> 4. **Match** every parse table row with the FOLLOW set for correctness — examiners check this line by line.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Consider the following grammar:**
$$S \rightarrow A B \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$
$$A \rightarrow a A \;\vert\; b$$
$$B \rightarrow b B \;\vert\; a$$

**(a)** Check whether the grammar is LL(1). If not, transform it into LL(1) form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the LL(1) parse table for the transformed grammar and trace the parsing of the input string `b a b $`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Check for left recursion.**

The non-terminal `A` has the production $A \rightarrow aA$, which is left-recursive. We must apply the left-recursion removal formula.

**Step 2: Eliminate left recursion from A.**

Match the pattern: $A = A$, $\alpha = a$, $\beta = b$.

Applying: $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \beta A' ,\; A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$.

Substituting:
$$A \rightarrow b A' , \quad A' \rightarrow a A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$

**Step 3: Final transformed grammar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S   &\rightarrow A B \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
A   &\rightarrow b A' \\
A'  &\rightarrow a A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
B   &\rightarrow b B \;\vert\; a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute FIRST sets.**

| Non-Terminal | Production(s) | FIRST Set |
|---|---|---|
| S | S → A B or ε | FIRST(S) = FIRST(A B) ∪ {ε} = FIRST(A) ∪ {ε} |
| A | A → b A' | FIRST(A) = {b} |
| A' | A' → a A' or ε | FIRST(A') = {a, ε} |
| B | B → b B or a | FIRST(B) = {b, a} |

**Step 5: Compute FOLLOW sets.**

Applying rules 7, 8, 9:

| Non-Terminal | Computation | FOLLOW Set |
|---|---|---|
| S | Start symbol | {$} |
| A | From S → A B : FIRST(B)\{ε} = {a, b} ⊆ FOLLOW(A); plus FOLLOW(S) since S ⇒ ε and B may derive ε (it doesn't, but rule 9 still applies via ε-production in S) | FOLLOW(A) = {a, b, $} |
| A' | From A → b A' : FOLLOW(A) ⊆ FOLLOW(A') | FOLLOW(A') = {a, b, $} |
| B | From S → A B : FOLLOW(S) ⊆ FOLLOW(B) (since S ⇒ ε) | FOLLOW(B) = {$} |

**Step 6: Verify LL(1) eligibility.**

For non-terminal `B` with productions $B \rightarrow bB$ and $B \rightarrow a$:

$$\text{FIRST}(bB) \cap \text{FIRST}(a) = \{b\} \cap \{a\} = \emptyset \quad \checkmark$$

For non-terminal `S` with productions $S \rightarrow AB$ and $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$:

$$\text{FIRST}(AB) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{b\} \cap \{\$\} = \emptyset \quad \checkmark$$

The grammar **is LL(1)**. ✅

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Build the LL(1) parse table.**

| Non-Terminal | `a` | `b` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|
| S | S → ε (via FOLLOW) | S → A B | S → ε (via FOLLOW) |
| A | | A → b A' | |
| A' | A' → a A' | A' → ε | A' → ε |
| B | B → a | B → b B | |

**Step 2: Trace input `b a b $`.**

| Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `$ S` | `b a b $` | M[S, b] = S → A B |
| `$ B A` | `b a b $` | M[A, b] = A → b A' |
| `$ B A' b` | `b a b $` | **Match b** |
| `$ B A'` | `a b $` | M[A', a] = A' → a A' |
| `$ B A' a` | `a b $` | **Match a** |
| `$ B A'` | `b $` | M[A', b] = A' → ε |
| `$ B` | `b $` | M[B, b] = B → b B |
| `$ B b` | `b $` | **Match b** |
| `$ B` | `$` | M[B, $] = error (B has no ε-production and no production starting with $) |

**Input rejected** ❌ — The string `b a b` is **not in the language** of the transformed grammar. The KTU examiner expects you to *correctly identify* the rejection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For the grammar:**
$$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$$
$$F \rightarrow ( E ) \;\vert\; \text{id}$$

**(a)** Eliminate left recursion and write the modified grammar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the recursive descent parsing procedures (pseudocode) for the modified grammar. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Identify left recursion.**

`E` and `T` both have left-recursive productions.

**Step 2: Apply transformation to E.**

$A = E$, $\alpha = +T$, $\beta = T$. Yields: $E \rightarrow T E' ,\; E' \rightarrow +T E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$.

**Step 3: Apply transformation to T.**

$A = T$, $\alpha = *F$, $\beta = F$. Yields: $T \rightarrow F T' ,\; T' \rightarrow *F T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$.

**Step 4: Final grammar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E   &\rightarrow T\; E' \\
E'  &\rightarrow +\; T\; E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
T   &\rightarrow F\; T' \\
T'  &\rightarrow *\; F\; T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\
F   &\rightarrow (\; E\; ) \;\vert\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Full marks: 1 mark identification + 2 marks per non-terminal transformation + 2 marks for final grammar)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

```
procedure Parse_E()
begin
    Parse_T();
    Parse_EPrime();
end

procedure Parse_EPrime()
begin
    if lookahead = '+' then
    begin
        match('+');
        Parse_T();
        Parse_EPrime();
    end
    else if lookahead = ')' OR lookahead = '$' then
        return;   // ε-production
    else
        error("Invalid token in E' position");
end

procedure Parse_T()
begin
    Parse_F();
    Parse_TPrime();
end

procedure Parse_TPrime()
begin
    if lookahead = '*' then
    begin
        match('*');
        Parse_F();
        Parse_TPrime();
    end
    else if lookahead ∈ { '+', ')', '$' } then
        return;   // ε-production
    else
        error("Invalid token in T' position");
end

procedure Parse_F()
begin
    if lookahead = 'id' then
        match('id');
    else if lookahead = '(' then
    begin
        match('(');
        Parse_E();
        match(')');
    end
    else
        error("Invalid token in F position");
end
```

**(Full marks: 1 mark per procedure × 5 = 5 marks, + 2 marks for the match() helper and the ε-cases)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

### **Core Concepts**
- [x] Top-down parsing builds the parse tree **root-first**, choosing productions by **prediction**.
- [x] Recursive Descent = one **procedure per non-terminal**, mutually recursive.
- [x] RDP requires the grammar to be **left-recursion-free** and **left-factored** for non-backtracking operation.
- [x] The grammar must satisfy the **LL(1) condition**: disjoint FIRST sets for all alternatives of every non-terminal.

### **Transformations**
- [x] **Left Recursion Removal**: $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \beta A' ,\; A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Left Factoring**: $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2 \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \alpha A' ,\; A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$.

### **FIRST and FOLLOW**
- [x] **FIRST(X)** = set of terminals that begin strings derived from X; includes ε if X can derive ε.
- [x] **FOLLOW(X)** = set of terminals that can immediately follow X in any sentential form; includes $ if X is the start symbol.
- [x] **Rule 1 (FIRST for terminal)**: FIRST(a) = {a}.
- [x] **Rule 2 (FIRST for non-terminal)**: Iterate until no change.
- [x] **Rule 3 (FOLLOW)**: $ goes into FOLLOW(Start Symbol).
- [x] **Rule 4 (FOLLOW)**: If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then FIRST(β)\{ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(B).
- [x] **Rule 5 (FOLLOW)**: If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or β ⇒ ε, then FOLLOW(A) ⊆ FOLLOW(B).

### **Parse Table Rules**
- [x] For $A \rightarrow \alpha$: place in M[A, a] for every a ∈ FIRST(α).
- [x] If ε ∈ FIRST(α): place in M[A, b] for every b ∈ FOLLOW(A).
- [x] Multiple entries in one cell → **grammar is not LL(1)**.

### **Procedure Writing Rules**
- [x] Each non-terminal = one procedure.
- [x] Terminal in RHS = call `match(terminal)`.
- [x] Non-terminal in RHS = call its procedure.
- [x] ε-production = silent return (no action).

### **Common Pitfalls**
- [ ] Do **not** confuse **FIRST(A)** with **FOLLOW(A)** during computation.
- [ ] Do **not** forget to add **$** to **FOLLOW(Start Symbol)**.
- [ ] Do **not** skip the LL(1) verification step after computing FIRST/FOLLOW.
- [ ] Do **not** write the **procedure body for `ε`-productions** with `match()` calls — they should be silent returns.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy for KTU Module 2**: For a 14-mark question, allocate **~3 minutes** for identifying left recursion, **~4 minutes** for elimination, **~4 minutes** for FIRST/FOLLOW computation, and **~3 minutes** for parse table construction and tracing. Always draw boxes around your parse table cells to highlight entries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictive Parser - LL(1)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Predictive Parser - LL(1)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Predictive Parser (LL(1))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman – Dragon Book, KTU Module 2):**
> A **Predictive Parser** is a special form of **recursive-descent parser** that requires **no backtracking**. It works by looking at the **next k input symbols** (the *lookahead*) to unambiguously decide which production of a non-terminal to apply. When **k = 1**, the parser is called an **LL(1) Parser**.

### Decoding the Acronym **LL(1)**

The name **LL(1)** encodes the parser's three fundamental operational characteristics:

| Letter | Meaning | Implication |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1st L** | **L**eft-to-right scan of input | Input tokens are consumed strictly from left to right. |
| **2nd L** | **L**eftmost derivation | The parser always derives the **leftmost non-terminal** at each step. |
| **1** | **1 symbol of lookahead** | Only **one** input symbol is inspected to choose the production. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving with a GPS that Pre-Loads the Map**
>
> Imagine you are driving through a city with a **smart GPS**. The GPS does **not** explore dead-ends (no backtracking). Instead, it **pre-computes the entire route** before you start, knowing the *next one street sign* (lookahead) at every intersection (non-terminal).
>
> - The **intersections** = Non-terminals (E, T, F).
> - The **street signs ahead** = The next input token (the lookahead).
> - The **pre-computed map** = The **LL(1) Parsing Table M**.
> - The **decision rule** = "Look at table M[Non-Terminal, Lookahead] to know exactly which road (production) to take."
>
> If, at any intersection, the table gives **two conflicting roads** for the same sign, the city map (grammar) is **not LL(1)**.

---

## 1.3 The Two Architectural Variants of LL(1)

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires knowledge of **two implementations** of the predictive parser:

1. **Recursive-Descent Predictive Parser** → Each non-terminal has its own *recursive procedure*. The lookahead symbol selects the call path.
2. **Non-Recursive (Table-Driven) Predictive Parser** → Uses an explicit **Parsing Table M** and an explicit **Stack**. This is the **canonical LL(1)** model examined in exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A grammar is LL(1) if and only if its associated parsing table M contains NO cell with more than one production.** Such a grammar guarantees deterministic, backtrack-free parsing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of the Table-Driven LL(1) Parser

The non-recursive predictive parser consists of four tightly coupled components:

1. **Input Buffer** — Holds the input string followed by the end-marker **$**.
2. **Stack** — Initially contains **$S** (where S is the start symbol and $ is the bottom-of-stack marker). The top of the stack drives the parser.
3. **Parsing Table M** — A 2-D table of the form `M[Non-Terminal, Terminal ∪ {$}] → Production`.
4. **Output Stream** — Records the productions applied (yields a leftmost derivation).

### Master Algorithm (Pseudocode)

```
let a = ip (first symbol of input buffer)
let X = top of stack
while (X ≠ $) do
    if (X == a) then
        pop X;   advance ip     // MATCH
    else if (X is a terminal) then
        ERROR()
    else if (M[X, a] is empty) then
        ERROR()
    else if (M[X, a] = X → Y1 Y2 ... Yk) then
        output the production X → Y1 Y2 ... Yk
        pop X
        push Yk ... Y2 Y1      // right-to-left push
end while
```

> [!NOTE]
> The push is **right-to-left** so that `Y1` ends up on the top of the stack (it is the leftmost symbol to be matched next).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — FIRST, FOLLOW, and Table Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **FIRST**, **FOLLOW** sets, and the **Table Construction Rule** are the three pillars for any KTU LL(1) question. Memorize these rigorously.

### 2.2.1 FIRST(α) — Set of terminals that begin strings derived from α

| Rule | Statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **R1** | If **X is a terminal**, then **FIRST(X) = { X }**. |
| **R2** | If **X → ε** is a production, then add **ε** to **FIRST(X)**. |
| **R3** | If **X → Y₁ Y₂ ... Yₖ**, then add **FIRST(Y₁) − {ε}** to **FIRST(X)**. If **ε ∈ FIRST(Y₁)**, then also add **FIRST(Y₂) − {ε}**, and so on. If **ε** is in **FIRST(Yᵢ)** for **all i = 1..k**, then add **ε** to **FIRST(X)**. |

### 2.2.2 FOLLOW(A) — Set of terminals that can immediately follow A in some sentential form

| Rule | Statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **R4** | Place **$** in **FOLLOW(S)**, where **S** is the start symbol. |
| **R5** | If **A → α B β**, then every element of **FIRST(β) − {ε}** is in **FOLLOW(B)**. |
| **R6** | If **A → α B** OR if **A → α B β** with **ε ∈ FIRST(β)**, then everything in **FOLLOW(A)** is in **FOLLOW(B)**. |

### 2.2.3 LL(1) Parsing Table Construction Rule

For **every** production **A → α** of the grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each terminal } a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha):\quad & M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha \\
\text{If } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha), \text{ then for each } b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A):\quad & M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha \\
\text{If } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \text{ and } \$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A):\quad & M[A, \$] = A \rightarrow \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.3 The Two Types of LL(1) Conflicts

> [!WARNING]
> A grammar fails to be LL(1) **only** when the table M has an **ambiguous cell** (a cell holding ≥ 2 productions). There are exactly two flavours:

| Conflict Type | Trigger | Symptom |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **FIRST / FIRST** | Two productions of the same non-terminal share a common terminal in their FIRST sets. | Same terminal `a` appears in M[A, a] for two rules. |
| **FIRST / FOLLOW** | A production **A → ε** clashes with another production of A. | If `b ∈ FIRST(β)` and `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`, then M[A, b] gets two rules. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution (not in syllabus, but examiner-rewarding):** *Left factoring* removes FIRST/FIRST conflicts; *Left recursion elimination* and *substitution* remove FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LL(1) parsers are the conceptual backbone of **hand-written parsers** in domain-specific languages (DSLs), **configuration file processors**, **JSON/XML strict-mode readers**, and **expression evaluators** in calculators and spreadsheets. The deterministic O(n) parse time and tiny memory footprint make LL(1) ideal for **embedded compilers**, **firmware boot-loaders**, and **IoT scripting engines** (e.g., *mJS*, *JerryScript*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Grammar (KTU-Favourite Canonical Example)

Consider the following **ambiguity-free arithmetic grammar** for `+` and `*`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +\,T\,E' \mid \varepsilon \\
T &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *\,F\,T' \mid \varepsilon \\
F &\rightarrow ( \, E \, ) \mid \textbf{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

We shall derive **FIRST**, **FOLLOW**, the **Parsing Table M**, and a **full parse trace** for the input **`id + id $`**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive FIRST-Set Derivation

We apply the **R1–R3** rules iteratively until fixed-point.

### Step 1 — Apply Rule R1 / R2 to terminals and ε-productions

$$
\text{FIRST}(E') \supseteq \{\varepsilon\}, \qquad \text{FIRST}(T') \supseteq \{\varepsilon\}
$$

### Step 2 — Derive FIRST(F)

$F \rightarrow ( \, E \, )$ gives `(`. $F \rightarrow \textbf{id}$ gives `id`.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(F) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{id}\,\}}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive FIRST(T')

$T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ gives `*`. Combined with Step 1:

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(T') = \{\,*\,,\; \varepsilon\,\}}
$$

### Step 4 — Derive FIRST(T)

$T \rightarrow F\,T'$. Since FIRST(F) contains no ε, FIRST(T) = FIRST(F):

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(T) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{id}\,\}}
$$

### Step 5 — Derive FIRST(E')

$E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ gives `+`. Combined with Step 1:

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(E') = \{\,+\,,\; \varepsilon\,\}}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive FIRST(E)

$E \rightarrow T\,E'$. Since FIRST(T) contains no ε, FIRST(E) = FIRST(T):

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(E) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{id}\,\}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive FOLLOW-Set Derivation

Apply **R4–R6** iteratively until fixed-point.

### Iteration 0 — Apply Rule R4

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E) \supseteq \{\$\}
$$

### Apply Rule R5 (FIRST of RHS-tail)

| Production | Non-Terminal Examined | Tail β | FIRST(β) − {ε} added to FOLLOW |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $F \rightarrow ( \, E \, )$ | E | `)` | `{ ) }` |
| $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ | T | `E'` | `{ + }` |
| $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ | T | `E'` | `{ + }` |
| $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ | F | `T'` | `{ * }` |
| $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ | F | `T'` | `{ * }` |

### Apply Rule R6 (FOLLOW of LHS propagates when β is nullable)

| Production | β Nullable? | Propagation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ | **Yes** (E' ⇒ ε) | FOLLOW(E) → FOLLOW(T) ⇒ `{ $, ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(T) |
| $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ | Yes | FOLLOW(E) → FOLLOW(E') ⇒ `{ $, ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(E') |
| $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ | Yes | FOLLOW(E') → FOLLOW(T) ⇒ (already added) |
| $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ | Yes | FOLLOW(E') → FOLLOW(E') ⇒ (trivial) |
| $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ | **Yes** (T' ⇒ ε) | FOLLOW(T) → FOLLOW(F) ⇒ `{ +, $, ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(F) |
| $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ | Yes | FOLLOW(T) → FOLLOW(T') ⇒ `{ +, $, ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(T') |
| $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ | Yes | FOLLOW(T') → FOLLOW(F) ⇒ (already added) |

### Final FOLLOW Sets (After Closure)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOLLOW}(E) &= \{\$\,,\;\text{)}\} \\
