\text{FOLLOW}(E') &= \{\$\,,\;\text{)}\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T) &= \{\,+\,,\;\$\,,\;\text{)}\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T') &= \{\,+\,,\;\$\,,\;\text{)}\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(F) &= \{\,*\,,\;+\,,\;\$\,,\;\text{)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Parsing Table M — Construction Walkthrough

Apply the Table Construction Rule to **each production**:

| Production | FIRST(α) | FOLLOW (if ε ∈ FIRST) | Cells filled in M |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ | `{ (, id }` | — | M[E, (] = M[E, id] = $E \rightarrow TE'$ |
| $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ | `{ + }` | — | M[E', +] = $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ |
| $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ε ∈ FIRST | FOLLOW(E') = `{ $, ) }` | M[E', $] = M[E', )] = $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ | `{ (, id }` | — | M[T, (] = M[T, id] = $T \rightarrow FT'$ |
| $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ | `{ * }` | — | M[T', *] = $T' \rightarrow *FT'$ |
| $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ε ∈ FIRST | FOLLOW(T') = `{ +, $, ) }` | M[T', +] = M[T', $] = M[T', )] = $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| $F \rightarrow ( \, E \, )$ | `{ ( }` | — | M[F, (] = $F \rightarrow (E)$ |
| $F \rightarrow \textbf{id}$ | `{ id }` | — | M[F, id] = $F \rightarrow \textbf{id}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Every cell in M contains **at most one** production ⇒ the grammar is **LL(1)** ✓

### Compiled Parsing Table (Final Form)

| | **id** | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **$** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **E** | E → TE' | | | E → TE' | | |
| **E'** | | E' → +TE' | | | E' → ε | E' → ε |
| **T** | T → FT' | | | T → FT' | | |
| **T'** | | T' → ε | T' → \*FT' | | T' → ε | T' → ε |
| **F** | F → id | | | F → (E) | | |

---

## 3.5 Full Parsing Trace for Input `id + id $`

Each row records: **Stack contents (top on right)**, **Remaining Input**, and **Action**.

| Step | Stack (top → right) | Input (left → right) | Action |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `$ E` | `id + id $` | Output **E → TE'** (M[E, id]) |
| 2 | `$ E' T` | `id + id $` | Output **T → FT'** (M[T, id]) |
| 3 | `$ E' T' F` | `id + id $` | Output **F → id** (M[F, id]) |
| 4 | `$ E' T' id` | `id + id $` | **Match id** |
| 5 | `$ E' T'` | `+ id $` | Output **T' → ε** (M[T', +]) |
| 6 | `$ E'` | `+ id $` | Output **E' → +TE'** (M[E', +]) |
| 7 | `$ E' T +` | `+ id $` | **Match +** |
| 8 | `$ E' T` | `id $` | Output **T → FT'** (M[T, id]) |
| 9 | `$ E' T' F` | `id $` | Output **F → id** (M[F, id]) |
| 10 | `$ E' T' id` | `id $` | **Match id** |
| 11 | `$ E' T'` | `$` | Output **T' → ε** (M[T', $]) |
| 12 | `$ E'` | `$` | Output **E' → ε** (M[E', $]) |
| 13 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> The output productions, read top-to-bottom, form the **leftmost derivation** of `id + id`. The parser never backtracks — every decision is a *single table lookup*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Non-Recursive Predictive Parser Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Buffer"]
        ip1["ip pointer -> a"]
    end

    subgraph Stack["Explicit Stack"]
        stk1["Top of Stack X"]
        stk2["...intermediate..."]
        stk3["Bottom $"]
    end

    subgraph Table["Parsing Table M"]
        tbl1["M Non-Terminal, Terminal"]
    end

    subgraph Out["Output Stream"]
        out1["Productions Applied"]
    end

    ip1 -- "current lookahead a" --> Drive
    stk1 -- "top symbol X" --> Drive
    Drive{{"Parser Driver Algorithm"}}
    Drive -- "Lookup" --> Table
    Table -- "Production X -> Y1..Yk" --> Drive
    Drive -- "Emit Production" --> Out
    Drive -- "Pop X, Push Yk..Y1" --> Stack
    Drive -- "Advance ip on match" --> Input
    Drive -- "Accept or Error" --> Term["Accept OR Error"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — FIRST / FOLLOW Set Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Symbol X]) --> checkT{Is X a Terminal?}
    checkT -- Yes --> termAns["FIRST X = X"]
    checkT -- No --> checkE{Does X derive epsilon?}
    checkE -- Yes --> addEps["Add epsilon to FIRST X"]
    checkE -- No --> skipEps["Skip epsilon"]
    addEps --> checkProd
    skipEps --> checkProd{Iterate productions X -> Y1 Y2 ... Yk}
    termAns --> stop1([Stop])
    checkProd -- For each Yi --> addFirst["Add FIRST Yi minus epsilon to FIRST X"]
    addFirst --> allEps{epsilon in FIRST Yi for all i?}
    allEps -- Yes --> addEpsFinal["Add epsilon to FIRST X"]
    allEps -- No --> next
    addEpsFinal --> stop2([Stop])
    next([Next iteration or Stop])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — LL(1) Parsing Algorithm Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    begin([Initialize: Stack = $S, ip points to first symbol]) --> readX[Read X = Top of Stack]
    readX --> readA[Read a = Current Input Symbol]
    readA --> checkX{X is $?}
    checkX -- Yes --> checkA{a is $?}
    checkA -- Yes --> accept[ACCEPT and Stop]
    checkA -- No --> errA[Error: Input Not Fully Consumed]
    checkX -- No --> eqCheck{X equals a?}
    eqCheck -- Yes --> match[Pop X, Advance ip]
    eqCheck -- No --> isTerm{X is Terminal?}
    isTerm -- Yes --> errMatch[Error: Terminal Mismatch]
    isTerm -- No --> tbl{M of X comma a is Empty?}
    tbl -- Yes --> errTbl[Error: No Production in Table]
    tbl -- No --> apply[Output Production X -> Y1 Y2 ... Yk, Pop X, Push Yk ... Y2 Y1]
    match --> readX
    apply --> readX
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Building the Parsing Table

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1["Grammar G"] --> f1["Compute FIRST of all symbols"]
    g1 --> f2["Compute FOLLOW of all non-terminals"]
    f1 --> tbl["Initialize empty Parsing Table M"]
    f2 --> tbl
    tbl --> loop{For each production A -> alpha}
    loop -- "For each a in FIRST alpha" --> fill1["M of A comma a = A -> alpha"]
    loop -- "If epsilon in FIRST alpha" --> fill2["For each b in FOLLOW A, set M of A comma b = A -> alpha"]
    fill1 --> conflict{Any cell has multiple productions?}
    fill2 --> conflict
    conflict -- Yes --> notLL1["Grammar is NOT LL(1) - Apply Left Factoring or Recursion Removal"]
    conflict -- No --> isLL1["Grammar IS LL(1) - Table Ready"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**What is an LL(1) grammar? State the conditions under which a grammar is LL(1).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A grammar is **LL(1)** if its parsing table contains **no multiply-defined entries**. Equivalently:

1. The grammar must be **non-ambiguous** and **free of left recursion**.
2. For every non-terminal `A` with two distinct productions `A → α` and `A → β`:
   - **FIRST(α) ∩ FIRST(β) = ∅**
   - If `ε ∈ FIRST(α)`, then **FIRST(β) ∩ FOLLOW(A) = ∅**, and vice versa.

The acronym **LL(1)** denotes **L**eft-to-right input scan, **L**eftmost derivation, and **1** symbol of lookahead. Such grammars can be parsed deterministically without backtracking by a top-down predictive parser.

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for the acronym decoding, ~1 mark for the conditions, ~1 mark for the table-uniqueness property.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 2]`
**Differentiate between recursive-descent parsing and table-driven LL(1) parsing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recursive-Descent | Table-Driven LL(1) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Implementation** | One procedure per non-terminal. | A single driver + explicit stack + table. |
| **Backtracking** | May backtrack if grammar is ambiguous. | **No backtracking** — deterministic. |
| **Lookahead use** | Lookahead `a` selects the `if`/`switch` branch. | Lookahead `a` indexes the parsing table M[A, a]. |
| **Maintenance** | Harder for large grammars (procedural). | Easier — table-driven, declarative. |
| **Execution Speed** | Direct procedure calls; fast. | Slightly slower due to table lookups. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (predictive-parser specific):**
> - **-2 marks** if FIRST/FOLLOW tables are *not labelled column-wise and row-wise* — examiners verify every set entry.
> - **-1 mark** if you forget to include **ε** explicitly when FIRST contains it.
> - **-1 mark** if you forget **$** in FOLLOW(start-symbol).
> - **-2 marks** if the parsing table has a *blank cell* where a rule should exist — re-verify using the **FOLLOW-of-ε-productions** rule.
> - **-3 marks** if the parsing-trace stack is shown in the wrong order (top must be on the **right** of the stack string).
> - Always **state whether the grammar is LL(1) or not** at the end — missing this loses **1 mark**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**(a)** Consider the following grammar:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow ( \, L \, ) \mid \textbf{a} \\
L &\rightarrow L \, \text{,} \, S \mid S
\end{aligned}
$$
**Compute FIRST and FOLLOW for every non-terminal. Construct the LL(1) parsing table. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the parsing table from part (a), show the **complete parse** for the input **`(a, (a, a)) $`**. Indicate stack, input, and action at every step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: FIRST Sets** (apply R1–R3)

- $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{a}\,\}$ — from `(L)` and `a`.
- $\text{FIRST}(L)$: From `L → L,S` and `L → S`, FIRST(L) = FIRST(L) ∪ FIRST(S) ⇒ $\text{FIRST}(L) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{a}\,\}$ (no ε-productions).
- $\text{FIRST}(L,S) = \text{FIRST}(L) = \{\,(\,,\; \textbf{a}\,\}$ (`,` is not nullable).

**Step 2: FOLLOW Sets** (apply R4–R6)

| Production | Non-Terminal | Rule used | Addition |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Start | S | R4 | `{ $ }` ⊆ FOLLOW(S) |
| $S \rightarrow (L)$ | L | R5 | FIRST(`)` − {ε}) = `{ ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(L) |
| $S \rightarrow (L)$ | L | (right-end) | FOLLOW(S) = `{ $, ) }` ⊆ FOLLOW(L) |
| $L \rightarrow L,S$ | L | R5+R6 | FIRST(`,S`) = `{ , }` ⊆ FOLLOW(L); FOLLOW(L) ⊆ FOLLOW(L) (trivial) |
| $L \rightarrow L,S$ | S | R5 | FIRST(`,S`) minus ε not empty ⇒ `{ , }` ⊆ FOLLOW(S) |
| $L \rightarrow S$ | S | (right-end) | FOLLOW(L) = `{ $, ), , }` ⊆ FOLLOW(S) |

Final FOLLOW sets after closure:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{\,\$\,,\;\text{)}\,,\;\text{,}\,\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(L) &= \{\,\$\,,\;\text{)}\,,\;\text{,}\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Parsing Table**

| | **a** | **(** | **)** | **,** | **$** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | S → a | S → (L) | | | |
| **L** | L → S | L → S | | L → L,S | |

**Step 4: LL(1) Verdict**

Every table cell contains **at most one production** ⇒ **The grammar IS LL(1)** ✓ *(+1 mark for justification)*

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

| Step | Stack (top right) | Input | Action |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `$ S` | `(a,(a,a))$` | S → (L) |
| 2 | `$ S ) L (` | `(a,(a,a))$` | Match `(` |
| 3 | `$ S ) L` | `a,(a,a))$` | L → S |
| 4 | `$ S ) S` | `a,(a,a))$` | S → a |
| 5 | `$ S ) a` | `a,(a,a))$` | Match `a` |
| 6 | `$ S )` | `,(a,a))$` | Match `)` |
| 7 | `$ S` | `,(a,a))$` | **Error** (M[S, `,`] is empty) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner note:** A common student mistake is to keep applying rules even after a parse error. Step 7 correctly signals the rejection. *(Full 7 marks awarded only if the parser is shown terminating with an explicit error message and not silently continuing.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Define **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets. For the grammar below, compute FIRST and FOLLOW for all non-terminals: **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A\,B\,C \\
A &\rightarrow \textbf{a} \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\rightarrow \textbf{b} \mid \varepsilon \\
C &\rightarrow \textbf{c} \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Construct the **LL(1) parsing table** and show that the grammar **is LL(1)**. Identify what property of the FIRST/FOLLOW sets guarantees LL(1)-ness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **FIRST(α)** is the set of terminals that appear as the leftmost symbol in any string derivable from α. It includes **ε** if α can derive the empty string.
- **FOLLOW(A)** is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of non-terminal A in some *sentential form*. It includes **$** if A can be the rightmost symbol.

**FIRST Computation (2.5 marks):**

- FIRST(S) = FIRST(A) = `{ a, ε }` (since ε ∈ FIRST(A) ⇒ also FIRST(B) = `{ b, ε }`).
- FIRST(A) = `{ a, ε }`, FIRST(B) = `{ b, ε }`, FIRST(C) = `{ c, ε }`.
- FIRST(S) (from `A B C` with all nullable):
  - From A ⇒ add `{ a }`, then since ε ∈ FIRST(A), look at B ⇒ add `{ b }`, then since ε ∈ FIRST(B), look at C ⇒ add `{ c }`. All three are nullable, so add ε.
  - $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{\textbf{a},\; \textbf{b},\; \textbf{c},\; \varepsilon\}$

**FOLLOW Computation (2.5 marks):**

- FOLLOW(S) = `{ $ }` (R4).
- FOLLOW(A) = FIRST(B C) − {ε} = `{ b, c }` ∪ FOLLOW(S) = `{ b, c, $ }`.
- FOLLOW(B) = FIRST(C) − {ε} = `{ c }` ∪ FOLLOW(S) = `{ c, $ }`.
- FOLLOW(C) = FOLLOW(S) = `{ $ }`.

Final:

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S | { a, b, c, ε } | { $ } |
| A | { a, ε } | { b, c, $ } |
| B | { b, ε } | { c, $ } |
| C | { c, ε } | { $ } |

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Parsing Table (5 marks):**

| | **a** | **b** | **c** | **$** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | S → ABC | S → ABC | S → ABC | S → ABC |
| **A** | A → a | A → ε | A → ε | A → ε |
| **B** | | B → b | B → ε | B → ε |
| **C** | | | C → c | C → ε |

**LL(1) Verification (2 marks):**

The grammar **is LL(1)** because every cell of M contains at most one production. The **guaranteeing property** is:

$$
\forall A,\; \text{productions } A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta:\quad \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset \;\;\wedge\;\; (\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(\beta) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset)
$$

In our table, observe that `A → a` occupies only the `a` column, and `A → ε` occupies the `{ b, c, $ }` columns (which are precisely FOLLOW(A)). No column receives two entries for any row.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Predictive Parser LL(1)**
>
> - **LL(1)** = **L**eft scan + **L**eftmost derivation + **1** lookahead symbol.
> - A grammar is LL(1) **iff** its parsing table **M** has **no multiply-defined entry**.
> - **FIRST(X)** collects terminals that can begin a string derived from X; includes **ε** if X ⇒ ε.
> - **FOLLOW(A)** collects terminals (and **$**) that can immediately follow A; **always include $** in FOLLOW(start).
> - Table rule: for **A → α**, put the rule in **M[A, a]** for every `a ∈ FIRST(α)`. **If ε ∈ FIRST(α)**, additionally put it in **M[A, b]** for every `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`.
> - The two failure modes are **FIRST/FIRST** and **FIRST/FOLLOW** conflicts — both make a grammar non-LL(1).
> - Stack trace convention: **top of stack is on the right** of the stack string; **push productions right-to-left** so the leftmost symbol ends on top.
> - Termination = **stack top `$` = input `$`** ⇒ **ACCEPT**.
> - The parser is **linear time O(n)** where n = number of input tokens.
> - LL(1) grammars cannot be **left-recursive** and must be **non-ambiguous**.
> - Resolution tools: **Left Factoring** (kills FIRST/FIRST) and **Left Recursion Removal** (kills FIRST/FOLLOW).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-Up Parsing: LR(0) Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottom-Up Parsing: LR(0) Parser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2: Bottom-Up Parsing — The LR(0) Parser

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LR(0) Parser (Canonical Definition per Aho, Sethi, Ullman — Dragon Book Standard):**
> An **LR(0) parser** is a type of **bottom-up, shift-reduce parser** that scans the input from **Left to right**, constructs a **Rightmost derivation in reverse**, and uses **zero (0) lookahead tokens** to make its parsing decisions (shift, reduce, accept, or error). The "0" denotes that the parser's decisions depend *solely* on the current state of the parser stack and the current state of the **finite automaton** (the LR(0) item DFA), without peeking at any upcoming input symbols.

### Components of an LR(0) Parsing System:
The LR(0) parser is constructed using three formal mathematical artifacts:

1. **The Augmented Grammar** $G'$ — Derived from $G$ by adding a unique start production $S' \rightarrow S$.
2. **The Canonical Collection of LR(0) Items** $\mathcal{C} = \{I_0, I_1, \dots, I_n\}$ — A finite set of "states" of the parser.
3. **The Parsing Table** consisting of two parts:
   - **ACTION Table** — Mapping $\text{Action}[s, a]$ where $s$ is a state and $a$ is a *terminal*.
   - **GOTO Table** — Mapping $\text{Goto}[s, A]$ where $s$ is a state and $A$ is a *non-terminal*.

### Grammar Hierarchy Position:
$$\text{Recursive Descent} \subset \text{LL(1)} \subset \text{LR(0)} \subset \text{SLR(1)} \subset \text{LALR(1)} \subset \text{CLR(1)}$$

This means LR(0) is more powerful than LL(1) (can parse more grammars), but less powerful than SLR, LALR, and CLR (cannot handle some real-world grammars due to conflicts).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍔 The Burger Assembly Line Analogy:**
> Imagine a conveyor belt where ingredients (tokens) arrive one by one, and a worker (the parser) is assembling a burger (the parse tree) from the bottom up.
>
> - **Shift** = Pick up the next ingredient from the belt and place it on your work tray (push onto stack).
> - **Reduce** = You notice a complete "patty + bun + sauce" assembly rule matches what's on your tray. You package it into a labeled box marked "burger-patty-unit" (pop from stack, push the non-terminal).
> - **LR(0) magic** = The worker decides Shift or Reduce **without even looking at the next ingredient**. They rely entirely on *what's currently on the tray* (the stack state). This works only for very simple, predictable recipes.
> - **Conflicts** = What if the worker has both "lettuce + tomato" (which could be a 'salad' reduce) *and* the next item will make it a 'sandwich' (which needs a shift)? That ambiguity is a **shift-reduce conflict** — the LR(0) parser fails here.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Anchor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Glossary Terms (must memorize for 2-mark questions):**
> - **Handle:** A substring that matches the right-hand side of a production and whose reduction represents one step in a rightmost derivation.
> - **Viable Prefix:** A prefix of a right-sentential form that can appear on the stack during shift-reduce parsing (never extends past a handle).
> - **LR(0) Item:** A production with a dot (•) somewhere on its RHS indicating parsing progress.
> - **Augmented Grammar:** $G$ with a new start symbol $S'$ and production $S' \rightarrow S$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Module 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Operations on Items

### 🔹 Operation 1: CLOSURE(I)
If $I$ is a set of LR(0) items for grammar $G'$, then **CLOSURE(I)** is computed as:

**Algorithm (Iterative):**
1. Initialize $\text{CLOSURE}(I) = I$.
2. Repeat for every item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet B\beta] \in \text{CLOSURE}(I)$:
   - For every production $B \rightarrow \gamma$ in $G'$:
     - If $[B \rightarrow \bullet \gamma] \notin \text{CLOSURE}(I)$, add it.
3. Until no new items can be added.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If we expect to see non-terminal $B$ next, we must be ready to parse *any* production of $B$ from scratch (with the dot at the beginning).

### 🔹 Operation 2: GOTO(I, X)
For a set of items $I$ and a grammar symbol $X$:

$$\text{GOTO}(I, X) = \text{CLOSURE}\left( \left\{ [A \rightarrow \alpha X \bullet \beta] \;\Big|\; [A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet X\beta] \in I \right\} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** "Advance the dot past symbol $X$" — collect all items where we just consumed $X$, then close the new set.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Mathematical Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $G'$ | Augmented Grammar $G \cup \{S' \rightarrow S\}$ | Required to signal ACCEPT state |
| $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet \beta]$ | An LR(0) Item | State representation |
| $I_0$ | Initial state (CLOSURE of $\{[S' \rightarrow \bullet S]\}$) | Start state of DFA |
| $\mathcal{C} = \{I_0, I_1, \dots, I_n\}$ | Canonical Collection of LR(0) Item Sets | All DFA states |
| $\text{Action}[s, a]$ | Parsing action for state $s$, terminal $a$ | Shift/Reduce/Accept/Error |
| $\text{Goto}[s, A]$ | Next state for state $s$, non-terminal $A$ | Stack transition |
| $s_n, X_n$ | Top state & symbol on the stack | Top of parsing stack |
| $k$ | Number of symbols on RHS of production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Used in reduce pops |
| Shift $j$ | Push state $j$ onto stack | Reading a terminal |
| Reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Pop $2 \cdot \vert\alpha\vert$ entries, push GOTO | Applying a rule |
| Accept | Parsing successfully completed | Only in $[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** LR(0) parsers are foundational to compiler generators like **YACC** and **Bison**. While pure LR(0) is rarely deployed standalone in industry (due to conflicts), the item-construction DFA concept forms the *backbone* of every industrial parser-generator toolchain.

---

## 2.3 The Parsing Algorithm (Driver Function)

The LR parsing algorithm uses a **stack** containing pairs of the form $(s_0, X_1, s_1, X_2, s_2, \dots, X_n, s_n)$ where $s_i$ are DFA states and $X_i$ are grammar symbols.

```
input:  w$ (input string + EOF marker)
stack:  [0]                     // initially state 0
output: sequence of productions // for reverse rightmost derivation

Repeat:
    Let s = top state of stack
    Let a = next input symbol
    Action = Action[s, a]

    if Action = Shift s' then
        push a, push s'
        advance input
    else if Action = Reduce A → β then
        pop 2*|β| symbols
        let s' = new top state
        push A, push Goto[s', A]
        output production A → β
    else if Action = Accept then
        return SUCCESS
    else
        ERROR (parsing failed)
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Module 2: Step-by-Step Construction — Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Grammar (Standard KTU Example):**
> Consider the augmented grammar $G'$:
>
> $S' \rightarrow S$
> $S \rightarrow CC$
> $C \rightarrow cC \;\mid\; d$

We will construct the full LR(0) automaton, item sets, and parsing table.

---

## 3.1 Step 1: Compute the Initial Item Set $I_0$

Start with $I_0 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow \bullet S]\})$.

Applying the closure rule iteratively:

**Iteration 1:** Item $[S' \rightarrow \bullet S]$ has $S$ to the right of the dot.
- Add all productions of $S$ with dot at start:
  - $[S \rightarrow \bullet CC]$
- Item $[S \rightarrow \bullet CC]$ has $C$ to the right of the dot.
- Add all productions of $C$ with dot at start:
  - $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC]$
  - $[C \rightarrow \bullet d]$

**Iteration 2:** Items $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC]$ and $[C \rightarrow \bullet d]$ have terminals right of the dot. No new non-terminals to expand.

$$\boxed{I_0 = \{[S' \rightarrow \bullet S],\; [S \rightarrow \bullet CC],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet cC],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet d]\}}$$

---

## 3.2 Step 2: Compute All GOTO Transitions

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_0, S)$
Find items in $I_0$ with $S$ right after the dot:
- $[S' \rightarrow \bullet S] \Rightarrow [S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$

$$\boxed{I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]\}}$$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_0, C)$
- $[S \rightarrow \bullet CC] \Rightarrow [S \rightarrow C \bullet C]$
- Closure: $[S \rightarrow C \bullet C]$ has $C$ after dot → add $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC], [C \rightarrow \bullet d]$

$$\boxed{I_2 = \{[S \rightarrow C \bullet C],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet cC],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet d]\}}$$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_0, c)$
- $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC] \Rightarrow [C \rightarrow c \bullet C]$
- Closure: $[C \rightarrow c \bullet C]$ has $C$ after dot → add $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC], [C \rightarrow \bullet d]$

$$\boxed{I_3 = \{[C \rightarrow c \bullet C],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet cC],\; [C \rightarrow \bullet d]\}}$$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_0, d)$
- $[C \rightarrow \bullet d] \Rightarrow [C \rightarrow d \bullet]$

$$\boxed{I_4 = \{[C \rightarrow d \bullet]\}}$$

---

## 3.3 Step 3: Compute Remaining States from $I_2$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_2, C)$
- $[S \rightarrow C \bullet C] \Rightarrow [S \rightarrow CC \bullet]$

$$\boxed{I_5 = \{[S \rightarrow CC \bullet]\}}$$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_2, c)$ and $\text{GOTO}(I_2, d)$
These re-produce the **same items** as $I_3$ and $I_4$ respectively (because $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC]$ and $[C \rightarrow \bullet d]$ are already in $I_2$).

Therefore:
$$\text{GOTO}(I_2, c) = I_3 \qquad \text{GOTO}(I_2, d) = I_4$$

---

## 3.4 Step 4: Compute Remaining States from $I_3$

### 🔹 $\text{GOTO}(I_3, C)$
- $[C \rightarrow c \bullet C] \Rightarrow [C \rightarrow cC \bullet]$

$$\boxed{I_6 = \{[C \rightarrow cC \bullet]\}}$$

($\text{GOTO}(I_3, c) = I_3$ and $\text{GOTO}(I_3, d) = I_4$ — self-loops)

---

## 3.5 Step 5: Verify Canonical Collection is Closed

After exhausting all transitions, the canonical collection is:

$$\mathcal{C} = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_6\}$$

This is the **complete set of LR(0) states**.

---

## 3.6 Step 6: Build the LR(0) Parsing Table

**Rule Set for Table Construction:**

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet a\beta]$ in $I_i$, $a$ is terminal, $\text{GOTO}(I_i, a) = I_j$ | $\text{Action}[i, a] = \text{Shift } j$ |
| Item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet]$ in $I_i$, $A \neq S'$ | $\text{Action}[i, *] = \text{Reduce } A \rightarrow \alpha$ (all terminals) |
| Item $[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$ in $I_i$ | $\text{Action}[i, \$] = \text{Accept}$ |
| Item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet B\beta]$ in $I_i$, $B$ non-terminal, $\text{GOTO}(I_i, B) = I_j$ | $\text{Goto}[i, B] = j$ |

**Applying to our example:**

For $I_4 = \{[C \rightarrow d \bullet]\}$: reduce $C \rightarrow d$ on *all* terminals.
For $I_6 = \{[C \rightarrow cC \bullet]\}$: reduce $C \rightarrow cC$ on *all* terminals.
For $I_5 = \{[S \rightarrow CC \bullet]\}$: reduce $S \rightarrow CC$ on *all* terminals.
For $I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]\}$: Accept on $\$$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Conflict Alert:** Notice in states $I_4$ and $I_6$, the reduce action applies to *every* terminal indiscriminately. If the grammar had even one shift-reduce conflict (e.g., the famous **dangling-else** grammar), LR(0) would fail to construct a valid table. SLR(1) fixes this by restricting reduces to FOLLOW sets.

**Final Parsing Table:**

| State | Action[c] | Action[d] | Action[\$] | Goto[C] | Goto[S] |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | s3 | s4 | | 2 | 1 |
| 1 | | | acc | | |
| 2 | s3 | s4 | | 5 | |
| 3 | s3 | s4 | | 6 | |
| 4 | r3 | r3 | r3 | | |
| 5 | r1 | r1 | r1 | | |
| 6 | r2 | r2 | r2 | | |

Where productions are numbered:
- (1) $S \rightarrow CC$
- (2) $C \rightarrow cC$
- (3) $C \rightarrow d$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Module 2: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LR(0) Item DFA (State Transition Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node_I0((I0))
    node_I1((I1))
    node_I2((I2))
    node_I3((I3))
    node_I4((I4))
    node_I5((I5))
    node_I6((I6))

    node_I0 -->|"S"| node_I1
    node_I0 -->|"C"| node_I2
    node_I0 -->|"c"| node_I3
    node_I0 -->|"d"| node_I4

    node_I2 -->|"C"| node_I5
    node_I2 -->|"c"| node_I3
    node_I2 -->|"d"| node_I4

    node_I3 -->|"C"| node_I6
    node_I3 -->|"c"| node_I3
    node_I3 -->|"d"| node_I4

    node_I5 -. "Accept on $" .- node_I1

    classDef startState fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:3px
    classDef acceptState fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    classDef reduceState fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px

    class node_I0 startState
    class node_I1 acceptState
    class node_I4,node_I5,node_I6 reduceState
```

**Diagram Legend:**
- 🔵 **Blue Node (I0)** = Start State
- 🟢 **Green Node (I1)** = Accept State (contains $[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$)
- 🟡 **Yellow Nodes (I4, I5, I6)** = Reduce States (contain $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet]$ items)

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the LR Parser System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input String w$]
    Lex[Lexical Analyzer / Token Stream]
    Stack[Parsing Stack: s0 X1 s1 X2 s2]
    ActionTab[ACTION Table]
    GotoTab[GOTO Table]
    DFA[LR0 Item DFA States]
    Output[Production Sequence]

    Input --> Lex
    Lex --> Stack
    Stack -->|"top state s, next symbol a"| ActionTab
    ActionTab -->|"Shift s'| Stack
    ActionTab -->|"Reduce A to B"| GotoTab
    GotoTab -->|"Goto s'| Stack
    ActionTab -->|"Accept"| Output
    DFA -.->|"precomputed states"| ActionTab
    DFA -.->|"precomputed states"| GotoTab

    classDef io fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    classDef table fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20

    class Input,Output io
    class ActionTab,GotoTab,DFA table
    class Stack output
```

---

## 4.3 Item Reduction Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Parser at State s])
    Read{a Terminal Right of Dot?}
    ShiftItem[Shift Item: A to alpha bullet a beta]
    ShiftOp[Action s, a = Shift j]
    ReduceItem[Reduce Item: A to alpha bullet]
    ReduceOp[Action s, x = Reduce A to alpha]
    AcceptItem[Accept Item: S' to S bullet]
    AccOp[Action s, dollar = Accept]
    Error[Error: No Action Defined]

    Start --> Read
    Read -->|Yes| ShiftItem --> ShiftOp
    Read -->|No, A not S prime| ReduceItem --> ReduceOp
    Read -->|No, A is S prime| AcceptItem --> AccOp
    Read -.->|No valid entry| Error

    classDef ok fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    classDef err fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    classDef proc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825

    class ShiftOp,ReduceOp,AccOp ok
    class Error err
    class ShiftItem,ReduceItem,AcceptItem proc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Module 2: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define an LR(0) item. Explain with an example how the CLOSURE operation is computed on a set of LR(0) items.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **LR(0) item** is a production of a grammar with a special marker (a dot **•**) placed somewhere on the right-hand side, indicating how much of the production's RHS has been recognized by the parser so far.

**Example:** For production $S \rightarrow CC$, the four possible LR(0) items are:
- $[S \rightarrow \bullet CC]$
- $[S \rightarrow C \bullet C]$
- $[S \rightarrow CC \bullet]$

**CLOSURE Operation:**
For a set of items $I$, CLOSURE($I$) is the smallest set containing $I$ such that if $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet B\beta] \in I$ and $B \rightarrow \gamma$ is a production, then $[B \rightarrow \bullet \gamma]$ is also in CLOSURE($I$). For example, starting with $\{[S' \rightarrow \bullet S]\}$, the closure expands to include $[S \rightarrow \bullet CC]$ and then $[C \rightarrow \bullet cC]$, $[C \rightarrow \bullet d]$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**Q: What is the difference between a shift-reduce conflict and a reduce-reduce conflict in LR(0) parsing? When does each arise?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Shift-Reduce Conflict:** Occurs in a state $I_i$ where both an item of the form $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet]$ (suggesting reduce) and an item of the form $[B \rightarrow \beta \bullet a\gamma]$ (suggesting shift on terminal $a$) are present **simultaneously**. The parser cannot decide whether to reduce $A$ or shift $a$.

- **Reduce-Reduce Conflict:** Occurs in state $I_i$ when two items $[A \rightarrow \alpha \bullet]$ and $[B \rightarrow \beta \bullet]$ exist, both complete (dot at end). The parser cannot choose which production to reduce by.

LR(0) resolves *neither* conflict — such grammars are simply **not LR(0)**. SLR(1), LALR(1), and CLR(1) provide progressively smarter resolution strategies.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: (a)** Construct the LR(0) parsing table for the following augmented grammar:
$$S' \rightarrow S, \quad S \rightarrow AAd \;\mid\; bAc \;\mid\; bca$$
$$A \rightarrow a$$
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Trace the parsing of the input string **"b a c a d"** using the constructed table, showing the stack contents and input pointer at each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Augmented Grammar $G'$:**
- (1) $S' \rightarrow S$
- (2) $S \rightarrow AAd$
- (3) $S \rightarrow bAc$
- (4) $S \rightarrow bca$
- (5) $A \rightarrow a$

**Step 2: Compute LR(0) Item Sets (Closure + Goto Iterations):**

$$\boxed{I_0 = \{[S' \rightarrow \bullet S],\; [S \rightarrow \bullet AAd],\; [S \rightarrow \bullet bAc],\; [S \rightarrow \bullet bca],\; [A \rightarrow \bullet a]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_1 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, S) = \{[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_2 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, A) = \{[S \rightarrow A \bullet Ad],\; [A \rightarrow a \bullet]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_3 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, b) = \{[S \rightarrow b \bullet Ac],\; [S \rightarrow b \bullet ca],\; [A \rightarrow \bullet a]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_4 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, a) = \{[A \rightarrow a \bullet]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_5 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, A) = \{[S \rightarrow AA \bullet d]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_6 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, d) = \{[S \rightarrow AAd \bullet]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_7 = \text{GOTO}(I_3, A) = \{[S \rightarrow bA \bullet c]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_8 = \text{GOTO}(I_3, c) = \{[S \rightarrow b \bullet ca] \text{ already in } I_3, \text{plus } [S \rightarrow bc \bullet a]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_9 = \text{GOTO}(I_3, a) = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow bca \bullet]\}) = \{[S \rightarrow bca \bullet]\}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{10} = \text{GOTO}(I_5, d) = \{[S \rightarrow AAd \bullet]\} = I_6}$$

$$\boxed{I_{11} = \text{GOTO}(I_7, c) = \{[S \rightarrow bAc \bullet]\}}$$

**Step 3: Parsing Table (only non-conflicting entries shown):**

| State | a | b | c | d | \$ | A | S |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | s4 | s3 | | | | 2 | 1 |
| 1 | | | | | acc | | |
| 2 | r(A→a) | | | s6 | | | |
| 3 | s4 | | | | | 7 | |
| 4 | r(A→a) | r(A→a) | r(A→a) | r(A→a) | r(A→a) | | |
| 5 | | | | s6 | | | |
| 6 | r(S→AAd) | r(S→AAd) | r(S→AAd) | r(S→AAd) | r(S→AAd) | | |
| 7 | | | s11 | | | | |
| 8 | | | | | | | |
| 9 | r(S→bca) | r(S→bca) | r(S→bca) | r(S→bca) | r(S→bca) | | |
| 11 | r(S→bAc) | r(S→bAc) | r(S→bAc) | r(S→bAc) | r(S→bAc) | | |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly forget to apply **CLOSURE** after every GOTO computation, leading to missing items in states. Also, students often confuse the reduce action scope — in LR(0), reduce applies to **ALL** terminals (not just FOLLOW), which is why many grammars have conflicts.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

Trace of parsing **"bacad$"**:

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | 0 | bacad$ | Shift 3 (GOTO 0,b) |
| 2 | 0 b 3 | acad$ | Shift 4 (GOTO 3,a) |
| 3 | 0 b 3 a 4 | cad$ | Reduce $A \rightarrow a$ (pop 2, push A) |
| 4 | 0 b 3 A 7 | cad$ | Shift 11 (GOTO 7,c) |
| 5 | 0 b 3 A 7 c 11 | ad$ | Reduce $S \rightarrow bAc$ (pop 6, push S) |
| 6 | 0 S 1 | ad$ | ERROR (expected \$) |

The string is **rejected** because the grammar requires $bAc$ to be followed by nothing (since $S$ is now on stack and only $\$$ is accepted).

If the input were **"bacad$"** properly handled by grammar $S \rightarrow AAd$, we'd need "**baad**" instead.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 / Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: (a)** Define an augmented grammar. Why is augmentation necessary for LR(0) parser construction? Construct the augmented grammar for $E \rightarrow E + T \;\mid\; T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \;\mid\; F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \;\mid\; \text{id}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute all LR(0) item sets (the canonical collection) for the augmented grammar in (a). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Augmented Grammar Definition:** If $G$ is a grammar with start symbol $S$, then the augmented grammar $G'$ has a new start symbol $S'$ and a new production $S' \rightarrow S$. The original start symbol $S$ may now appear on the RHS of productions.

**Necessity of Augmentation:**
1. To provide a **unique ACCEPT state** (the state containing $[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$).
2. To signal successful parsing — the LR parser only accepts when it sees this special item.
3. Without augmentation, there would be no formal way to detect completion of parsing.

**Augmented Grammar Construction:**

- (1) $E' \rightarrow E$
- (2) $E \rightarrow E + T$
- (3) $E \rightarrow T$
- (4) $T \rightarrow T * F$
- (5) $T \rightarrow F$
- (6) $F \rightarrow (E)$
- (7) $F \rightarrow \text{id}$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

$$I_0 = \{[E' \rightarrow \bullet E],\; [E \rightarrow \bullet E+T],\; [E \rightarrow \bullet T],\; [T \rightarrow \bullet T*F],\; [T \rightarrow \bullet F],\; [F \rightarrow \bullet (E)],\; [F \rightarrow \bullet \text{id}]\}$$

$$I_1 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, E) = \{[E' \rightarrow E \bullet],\; [E \rightarrow E \bullet +T]\}$$

$$I_2 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, T) = \{[E \rightarrow T \bullet],\; [T \rightarrow T \bullet *F]\}$$

$$I_3 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, F) = \{[T \rightarrow F \bullet]\}$$

$$I_4 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, () = \{[F \rightarrow ( \bullet E)],\; [E \rightarrow \bullet E+T],\; [E \rightarrow \bullet T],\; [T \rightarrow \bullet T*F],\; [T \rightarrow \bullet F],\; [F \rightarrow \bullet (E)],\; [F \rightarrow \bullet \text{id}]\}$$

$$I_5 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, \text{id}) = \{[F \rightarrow \text{id} \bullet]\}$$

(Further states for $+$ and $*$ and $\text{id}$ from $I_1$, $I_2$, $I_4$ are computed similarly in a complete solution.)

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - ✅ **LR(0) uses ZERO lookahead** — only stack + DFA state matters.
> - ✅ **Augmented grammar** $G' = G + \{S' \rightarrow S\}$ is **mandatory** for ACCEPT state.
> - ✅ **CLOSURE** expands items when a non-terminal appears right of the dot.
> - ✅ **GOTO** advances the dot past a symbol and re-closes.
> - ✅ **Reduce** in LR(0) applies to **ALL** terminals → causes many conflicts.
> - ✅ **Accept** only when item is $[S' \rightarrow S \bullet]$ AND input is $\$$.
> - ✅ **Shift-Reduce Conflict** → grammar is **not LR(0)**.
> - ✅ **Reduce-Reduce Conflict** → grammar is **not LR(0)**.
> - ✅ LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1) — LR(0) is the **weakest** in the LR family.
> - ✅ **YACC/Bison** use **LALR(1)** internally (a refinement of LR(0) item sets).
> - ✅ Each state $I_i$ corresponds to one row in the **ACTION** and **GOTO** tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLR(1) Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SLR(1) Parser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **SLR(1) Parser** stands for **Simple LR(1) Parser**. It is a bottom-up, shift-reduce parsing technique that uses the **LR(0) canonical collection of items** as its state-transition backbone, but augments the reduction decision with a **one-token lookahead** drawn from the **FOLLOW** set of the left-hand side non-terminal.

Formally, an SLR(1) grammar is one for which the SLR parsing table can be constructed **without any shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts** when the rule for reduction is:

> A reduction by production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ in state $I_i$ on lookahead $a$ is permitted **only if** $a \in FOLLOW(A)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The SLR(1) parser is positioned in the hierarchy of LR parsers as a strict middle-ground. It is **more powerful than LR(0)** (because it uses lookahead) and **less powerful than canonical LR(1) and LALR(1)** (because it consults a coarse-grained FOLLOW set instead of a precise per-item lookahead set).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of an SLR(1) parser as an **airport immigration counter**.

- Each **LR(0) state** is a **check-in gate**.
- Each **item** $A \rightarrow \alpha.\beta$ is a **passenger** whose passport lists the destinations ($\beta$) still to be visited.
- When a passenger reaches a state where his trip is complete (the dot is at the end, e.g. $A \rightarrow \alpha.$), the officer asks: *"Where are you headed next?"* This is the **lookahead token**.
- The officer consults a **whitelist** — the **FOLLOW(A)** set — to confirm that the next country is on the approved list. If yes, the passenger is "reduced" (allowed to proceed to gate $A$); if not, he is held (a parsing error).

This is in contrast to LR(0), which would allow the passenger through **without checking** the destination — leading to chaotic conflicts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: SLR(1) inherits the *small* LR(0) state machine, but adds an *intelligent* gate-keeping layer via FOLLOW sets — hence the name "Simple" LR.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Building Blocks

#### A. LR(0) Item
An **LR(0) item** (or simply *item*) of a grammar $G$ is a production of $G$ with a distinguished dot $\cdot$ at some position on its right-hand side.

$$A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_{i-1} \cdot X_i \dots X_n$$

The dot indicates how much of the production has been recognized so far.

#### B. Augmented Grammar
If $G$ has start symbol $S$, the augmented grammar $G'$ has a new start symbol $S'$ and an additional production:

$$S' \rightarrow S$$

The purpose is to force the parser to declare acceptance exactly when $S'$ has been reduced.

#### C. The CLOSURE Operation

Given a set of items $I$:

$$CLOSURE(I) = I \cup \{ B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma \mid \exists A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta \in I \text{ and } B \rightarrow \gamma \in G \}$$

In plain English: *if we have just seen something that could lead to non-terminal $B$, we must also consider all possible ways $B$ might begin*.

#### D. The GOTO Operation

$$GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE(\{ A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta \mid A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta \in I \})$$

It computes the state obtained by "advancing the dot over symbol $X$".

#### E. Canonical Collection
The set of all reachable states $\{I_0, I_1, I_2, \dots, I_n\}$ is constructed starting from $CLOSURE(\{S' \rightarrow \cdot S\})$ and applying GOTO until no new states appear.

#### F. The SLR(1) Parsing Table

The table has two parts: **ACTION** and **GOTO**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation Key |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Augmented Production | $S' \rightarrow S$ | $S'$ is fresh start symbol |
| 2 | CLOSURE trigger | If $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta \in I$, add $B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ for all $B$-productions | $B$ non-terminal, $\gamma$ any RHS |
| 3 | GOTO definition | $GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE(\{A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta\})$ | $X$ a grammar symbol |
| 4 | FOLLOW seed | $\$ \in FOLLOW(S)$ | $S$ is original start symbol |
| 5 | FOLLOW propagation | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then $FIRST(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq FOLLOW(B)$ | Standard follow rule |
| 6 | FOLLOW cascade | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\beta)$, then $FOLLOW(A) \subseteq FOLLOW(B)$ | Cascade rule |
| 7 | Shift action | $ACTION[i, a] = \text{shift } j$ if $GOTO(I_i, a) = I_j$ and $a$ is terminal | $i, j$ state indices |
| 8 | Reduce action | $ACTION[i, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \alpha$ if $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \in I_i$ AND $a \in FOLLOW(A)$ | The crucial SLR rule |
| 9 | Accept action | $ACTION[i, \$] = \text{accept}$ if $S' \rightarrow S \cdot \in I_i$ | Only on end-of-input |
| 10 | GOTO entry | $GOTO[i, A] = j$ if $GOTO(I_i, A) = I_j$ and $A$ non-terminal | Pure state transition |
| 11 | Shift-Reduce Conflict | $\exists\, i, a$ such that both shift $j$ and reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$ are mandated on $a$ | Grammar is **not** SLR(1) |
| 12 | Reduce-Reduce Conflict | $\exists\, i, a$ such that reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$ and reduce $B \rightarrow \beta$ are both mandated on $a$ | Grammar is **not** SLR(1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical-Bar Caution for Tables**: The cardinality of a set, e.g. $\vert FOLLOW(A) \vert$, must be written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table cells.

### 2.3 Why SLR(1) Matters in Engineering

SLR(1) parsers are a cornerstone of **compiler front-ends, IDEs, and static analysis tools** (e.g., GCC's `bison` default mode uses a closely related LALR(1) variant). Understanding SLR(1) equips you to:

- Diagnose grammar ambiguity in language specifications.
- Decide when to upgrade to LALR(1) or LR(1) tables.
- Build parsers for configuration languages, query languages, and DSLs in real industrial systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Running Example Grammar

We will use the **classical arithmetic expression grammar** (Aho et al.) throughout.

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &: \quad E \rightarrow E + T \mid T \\
  & \quad\quad T \rightarrow T * F \mid F \\
  & \quad\quad F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Augmented Grammar** $G'$ (adding $E' \rightarrow E$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0) & \quad E' \rightarrow E \\
(1) & \quad E \rightarrow E + T \\
(2) & \quad E \rightarrow T \\
(3) & \quad T \rightarrow T * F \\
(4) & \quad T \rightarrow F \\
(5) & \quad F \rightarrow ( E ) \\
(6) & \quad F \rightarrow \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Building the Canonical Collection of LR(0) Items

We start with $I_0 = CLOSURE(\{E' \rightarrow \cdot E\})$.

**CLOSURE($I_0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E' \rightarrow \cdot E \quad \text{(seed)} \\
& E \rightarrow \cdot E + T \quad \text{(because of } \cdot E \text{)} \\
& E \rightarrow \cdot T \quad \text{(because of } \cdot E \text{)} \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot T * F \quad \text{(because of } \cdot T \text{)} \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot F \quad \text{(because of } \cdot T \text{)} \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot ( E ) \quad \text{(because of } \cdot F \text{)} \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id} \quad \text{(because of } \cdot F \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_1 = GOTO(I_0, E)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E' \rightarrow E \cdot \\
& E \rightarrow E \cdot + T
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_2 = GOTO(I_0, T)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E \rightarrow T \cdot \\
& T \rightarrow T \cdot * F
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_3 = GOTO(I_0, F)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T \rightarrow F \cdot
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_4 = GOTO(I_0, ()$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& F \rightarrow ( \cdot E ) \\
& E \rightarrow \cdot E + T \\
& E \rightarrow \cdot T \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot T * F \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot F \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot ( E ) \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_5 = GOTO(I_0, \text{id})$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& F \rightarrow \text{id} \cdot
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_6 = GOTO(I_1, +)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E \rightarrow E + \cdot T \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot T * F \\
& T \rightarrow \cdot F \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot ( E ) \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_7 = GOTO(I_2, *)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T \rightarrow T * \cdot F \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot ( E ) \\
& F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_8 = GOTO(I_4, E)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& F \rightarrow ( E \cdot ) \\
& E \rightarrow E \cdot + T
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_9 = GOTO(I_6, T)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E \rightarrow E + T \cdot \\
& T \rightarrow T \cdot * F
\end{aligned}
$$

**State $I_{10} = GOTO(I_7, F)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T \rightarrow T * F \cdot
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Re-entrant transitions: $GOTO(I_4, T) = I_2$, $GOTO(I_4, F) = I_3$, $GOTO(I_4, () = I_4$, $GOTO(I_4, \text{id}) = I_5$; similarly for $I_6, I_7$. These are already-known states and do not create new ones.

The canonical collection therefore contains **11 states**: $I_0, I_1, \dots, I_{10}$.

### 3.3 Computing FOLLOW Sets

Using the standard rules on the augmented grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOLLOW(E') &= \{ \,\$\,\} \\
FOLLOW(E)   &= FOLLOW(E') \cup \{ ) \} \cup \{+\} = \{ \$\,\text{,}\, + \text{,}\, ) \} \\
FOLLOW(T)   &= FOLLOW(E) \cup \{*\} = \{ \$\,\text{,}\, + \text{,}\, * \text{,}\, ) \} \\
FOLLOW(F)   &= FOLLOW(T) = \{ \$\,\text{,}\, + \text{,}\, * \text{,}\, ) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step-by-step justification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \$ \in FOLLOW(E') && \text{(rule 4)} \\
& \$ \in FOLLOW(E)  && \text{(from } E' \rightarrow E\text{)} \\
& + \in FOLLOW(E)   && \text{(from } E \rightarrow E+T\text{, rule 6 cascade)} \\
& ) \in FOLLOW(E)   && \text{(from } F \rightarrow (E)\text{, rule 5)} \\
& * \in FOLLOW(T)   && \text{(from } T \rightarrow T*F\text{, rule 5)} \\
& FOLLOW(F) = FOLLOW(T) && \text{(from } T \rightarrow F\text{, rule 6 cascade)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 The SLR(1) Parsing Table

Applying the rules from the formula sheet:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{State} & \textbf{id} & \textbf{+} & \textbf{*} & \textbf{(} & \textbf{)} & \textbf{\$} & \textbf{E} & \textbf{T} & \textbf{F} \\
\hline
I_0  & s_5 &       &       & s_4 &       &       & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
\hline
I_1  &     & s_6   &       &     &       & \text{acc} &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_2  &     & r_2   & s_7   &     & r_2   & r_2   &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_3  &     & r_4   & r_4   &     & r_4   & r_4   &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_4  & s_5 &       &       & s_4 &       &       & 8 & 2 & 3 \\
\hline
I_5  &     & r_6   & r_6   &     & r_6   & r_6   &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_6  & s_5 &       &       & s_4 &       &       &   & 9 & 3 \\
\hline
I_7  & s_5 &       &       & s_4 &       &       &   &   & 10 \\
\hline
I_8  &     & s_6   &       &     & s_9   &       &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_9  &     & r_1   & s_7   &     & r_1   & r_1   &   &   &   \\
\hline
I_{10} &   & r_3   & r_3   &     & r_3   & r_3   &   &   &   \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Reduction derivation for every state with a final-dot item:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &: \; E \rightarrow T \cdot \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (2)\; E \rightarrow T \text{ on } FOLLOW(E) = \{+, \text{,}\, \$\} \\
I_3 &: \; T \rightarrow F \cdot \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (4)\; T \rightarrow F \text{ on } FOLLOW(T) = \{+, *, \text{,}\, \$\} \\
I_5 &: \; F \rightarrow \text{id} \cdot \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (6)\; F \rightarrow \text{id} \text{ on } FOLLOW(F) = \{+, *, \text{,}\, \$\} \\
I_8 &: \; F \rightarrow (E \cdot) \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (5)\; F \rightarrow (E) \text{ on } FOLLOW(F) = \{+, *, \text{,}\, \$\} \\
I_9 &: \; E \rightarrow E+T \cdot \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (1)\; E \rightarrow E+T \text{ on } FOLLOW(E) = \{+, \text{,}\, \$\} \\
I_{10} &: \; T \rightarrow T*F \cdot \quad &&\Rightarrow\; \text{reduce } (3)\; T \rightarrow T*F \text{ on } FOLLOW(T) = \{+, *, \text{,}\, \$\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Conflict Verification

A grammar is SLR(1) iff every table cell contains **at most one entry**.

- **State $I_2$**: column `*` has `s_7`; the FOLLOW(E) entries `$+)$` are reductions. Since $*$ is **not** in FOLLOW(E), no conflict.
- **State $I_8$**: column `)` has `s_9`. Reduction by (5) is also on `)`. **Conflict?** Check: shift `s_9` requires $GOTO(I_8, )) = I_9$; reduction requires $) \in FOLLOW(F)$. But $I_9$ exists from $GOTO(I_1, +)$, not from $GOTO(I_8, ))$. In our canonical collection, $GOTO(I_8, ))$ is **not** computed as $I_9$. So $I_8$'s `)` slot holds only the reduction `r_5`. **No conflict.**

All 11 states pass the conflict test → the grammar **is SLR(1)**.

### 3.6 Parsing Trace: Input `id + id * id`

| Step | Stack | Input | Action | Justification |
|------|-------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | `0` | `id+id*id$` | shift 5 | $ACTION[0, \text{id}] = s_5$ |
| 2 | `0 id 5` | `+id*id$` | reduce by $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $ACTION[5, +] = r_6$ |
| 3 | `0 F 3` | `+id*id$` | reduce by $T \rightarrow F$ | $ACTION[3, +] = r_4$ |
| 4 | `0 T 2` | `+id*id$` | reduce by $E \rightarrow T$ | $ACTION[2, +] = r_2$ |
| 5 | `0 E 1` | `+id*id$` | shift 6 | $ACTION[1, +] = s_6$ |
| 6 | `0 E 1 + 6` | `id*id$` | shift 5 | $ACTION[6, \text{id}] = s_5$ |
| 7 | `0 E 1 + 6 id 5` | `*id$` | reduce by $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $ACTION[5, *] = r_6$ |
| 8 | `0 E 1 + 6 F 3` | `*id$` | reduce by $T \rightarrow F$ | $ACTION[3, *] = r_4$ |
| 9 | `0 E 1 + 6 T 9` | `*id$` | shift 7 | $ACTION[9, *] = s_7$ |
| 10 | `0 E 1 + 6 T 9 * 7` | `id$` | shift 5 | $ACTION[7, \text{id}] = s_5$ |
| 11 | `0 E 1 + 6 T 9 * 7 id 5` | `$` | reduce by $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $ACTION[5, \$] = r_6$ |
| 12 | `0 E 1 + 6 T 9 * 7 F 10` | `$` | reduce by $T \rightarrow T*F$ | $ACTION[10, \$] = r_3$ |
| 13 | `0 E 1 + 6 T 9` | `$` | reduce by $E \rightarrow E+T$ | $ACTION[9, \$] = r_1$ |
| 14 | `0 E 1` | `$` | **ACCEPT** | $ACTION[1, \$] = \text{acc}$ |

The string is accepted, demonstrating **correct operator-precedence parsing** ($*$ binds tighter than $+$).

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: SLR(1) vs Siblings

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{LR(0)} & \textbf{SLR(1)} & \textbf{Canonical LR(1)} \\
\hline
\text{State count} & \text{Small} & \text{Small} & \text{Very large} \\
\hline
\text{Lookahead} & \text{None} & \text{FOLLOW-based} & \text{Per-item} \\
\hline
\text{Power} & \text{Lowest} & \text{Medium} & \text{Highest} \\
\hline
\text{Conflicts to resolve} & \text{Too many} & \text{Fewer} & \text{Fewest} \\
\hline
\text{Industrial use} & \text{Pedagogical} & \text{Teaching} & \text{Rare in practice} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SLR(1) Parser Engine — Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the run-time architecture of an SLR(1) parsing engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Token Stream and Parser Stack] --> B[Read Next Token]
    B --> C[Lookup ACTION Table at Top State]
    C --> D{Action Type}
    D --> E[SHIFT Push Token and Target State]
    D --> F[REDUCE Pop RHS Times Two and Lookup GOTO Table]
    D --> G[ACCEPT Parsing Successful]
    D --> H[ERROR Invoke Error Recovery Routine]
    E --> B
    F --> I[Push Left Side Symbol and New State]
    I --> B
    H --> J[Log Error and Report Diagnostics]
```

### 4.2 Construction Pipeline — Offline Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Grammar G] --> B[Augment Grammar Add New Start Production]
    B --> C[Compute Augmented Grammar G Prime]
    C --> D[Initialize I0 CLOSURE of Seed Item]
    D --> E[Apply GOTO for Each Grammar Symbol]
    E --> F{New State Appeared?}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| G[Canonical Collection Complete]
    G --> H[Compute FOLLOW Sets for All Non Terminals]
    H --> I[Build ACTION Table Using Shift Reduce Accept Rules]
    I --> J[Build GOTO Table for Non Terminals]
    J --> K{SLR One Conflict Check}
    K -->|No Conflicts| L[Valid SLR One Grammar]
    K -->|Conflicts Found| M[Upgrade to LALR One or LR One]
```

### 4.3 Canonical Collection — Partial State Transition (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0 -->|id shift 5| I5
    I0 -->|open paren shift 4| I4
    I0 -->|E goto 1| I1
    I0 -->|T goto 2| I2
    I0 -->|F goto 3| I3
    I1 -->|plus shift 6| I6
    I2 -->|star shift 7| I7
    I4 -->|E goto 8| I8
    I6 -->|T goto 9| I9
    I7 -->|F goto 10| I10
    I8 -->|close paren reduce by 5| I8r
    I9 -->|star shift 7| I7
    I10 -->|reduce by 3| I10r
```

> [!NOTE]
> `I8r` and `I10r` are not separate states; they represent the **reduction decision points** in those states — useful for visualizing where FOLLOW-set logic kicks in.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is an SLR(1) parser? How does it differ from an LR(0) parser?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **SLR(1) parser** is a shift-reduce parser that uses the LR(0) canonical collection of items as its DFA, but consults the **FOLLOW set** of the left-hand side non-terminal before deciding to reduce on a given lookahead. It is "simple" because it reuses the small LR(0) state machine and adds only a one-token lookahead check.
>
> It differs from **LR(0)** in two key ways:
> 1. **LR(0)** reduces by a production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ in state $I_i$ **unconditionally** on *any* lookahead (effectively no lookahead).
> 2. **SLR(1)** reduces by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ in state $I_i$ **only when the lookahead $a \in FOLLOW(A)$**. This single restriction resolves many spurious conflicts, making SLR(1) strictly more powerful than LR(0).
>
> Thus, every LR(0) grammar is also SLR(1), but not vice-versa.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the role of FOLLOW sets in the construction of an SLR(1) parsing table.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> FOLLOW sets are the **gatekeepers** for reductions in an SLR(1) parser. The parsing table algorithm fills the reduction entries using the rule:
> *"If state $I_i$ contains an item $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot$ and the next input token $a$ belongs to $FOLLOW(A)$, place a reduce-by-$A \rightarrow \alpha$ entry in $ACTION[i, a]$."*
>
> Without FOLLOW sets, the parser would attempt reductions on tokens that can never legally follow $A$ in any sentential form, producing spurious shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts.
>
> Hence FOLLOW sets convert an LR(0)-style "reduce-everywhere" policy into a context-sensitive decision, increasing parser power without enlarging the state set.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Consider the grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A \\
A &\rightarrow aA \;\mid\; b
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)** Construct the SLR(1) parsing table for the above grammar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the parsing of the input string **`a a b`** using the constructed table. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Step 1 — Augmented Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0)\;& S' \rightarrow S \\
(1)\;& S \rightarrow A \\
(2)\;& A \rightarrow aA \\
(3)\;& A \rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Canonical Collection of LR(0) Items:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &: \{ S' \rightarrow \cdot S,\;\; S \rightarrow \cdot A,\;\; A \rightarrow \cdot aA,\;\; A \rightarrow \cdot b \} \\
I_1 &: GOTO(I_0, S) = \{ S' \rightarrow S \cdot \} \\
I_2 &: GOTO(I_0, A) = \{ S \rightarrow A \cdot \} \\
I_3 &: GOTO(I_0, a) = \{ A \rightarrow a \cdot A,\;\; A \rightarrow \cdot aA,\;\; A \rightarrow \cdot b \} \\
I_4 &: GOTO(I_0, b) = \{ A \rightarrow b \cdot \} \\
I_5 &: GOTO(I_3, A) = \{ A \rightarrow aA \cdot \} \\
I_6 &: GOTO(I_3, a) = I_3 \quad (\text{re-entrant}) \\
I_7 &: GOTO(I_3, b) = I_4 \quad (\text{re-entrant})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — FOLLOW Sets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOLLOW(S') &= \{ \,\$\,\} \\
FOLLOW(S)   &= \{ \,\$\,\} \\
FOLLOW(A)   &= FOLLOW(S) \cup FOLLOW(A) = \{ \,\$\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SLR(1) Parsing Table:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{State} & \textbf{a} & \textbf{b} & \textbf{\$} & \textbf{S} & \textbf{A} \\
\hline
I_0 & s_3 & s_4 &       & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
I_1 &     &     & \text{acc} &   &   \\
\hline
I_2 &     &     & r_1   &   &   \\
\hline
I_3 & s_3 & s_4 &       &   & 5 \\
\hline
I_4 &     &     & r_3   &   &   \\
\hline
I_5 &     &     & r_2   &   &   \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Since every cell has at most one entry, the grammar **is SLR(1)**.

**(b) Parse Trace for `a a b`:**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action | Reasoning |
|------|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | `0` | `aab$` | shift 3 | $ACTION[0, a] = s_3$ |
| 2 | `0 a 3` | `ab$` | shift 3 | $ACTION[3, a] = s_3$ |
| 3 | `0 a 3 a 3` | `b$` | shift 4 | $ACTION[3, b] = s_4$ |
| 4 | `0 a 3 a 3 b 4` | `$` | reduce by $A \rightarrow b$ | $ACTION[4, \$] = r_3$, then $GOTO[3, A] = 5$ |
| 5 | `0 a 3 a 3 A 5` | `$` | reduce by $A \rightarrow aA$ | $ACTION[5, \$] = r_2$, then $GOTO[3, A] = 5$ |
| 6 | `0 a 3 A 5` | `$` | reduce by $A \rightarrow aA$ | $ACTION[5, \$] = r_2$, then $GOTO[0, A] = 2$ |
| 7 | `0 A 2` | `$` | reduce by $S \rightarrow A$ | $ACTION[2, \$] = r_1$, then $GOTO[0, S] = 1$ |
| 8 | `0 S 1` | `$` | **ACCEPT** | $ACTION[1, \$] = \text{acc}$ |

The string `aab` is accepted in 8 moves.

---

#### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the SLR(1) parsing table construction algorithm with its auxiliary functions (CLOSURE, GOTO, FOLLOW). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts. Give one example grammar each where SLR(1) fails and justify why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) SLR(1) Construction Algorithm (7 marks):**

> **Algorithm Outline:**
>
> 1. **Augment** the grammar with $S' \rightarrow S$.
> 2. **Compute** the canonical collection of LR(0) items using CLOSURE and GOTO.
>    - $CLOSURE(I)$ adds every item $B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ whenever $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta \in I$.
>    - $GOTO(I, X)$ advances the dot over symbol $X$ and re-applies CLOSURE.
> 3. **Compute** FOLLOW(A) for every non-terminal using the standard rules (start symbol seeds with $\$$, propagation through RHS).
> 4. **Build the ACTION table:**
>    - Shift: if $GOTO(I_i, a) = I_j$ for terminal $a$, then $ACTION[i, a] = \text{shift } j$.
>    - Reduce: if $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \in I_i$ and $a \in FOLLOW(A)$, then $ACTION[i, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \alpha$.
>    - Accept: if $S' \rightarrow S \cdot \in I_i$, then $ACTION[i, \$] = \text{accept}$.
> 5. **Build the GOTO table:** for every $GOTO(I_i, A) = I_j$ with $A$ non-terminal, set $GOTO[i, A] = j$.
> 6. **Verify** that no table cell has multiple entries; if it does, the grammar is not SLR(1).

**(b) Conflicts in SLR(1) (7 marks):**

> **Shift-Reduce Conflict**: Occurs when a state $I_i$ contains both
> $$A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a \beta \quad \text{and} \quad B \rightarrow \gamma \cdot$$
> with $a \in FOLLOW(B)$. The parser cannot decide whether to **shift** $a$ or **reduce** by $B \rightarrow \gamma$.
>
> **Example — Not SLR(1):**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S &\rightarrow L = R \;\mid\; R \\
> L &\rightarrow * R \;\mid\; \text{id} \\
> R &\rightarrow L
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> In state containing $\{ S \rightarrow L \cdot = R,\; R \rightarrow L \cdot \}$, on `=` the parser is torn between shifting `=` (to eventually form $L = R$) and reducing $R \rightarrow L$ (since $R$ is allowed before `=` because $S \Rightarrow L = R$). This is an SLR(1) shift-reduce conflict.
>
> **Reduce-Reduce Conflict**: Occurs when a state $I_i$ contains
> $$A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \quad \text{and} \quad B \rightarrow \beta \cdot$$
> with $FOLLOW(A) \cap FOLLOW(B) \neq \emptyset$. The parser cannot choose which production to reduce by.
>
> **Example — Not SLR(1):**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S &\rightarrow aA \;\mid\; bB \;\mid\; c \\
> A &\rightarrow d \\
> B &\rightarrow d
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> The state reached after reading $d$ contains both $A \rightarrow d \cdot$ and $B \rightarrow d \cdot$. Since $FOLLOW(A) \cap FOLLOW(B)$ is non-empty (both end with `c` in some derivations), we get a reduce-reduce conflict.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **LR(0) item** with **LR(1) item**. An LR(0) item has *no* lookahead component — the dot is the only marker. Don't write `$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, a]$` for SLR(1); the lookahead `$a$` is used only during **table construction**, not stored in the item itself.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to use the **augmented production** $S' \rightarrow S$ when computing CLOSURE — without it, the parser has no formal accept action.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing `accept` in $ACTION[i, \$]$ for any state containing $S \rightarrow \alpha \cdot$; the accept action is **only** for $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$, not for $S$.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Conflating SLR(1) and LALR(1). LALR(1) merges LR(1) states with identical *cores*; SLR(1) does **not** merge anything — it just uses the LR(0) automaton with FOLLOW-based reductions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition**: SLR(1) = LR(0) items + 1-token lookahead restricted by FOLLOW(LHS).
- **Power Hierarchy**: $\text{LR}(0) \subsetneq \text{SLR}(1) \subsetneq \text{LALR}(1) \subsetneq \text{LR}(1)$.
- **State Count**: Same as LR(0) — typically a few hundred for real languages.
- **Augmented Grammar**: Always add $S' \rightarrow S$ before any analysis.
- **CLOSURE Rule**: Add $B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ for every $B$ appearing immediately after the dot.
- **GOTO Rule**: Move the dot over symbol $X$, then CLOSE.
- **Reduction Condition**: $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \in I_i$ AND $a \in FOLLOW(A)$ $\Rightarrow$ reduce on $a$.
- **Conflict Types**: shift-reduce and reduce-reduce; both make the grammar **not** SLR(1).
- **Accept Cell**: Only $ACTION[i, \$]$ where $S' \rightarrow S \cdot \in I_i$.
- **GOTO Table**: Populated **only for non-terminals**; terminal shifts go into ACTION.
- **Vertical-Bar Warning**: Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table renderer.
- **Industrial Connection**: GNU Bison's default mode is LALR(1), a direct descendant of SLR(1); understanding SLR(1) is essential for debugging Bison `shift/reduce` and `reduce/reduce` errors.
- **Typical Exam Trap**: When asked "Is this grammar SLR(1)?", never answer by inspecting the grammar alone — **always build the canonical collection and the table**, then check for multi-entry cells.
- **Memory Aid**: SLR = **S**hift-**L**ookahead using **R**eduction rule based on FOLLOW.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CLR(1) Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CLR(1) Parser ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CLR(1) Parser** stands for **Canonical LR(1) Parser**. It is the most powerful, most granular, and most expressive member of the LR family of bottom-up shift-reduce parsers. A CLR(1) parsing table is constructed from the **canonical collection of sets of LR(1) items**, where each item carries exactly **one terminal symbol of lookahead**. An **LR(1) item** is a pair of the form: $$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \quad a]$$ where: - $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta$ is a production of the augmented grammar $G'$. - The dot $\cdot$ indicates the parser's current position within the production. - $a \in T \cup \{\$\}$ is the **lookahead symbol** — a terminal (or end-marker $\$$) that must immediately follow the string derivable from $\beta$ in order for this item to be relevant for parsing. The **1** in **CLR(1)** explicitly denotes that **one lookahead symbol** is attached to each item. This is in contrast to **SLR(1)** (Simple LR) and **LALR(1)** (LookAhead LR), both of which are space-optimized approximations of CLR(1). ### 1.2 The Hierarchy of LR Parsers (KTU Board Hot-Spot) > [!NOTE] > The KTU syllabus and previous university questions repeatedly stress the comparison: **CLR(1) ⊃ LALR(1) ⊃ SLR(1) ⊃ LR(0)** in terms of language-recognition power. CLR(1) recognizes the largest class of grammars; however, it also requires the largest parsing table. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Detective" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for CLR(1) items with lookaheads:** > > Imagine a detective who is reconstructing a sentence word by word (a shift-reduce parse). At each step, the detective holds an **incomplete phrase template** (the production with the dot) — for example, "*subject is being verified, but the object is missing.*" In an LR(0) world, the detective would commit to reducing as soon as the template is complete, regardless of what comes next. In the **CLR(1) world**, however, the detective is given a **hint about the very next word** (the lookahead). Only if the next word actually matches the hint will the reduction be performed. This single extra hint eliminates many of the reduce/reduce and shift/reduce conflicts that plague weaker parsers. ### 1.4 The Augmented Grammar Convention Every CLR(1) construction begins by augmenting the original grammar $G$ with a fresh start production: $$S' \rightarrow S$$ where $S' \notin N$ (non-terminals of $G$) and $\$$ (end-marker) is appended to the input. This guarantees a single accepting state identified by the item $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \$$]. ### 1.5 Position of CLR(1) in the Compiler Pipeline Within a KTU-style LALR(1) / YACC-driven compiler, CLR(1) serves as the **theoretical upper bound** — the strongest parser that fully utilizes one-symbol lookahead. In practice, tools like **GNU Bison** and **YACC** use LALR(1) tables, but the canonical LR(1) automaton is the mathematical reference from which they are derived. | Property | CLR(1) Value | |---|---| | Lookahead count | **1** terminal | | Table size (states) | Largest in LR family | | Conflict rate | Lowest in LR family | | Practical tool support | Mostly academic; industrial tools approximate it | ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Set Operations The canonical collection of LR(1) item sets is built using two mutually recursive operations: **CLOSURE** and **GOTO**. #### 2.1.1 CLOSURE Operation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU standard form).** Given a set $I$ of LR(1) items, $\text{CLOSURE}(I)$ is the smallest set satisfying: > > 1. Every item in $I$ is in $\text{CLOSURE}(I)$. > 2. If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta, \quad a] \in \text{CLOSURE}(I)$ and $B \rightarrow \gamma$ is a production, then for every $b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a)$, the item $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma, \quad b]$ is added to $\text{CLOSURE}(I)$. > 3. Step 2 is repeated until no new items can be added. The lookahead $b$ is computed from $\text{FIRST}(\beta a)$ — the union of $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ and, if $\beta \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $a$ is also included. #### 2.1.2 GOTO Operation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU standard form).** Given a set $I$ of LR(1) items and a grammar symbol $X$ (terminal or non-terminal): > $$\text{GOTO}(I, X) = \text{CLOSURE}\big(\{[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta, \quad a] \;\vert\; [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta, \quad a] \in I\}\big)$$ In plain English: shift the dot past $X$ in every applicable item, then take the closure of the resulting set. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — CLR(1) Construction > [!NOTE] > The table below condenses every equation, condition, and bound the KTU examiner can test. **All rows use $\vert$ or `\mid` to avoid markdown-pipe collisions.** | # | Quantity / Step | Formal Expression | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | LR(1) item structure | $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \; a]$ | Atomic state of the parser stack | | 2 | Initial item | $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]$ | Sole seed of the canonical collection | | 3 | Lookahead for kernel expansion | $b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a)$ | Where $\beta a$ means $\beta$ concatenated with $a$ | | 4 | CLOSURE termination | $\text{CLOSURE}(I) = I$ | Fixed-point reached | | 5 | GOTO transition trigger | $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta, \; a] \in I$ | $X$ read by parser shifts dot past $X$ | | 6 | Accepting item | $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]$ | Identifies the single accept state | | 7 | Reduce action | Item is $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot, \; a]$ and lookahead $= a$ | Triggered only when input symbol equals $a$ | | 8 | Shift action | Item is $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a \beta, \; b]$ with $a$ on input | Push state $\text{GOTO}(I_i, a)$ | | 9 | Total states | $\vert C \vert$ where $C$ is the canonical collection | Scales linearly with grammar size | | 10 | Canonical collection | $C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n\}$ | Final collection after fixed-point | ### 2.3 Construction Algorithm — The Big Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Style Algorithm — Canonical LR(1) Collection:** > > - **Input:** Augmented grammar $G'$. > - **Output:** Canonical collection $C = \{I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n\}$. > > **Step 1.** Initialize $C = \{\text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]\})\}$; label this $I_0$. > **Step 2.** For every $I_i \in C$ and every grammar symbol $X$ such that $\text{GOTO}(I_i, X) \neq \varnothing$ and $\text{GOTO}(I_i, X) \notin C$: add the new set as $I_{k+1}$. > **Step 3.** Repeat Step 2 until no new item sets are added (fixed-point). > **Step 4.** Number every item set $I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n$ — these become **states** of the DFA. ### 2.4 Building the CLR(1) Parsing Table From the canonical collection $C$, the parser constructs two tables: **ACTION** and **GOTO**. | Sub-Table | Dimension | Cell Content | |---|---|---| | ACTION | states $\times$ terminals | **shift $s_j$**, **reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$**, **accept**, **error** | | GOTO | states $\times$ non-terminals | Next state index (or blank) | The **filling rules** for any state $i$ derived from item set $I_i$: - **Shift:** If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a\beta, \; b] \in I_i$ and $\text{GOTO}(I_i, a) = I_j$, then $\text{ACTION}[i, a] = \text{shift } j$. - **Reduce:** If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot, \; a] \in I_i$ (and $A \neq S'$), then $\text{ACTION}[i, a] = \text{reduce by } A \rightarrow \alpha$. - **Accept:** If $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$] \in I_i$, then $\text{ACTION}[i, \$] = \text{accept}$. - **GOTO fill:** If $\text{GOTO}(I_i, A) = I_j$ for a non-terminal $A$, then $\text{GOTO-TABLE}[i, A] = j$. ### 2.5 Why CLR(1) Has the Highest Power The lookahead attached to each item restricts reduce actions to **only those input symbols** that could legitimately follow the handle. SLR(1), in contrast, uses the entire FOLLOW set — a coarse over-approximation — which produces spurious reductions and thus spurious conflicts. CLR(1)'s fine-grained lookaheads eliminate these spurious conflicts, expanding the class of grammars it can parse. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility CLR(1) parsing underpins several precision-critical tools: - **Compiler-verification frameworks** that mathematically prove a grammar is conflict-free under LR(1). - **Parser generators** such as the academic **CUP / Beaver** (LALR(1)-by-default, but offer LR(1) modes). - **Formal language research** in parsing expression grammars (PEGs), GLR variants, and certified compilers like **CompCert**. - **Domain-specific languages** in aerospace, financial modeling, and protocol design where grammars are deliberately complex and require the strongest available parser. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Canonical Collection — KTU Standard Example.** > This exhaustive construction appears frequently in KTU university examinations. We trace every closure, every goto, and every state. ### 3.1 The Grammar Consider the following classic KTU-exam grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\;\rightarrow\; C\,C \\ C &\;\rightarrow\; c\,C \\ C &\;\rightarrow\; d \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Augmented Grammar $$ \begin{aligned} S' &\;\rightarrow\; S \\ S &\;\rightarrow\; C\,C \\ C &\;\rightarrow\; c\,C \\ C &\;\rightarrow\; d \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Building $I_0$ — The Initial Item Set Start with the initial item and apply CLOSURE. **Initial kernel:** $$[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]$$ **Step 1 (expansion):** The dot is before $S$. For every production of $S$ — namely $S \rightarrow CC$ — and lookahead $b \in \text{FIRST}(\$) = \{\$\}$, add: $$[S \rightarrow \cdot CC, \; \$]$$ **Step 2 (expansion on $C$):** The new item has the dot before $C$. For each production $C \rightarrow cC$ and $C \rightarrow d$, lookahead $b \in \text{FIRST}(C \cdot \$) = \text{FIRST}(C\$) = \{c, d, \$\}$. However, $\text{FIRST}(\beta a)$ here is $\text{FIRST}(C \cdot \$)$ where $\beta = \varepsilon$ and $a = \$$ — so $b \in \text{FIRST}(\$) = \{\$\}$. But because $C$ is preceded by $C$ in $S \rightarrow CC$, we consider all of $C$'s derivable first terminals: $$[C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$/c] \quad \text{(two items)}$$ $$[C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$/c] \quad \text{(two items)}$$ **Resulting $I_0$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_0 = \{\;&[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$],\\ &[S \rightarrow \cdot CC, \; \$],\\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$],\\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c],\\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$],\\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; c]\;\} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Reading the lookaheads:** The lookahead $c$ on some $C$-items arises because $C$ appears in the right-hand side of $S \rightarrow CC$, where the second $C$ can be followed by $c$ via the production $C \rightarrow cC$. ### 3.4 GOTO Transitions from $I_0$ #### 3.4.1 GOTO$(I_0, S)$ Shift dot past $S$: $$[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]$$ **Closure adds nothing further.** Therefore: $$I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]\}$$ #### 3.4.2 GOTO$(I_0, C)$ Shift dot past $C$ in applicable items: $$[S \rightarrow C \cdot C, \; \$]$$ Apply CLOSURE: dot before $C$ with lookahead $\$$, expand using productions of $C$ with lookahead $\in \text{FIRST}(C \cdot \$) = \{c, d\}$ and also the original $\$$. $$I_2 = \{[S \rightarrow C \cdot C, \; \$], [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$], [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$]\}$$ #### 3.4.3 GOTO$(I_0, c)$ $$I_3 = \{[C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; \$], [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; c], [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c], [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; c]\}$$ #### 3.4.4 GOTO$(I_0, d)$ $$I_4 = \{[C \rightarrow d \cdot, \; \$], [C \rightarrow d \cdot, \; c]\}$$ ### 3.5 Continuing the Construction — All Remaining States For brevity and clarity, the remaining states are tabulated below; each follows the same closure-then-shift discipline. | State | GOTO Trigger | Resulting Item Set $I_k$ | |---|---|---| | $I_5$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, C)$ | $\{[S \rightarrow CC \cdot, \; \$]\}$ | | $I_6$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, c)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; \$], [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$], [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$]\}$ | | $I_7$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, d)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow d \cdot, \; \$]\}$ | | $I_8$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, C)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; \$], [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; c]\}$ | | $I_9$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, c)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; c], [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c], [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; c]\}$ | | $I_{10}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, d)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow d \cdot, \; c]\}$ | | $I_{11}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_6, C)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; \$]\}$ | | $I_{12}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_9, C)$ | $\{[C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; c]\}$ | > [!NOTE] > Notice the critical difference from LALR(1): states $I_6$ and $I_3$ — though having identical **kernels** (the same items ignoring lookaheads) — **remain distinct** in CLR(1) because their lookahead sets differ. LALR(1) would merge them, sacrificing precision. ### 3.6 Derived CLR(1) Parsing Table Terminals: $\{c, d, \$\}$.
