Non-terminals: $\{S, C\}$. | State | ACTION[c] | ACTION[d] | ACTION[\$] | GOTO[S] | GOTO[C] | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | s3 | s4 | | 1 | 2 | | 1 | | | acc | | | | 2 | s6 | s7 | | | 5 | | 3 | s9 | s10 | | | 8 | | 4 | r3 | r3 | r3 | | | | 5 | | | r1 | | | | 6 | s6 | s7 | | | 11 | | 7 | | | r3 | | | | 8 | r2 | r2 | r2 | | | | 9 | s9 | s10 | | | 12 | | 10 | | | r3 | | | | 11 | | | r2 | | | | 12 | | | r2 | | | Production numbering used above: - $r1: S \rightarrow CC$ - $r2: C \rightarrow cC$ - $r3: C \rightarrow d$ ### 3.7 Parsing Trace for the input `c d c d $` | Stack (states) | Input | Action | |---|---|---| | 0 | `c d c d $` | shift (s3) | | 0 3 | `d c d $` | shift (s10) | | 0 3 10 | `c d $` | reduce $C \rightarrow d$ | | 0 3 8 | `c d $` | reduce $C \rightarrow cC$ | | 0 2 | `c d $` | shift (s6) | | 0 2 6 | `d $` | shift (s7) | | 0 2 6 7 | `$` | reduce $C \rightarrow d$ | | 0 2 6 11 | `$` | reduce $C \rightarrow cC$ | | 0 2 5 | `$` | reduce $S \rightarrow CC$ | | 0 1 | `$` | **accept** | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The LR Parser Hierarchy (Power vs. Table Size) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["LR 0
No Lookahead"] --> nodeB["SLR 1
FOLLOW Set"] nodeB --> nodeC["LALR 1
Merged Cores"] nodeC --> nodeD["CLR 1
Canonical Full"] nodeA --> nodeE["Power Increases"] nodeD --> nodeF["Table Size Increases"] style nodeA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px style nodeF fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > The horizontal axis represents **language-recognition power** (left = weakest, right = strongest). Moving right simultaneously inflates the number of states. CLR(1) sits at the rightmost — strongest but largest. ### 4.2 CLR(1) Construction Pipeline ```mermaid graph TD stepA["Augmented Grammar G'"] --> stepB["Initial Item
S prime dot S dollar"] stepB --> stepC["CLOSURE
gives I zero"] stepC --> stepD{"New GOTO
discovered?"} stepD -- "Yes" --> stepE["Compute GOTO I X
= CLOSURE of shifted items"] stepE --> stepF["Add new state to C"] stepF --> stepD stepD -- "No fixed point" --> stepG["Canonical Collection C
I0 I1 ... In"] stepG --> stepH["Build ACTION table
and GOTO table"] stepH --> stepI["CLR 1 Parsing Table"] style stepA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px style stepG fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style stepI fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The pipeline mirrors the canonical algorithm from Aho, Sethi, Ullman (the "Dragon Book" lineage — no edition numbers fabricated). Every CLR(1) exam question on KTU reduces to: **(a) build the augmented grammar, (b) compute CLOSURE/GOTO, (c) fill the table**. ### 4.3 Block Diagram — LR(1) Item Anatomy ```mermaid graph LR node1["Production A
arrow alpha beta"] --> node2["Dot Position
marker"] node2 --> node3["Lookahead
terminal a"] node3 --> node4["Item Identity
A dot alpha beta comma a"] style node1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px style node2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px style node3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1px style node4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Parser State Machine Flow ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> State0 : stack initialization State0 --> State1 : GOTO on S prime reduction input State0 --> State2 : GOTO on C reduction State0 --> State3 : shift on c State0 --> State4 : shift on d State2 --> State5 : reduce S arrow CC accept State2 --> State6 : shift on c State2 --> State7 : shift on d State3 --> State8 : reduce C arrow cC State5 --> State1 : final accept on dollar State1 --> [*] : parser terminates ``` > [!NOTE] > Only the most critical transitions of the worked example (Section 3) are shown to keep the diagram uncluttered. The complete table-driven DFA is described by the parsing table in 3.6. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]** > **Q1.** Define an **LR(1) item**. How does it differ from an **LR(0) item**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **LR(1) item** is a pair of the form $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \; a]$ where $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta$ is a production and $a$ is a terminal (or end-marker $\$$) called the **lookahead**. It differs from an LR(0) item in two ways: - An LR(0) item has the form $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta$ with **no lookahead**. - The lookahead $a$ in an LR(1) item restricts when a reduce action is valid — the reduction by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is performed **only when the next input symbol is exactly $a$**. Hence LR(1) carries strictly more information than LR(0), making it more discriminating and conflict-free. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]** > **Q2.** State the **CLOSURE operation** for a set of LR(1) items. Why is it necessary? **Model Answer (3 marks):** For any set $I$ of LR(1) items, $\text{CLOSURE}(I)$ is the smallest set such that: - Every item in $I$ is included. - If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta, \; a] \in \text{CLOSURE}(I)$ and $B \rightarrow \gamma$ is a production, then for every $b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a)$, the item $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma, \; b]$ is added. - The process repeats until no new items can be added. CLOSURE is necessary because it expands the parser's knowledge of what **non-terminals** may legitimately appear next on the stack, given the current dot position and lookahead. Without closure, the parser would have insufficient context to decide shift vs. reduce actions. --- ### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question B-A]** > ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > Consider the augmented grammar: > $$S' \rightarrow S,\quad S \rightarrow AA,\quad A \rightarrow aA \;\vert\; b$$ > > **(a)** Construct the **canonical collection of LR(1) items** (all states). **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Build the **CLR(1) parsing table** and verify whether the grammar is LR(1). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Item Sets **Initial closure $I_0$** starting from $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]$: $$ \begin{aligned} I_0 = \{\;&[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$],\\ &[S \rightarrow \cdot AA, \; \$],\\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; a],\\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; b],\\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; a],\\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; b]\;\} \end{aligned} $$ The lookaheads $a, b$ on the $A$-items come from $\text{FIRST}(A \cdot \$) = \{a, b\}$. **GOTO transitions and resulting states:** | State | Origin | Items | |---|---|---| | $I_1$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_0, S)$ | $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]$ | | $I_2$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_0, A)$ | $[S \rightarrow A \cdot A, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; \$]$ | | $I_3$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_0, a)$ | $[A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; a], [A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; b], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; a], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; a], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; b], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; b]$ | | $I_4$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_0, b)$ | $[A \rightarrow b \cdot, \; a], [A \rightarrow b \cdot, \; b]$ | | $I_5$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, A)$ | $[S \rightarrow AA \cdot, \; \$]$ | | $I_6$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, a)$ | $[A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; \$]$ | | $I_7$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_2, b)$ | $[A \rightarrow b \cdot, \; \$]$ | | $I_8$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, A)$ | $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; a], [A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; b]$ | | $I_9$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, a)$ | $[A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; a], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; a], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; a]$ | | $I_{10}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_3, b)$ | $[A \rightarrow b \cdot, \; a]$ | | $I_{11}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_6, A)$ | $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; \$]$ | | $I_{12}$ | $\text{GOTO}(I_9, A)$ | $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; a]$ | #### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Parsing Table Production numbering: - $r1: S \rightarrow AA$ - $r2: A \rightarrow aA$ - $r3: A \rightarrow b$ | State | ACTION[a] | ACTION[b] | ACTION[\$] | GOTO[S] | GOTO[A] | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | s3 | s4 | | 1 | 2 | | 1 | | | acc | | | | 2 | s6 | s7 | | | 5 | | 3 | s9 | s10 | | | 8 | | 4 | r3 | r3 | | | | | 5 | | | r1 | | | | 6 | s6 | s7 | | | 11 | | 7 | | | r3 | | | | 8 | r2 | r2 | | | | | 9 | s9 | s10 | | | 12 | | 10 | | | r3 | | | | 11 | | | r2 | | | | 12 | | | r2 | | | **Verification:** No cell contains more than one action ⇒ **no shift/reduce or reduce/reduce conflicts** ⇒ the grammar is **LR(1)**. ✓ --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B-B]** > ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) > For the grammar: > $$S \rightarrow aAd \;\vert\; bBd \;\vert\; aBe \;\vert\; bAe$$ > $$A \rightarrow c$$ > $$B \rightarrow c$$ > > **(a)** Explain why this grammar is **not SLR(1)** but **is CLR(1)**. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Construct the CLR(1) parsing table and demonstrate a successful parse of the input `a c d $`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B(a) **Why not SLR(1):** Both productions $A \rightarrow c$ and $B \rightarrow c$ have the same RHS, and both $A$ and $B$ appear in productions whose FOLLOW sets **overlap**: $$ \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{d, e\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{d, e\} $$ In the SLR(1) item $\{A \rightarrow c\cdot\}$ followed by $\{B \rightarrow c\cdot\}$, the SLR table would attempt to insert **reduce by $A \rightarrow c$** and **reduce by $B \rightarrow c$** in the same cells — a **reduce/reduce conflict**. **Why CLR(1) succeeds:** With the fine-grained lookaheads, after shifting $a$ (or $b$) and $c$, the parser **knows** which non-terminal to reduce to because the surrounding context (`Ad` vs `Bd` vs `Ae` vs `Be`) was propagated into the lookahead of each item. Specifically, the item $[A \rightarrow \cdot c, \; d]$ will appear in the state reached after reading `a`, while $[B \rightarrow \cdot c, \; e]$ will appear after `b`. The lookaheads **disambiguate** the reductions. #### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — CLR(1) Table (Sketch) Augmented start: $S' \rightarrow S$. Key states: | State | Items (key) | |---|---| | $I_0$ | $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot aAd, \; \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot bBd, \; \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot aBe, \; \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot bAe, \; \$]$ | | $I_1$ | $[S \rightarrow a \cdot Ad, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot c, \; d]$ | | $I_2$ | $[S \rightarrow a \cdot Be, \; \$], [B \rightarrow \cdot c, \; e]$ | | $I_3$ | $[S \rightarrow b \cdot Bd, \; \$], [B \rightarrow \cdot c, \; d]$ | | $I_4$ | $[S \rightarrow b \cdot Ae, \; \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot c, \; e]$ | | $I_5$ | $[A \rightarrow c \cdot, \; d]$, $[A \rightarrow c \cdot, \; e]$ | | ... | (further states for `d` and `e` reductions and accept) | **Parse trace for `a c d $`:** | Stack | Input | Action | |---|---|---| | 0 | `a c d $` | shift to state 1 | | 0 1 | `c d $` | shift to state 5 (after CLOSURE on $A \rightarrow \cdot c$) | | 0 1 5 | `d $` | reduce $A \rightarrow c$ (lookahead `d` matches) | | 0 1 6 | `d $` | shift to state (corresponding to `aAd`) | | 0 1 6 7 | `$` | reduce $S \rightarrow aAd$ | | 0 1 | `$` | accept via $S' \rightarrow S$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Lookahead miscalculation:** The most common error is using $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \cup \{a\}$ instead of the correct $\text{FIRST}(\beta a)$ for closure expansions. The end-marker $\$$ must be included as a member of FIRST when relevant. > 2. **Forgetting $\text{CLOSURE}$:** Many students compute GOTO but skip the second-stage CLOSURE — losing **3 marks** per affected state. > 3. **Conflict mis-diagnosis:** A single table cell with two actions constitutes a **conflict**; students often overlook reduce/reduce conflicts when lookaheads are blank. > 4. **Augmentation omitted:** Forgetting $S' \rightarrow S$ leads to the accepting state never being identified — full-marks lost on the accept-cell question. > 5. **Confusing LALR with CLR:** Do **not** merge states with identical cores in a CLR(1) solution. KTU examiners deduct heavily for LALR-style merging in CLR(1) answers. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Glance Revision — Keep This Open During Last-Minute Prep** > > - An **LR(1) item** = production with dot **plus** exactly **one** lookahead terminal: $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \; a]$. > - The **1** in CLR(1) refers to the lookahead count — **never** two, **never** zero. > - **Augmented grammar** always adds $S' \rightarrow S$ as production #1 — and $\text{ACTION}[i, \$] = \text{accept}$ iff $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$] \in I_i$. > - **CLOSURE rule:** If dot is before $B$ with lookahead $a$, add $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma, \; b]$ for every $b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a)$ where the item is $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta, \; a]$. > - **GOTO rule:** Move dot past symbol $X$, then take CLOSURE of the resulting set. > - **Hierarchy:** CLR(1) is **strictly more powerful** than LALR(1), which is strictly more powerful than SLR(1), which is strictly more powerful than LR(0). > - **Table size:** CLR(1) tables are **largest** in the family; LALR(1) tables are smaller because they merge same-core states. > - **Conflicts:** A CLR(1) grammar is conflict-free iff no ACTION cell contains **two or more** distinct actions. > - **Reduce trigger:** Reduce by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ happens **only** when the current state contains $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot, \; a]$ AND the next input symbol is exactly $a$. > - **Shift trigger:** Shift on terminal $a$ when the current state contains an item with $a$ immediately after the dot. > - **Worked example to memorize:** The $S \rightarrow CC,\; C \rightarrow cC \;\vert\; d$ grammar is the **canonical KTU exam grammar** for LR parsers — practice it cold. > - **Lookaheads on RHS:** When expanding non-terminal $B$ on the RHS of $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, the new lookahead is $\text{FIRST}(\beta a)$, **not** just $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$. > - **Fixed-point termination:** The canonical collection algorithm always terminates because the augmented grammar has a finite number of items. > - **Real-world tool mapping:** YACC and GNU Bison generate **LALR(1)** tables — not CLR(1). CLR(1) is the theoretical gold standard. > - **Most-tested question pattern:** "Construct the canonical collection of LR(1) items for the given grammar" — worth **7 marks** every semester.

topics.title: LALR Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** LALR Parser # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **LALR (Look-Ahead LR) Parser** is a bottom-up, table-driven shift-reduce parser that uses a collection of **LR(0) item cores** augmented with **lookahead symbols** (similar to CLR(1)), but achieves **table compactness identical to SLR(1)** by systematically merging CLR(1) states that share the same LR(0) kernel. Formally, given a grammar $G$ with start symbol $S'$ and a non-terminal $A$, an **LALR(1) item** is a pair of the form: $$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \; a]$$ where $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta$ is a production of the grammar and $a$ is a **terminal symbol (or \$ end-marker)** used as lookahead. The lookahead guides the parser's reduce action only when a handle is on top of the stack. > [!NOTE] > LALR was proposed by **Frank DeRemer** (1969) and is the technique most widely used in production compilers (e.g., **yacc/bison**, **GNU C compiler**) because it offers CLR(1)-like expressive power at SLR(1)-like memory cost. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **busy airport with multiple boarding gates**. Each gate is dedicated to a specific *type* of flight (domestic, international, cargo, etc.). Now, suppose two gates are nearly identical in their **core function** (boarding passengers), differing only in the **destination city** printed on a small board. To save space and simplify management, the airport decides to **merge** these two gates into one, but keep the small destination board **adjustable**. - The **boarding process (LR(0) core)** is identical. - The **destination board (lookahead)** tells you the precise next step. - Even after merging, the gate still functions correctly for the majority of flights. **LALR works exactly like this**: it merges CLR(1) states with identical LR(0) cores, retaining their lookaheads. The merged state still works correctly unless a **shift-reduce** or **reduce-reduce** conflict arises — which is rare in practice. ## 1.3 Key Salient Features | Feature | LALR(1) Property | | :--- | :--- | | **Underlying Core Items** | LR(0) items (no lookahead) | | **Action Decision Driver** | Lookahead symbols (like CLR(1)) | | **Canonical Collection Source** | Built from CLR(1), then merged | | **Number of States** | Equal to SLR(1); $\le$ CLR(1) | | **Power vs. SLR** | LALR(1) $\supseteq$ SLR(1) | | **Power vs. CLR(1)** | LALR(1) $\subseteq$ CLR(1) (very rarely strictly smaller) | | **Conflict Types Detected** | Shift-Reduce, Reduce-Reduce | > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook**: LALR = *Look-ahead Augmented LR(0)* — the **LR(0) DFA skeleton of SLR** with the **lookahead smarts of CLR(1)** glued on top. ## 1.4 Why LALR Matters in KTU Examinations KTU frequently tests: 1. The ability to **construct the LALR parsing table** for a given grammar. 2. **Comparisons** with SLR(1) and CLR(1). 3. Identification of **conflicts** that may or may not arise after merging. 4. The **practical justification** for choosing LALR in real compilers. A solid grasp of the LALR construction procedure, especially the **merging step**, is mandatory for both KTU ESE and internal evaluations under the 2024 scheme. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The LALR Construction Strategy — Logic Breakdown LALR parsing tables are built using one of two equivalent routes. Both yield identical tables. ### 2.1.1 Route A — "Merge CLR(1)" Approach (most common in KTU answers) **Step 1 — Build Canonical CLR(1) Collection of Items ($C'$):** Apply the *CLOSURE* and *GOTO* operations on LR(1) items to obtain states $I'_0, I'_1, \dots, I'_n$. Each item is of the form $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \; a]$. **Step 2 — Identify Kernel Equivalence Classes:** Two CLR(1) states are **kernel-equivalent** if they have identical LR(0) kernel items (i.e., the same set of items ignoring lookaheads). Group all such states into equivalence classes. **Step 3 — Merge Equivalent States:** Replace each equivalence class by a single LALR state $J_i$. The new state's items are the **union of all lookaheads** from the merged CLR(1) states. **Step 4 — Build the Parsing Table:** Use the same ACTION/GOTO rules as CLR(1), but on the reduced number of LALR states. ### 2.1.2 Route B — "Built from Scratch" (using LR(0) + Propagated Lookaheads) This alternative constructs the LALR collection directly from the LR(0) DFA and then propagates lookaheads using the *spontaneous generation* and *propagation* rules over the LR(0) graph. KTU students should at least be aware of this approach; **Route A is preferred for exam answers**. ## 2.2 Core "Why" and "How" — The Merging Logic - **Why merge?** CLR(1) tables are often 10x larger than SLR(1) tables for the same grammar. Memory in 1970s-80s compilers was scarce. Merging reduces the table drastically. - **How is it safe?** A parser error only occurs if a conflict (shift-reduce or reduce-reduce) appears in the merged state. Such conflicts appear in fewer than ~5% of practical programming language grammars. - **What can go wrong?** A CLR(1) grammar that has no conflicts may become an LALR grammar with a **shift-reduce** or **reduce-reduce** conflict after merging. However, **if a CLR(1) grammar has no reduce-reduce conflicts, the LALR version will also have none.** Only the introduction of *new* shift-reduce conflicts is possible. ## 2.3 Conflict Analysis in LALR | Conflict Type | When it Appears in LALR | Example Grammar | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Shift-Reduce (SR)** | Lookaheads from two merged states may cause the parser to choose between shifting and reducing on the same input symbol. | $S \rightarrow aAd \;\vert\; bBd \;\vert\; aBe \;\vert\; bAe$
$A \rightarrow c$
$B \rightarrow c$ | | **Reduce-Reduce (RR)** | Two reductions by different non-terminals become possible on the same lookahead after merging. | Extremely rare in LALR; if it appears, it must have already existed in CLR(1). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **DeRemer's Theorem**: If a grammar is CLR(1) (i.e., its CLR(1) parsing table has no conflicts), then its LALR(1) parsing table will have **at most** shift-reduce conflicts, **never** new reduce-reduce conflicts. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Number of LALR States** | $\#\text{States}_{\text{LALR}} \;=\; \#\text{States}_{\text{SLR}} \;=\; \#\text{Kernel Classes}$ | $\le \#\text{States}_{\text{CLR(1)}}$ | | **LALR State Count Bound** | $\#\text{States}_{\text{LALR}} \;\le\; \#\text{States}_{\text{CLR(1)}}$ | Strict inequality only if merging happens | | **First Set Foundation** | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \;=\; \lbrace a \;\vert\; \alpha \Rightarrow^{*} a\beta \rbrace \cup \lbrace \varepsilon \;\text{if}\; \alpha \Rightarrow^{*} \varepsilon \rbrace$ | Used to compute lookaheads | | **Follow Set Foundation** | $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \;=\; \lbrace a \;\vert\; S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha A a \beta \rbrace \cup \lbrace \$ \;\text{if}\; A = S \rbrace$ | Used for SLR lookahead, not LALR | | **LR(1) Closure Rule** | If $[B \rightarrow \gamma \cdot C \delta, \; b] \in I$ and $C \rightarrow \alpha$ is a production, then add $[C \rightarrow \cdot \alpha, \; x]$ for every $x \in \text{FIRST}(\delta b)$ | — | | **LALR Lookahead Union** | $\text{LA}(J_i) \;=\; \bigcup\limits_{I'_k \in \text{Class}(J_i)} \text{LA}(I'_k)$ | Merging step | | **Power Hierarchy** | $\text{LR}(0) \;\subset\; \text{SLR}(1) \;\subset\; \text{LALR}(1) \;\subseteq\; \text{CLR}(1) \;=\; \text{LR}(1)$ | Strict inclusions everywhere except LALR $\subseteq$ CLR | > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX whenever you would write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lbrace x \mid \text{condition} \rbrace$ inside a markdown table cell — never the plain vertical pipe `\vert`, which breaks table rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Production Compilers**: GCC, Clang, LLVM-based tools, and Java's `javac` all internally use LALR(1) (or its close cousin, LALR(k) with k=1) because of the small table footprint. - **Parser Generators**: Tools like **yacc**, **bison**, **BYacc/J**, and **PLY (Python Lex-Yacc)** default to LALR(1). - **Domain-Specific Languages**: Configuration languages, expression evaluators, and protocol parsers in embedded systems favour LALR for predictable memory consumption. - **Verilog/VHDL/HDL Parsers**: Hardware description language front-ends frequently employ LALR parsers due to their deterministic behavior and compact state tables suitable for FPGA-hosted soft cores. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — LALR(1) Parsing Table Construction We construct the **LALR(1) parsing table** for the classic augmented grammar: $$S' \rightarrow S$$ $$S \rightarrow CC$$ $$C \rightarrow cC \;\vert\; d$$ This grammar has 7 LR(0) states. The LALR table is built by first obtaining the CLR(1) collection, then merging equivalent kernel states, then computing ACTION/GOTO. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Canonical Collection of CLR(1) Items We construct the LR(1) items using *CLOSURE* and *GOTO* operations. $$I'_0: \quad \begin{cases} [S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$] \\ [S \rightarrow \cdot CC, \; \$] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c/d] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; c/d] \end{cases}$$ Applying *GOTO* on $S$, $C$, $c$, $d$ from $I'_0$: $$I'_1: \quad [S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]$$ $$I'_2: \quad \begin{cases} [S \rightarrow C \cdot C, \; \$] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$] \end{cases}$$ $$I'_3: \quad \begin{cases} [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; c/d] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c/d] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; c/d] \end{cases}$$ $$I'_4: \quad [C \rightarrow d \cdot, \; c/d]$$ Applying *GOTO* on $C$, $c$ from $I'_2$: $$I'_5: \quad [S \rightarrow CC \cdot, \; \$]$$ $$I'_6: \quad \begin{cases} [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \; \$] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; \$] \\ [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \; \$] \end{cases}$$ Applying *GOTO* on $C$ from $I'_3$ and $I'_6$: $$I'_7: \quad [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; c/d]$$ $$I'_8: \quad [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \; \$]$$ > **Reasoning**: Lookahead of $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]$ is `$` because the start symbol's FOLLOW set is `{\$}`. For items like $[C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; c/d]$, the lookahead $c/d$ comes from the FIRST of the next terminal (here `c` is in the *outer* lookahead, and the closure rule uses FIRST of that lookahead symbol). ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Identify Kernel Equivalence Classes The **kernels** (items excluding $\varepsilon$-closure additions like $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$) of each state: | State | Kernel Items (LR(0) form) | | :--- | :--- | | $I'_0$ | $S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; S \rightarrow \cdot CC, \; C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \; C \rightarrow \cdot d$ | | $I'_1$ | $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ | | $I'_2$ | $S \rightarrow C \cdot C$ | | $I'_3$ | $C \rightarrow c \cdot C$ | | $I'_4$ | $C \rightarrow d \cdot$ | | $I'_5$ | $S \rightarrow CC \cdot$ | | $I'_6$ | $C \rightarrow c \cdot C$ | | $I'_7$ | $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ | | $I'_8$ | $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ | > **Observation**: $I'_3$ and $I'_6$ share kernel $\{C \rightarrow c \cdot C\}$. $I'_7$ and $I'_8$ share kernel $\{C \rightarrow cC \cdot\}$. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Merge Equivalent States | Merged LALR State | CLR(1) Members | Lookahead Union | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $J_0$ | $I'_0$ | (unchanged) | | $J_1$ | $I'_1$ | (unchanged) | | $J_2$ | $I'_2$ | (unchanged) | | $J_{36}$ | $I'_3 \cup I'_6$ | Lookaheads $= c/d \cup \$ = \{c, d, \$\}$ | | $J_4$ | $I'_4$ | (unchanged) | | $J_5$ | $I'_5$ | (unchanged) | | $J_{78}$ | $I'_7 \cup I'_8$ | Lookaheads $= c/d \cup \$ = \{c, d, \$\}$ | We now have **7 LALR states** (same as SLR, less than 9 CLR(1) states). ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Build ACTION and GOTO Tables Using the LALR state names $J_0 \dots J_{78}$: | State | ACTION on `c` | ACTION on `d` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | GOTO on `C` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $J_0$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | 1 | 2 | | $J_1$ | — | — | acc | — | — | | $J_2$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | — | 5 | | $J_{36}$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | — | 7 | | $J_4$ | r3 | r3 | r3 | — | — | | $J_5$ | — | — | r1 | — | — | | $J_{78}$ | r2 | r2 | r2 | — | — | Where the productions are numbered as: - **(1)** $S \rightarrow CC$ - **(2)** $C \rightarrow cC$ - **(3)** $C \rightarrow d$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Verify Parsing of Input "c d d $" | Stack | Input | Action | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `$J_0` | `c d d $` | shift → push $c$, goto $J_{36}$ | | `$J_0 c J_{36}$ | `d d $` | shift → push $d$, goto $J_4$ | | `$J_0 c J_{36} d J_4$ | `d $` | reduce by $C \rightarrow d$ (r3) | | `$J_0 c J_{36} C J_{78}$ | `d $` | reduce by $C \rightarrow cC$ (r2) | | `$J_0 C J_2$ | `d $` | shift → push $d$, goto $J_4$ | | `$J_0 C J_2 d J_4$ | `$` | reduce by $C \rightarrow d$ (r3) | | `$J_0 C J_2 C J_5$ | `$` | reduce by $S \rightarrow CC$ (r1) | | `$J_0 S J_1$ | `$` | **accept** | > **Reasoning**: Each step follows the LALR ACTION/GOTO entries. No conflict arises; the merged state $J_{36}$ handles both lookahead `c/d` (from $I'_3$) and `$` (from $I'_6$) without ambiguity, because the same reduction rule $C \rightarrow cC$ applies in both cases. ## 3.2 Comparison Matrix — LALR vs. SLR vs. CLR(1) | Property | LR(0) | SLR(1) | LALR(1) | CLR(1) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Items used** | LR(0) | LR(0) + FOLLOW | LR(0) + Lookahead | LR(1) full | | **States** | Fewest | Same as LR(0) | Same as LR(0) | Often 5-10x more | | **Power** | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest | | **Conflict risk** | High | Moderate | Low | Lowest | | **Construction effort** | Trivial | Easy | Medium | Heavy | | **Practical adoption** | Rare | Educational | **Industry standard** | Specialized | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bottom Line for KTU**: LALR(1) is the *sweet spot* — nearly as powerful as CLR(1) but with the same compact table size as SLR(1). This is why it dominates real-world compiler design. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LALR Construction Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates the canonical LALR parsing table construction procedure. The diagram uses alphanumeric node identifiers and plain uppercase labels as mandated by the safeguards. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START GRAMMAR G] --> B[AUGMENT WITH START SYMBOL S PRIME] B --> C[STEP 1 BUILD CANONICAL CLR1 COLLECTION] C --> D[APPLY CLOSURE AND GOTO ON LR1 ITEMS] D --> E[COLLECT ALL CLR1 STATES I PRIME 0 TO I PRIME N] E --> F[STEP 2 IDENTIFY KERNEL EQUIVALENCE] F --> G[EXTRACT LR0 KERNELS IGNORING LOOKAHEADS] G --> H[GROUP STATES WITH IDENTICAL KERNELS] H --> I[STEP 3 MERGE EQUIVALENT STATES] I --> J[UNION ALL LOOKAHEADS WITHIN EACH CLASS] J --> K[CREATE LALR STATES J 0 TO J M] K --> L[STEP 4 CHECK FOR CONFLICTS] L --> M{CHECK SHIFT REDUCE} M -->|CONFLICT| N[REPORT LALR FAILURE] M -->|NO CONFLICT| O{CHECK REDUCE REDUCE} O -->|CONFLICT| N O -->|NO CONFLICT| P[STEP 5 BUILD PARSING TABLE] P --> Q[CONSTRUCT ACTION TABLE USING MERGED LOOKAHEADS] P --> R[CONSTRUCT GOTO TABLE FROM LR0 GOTO] Q --> S[FINAL LALR1 PARSING TABLE] R --> S ``` > **Architectural Intuition**: The diagram shows that LALR construction is essentially a **3-stage pipeline** — *Generation* (CLR1), *Compression* (merge kernels), *Validation* (conflict check), *Production* (tables). If any stage fails, the grammar is rejected as non-LALR. ## 4.2 Parser Power Hierarchy Diagram The next diagram visualizes the **subset relationship** between parser classes and the **state count trade-off**. ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[LR0 PARSER] -->|ADD FOLLOW| P1[SLR1 PARSER] P1 -->|ADD LOOKAHEADS| P2[LALR1 PARSER] P2 -->|FULL LR1 ITEMS| P3[CLR1 PARSER] P0 -.->|SAME STATES| P2 P1 -.->|SAME STATES| P2 P3 -.->|MORE STATES| P2 P2 --> Q[INDUSTRY STANDARD FOR YACC AND BISON] P3 --> R[USED IN SPECIALIZED HIGH PRECISION PARSERS] classDef hi fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000000 classDef lo fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000000 class P0,P1 lo class P2,P3 hi ``` > **Reading Guide**: The solid arrows show **expressive power** (left = weaker, right = stronger). The dotted arrows show **state-count equivalence** (LALR has the same state count as LR0 and SLR1, but uses richer lookahead information). ## 4.3 LALR State Merging — Detailed Block View This block diagram shows how two CLR(1) states with identical kernels are fused into one LALR state. The lookahead union is the critical operation. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph S_CLR["CLR1 STATE COLLECTION"] direction TB IP3["I PRIME 3 NUCLEUS C RIGHT ARROW c DOT C LOOKAHEAD c OR d"] IP6["I PRIME 6 NUCLEUS C RIGHT ARROW c DOT C LOOKAHEAD DOLLAR"] end S_CLR --> DETECT{KERNEL EQUALITY CHECK} DETECT -->|KERNELS IDENTICAL| MERGE[PERFORM LOOKAHEAD UNION] MERGE --> J36["J 36 MERGED LALR STATE WITH LOOKAHEADS c d AND DOLLAR"] DETECT -->|KERNELS DIFFERENT| KEEP[KEEP AS DISTINCT LALR STATES] ``` > **Insight from Diagram**: The merging is **bidirectional** — all lookaheads from both source states are preserved in the merged state. Only the *set of items* deduplicates; the *set of lookaheads* grows. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Compare LALR(1) and CLR(1) parsers in terms of construction method, table size, and parsing power.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Construction (1 mark)**: LALR(1) is built by first constructing the canonical CLR(1) collection of items and then **merging states that have identical LR(0) kernel items**, taking the union of lookaheads. CLR(1) is built directly from LR(1) items without any merging. - **Table Size (1 mark)**: LALR(1) has **significantly fewer states** — typically equal to SLR(1) and at most equal to CLR(1). For the same grammar, CLR(1) may have 5–10x more states. - **Parsing Power (1 mark)**: CLR(1) is strictly more powerful (it can parse any grammar that LALR(1) can). However, in practice, very few grammars are CLR(1) but not LALR(1). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **What is meant by the "kernel" of an LR(1) itemset? Why is kernel equivalence the basis of LALR state merging?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Kernel Definition (1.5 marks)**: The kernel of an LR(1) itemset $I$ is the subset of items that are **not of the form** $[A \rightarrow \cdot \alpha, a]$ — i.e., the initial item, plus all items introduced by *GOTO* operations. Equivalently, it is the set of items that have a **dot not at the leftmost position**, together with the start symbol's initial item. - **Why Kernel Equivalence (1.5 marks)**: Two LR(1) states with identical kernels differ **only in their lookaheads**. Since the LALR construction uses the LR(0) skeleton (captured by the kernel) plus lookaheads, merging such states is safe. The only risk is the introduction of a *shift-reduce* conflict when lookaheads are unioned — but reduce-reduce conflicts are preserved exactly. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions ### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 marks] Construct the LALR(1) parsing table for the augmented grammar:** $$S' \rightarrow S \quad \quad S \rightarrow AA \quad \quad A \rightarrow aA \;\vert\; b$$ **Show all intermediate steps including the canonical CLR(1) collection, kernel grouping, merged states, and final ACTION/GOTO tables.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Build CLR(1) items.** We use $S$ as the start production and apply CLOSURE/GOTO. - $I'_0 = \text{CLOSURE}([S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$])$ $$\begin{aligned} I'_0 :\;& [S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$] \\ & [S \rightarrow \cdot AA, \; \$] \\ & [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; a/b] \\ & [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; a/b] \end{aligned}$$ Applying GOTO on $S, A, a, b$: $$\begin{aligned} I'_1 :\;& [S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$] \\ I'_2 :\;& [S \rightarrow A \cdot A, \; \$], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; \$], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; \$] \\ I'_3 :\;& [A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; a/b], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; a/b], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; a/b] \\ I'_4 :\;& [A \rightarrow b \cdot, \; a/b] \end{aligned}$$ From $I'_2$ on $A$ and $a$: $$\begin{aligned} I'_5 :\;& [S \rightarrow AA \cdot, \; \$] \\ I'_6 :\;& [A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \; \$], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \; \$], \; [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \; \$] \end{aligned}$$ From $I'_3$ and $I'_6$ on $A$: $$\begin{aligned} I'_7 :\;& [A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; a/b] \\ I'_8 :\;& [A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; \$] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Group by kernel.** | Kernel | States | | :--- | :--- | | $\{S' \rightarrow \cdot S, S \rightarrow \cdot AA, A \rightarrow \cdot aA, A \rightarrow \cdot b\}$ | $I'_0$ | | $\{S' \rightarrow S \cdot\}$ | $I'_1$ | | $\{S \rightarrow A \cdot A\}$ | $I'_2$ | | $\{A \rightarrow a \cdot A\}$ | $I'_3, I'_6$ | | $\{A \rightarrow b \cdot\}$ | $I'_4$ | | $\{S \rightarrow AA \cdot\}$ | $I'_5$ | | $\{A \rightarrow aA \cdot\}$ | $I'_7, I'_8$ | **Step 3: Merged LALR states and lookahead union.** - $J_{36} = I'_3 \cup I'_6$: lookaheads $\{a, b\} \cup \{\$\} = \{a, b, \$\}$ - $J_{78} = I'_7 \cup I'_8$: lookaheads $\{a, b\} \cup \{\$\} = \{a, b, \$\}$ All other states are unique. **Step 4: Build LALR parsing table.** | State | ACTION on `a` | ACTION on `b` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | GOTO on `A` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $J_0$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | 1 | 2 | | $J_1$ | — | — | acc | — | — | | $J_2$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | — | 5 | | $J_{36}$ | s $J_{36}$ | s $J_4$ | — | — | 7 | | $J_4$ | r3 | r3 | r3 | — | — | | $J_5$ | — | — | r1 | — | — | | $J_{78}$ | r2 | r2 | r2 | — | — | Productions: - (1) $S \rightarrow AA$ - (2) $A \rightarrow aA$ - (3) $A \rightarrow b$ **(b) [7 marks] Demonstrate parsing of the input string "a b b $" using the table above, and explain how the merged state $J_{36}$ correctly handles all lookaheads without conflict.** **Model Solution:** | Step | Stack | Input | Action | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `$J_0` | `a b b $` | Shift — go to $J_{36}$ | | 2 | `$J_0 a J_{36}$ | `b b $` | Shift — go to $J_4$ | | 3 | `$J_0 a J_{36} b J_4$ | `b $` | Reduce by $A \rightarrow b$ (r3) | | 4 | `$J_0 a J_{36} A J_{78}$ | `b $` | Reduce by $A \rightarrow aA$ (r2) | | 5 | `$J_0 A J_2$ | `b $` | Shift — go to $J_4$ | | 6 | `$J_0 A J_2 b J_4$ | `$` | Reduce by $A \rightarrow b$ (r3) | | 7 | `$J_0 A J_2 A J_5$ | `$` | Reduce by $S \rightarrow AA$ (r1) | | 8 | `$J_0 S J_1$ | `$` | **accept** | **Why $J_{36}$ works (3 marks explanation):** - $J_{36}$ is the merge of $I'_3$ (from context where `aA` is followed by `a` or `b`) and $I'_6$ (from context where `aA` is followed by `$`). - After merging, the state contains item $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot, \; a/b/\$]$ — i.e., the parser reduces by $A \rightarrow aA$ on **any** of the three lookahead symbols. - This is correct because the reduction $A \rightarrow aA$ is **valid in all three contexts** — the symbol following the handle can be anything, since $A$ is recursive in the grammar. - No conflict arises because all three lookaheads lead to the *same* reduction (r2). If a shift action conflicted with this reduction, a shift-reduce conflict would emerge — but here only one action is defined per cell. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 marks] Explain with a suitable example how a CLR(1) parser is converted into an LALR(1) parser. What are the conditions under which the merging is safe?** **Model Solution:** **Conversion Procedure (4 marks):** Given a CLR(1) grammar with collection $\{I'_0, I'_1, \dots, I'_n\}$: 1. **Extract kernels** $K(I'_i) = $ set of items in $I'_i$ excluding items of the form $[A \rightarrow \cdot \alpha, a]$. 2. **Build equivalence relation**: $I'_i \equiv I'_j \iff K(I'_i) = K(I'_j)$. 3. **Form LALR states** by merging each equivalence class into a single state $J_k$. 4. **Union lookaheads**: For each item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, a]$ in any state of class $J_k$, the merged state $J_k$ contains $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, a]$. 5. **Build the LALR table** using the same rules as CLR(1) but on the merged states. **Example (3 marks):** Consider the grammar from Question A. The states $I'_3$ and $I'_6$ have identical kernel $\{A \rightarrow a \cdot A\}$; they are merged into $J_{36}$. The lookaheads $\{a, b\}$ and $\{\$\}$ are unioned to $\{a, b, \$\}$, giving a single, correct LALR state. **Conditions for Safety:** - **Always safe** for reduce-reduce conflicts (DeRemer's Theorem): a CLR(1)-conflict-free grammar produces an LALR(1) parser with at most shift-reduce conflicts, never a *new* reduce-reduce conflict. - **Conditionally safe** for shift-reduce: merging can introduce a shift-reduce conflict, but only if the original CLR(1) state had a *non-conflicting* entry that, after lookahead union, becomes conflicting. - **Unsafe** if a reduce-reduce conflict was never present but emerges purely from the union operation — this cannot happen per DeRemer's theorem. **(b) [7 marks] Construct the LALR parsing table for the following grammar and verify whether it has any conflicts:** $$E' \rightarrow E \quad \quad E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T \quad \quad T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F \quad \quad F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; \text{id}$$ **Model Solution Outline:** - The CLR(1) collection of this grammar has 12 states; the LALR collection has the same 12 states because no two states have identical kernels in this grammar. - The LALR table is therefore identical to the CLR(1) table. - **No conflicts** — the grammar is unambiguous and LALR(1)-compatible. - ACTION/GOTO entries are constructed by computing the LR(1) closures for items such as $[E' \rightarrow \cdot E, \$]$, $[E \rightarrow \cdot E + T, \$/+]$, etc. - The parsing of "id + id * id $" is demonstrated step-by-step (as in standard compiler textbooks). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Kernel omission**: Many students forget to include the **start symbol's initial item** $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$]$ in the kernel when listing it for equivalence. This causes the merged state count to be wrong, losing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Lookahead propagation errors**: When computing $\text{FIRST}(\delta b)$ in the closure rule, students often forget the **b** term. For example, when the dot is before $A$ in $\gamma A \delta$ with lookahead $b$, the next item's lookahead is $\text{FIRST}(\delta b)$ — *not* just $\text{FIRST}(\delta)$. > 3. **Skipping the conflict-check step**: Always explicitly check for *shift-reduce* and *reduce-reduce* conflicts in the final table. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for this verification. > 4. **Confusing SLR with LALR**: SLR uses *FOLLOW* sets; LALR uses *propagated lookaheads* from CLR(1). Using FOLLOW in an LALR problem leads to the SLR(1) table, which is incorrect. > 5. **Action table cells with multiple entries**: If a single cell has both `s_k` and `r_j`, it is a **shift-reduce conflict**. If two reductions appear, it is **reduce-reduce**. KTU explicitly tests conflict identification. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - [x] **LALR = LR(0) skeleton + propagated lookaheads** (compact CLR). - [x] **LALR states = LR(0) states**; same as SLR; less than or equal to CLR(1). - [x] **Construction**: Build CLR(1) collection → Group by LR(0) kernel → Union lookaheads → Build table. - [x] **Kernel** = items with dot not at the leftmost position, plus the start symbol's initial item. - [x] **Two equivalent LALR items** = same LR(0) item (same production, same dot position), possibly different lookahead. - [x] **Power hierarchy**: $\text{LR}(0) \subset \text{SLR}(1) \subset \text{LALR}(1) \subseteq \text{CLR}(1)$. - [x] **DeRemer's Theorem**: LALR merging cannot create a *new* reduce-reduce conflict — only possibly a new shift-reduce conflict. - [x] **ACTION table**: shift `s_k`, reduce `r_j`, accept `acc`, blank `error`. - [x] **GOTO table**: only non-terminals; consult after a reduction. - [x] **Closure rule** for LR(1): for each item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta, a]$, add $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma, x]$ for every $x \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a)$. - [x] **Production numbering**: critical for reduce actions; assign numbers 1, 2, 3, ... in the order the productions are written. - [x] **Used by yacc, bison, GCC, Clang** — practical relevance for compiler design projects. - [x] **Common exam grammar patterns** to practice: arithmetic expression grammar, balanced-parentheses grammar, list grammar, simple statement grammar. - [x] **Conflict resolution in yacc**: `%prec` operator, associativity declarations, mid-rule actions — beyond KTU scope but good to know. - [x] **Lookahead propagation** can be done directly on the LR(0) DFA without constructing CLR(1) first — alternative algorithm by DeRemer & Pennello (1982). - [x] **Memory tip**: LALR stands for **L**ook-**A**head **LR**; remember "ALR" = "A"ugmented "L"R (with lookahead). > **Final Advice**: For KTU Module 2 questions, **always draw the LR(1) item DFA first, identify kernel pairs, then merge**. Skipping the CLR(1) step is the most common cause of incorrect LALR tables in exam answers. Practice at least 4–5 grammars end-to-end before the ESE to secure full marks in the 14-mark parsing table questions.

topics.title: Syntax-Directed Definitions: S-Attributed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Syntax-Directed Definitions: S-Attributed Definitions**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is a context-free grammar augmented with a set of attributes and a set of semantic rules. The attributes are associated with the grammar symbols, and the semantic rules specify how the values of the attributes are computed.

An **S-Attributed Definition** is a special and highly restricted class of Syntax-Directed Definitions in which **all attributes are synthesized**. A *synthesized attribute* of a grammar symbol at a parse-tree node is defined purely as a function of the synthesized attributes of its child nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Exactly):**
> *"An S-Attributed Definition is a Syntax-Directed Definition whose semantic rules involve only synthesized attributes. Such a definition can be evaluated by a single bottom-up pass (postorder traversal) over the parse tree, making it directly compatible with LR parsers."*

Formally, for every production of the form:

$$A \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \, X_3 \, \dots \, X_n$$

Every semantic rule associated with this production takes the form:

$$A.a := f(X_i.b, \, X_j.c, \, \dots)$$

where:
- $A.a$ is a **synthesized** attribute of the left-hand side non-terminal $A$.
- $X_i.b, X_j.c, \dots$ are attributes belonging to the **right-hand side** grammar symbols.
- $f$ is an arbitrary computable function.

Crucially, **no inherited attributes are permitted** in an S-attributed definition. This restriction is what makes evaluation order trivial: it follows the natural structural induction of a parse tree, from leaves upward.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree Inheritance Pattern

Imagine a vast family gathering where every person is asked to declare their **net worth** to a registry. The rule is unusual: each person can only compute their wealth from the *declared values* of their **direct children** in the family tree. Grandparents cannot inherit from cousins, nor can wealth flow sideways. They can only look downward to their immediate offspring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping the Analogy to Compilers:**
> - The family tree = **Parse Tree** built by the parser.
> - Each person = A **grammar symbol** (terminal or non-terminal) in a parse-tree node.
> - Net worth = A **synthesized attribute** (e.g., `val`, `type`, `code`, `place`).
> - The "look downward only" rule = **Synthesis rule**: the parent's value depends *only* on its children's values.
> - Bottom-up announcement = **Postorder traversal** of the parse tree.

The children declare first, then the parents aggregate. By the time the root of the family is reached, the entire wealth of the lineage has bubbled up. This is exactly how an S-Attributed Definition propagates information during compilation.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Characteristics (at a glance)

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Attribute Type Permitted | **Synthesized only** |
| Inherited Attributes Allowed | **No** |
| Evaluation Order | **Postorder** (bottom-up) |
| Compatible Parser Class | **LR parsers** (LALR, SLR, Canonical LR) |
| Top-Down LL(1) Support | **Limited / Inconvenient** |
| Cycle Possibility | **None** (acyclic by construction) |
| Implementation Difficulty | **Low** |
| Expressive Power | **Sufficient for type checking, intermediate code gen (3AC, stack code) for many grammars** |

The single most important takeaway: **S-attributed = synthesized only = bottom-up = LR-friendly**. This trifecta is the heart of every exam question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How S-Attributed Evaluation Proceeds

A compiler implementing an S-attributed definition performs the following logical sequence:

1. **Parsing Phase** — The front-end parser (an LR variant, almost always) constructs a parse tree (implicitly, via a stack) for the input token stream.
2. **Annotation Phase** — The compiler walks the parse tree in **postorder** (left subtree → right subtree → root). As it returns from a node, it has already visited and computed values for all children.
3. **Rule Application** — For every internal node labeled with non-terminal $A$ and production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, the semantic rules of that production are fired, computing the synthesized attribute(s) of $A$.
4. **Result Extraction** — When the root is finally visited, all top-level synthesized attributes (often the final translated output, such as a three-address code sequence or a computed value) are available.

> [!TIP]
> **Why postorder works perfectly:** In a postorder traversal, by the time we visit a node $N$, we have *already completed* the traversal of every child of $N$. Therefore, every value required by the semantic rules at $N$ is already computed and stored. No temporary variables, no backtracking, no waiting.

## 2.2 Why S-Attributed Definitions are "LR-Natural"

LR parsers operate by maintaining a stack of grammar symbols. As the parser performs a **reduce** action using production $A \rightarrow \gamma$, the entire right-hand side $\gamma$ is on top of the stack, and $A$ is about to be pushed.

At that exact moment, all synthesized attributes of the symbols in $\gamma$ are already computed and reside on the stack (because they were computed when *their* sub-productions were reduced earlier). The reduction step for $A \rightarrow \gamma$ is therefore the perfect moment to apply the semantic rules and compute the synthesized attribute(s) of $A$. The new attribute value is pushed alongside $A$ on the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reduction-as-Annotation Principle:** Every *reduce* step in an LR parser can simultaneously execute the semantic rules of an S-attributed definition, with no auxiliary data structures beyond the parser's own stack. This is why **YACC, BYACC, and Bison** output S-attributed translators natively.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Rules

| Concept | Mathematical / Formal Statement | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generic S-Attributed Rule | $A.a := f(X_1.b, X_2.c, \dots, X_n.d)$ | All RHS symbols, all synthesized |
| Top-Down Rule Form | $\text{child} := f(\text{parent attributes})$ | **Not allowed** in pure S-attributed |
| Evaluation Traversal | $\text{postorder}(N) \Rightarrow \text{visit children first}$ | $N$ = parse tree node |
| Dependency Graph Edge | $\text{parent} \leftarrow \text{child}$ (only) | No edges go downward |
| Cycle Test | $\text{Topological sort of dependency DAG} = \text{always exists}$ | S-attributed DAGs are always acyclic |
| Stack Depth Augmentation | $\text{state}_{i+1} \Rightarrow \text{attrib}_{i+1}$ stored in parallel | Required for LR implementation |
| Code Emission Position | **Action routine executed at reduce** | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ reduction point |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Absolute Value Notation:** In this entire notes-set, the vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. For example, the absolute value of an attribute is rendered as $\vert A.val \vert$ — never as a raw `|` that would break markdown table delimiters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

S-attributed definitions are the **backbone of production compiler front-ends**. Concretely, they are used to:

- **Type Checking** — Synthesizing the `type` attribute upward to verify operand compatibility for arithmetic and assignment operations.
- **Constant Folding** — Synthesizing `val` attributes for numeric expressions in optimization passes.
- **Intermediate Code Generation** — Synthesizing `code` attributes (three-address instruction sequences) for sub-expressions, concatenating them via string concatenation as the tree is traversed.
- **Address Computation** — Synthesizing `addr` attributes for symbol table entries during declaration processing.
- **Stack-Machine Code Emission** — Synthesizing instruction lists for a Java Virtual Machine-style or x86-style stack machine.
- **Pretty Printing & Formatting** — Synthesizing pretty-printed string representations of language constructs.

In every industrial-strength compiler (GCC's early stages, Clang's AST builders, the V8 JavaScript engine's parser, the Roslyn C# compiler), the principles of S-attributed translation underpin the very first phases that turn source code into structured, type-checked, intermediate representations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical KTU Example — Desk Calculator Grammar

Consider the standard arithmetic expression grammar augmented for a "desk calculator" that performs immediate evaluation:

$$E \rightarrow E + T \quad \vert \quad E - T \quad \vert \quad T$$

$$T \rightarrow T * F \quad \vert \quad T / F \quad \vert \quad F$$

$$F \rightarrow ( E ) \quad \vert \quad \text{num}$$

We define a single synthesized attribute `val` for each of $E$, $T$, and $F$. The semantic rules are:

| Production | Semantic Rule | Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.val := E_1.val + T.val$ | Add child values |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | $E.val := E_1.val - T.val$ | Subtract child values |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.val := T.val$ | Pass up unchanged |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.val := T_1.val \times F.val$ | Multiply |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 / F$ | $T.val := T_1.val \div F.val$ | Divide |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.val := F.val$ | Pass up unchanged |
| $F \rightarrow ( E )$ | $F.val := E.val$ | Pass up inner value |
| $F \rightarrow \text{num}$ | $F.val := \text{num.lexval}$ | From lexical analyzer |

Notice critically: **every rule has a synthesized attribute on the LHS and only synthesized attributes on the RHS**. This is the defining signature of an S-attributed definition.

## 3.2 Annotation Walkthrough on the Input String `3 + 5 * 2`

We now perform the postorder annotation step-by-step, in the exact order an LR parser would execute the reductions.

**Parse Tree Construction (logical view):**

The input `3 + 5 * 2` parses to a tree with `E` at the root. We will annotate the synthesized `val` at every node by walking bottom-up.

### Step 1 — Process the leftmost leaf: `num` token `3`

The parser reduces $F \rightarrow \text{num}$. Apply semantic rule:

$$F.val := \text{num.lexval} = 3$$

So the $F$ node directly above the `3` token receives the value **3**.

### Step 2 — Bubble up via $T \rightarrow F$

The parser reduces $T \rightarrow F$ (since $F$ alone is a valid $T$). Apply:

$$T.val := F.val = 3$$

The $T$ node above the $F$ receives **3**.

### Step 3 — Bubble up via $E \rightarrow T$

The parser reduces $E \rightarrow T$. Apply:

$$E.val := T.val = 3$$

The leftmost $E$ node receives **3**.

### Step 4 — Process the middle leaf: `num` token `5`

Reduce $F \rightarrow \text{num}$:

$$F.val := 5$$

### Step 5 — Process the rightmost leaf: `num` token `2`

Reduce $F \rightarrow \text{num}$:

$$F.val := 2$$

### Step 6 — Apply $T \rightarrow T * F$ for the `5 * 2` subtree

The parser reduces $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ where $T_1.val = 5$ and $F.val = 2$. Apply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T.val &:= T_1.val \times F.val \\
      &= 5 \times 2 \\
      &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

The $T$ node spanning the `5 * 2` subtree receives **10**.

### Step 7 — Apply $E \rightarrow E + T$ at the root

The parser reduces $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ where $E_1.val = 3$ and $T.val = 10$. Apply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E.val &:= E_1.val + T.val \\
      &= 3 + 10 \\
      &= 13
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Annotated Tree (textual representation)

```
                  E.val = 13
                /   |    \
               /    |     \
         E.val=3   +   T.val=10
           |             /  |  \
           T.val=3      /   |   \
           |          T.val=5  *  F.val=2
           F.val=3     |            |
           |          F.val=5     num(2)
          num(3)
```

**The root $E.val = 13$** is the final evaluated result of the input expression. Notice that at no point did we need to look at a parent to compute a child's value — every value flowed strictly upward.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Synthesized vs Inherited (for clarity)

This matrix is critical for KTU exam answers, as "distinguish" and "compare" questions appear frequently.

| Dimension | Synthesized Attribute | Inherited Attribute |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information Flow | **Child → Parent** (upward) | **Parent → Child** (downward / sideways) |
| Dependency Source | Children's attributes | Parent's or sibling's attributes |
| Computation Time | **Postorder** of parse tree | **Preorder or mixed** |
| SDD Restriction | **Allowed in S-attributed** | **Forbidden in S-attributed** |
| LR Parser Compatibility | **Excellent** (reduce-action time) | **Requires stack augmentation or AST passes** |
| Typical Use | Type, value, code, address | Environment, scope, type-passing |
| Example Rule | $E.val := E_1.val + T.val$ | $T.type := E.type$ |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Building an S-Attributed Definition for Stack Code Emission

Suppose we want to translate the same arithmetic grammar into a simple **postfix (Reverse Polish) stack-machine code**. We attach a synthesized attribute `code` to each of $E$, $T$, and $F$.

**Semantic Rules (all synthesized):**

| Production | Semantic Rule (with `$\,\|\,$` denoting string concatenation) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.code := E_1.code \,\|\, T.code \,\|\, \text{`add'}$ |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | $E.code := E_1.code \,\|\, T.code \,\|\, \text{`sub'}$ |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.code := T.code$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.code := T_1.code \,\|\, F.code \,\|\, \text{`mul'}$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 / F$ | $T.code := T_1.code \,\|\, F.code \,\|\, \text{`div'}$ |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.code := F.code$ |
| $F \rightarrow ( E )$ | $F.code := E.code$ |
| $F \rightarrow \text{num}$ | $F.code := \text{`push '}\,\|\, \text{num.lexval}$ |

**Trace on the input `3 + 5 * 2`:**

We now trace attribute `code` upward. The string concatenation grows as we bubble up.

**Leaves first:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \rightarrow \text{num}(3) \;&\Rightarrow\; F.code = \text{`push 3'} \\
F \rightarrow \text{num}(5) \;&\Rightarrow\; F.code = \text{`push 5'} \\
F \rightarrow \text{num}(2) \;&\Rightarrow\; F.code = \text{`push 2'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass up through $T$ productions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \rightarrow F \;&(\text{for the `3'}):\; T.code = \text{`push 3'} \\
T \rightarrow F \;&(\text{for the `5'}):\; T.code = \text{`push 5'} \\
T \rightarrow T_1 * F \;&(\text{for `5 * 2'}):\\
T.code &= T_1.code \,\|\, F.code \,\|\, \text{`mul'} \\
       &= \text{`push 5'} \,\|\, \text{`push 2'} \,\|\, \text{`mul'} \\
       &= \text{`push 5 push 2 mul'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass up through $E$ productions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \rightarrow T \;&(\text{for the `3'}):\; E.code = \text{`push 3'} \\
E \rightarrow E_1 + T \;&(\text{at root}):\\
E.code &= E_1.code \,\|\, T.code \,\|\, \text{`add'} \\
       &= \text{`push 3'} \,\|\, \text{`push 5 push 2 mul'} \,\|\, \text{`add'} \\
       &= \text{`push 3 push 5 push 2 mul add'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final emitted code:** `push 3 push 5 push 2 mul add`

A stack machine executing this sequence would:
1. Push 3 → stack: [3]
2. Push 5 → stack: [3, 5]
3. Push 2 → stack: [3, 5, 2]
4. `mul` → pop 2 and 5, push 10 → stack: [3, 10]
5. `add` → pop 10 and 3, push 13 → stack: [13]

The result 13 sits on top of the stack — perfectly correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Postorder Annotation Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the strict bottom-up order in which an S-attributed definition is evaluated on a sample parse tree for the input `a * b + c`. Each box represents a parse-tree node with its synthesized attribute, and the arrows show the direction of information flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootE["E.val = result"]
    nodeTplus["T.val = left product"]
    nodeFplus["F.val = c"]
    nodeTmul["T.val = a * b"]
    nodeFmul["F1.val = a"]
    nodeFmul2["F2.val = b"]
    leafA["num : a"]
    leafB["num : b"]
    leafC["num : c"]

    rootE --> nodeTplus
    rootE --> nodeFplus
    nodeTplus --> nodeTmul
    nodeTmul --> nodeFmul
    nodeTmul --> nodeFmul2
    nodeFmul --> leafA
    nodeFmul2 --> leafB
    nodeFplus --> leafC

    leafA -.->|"synth : lexval"| nodeFmul
    leafB -.->|"synth : lexval"| nodeFmul2
    leafC -.->|"synth : lexval"| nodeFplus
    nodeFmul -.->|"synth : pass up"| nodeTmul
    nodeFmul2 -.->|"synth : multiply"| nodeTmul
    nodeTmul -.->|"synth : pass up"| nodeTplus
    nodeFplus -.->|"synth : pass up"| rootE
    nodeTplus -.->|"synth : add"| rootE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid lines represent the **parent-child structural edges** of the parse tree.
- The dotted lines represent the **attribute information flow**, all of which point *upward* — the defining signature of an S-attributed definition.
- The order of dotted-line evaluation is **postorder**: leaves first, root last.

## 4.2 S-Attributed vs L-Attributed: Flow Comparison Block Diagram

The following block diagram contrasts the two major SDD subclasses from a structural perspective.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S_ATT["S-Attributed Definition"]
        direction LR
        s1["Parent Node A"] -->|"uses child values"| s2["Child X1"]
        s1 -->|"uses child values"| s3["Child X2"]
        s2 -.->|"val flows up"| s1
        s3 -.->|"val flows up"| s1
    end

    subgraph L_ATT["L-Attributed Definition"]
        direction LR
        l1["Parent Node A"] -->|"inherits from parent or left sibling"| l2["Child X1"]
        l2 -->|"synthesizes up"| l1
        l1 -->|"passes down to right"| l3["Child X2"]
        l3 -->|"synthesizes up"| l1
        l4["Left Sibling Y"] -.->|"sideways inheritance"| l2
    end

    S_ATT -.->|"Strict subset"| L_ATT
```

**Key visual takeaway:**
- In the **S-Attributed** block, every flow arrow points **upward only**. There is no cross-sibling edge, no downward arrow.
- In the **L-Attributed** block, downward arrows (inheritance) and sideways arrows (sibling-to-sibling) are visible, but still no cycles and no right-to-left flows.

## 4.3 LR Reduction Stack: Where Attributes Reside

This diagram shows how a parallel attribute stack is maintained during LR parsing, with each reduce action updating the topmost attribute.

```mermaid
graph TD
    state0["State : Symbol\nval = ?"]
    state1["State : Symbol\nval = v1"]
    state2["State : Symbol\nval = v2"]
    state3["State : Symbol\nval = v3"]
    state4["State : A\nval = f(v1, v2, v3)"]

    state0 -->|"shift"| state1
    state1 -->|"shift"| state2
    state2 -->|"shift"| state3
    state3 -->|"reduce A to X1 X2 X3"| state4
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each stack frame holds a parse state and a synthesized attribute value.
- On every **reduce** action (e.g., $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 X_3$), the top three `val` values $v_1, v_2, v_3$ are popped, the function $f$ computes the new synthesized value for $A$, and a single new frame is pushed.
- This is the runtime embodiment of an S-attributed definition during LR parsing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing synthesized and inherited.** If a single rule has the form $X.b := Y.c$ where $X$ is a *right-hand side* symbol and $Y$ is the *left-hand side*, that is **inherited**, not synthesized. The SDD is no longer S-attributed. **2 marks lost** for misclassification.
> 2. **Forgetting the postorder evaluation order.** A common KTU question asks "in what order are attributes evaluated?" The answer is *postorder*. Writing "preorder" or "inorder" is **wrong by definition**.
> 3. **Claiming S-attributed SDDs are LL(1)-friendly.** They are not — they fit naturally with LR. LL parsers (top-down) cannot easily evaluate synthesized-only rules in a single pass because the parent's value is needed *before* the children are reduced.
> 4. **Missing the "all attributes synthesized" qualifier.** The defining property must be stated verbatim in 2-mark definition questions. Partial answers score only partial marks.
> 5. **Failing to draw the annotated parse tree.** A 7-mark "annotate the parse tree" question explicitly requires a tree diagram with attribute values at every node. Skipping the diagram costs 3 marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define S-Attributed Definition. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An S-Attributed Definition is a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) in which every semantic rule computes a **synthesized attribute**, meaning the attribute value at a parse-tree node depends *only* on the attribute values of its **child nodes**. Equivalently, no inherited attributes are present in any semantic rule.

Because all attributes are synthesized, the entire SDD can be evaluated by a **single bottom-up pass** (postorder traversal) over the parse tree. This evaluation order coincides naturally with the *reduce* actions of an **LR parser**, which is why S-attributed definitions are the standard choice for bottom-up translator-generator tools such as YACC and Bison. (3 marks)

### Question 2: Why cannot an S-Attributed Definition be evaluated in a single top-down (preorder) pass? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A top-down (preorder) pass visits a parent node *before* its children. In an S-attributed definition, however, the value of an attribute at the parent is defined as a function of the values of its **children's** attributes. When a preorder traversal first reaches the parent, the children's values have not yet been computed, so the parent's attribute cannot be evaluated.

By contrast, a **postorder** traversal visits every child *before* its parent, guaranteeing that all required inputs are available at the moment the semantic rule fires. Hence, a single postorder pass suffices for S-attributed evaluation, but a preorder pass does not. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024): Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

Consider the following grammar for arithmetic expressions:

$$S \rightarrow E\,\$\$

$$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; E - T \;\vert\; T$$

$$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; T / F \;\vert\; F$$

$$F \rightarrow ( E ) \;\vert\; \text{id}$$

**(a)** Construct an S-Attributed Definition that translates the input into **three-address code (3AC)** using a synthesized attribute `code` for each non-terminal. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the input string `a - b + c`, draw the **annotated parse tree** showing the value of the `code` attribute at every node. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The S-Attributed Definition (7 marks):**

We attach one synthesized attribute, `code`, to each of $S$, $E$, $T$, and $F$. The notation `$\|\,$` denotes string concatenation of intermediate-code instruction sequences.

| # | Production | Semantic Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow E\,\$\$ | $S.code := E.code$ |
| 2 | $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.code := E_1.code \,\|\, T.code \,\|\, \text{`t = '}\,\|\, E_1.place \,\|\, \text{` + '}\,\|\, T.place \,\|\, \text{`; E.place = t'}$ |
| 3 | $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | $E.code := E_1.code \,\|\, T.code \,\|\, \text{`t = '}\,\|\, E_1.place \,\|\, \text{` - '}\,\|\, T.place \,\|\, \text{`; E.place = t'}$ |
| 4 | $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.code := T.code; \quad E.place := T.place$ |
| 5 | $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.code := T_1.code \,\|\, F.code \,\|\, \text{`t = '}\,\|\, T_1.place \,\|\, \text{` * '}\,\|\, F.place \,\|\, \text{`; T.place = t'}$ |
| 6 | $T \rightarrow T_1 / F$ | $T.code := T_1.code \,\|\, F.code \,\|\, \text{`t = '}\,\|\, T_1.place \,\|\, \text{` / '}\,\|\, F.place \,\|\, \text{`; T.place = t'}$ |
| 7 | $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.code := F.code; \quad T.place := F.place$ |
| 8 | $F \rightarrow ( E )$ | $F.code := E.code; \quad F.place := E.place$ |
| 9 | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $F.code := \text{`'} \quad F.place := \text{id.lexval}$ |

**Validity check for S-Attributed (1 mark within the 7):** Every rule places a synthesized attribute on the LHS and uses only synthesized attributes (including the terminal's intrinsic lexical value) on the RHS. No inherited attributes appear. Hence the SDD is S-attributed.

**Part (b) — Annotated Parse Tree for `a - b + c` (7 marks):**

The input `a - b + c` parses with `+` as the *last* operation due to left-associativity. The full annotated tree:

```
                            S.code = final 3AC sequence
                            |
                            E.code = "t1 = a - b ; t2 = t1 + c"
                           / | \
                          /  |  \
                E.code = "t1 = a - b"  +  T.code = "t3 = c"  (T -> F -> id)
                       / | \                |
                      /  |  \               F.code = ""
              T.code=""  -  F.code=""      F.place = c
              (T->F->id)    (F->id)         id "c"
              |
              F.code=""; F.place = a
              id "a"
```

For brevity in textual form, the propagated `code` and `place` values are:

- $F \rightarrow \text{id}(a)$: `code = ""`, `place = a`
- $F \rightarrow \text{id}(b)$: `code = ""`, `place = b`
- $T \rightarrow F$ (above `a`): `code = ""`, `place = a`
- $E \rightarrow T$ (above `a`): `code = ""`, `place = a`
- $E_1 \rightarrow E$ (left of `-`): `code = ""`, `place = a`
- $T$ (between `-` and the inner `b`): `code = ""`, `place = b`
- $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$: `code = "t1 = a - b"`, `place = t1`
- $F \rightarrow \text{id}(c)$: `code = ""`, `place = c`
- $T \rightarrow F$ (above `c`): `code = ""`, `place = c`
- $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ (root of `E`): `code = "t1 = a - b ; t2 = t1 + c"`, `place = t2`
- $S \rightarrow E\,\$\$: `code = "t1 = a - b ; t2 = t1 + c"`

**Final emitted 3AC:**

```
t1 = a - b
t2 = t1 + c
```

This output is the result of the S-attributed translation. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted — Internal Choice Alternative]

Consider the grammar:

$$D \rightarrow T \, L$$

$$T \rightarrow \text{int} \;\vert\; \text{real}$$

$$L \rightarrow L \, \text{, id} \;\vert\; \text{id}$$

**(a)** Write an S-Attributed Definition that records each declared identifier's type using a synthesized attribute. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the input `int a, b, c`, draw the **annotated parse tree** showing how the type information propagates upward. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The S-Attributed Definition (7 marks):**

We attach a synthesized attribute `type` to $T$ and a synthesized attribute `entry` to $L$, plus a synthesized attribute `code` to $D$ if we wish to record the declarations. The rules:

| # | Production | Semantic Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $D \rightarrow T \, L$ | $L.inh := T.type; \; D.code := L.code$ (Inherited — but the **question requires we avoid this**. We use the alternative form below.) |
| 1' | $D \rightarrow T \, L$ | $D.code := \text{addType}(L.list, T.type)$ |
| 2 | $T \rightarrow \text{int}$ | $T.type := \text{integer}$ |
| 3 | $T \rightarrow \text{real}$ | $T.type := \text{real}$ |
| 4 | $L \rightarrow L_1 \, \text{, id}$ | $L.list := L_1.list \,\cup\, \{\text{id.lexval}\}; \; L.code := L_1.code \,\|\, \text{addType}(\text{id.lexval}, L.inh)$ |
| 5 | $L \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $L.list := \{\text{id.lexval}\}; \; L.code := \text{addType}(\text{id.lexval}, L.inh)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict S-Attributed Refinement:** A fully synthesized-only version is achievable by *not* using $L.inh$ and instead embedding the type into $L$ via a synthesized `L.type` propagated from a synthesized rule. However, the most natural and KTU-canonical form for declaration processing actually requires an **inherited attribute** $L.inh$ to carry the type downward. This is the textbook reason why *declaration processing is the canonical counterexample* showing that S-attributed definitions are not universally powerful — a 7-mark question on this point is a KTU favorite.

**Part (b) — Annotated Parse Tree for `int a, b, c` (7 marks):**

The tree (textual rendering):

```
                              D
                           /     \
                          T       L
                          |      / | \
                        int    L  ,  id "c"
                              /| \
                             L  ,  id "b"
                             |
                            id "a"
```

If we maintain only synthesized attributes (per S-attributed), then:
- $T.type = \text{integer}$
- $L \rightarrow \text{id}(a)$: $L.list = \{a\}$
- $L \rightarrow L_1 \text{, id}(b)$: $L.list = \{a, b\}$
- $L \rightarrow L_1 \text{, id}(c)$: $L.list = \{a, b, c\}$
- $D \rightarrow T\,L$: $D.code = \text{addType}(\{a, b, c\}, \text{integer})$

If we adopt the inherited version, then:
- $L.inh$ at the leftmost $L$ is set to $T.type = \text{integer}$ (this is the **inherited** step).
- $L.code$ at each level accumulates `addType(id.lexval, integer)`.

**Final symbol-table update for `int a, b, c`:**

| Identifier | Type |
| :--- | :--- |
| a | integer |
| b | integer |
| c | integer |

The example demonstrates that S-attributed definitions handle the *list* of identifiers via synthesized aggregation, but propagating the *type* downward typically requires an **inherited attribute**, which is precisely why the more flexible **L-attributed** class exists. (7 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** S-Attributed Definition = SDD where **all attributes are synthesized**. (1 mark definition question)
- **Information flow:** Strictly **child → parent** (upward only). No downward or sideways edges in the dependency graph.
- **Evaluation order:** **Postorder traversal** of the parse tree.
- **Algorithm:** Bottom-up pass; for every internal node, fire the semantic rules after all children are processed.
- **Parser compatibility:** Native to **LR parsers** (LALR, SLR, Canonical LR). Each *reduce* action in LR corresponds to a node visit in postorder.
- **Inherited attributes:** **Forbidden** in pure S-attributed definitions. Their presence demotes the SDD to the larger L-attributed class.
- **Dependency graph:** Always **acyclic** (no cycles possible) — guarantees a valid evaluation order.
- **Tool support:** YACC, BYACC, GNU Bison generate S-attributed translators from grammar specifications.
- **Common uses:** Type checking (`type`), value computation (`val`), intermediate code emission (`code`), symbol table entry creation, stack-code generation.
- **Counter-example (high-yield):** Declaration processing with type propagation to identifier lists typically **requires inherited attributes** — a classic KTU exam point.
- **Pairing principle:** Reduce-action in LR ↔ Node annotation in postorder ↔ Semantic rule firing.
- **Formula:** $A.a := f(X_1.b, X_2.c, \dots, X_n.d)$ where $A.a$ is synthesized and $X_i.b, X_j.c, \dots$ are all synthesized.
- **Evaluation order mnemonic:** **"S for Synthesized, S for Stack-friendly (LR), S for Simple, S for Successive bubbling up."**
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "show the annotation," draw the full tree with attribute values at every node. Numerical or string values must be visible. **No tree, no full marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: L-Attributed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** L-Attributed Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **L-Attributed Definition** is a special class of **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** in which the attributes of every production node can be evaluated in a single **left-to-right depth-first traversal** of the parse tree, without requiring any backward or upward jumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman — Dragon Book standard):**
> A Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) is said to be **L-attributed** if, for each production $A \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \, \dots \, X_n$, and for each **inherited attribute** $X_i.b$ (where $1 \leq i \leq n$), the semantic rule defining $X_i.b$ uses **only** the attributes from the following three permissible sources:
> 1. **Inherited attributes of the parent node $A$** (i.e., $A.inh$).
> 2. **Either inherited or synthesized attributes of the siblings $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$**, i.e., **only those appearing strictly to the LEFT of $X_i$** in the production body.
> 3. **The own synthesized attributes of $X_i$** itself, **only if they are already computed** before $X_i.b$ (in some fixed topological order on attributes of a single node).

If a synthesized attribute $A.syn$ is associated with the LHS symbol $A$, then it may depend on **any attribute of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$** (since all of them appear on the right-hand side).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Reading a Newspaper Headline":**
> Imagine you are a journalist building a newspaper layout. You write the **headline first**, then the **leftmost column**, then the **middle column**, then the **rightmost column**. Each column may reference information from the headline OR from any column already written to its **left**, but **never** from a column yet to be written.
>
> - The **headline** is like a **parent's inherited attribute** (propagated downward).
> - The **left-side columns** are like **siblings on the left** that have already been "evaluated."
> - The **right-side column** is like the current node $X_i$ whose attribute is being computed — it can only look at things **above** (parent) or **to the left** (earlier siblings).
>
> This **left-to-right, parent-to-child dependency** is the **essence of L-attributedness**.

## 1.3 Why is "L" Important?

> [!TIP]
> The letter **"L" stands for "Left."** This means information flows strictly in **one direction**: from the **left sibling** and from the **parent**, into the current node. This restriction is what makes L-attributed SDDs **practically implementable** during both:
> - **Predictive top-down parsing (LL parsers)**, and
> - **Bottom-up parsing (LR parsers)** — using either an SDT scheme or a postfix traversal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the L-Attributed Property Works

An L-attributed SDD must satisfy the following **structural constraints** for **every production** in the grammar:

1. **Constraint A — Parent Dependency is Permitted:** Any rule of the form $X_i.b := f(A.inh, \dots)$ is allowed because $A$ (the parent) is evaluated before its children during a top-down traversal.

2. **Constraint B — Left-Sibling Dependency is Permitted:** Any rule of the form $X_i.b := f(X_j.attr)$ where $j < i$ is allowed because $X_j$ appears to the **left** of $X_i$ and would be visited first in a left-to-right traversal.
