> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?** Bottom-up evaluation is the **only** mechanism that allows inherited attributes to coexist with **shift-reduce (LR) parsers**, which are the workhorses of industrial compiler front-ends (GCC, Clang, javac).

## 1.3 Quick Symbol Reference

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $X$ | Grammar non-terminal |
| $X.syn$ | Synthesized attribute of $X$ |
| $X.inh$ | Inherited attribute of $X$ |
| $\Rightarrow$ | Production in grammar |
| $\text{post-order}$ | Children visited before parent |
| $\text{LR}(1)$ | Bottom-up parser with 1-token lookahead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The minimum number of **traversals** required to evaluate a *general* SDD with inherited attributes is **two passes** (one bottom-up for synthesized, one top-down for inherited). The bottom-up evaluation goal is to collapse these into a **single bottom-up pass**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Attribute Classification Recap

A production of the form $A \rightarrow B \, C \, D$ yields the following attribute dependencies:

- **Synthesized:** $A.syn = f(B.syn, C.syn, D.syn)$ — depends only on children.
- **Inherited on $B$:** $B.inh = f(A.inh)$ or $f(A.inh, \text{constants})$ — depends on parent.
- **Inherited on $C$:** $C.inh = f(A.inh, B.syn)$ — depends on parent + **left sibling's** synthesized attribute.
- **Inherited on $D$:** $D.inh = f(A.inh, B.syn, C.syn)$ — depends on parent + **all left siblings**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **L-Attributed Definition (KTU High-Yield Term):**
> An SDD is **L-attributed** if, for every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, each inherited attribute $X_i.inh$ depends **only** on:
> 1. The **inherited attributes of $A$**, and
> 2. The **attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$** (the symbols strictly to the **left** of $X_i$).
>
> L-attributed SDDs can be evaluated in a **single left-to-right depth-first traversal** — but this is *top-down + lateral*, not strictly bottom-up.

## 2.2 The Three Necessary Conditions for Bottom-Up Evaluation

For an inherited attribute $X_j.inh$ in production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ to be evaluated in a **post-order (bottom-up) pass**, **all three** of the following must hold:

1. **Parent-Only Source:** The defining expression for $X_j.inh$ depends only on the **inherited attributes of the LHS non-terminal $A$**.
2. **No Right-Sibling Reference:** The defining expression **must not** reference attributes of $X_{j+1}, X_{j+2}, \dots, X_n$ (since they are not yet reduced).
3. **Synthesized Mirror:** An equivalent **synthesized attribute** $A.syn'$ must be available on the LHS such that the value of $X_j.inh$ is recoverable from $A.syn'$ during the reduction step.

If condition (3) holds, we can **embed the inherited value into a synthesized attribute of the parent**, which is naturally computed bottom-up.

## 2.3 The Two Standard Transformation Techniques

### Technique A — **Marker Non-Terminal Insertion**

To pass an inherited attribute $X.inh = A.inh$ from parent $A$ to child $X$ in a production $A \rightarrow \alpha \, X \, \beta$:

- Insert a fresh non-terminal $M$ (a **marker**) between $\alpha$ and $X$.
- The new production becomes $A \rightarrow \alpha \, M \, X \, \beta$.
- Add the rule: $M.syn = A.inh$.
- Rewrite: $X.inh = M.syn$.

Because $M$ is a child of $A$, its synthesized attribute $M.syn$ is computed *before* $A$ is reduced — hence available bottom-up. The marker acts as a **carrier**.

### Technique B — **Synthesized Attribute Mirroring (Copy-Rule Transformation)**

If $X.inh = A.inh$ (a pure copy), declare a new synthesized attribute $A.copy$ such that:

$$A.copy = A.inh$$

Then modify $X.inh = A.copy$ during the reduction of the production containing $X$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Bottom-up inherited attribute evaluation is the foundation of **attribute evaluation in YACC/Bison-generated parsers**. Without it, any construct requiring context to be passed downward (e.g., type information for variable declarations, scope environments, intermediate code generation for expressions) would be impossible to implement in a shift-reduce framework — the dominant parsing paradigm in modern compilers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Applicability | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synthesized attribute** | $A.syn = f(\text{children's attrs})$ | Always bottom-up | High |
| **Inherited attribute (general)** | $X_i.inh = f(A.inh, X_1 \dots X_{i-1})$ | Top-down traversal | High |
| **L-attributed test** | $X_i.inh$ depends only on $A.inh$ + left siblings | Depth-first L→R | High |
| **Bottom-up eligibility** | $X_i.inh = f(A.inh)$ ONLY | Post-order | High |
| **Marker insertion** | $A \rightarrow \alpha \, M \, X \, \beta$, $M.syn = A.inh$ | Always works | Very High |
| **Copy rule** | $X.inh = A.inh$ via $A.syn'$ | Pure copies only | Very High |
| **Two-pass fallback** | Bottom-up + Top-down | Always works | Medium |
| **LR(1) compatibility** | Requires bottom-up OR transformations | All LR parsers | High |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Type Declaration with Inherited Type

Consider a small language fragment for variable declarations where each variable must inherit the type from its enclosing declaration:

**Original SDD (Top-Down):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production } D &\rightarrow T \, L \\
T.type &= \text{keyword type from lexer} \\
L.inh &= T.type \\
L \rightarrow L^{(1)} \,,\; id \;&|\; id \\
L.syn &= \text{append}(L^{(1)}.syn,\; id.entry) \quad \text{or} \quad L.syn = id.entry
\end{aligned}
$$

**Problem:** The inherited attribute $L.inh$ depends on $T.type$ (a child of $D$). In a strict bottom-up pass, $T.type$ is computed *first*, but $L.inh$ is required *before* $L$ is reduced. Thus, $L.inh$ cannot be evaluated bottom-up in its current form.

**Step-by-Step Transformation to Bottom-Up:**

### Step 1 — Identify the inherited attribute
The attribute $L.inh$ must equal $T.type$. This depends only on the **parent's child** — exactly the form eligible for marker transformation.

### Step 2 — Insert marker non-terminal $M$
Introduce a fresh non-terminal $M$ with a synthesized attribute. Rewrite the production:

$$D \rightarrow T \, M \, L$$

### Step 3 — Define the marker's synthesized attribute

$$M.syn = T.type$$

### Step 4 — Rewrite the inherited attribute to use the marker

$$L.inh = M.syn$$

### Step 5 — Verify bottom-up evaluability
During a bottom-up reduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When reducing } T &\rightarrow \text{terminal: } T.type \text{ available (synthesized).} \\
\text{When reducing } M &\rightarrow \epsilon \text{ (or auxiliary): } M.syn = T.type \text{ available.} \\
\text{When reducing } L \rightarrow \dots &: L.inh = M.syn \text{ available BEFORE reduction.} \\
\text{When reducing } D &\rightarrow T\,M\,L: D.syn = L.syn \text{ available.}
\end{aligned}
$$

All reductions proceed **bottom-up without any additional pass**. The transformed grammar is **LR(1)-compatible**.

### Step 6 — Final Transformed SDD

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\rightarrow T \, M \, L \\
M &\rightarrow \epsilon \quad \text{(marker production)} \\
M.syn &= T.type \\
L.inh &= M.syn \\
L &\rightarrow L^{(1)} \,,\; id \quad \text{or} \quad L \rightarrow id \\
L.syn &= \text{append}(L^{(1)}.syn,\; id.entry) \\
L^{(1)}.inh &= L.inh \quad \text{(propagation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Copy-Rule Case

Consider the production where an inherited attribute is a pure copy:

**Original:**

$$A \rightarrow B \, C \qquad B.inh = A.inh$$

**Transformation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Add synthesized } A.copy &\text{ to the LHS } A. \\
A.copy &= A.inh \quad \text{(defined when } A \text{ is used by parent)} \\
B.inh &= A.copy \quad \text{(rewritten, evaluated during reduction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before reducing } A \rightarrow BC &: A.copy = A.inh \text{ is already known (passed from above).} \\
\text{During reduction} &: B.inh \leftarrow A.copy \text{ is assigned.} \\
\text{Then } &: B \text{ uses } B.inh \text{ in its own reductions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **copy-attribute trick** is the simplest form and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Technique

| Scenario | Inherited Attribute Form | Recommended Technique | LR(1) Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure copy from parent | $X.inh = A.inh$ | Copy-rule mirroring | ✅ Yes |
| Copy of left sibling's synthesized | $X.inh = Y.syn$ ($Y$ is left sibling) | Marker insertion before $X$ | ✅ Yes |
| Function of parent's inherited | $X.inh = f(A.inh)$ | Marker insertion with $M.syn = f(A.inh)$ | ✅ Yes |
| Function of right sibling | $X.inh = f(Z.syn)$, $Z$ is right of $X$ | ❌ **Not possible bottom-up** | ❌ No |
| Recursive reference cycle | $A.inh \leftrightarrow B.inh$ mutual | ❌ Requires multi-pass | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Questions involving the **marker non-terminal technique** carry the highest marks (typically 7–10 of 14) because they test (a) recognition of eligibility, (b) correct insertion, (c) redefinition of attributes, and (d) proof of bottom-up evaluability.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Trace — Bottom-Up Parse with Inherited Attribute

Let us trace through the input `int x , y` against the transformed grammar $D \rightarrow T\,M\,L$:

| Stack (bottom-up) | Action | Attribute State |
|---|---|---|
| `int` | Shift | `$T.type = \text{int}$` |
| `int T` | Reduce $T \rightarrow \text{int}$ | `$T.type = \text{int}$` (synthesized) |
| `int T M` | Reduce $M \rightarrow \epsilon$ | `$M.syn = T.type = \text{int}$` |
| `int T M id` | Shift `$x$` | — |
| `int T M id ,` | Shift `,` | — |
| `int T M id , id` | Shift `$y$` | — |
| `int T M L` | Reduce $L \rightarrow L^{(1)}, id$ | `$L.inh = M.syn = \text{int}$; $L^{(1)}.inh = \text{int}$` |
| `int T M L` | Reduce $L \rightarrow id$ | `$L^{(1)}.inh = \text{int}$; final `$L.syn = [x:int, y:int]$` |
| `D` | Reduce $D \rightarrow T\,M\,L$ | `$D.syn = L.syn = [x:int, y:int]$` |

Notice: **zero top-down passes** were required. The inherited attribute was evaluated **entirely bottom-up**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parse-Tree Attribute Flow — Original vs Transformed

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **direction of attribute flow** in the original (top-down) versus transformed (bottom-up) parse tree for the production $D \rightarrow T\,L$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeD["D"]
    nodeT["T"]
    nodeL["L"]
    nodeL1["L1"]
    nodeComma["comma"]
    nodeID["id"]
    nodeID2["id2"]

    nodeD --- nodeT
    nodeD --- nodeL
    nodeL --- nodeL1
    nodeL --- nodeComma
    nodeL --- nodeID
    nodeL1 --- nodeID2

    flowA["T.type flows DOWN to L.inh TOP-DOWN"] -.-> nodeL
    flowB["Original: requires pre-order traversal"] -.-> nodeD

    style nodeD fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeT fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeL fill:#fff5e5,stroke:#996600
    style flowA fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style flowB fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

## 4.2 Transformation Architecture — Marker Non-Terminal Insertion

The block-level functional architecture below shows the **mechanical transformation pipeline** that converts an LR-incompatible inherited attribute into an LR-compatible form.

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1["INPUT: Original SDD with A to X inherited attr"]
    step2["STEP 1: Identify eligible inherited attr depending only on parent"]
    step3["STEP 2: Insert marker non-terminal M between parent and child"]
    step4["STEP 3: Define M.syn equal to the inherited value source"]
    step5["STEP 4: Rewrite X.inh as M.syn"]
    step6["STEP 5: Verify LR1 compatibility via bottom-up trace"]
    step7["OUTPUT: Transformed SDD evaluable in single post-order pass"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7

    style step1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333
    style step7 fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
    style step2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style step3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style step4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style step5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style step6 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.3 Bottom-Up Evaluation Trace — Flow Diagram

The following schematic visualizes the **stack-based reduction sequence** during LR parsing with the transformed grammar. Each reduction step shows how the inherited attribute becomes available **before** the reduction it is needed in.

```mermaid
graph TD
    phase1["Phase 1: Shift terminals onto stack"]
    phase2["Phase 2: Reduce T to terminal - T.type synthesized"]
    phase3["Phase 3: Reduce M to epsilon - M.syn captures T.type"]
    phase4["Phase 4: Propagate L.inh = M.syn bottom-up"]
    phase5["Phase 5: Reduce D - D.syn = L.syn complete"]

    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5

    note1["NO top-down traversal needed"] -.-> phase4

    style phase1 fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666
    style phase2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style phase4 fill:#e5f4d4,stroke:#336600
    style phase5 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc3300
    style note1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why cannot all inherited attributes be evaluated using a bottom-up parse tree traversal? State the necessary condition under which bottom-up evaluation becomes possible.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bottom-up traversal processes children **before** the parent, so attributes that depend on the **parent** or on **right siblings** are unavailable at the time they are needed.

An inherited attribute $X_j.inh$ in production $A \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_n$ can be evaluated bottom-up **only if** its defining expression depends **solely on the inherited attributes of the LHS non-terminal $A$** (or equivalently, on already-computed synthesized attributes of left siblings), with **no reference to any right sibling**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "inherited attributes always need top-down traversal" — this is **incorrect**. The **correct** statement is "inherited attributes depending on right siblings cannot be evaluated bottom-up." This distinction costs **1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a marker non-terminal? How does it enable bottom-up evaluation of inherited attributes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **marker non-terminal** $M$ is a freshly introduced grammar symbol inserted into a production to act as a **carrier** for attribute values that must flow from a parent to a child.

**Mechanism:**

1. Insert $M$ immediately before the child $X$ whose inherited attribute is required.
2. Define $M.syn$ to equal the value the parent would have passed (e.g., $M.syn = A.inh$).
3. Rewrite the inherited attribute as $X.inh = M.syn$.

Since $M$ is a **child** of $A$, its synthesized attribute is computed **before** $A$ is reduced. Thus, $M.syn$ is available during the bottom-up reduction phase — enabling $X.inh$ to be assigned **without** a separate top-down pass.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following SDD for a simplified declaration grammar where each identifier inherits its type:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\rightarrow T \, L \\
T.type &= \text{lexical type} \\
L.inh &= T.type \\
L &\rightarrow L^{(1)} \,,\; id \;|\; id \\
L.syn &= L^{(1)}.syn \,\vert\, id.entry
\end{aligned}
$$

Show the step-by-step transformation of this SDD into an equivalent bottom-up-evaluable form using **marker non-terminals**. Draw the transformed parse tree for the input `real a, b, c`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the bottom-up reduction sequence for the input `real a, b, c` on the transformed grammar. Show the contents of the parser stack and the state of all attributes at each reduction step.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Identify Inherited Attribute:**
$L.inh$ depends on $T.type$. $T$ is a **left sibling** of $L$. This is eligible for marker transformation.

**Step 2 — Insert Marker $M$:**

$$D \rightarrow T \, M \, L$$

**Step 3 — Define Marker Rule:**

$$M \rightarrow \epsilon \qquad M.syn = T.type$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite Inherited Attribute:**

$$L.inh = M.syn$$

**Step 5 — Complete Transformed SDD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\rightarrow T \, M \, L \\
M &\rightarrow \epsilon \\
T.type &= \text{lexical type} \\
M.syn &= T.type \\
L.inh &= M.syn \\
L &\rightarrow L^{(1)} \,,\; id \quad L^{(1)}.inh = L.inh, \quad L.syn = L^{(1)}.syn \cup \{id.entry\} \\
L &\rightarrow id \quad L.syn = \{id.entry\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transformed Parse Tree for `real a, b, c`:**

```
            D
          / | \
         T  M  L
         |  |  /|\ 
       real ε L  , id(c)
              /|\
             L , id(b)
             /|\
            L , id(a)
            |
           id(a)
```

(Diagrammatic representation; each $L$ carries $inh = real$ propagated via $M.syn$.)

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Bottom-Up Reduction Trace for `real a, b, c`:**

| Step | Stack | Input Remaining | Action | Attribute Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$` | `real a , b , c $` | Shift `real` | — |
| 2 | `$ real` | `a , b , c $` | Shift `a` | — |
| 3 | `$ real a` | `, b , c $` | Reduce $L \rightarrow id$ | `$L.syn = \{a\}$` |
| 4 | `$ real L` | `, b , c $` | Reduce $M \rightarrow \epsilon$ | `$M.syn = T.type = real$` |
| 5 | `$ real L M` | `, b , c $` | Shift `,` | — |
| 6 | `$ real L M ,` | `b , c $` | Shift `b` | — |
| 7 | `$ real L M , b` | `, c $` | Reduce $L \rightarrow id$ | `$L.syn = \{b\}$` |
| 8 | `$ real L M , L` | `, c $` | Reduce $L \rightarrow L, id$ | `$L^{(1)}.inh = M.syn = real$`; `$L.syn = \{a,b\}$` |
| 9 | `$ real L M L` | `, c $` | Shift `,` | — |
| 10 | `$ real L M L ,` | `c $` | Shift `c` | — |
| 11 | `$ real L M L , c` | `$` | Reduce $L \rightarrow id$ | `$L.syn = \{c\}$` |
| 12 | `$ real L M L , L` | `$` | Reduce $L \rightarrow L, id$ | `$L^{(1)}.inh = real$`; `$L.syn = \{a,b,c\}$` |
| 13 | `$ real L M L` | `$` | Reduce $D \rightarrow T\,M\,L$ | `$D.syn = L.syn = \{a,b,c\}$ all of type $real$` |
| 14 | `$ D` | `$` | **Accept** | — |

**Key Observation:** The attribute `inh = real` flows from $M.syn$ into every $L$ node **during reduction**, never requiring a separate top-down pass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **omit the $M \rightarrow \epsilon$ production** in their transformed grammar. This is a **2-mark deduction** because the marker requires an explicit production rule to be syntactically valid. Also, students forget to propagate $L^{(1)}.inh = L.inh$ in recursive productions — another **1 mark** lost.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **L-attributed definition**. Explain why every L-attributed SDD cannot be evaluated strictly bottom-up. Give a counterexample involving a right-sibling reference.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following SDD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow B \, C \\
B.inh &= f(A.inh) \\
C.inh &= g(B.syn)
\end{aligned}
$$

Transform it into an equivalent form evaluable in a **single bottom-up pass**. State clearly which technique you use and justify why no second top-down pass is needed.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition of L-Attributed SDD (3 marks):**

An SDD is **L-attributed** if for every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ and for every inherited attribute $X_i.inh$, the defining expression depends **only** on:
1. The inherited attributes of $A$, and
2. The attributes of symbols $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$ (those to the **left** of $X_i$).

**Why L-attributed ≠ Bottom-up (2 marks):**

L-attributed permits reference to **all left siblings**, including synthesized attributes of $X_1 \dots X_{i-1}$. While these are technically available before $X_i$ in a left-to-right traversal, a **strict bottom-up post-order traversal** computes attributes in the order: all children of a node, then the node itself. A reference like $X_i.inh = X_{i-1}.syn$ is fine, but a reference to a **right sibling** is not.

**Counterexample (2 marks):**

$$
A \rightarrow B \, C \qquad C.inh = B.inh + 1
$$

Here $C$ references $B$'s attribute. While $B.inh$ would need $A.inh$ (top-down), $C.inh$ references $B$ which is its **left sibling** — this *is* technically bottom-up compatible if $B.inh$ is pre-computed.

A genuine non-bottom-up counterexample:

$$
A \rightarrow B \, C \qquad B.inh = C.syn
$$

Here $B$ references its **right sibling** $C$'s synthesized attribute. Since $C$ is not yet reduced when $B$ is being evaluated, this is **impossible bottom-up** — it requires either a two-pass approach or a pre-computation trick.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Analysis (2 marks):**

The SDD has two inherited attributes:
- $B.inh = f(A.inh)$ — depends only on parent. **Eligible** for bottom-up.
- $C.inh = g(B.syn)$ — depends on $B$'s synthesized attribute. **Eligible** if we ensure $B.syn$ is computed before $C$ (it is, since $B$ is reduced first).

**Technique: Marker Non-Terminal for $C.inh$ (3 marks):**

The issue is that $C.inh$ requires $B.syn$, which is fine in post-order, BUT we want a **single** bottom-up pass. The simplest approach: rewrite $C.inh$ to use $B.syn$ directly, since $B$ is always reduced before $C$ in any post-order traversal. Verify with the trace:

When reducing $B \rightarrow \dots$: $B.syn$ becomes available.
When reducing $C \rightarrow \dots$ (still before $A$ is reduced): $C.inh = g(B.syn)$ is computable.
When reducing $A \rightarrow B\,C$: $A.syn = h(B.syn, C.syn)$ is computable.

**Transformed SDD (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow B \, C \\
B.inh &= A.inh \quad \text{(copy from parent — evaluated when A's parent provides A.inh)} \\
B.syn &= \text{computed from B's children (unchanged)} \\
C.inh &= g(B.syn) \quad \text{(computed during C's reduction using already-available B.syn)} \\
A.syn &= h(B.syn, C.syn)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification (bonus mark):** No top-down pass is needed because:
1. $B.inh$ is a copy of $A.inh$ — provided when the parent reduces to $A$.
2. $C.inh$ uses $B.syn$ which is computed when $B$ is reduced (before $C$).
3. Both reductions happen **before** $A$ is reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Valuation:** Students often introduce **two marker non-terminals** when only one is needed. For $B.inh$ which is a pure copy from $A.inh$, the **copy-rule technique** (Technique B) suffices — adding a marker here is overkill and loses **1 mark** for grammar bloat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Synthesized attributes** always flow bottom-up; **inherited attributes** usually flow top-down.
- ✅ An inherited attribute can be evaluated **bottom-up only if** it depends exclusively on the parent's inherited attributes (and/or left siblings' already-computed synthesized attributes).
- ✅ **Marker non-terminals** are the **universal technique** for converting top-down inherited attributes into bottom-up form — insert $M$, define $M.syn$, rewrite $X.inh = M.syn$.
- ✅ **Copy-rule technique** is a special case for $X.inh = A.inh$ — use a synthesized mirror attribute.
- ✅ **L-attributed SDDs** are *necessary but not sufficient* for bottom-up evaluation.
- ✅ **Right-sibling references** in inherited attributes **cannot** be evaluated bottom-up under any transformation — they require a two-pass approach.
- ✅ **LR(1) parsers (YACC/Bison)** demand bottom-up attribute evaluation — hence the practical importance.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always **draw the transformed parse tree** AND **trace the bottom-up reductions** for full marks.
- ✅ **Forgetting the $M \rightarrow \epsilon$ production** costs 2 marks.
- ✅ **Forgetting $L^{(1)}.inh = L.inh$** in recursive productions costs 1 mark.
- ✅ Always **state the technique used** (marker vs copy) and **justify bottom-up evaluability** with a reduction trace.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermediate Code Generation (ICG): Need of ICG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Code Generation (ICG): Need of ICG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intermediate Code Generation (ICG)** is the phase of a compiler that translates the **syntax tree** (or AST — Abstract Syntax Tree) of the source program into an **intermediate representation (IR)** — a machine-independent, abstract code form that lies *between* the high-level source language and the final target machine code.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the ICG phase is positioned *after* **Semantic Analysis** (Type Checking) and *before* **Code Optimization** and **Code Generation**.

The output of ICG is an internal, structured form such as:
- **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — most common in KTU syllabus
- **Postfix Notation** (Reverse Polish Notation)
- **Syntax Trees / Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAG)**
- **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** form

A generic three-address instruction has the form:
$$ x = y \;\text{op}\; z $$

where $x, y, z$ are names (variables, temporaries, or constants) and $\text{op}$ is a binary operator. At most **one operator** appears on the right-hand side.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Universal Translator in a Multilingual Conference.**
>
> Imagine a UN-style conference where delegates from **Country A** (Source Language: C/Java/Python) must communicate with delegates from **Country B, C, D…** (Target Machines: x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS).
>
> Instead of hiring *N × M* separate translators (one pair for every source–target combination), we appoint **one common translator** — an **Esperanto-like neutral language** (the Intermediate Representation).
>
> - **Source → Esperanto** = Front-end + ICG (one effort)
> - **Esperanto → Target Machine** = Back-end Code Generation (reusable per machine)
>
> Result: To support $N$ source languages on $M$ target machines, we need only **$N + M$ translation modules**, instead of $N \times M$. This is the *central economic argument* for the existence of ICG.

## 1.3 Position in the Compilation Pipeline

The placement of ICG in a modern multi-pass compiler is:

$$\text{Source} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lexical Analysis} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Syntax Analysis (AST)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Semantic Analysis} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{ICG (IR)}} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Optimization} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Code Generation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Target Code}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IR is **machine-independent**, meaning the *same* IR can be retargeted to multiple architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V) without rewriting the front-end. This property is called **Retargetability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Six Fundamental Reasons for Needing ICG

A compiler *could* in theory translate source code directly to machine code in a single pass. The ICG phase exists because of the following engineering imperatives:

### 2.1.1 **Retargetability (Machine Independence)**
- The front-end (Lex → Parse → Semantic → IR) is written **once**.
- A new target machine only requires a **new back-end** that consumes the IR.
- Cost model: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M) \rightarrow \mathcal{O}(N + M)$ where $N$ = number of source languages, $M$ = number of target machines.

### 2.1.2 **Reusability of Optimization Modules**
- Machine-independent optimizations (constant folding, dead-code elimination, common subexpression elimination) are performed on the **IR**, not on raw machine code.
- The same optimizer works for *every* target.

### 2.1.3 **Easier Implementation of Code Generation**
- The back-end only has to translate a **simple, regular IR** into machine instructions, rather than parsing complex high-level constructs.
- Instruction selection becomes a tractable pattern-matching problem.

### 2.1.4 **Clean Separation of Concerns (Modularity)**
- Front-end team and back-end team can work in **parallel**.
- Each phase can be tested, verified, and replaced independently.
- Aligns with the **NEP 2020 outcome-based modular curriculum** philosophy.

### 2.1.5 **Easier Application of Compiler Theory**
- Data-flow analysis, control-flow analysis, and SSA construction are mathematically elegant on a uniform IR.
- Tools like **LLVM IR** (an industry reference) demonstrate this principle at scale.

### 2.1.6 **Portability of the Compiler Itself**
- A compiler written with a clean IR can be **ported** to a new architecture with minimal effort — only the back-end changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked *"Why is ICG needed?"*, examiners expect you to mention **retargetability**, **machine-independent optimization**, and **modularity** at minimum. Always cite these three in your answer for full marks.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property** | **Description** | **Engineering Benefit** |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Independence | IR is not bound to any specific CPU | Same compiler → multiple architectures |
| Simplicity | Each IR instruction performs ≤ 1 operation | Easy pattern matching in code generator |
| Three-Address Form | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Fits naturally into register allocation |
| Explicit Temporaries | Compiler-generated names $t_1, t_2, \dots$ | Trivial data-flow analysis |
| Explicit Control Flow | Uses labels & jumps (goto-like) | CFG construction is straightforward |
| Lossless | Original program semantics preserved 1-to-1 | Optimizations are safe & provable |
| Uniform Type System | Typed temporaries / operands | Type-safe optimization passes |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **software industry**, ICG is not a theoretical exercise — it powers every major toolchain:

- **GCC** uses **GIMPLE** (an SSA-based IR).
- **LLVM** uses **LLVM IR** (SSA + infinite virtual registers).
- **Microsoft .NET** compiles C#/F#/VB to **CIL (Common Intermediate Language)** — executed by the CLR.
- **Java** compiles to **JVM Bytecode** — interpreted/JIT-compiled by the JVM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** If a student understands ICG at a theoretical level, they can directly grasp *Bytecode*, *JIT*, and *AOT compilation* concepts — a frequent question in viva-voce and placement interviews.

## 2.4 Properties of a "Good" Intermediate Representation

A well-designed IR must satisfy:

1. **Ease of generation** — straightforward to emit from AST.
2. **Ease of manipulation** — supports optimization transformations.
3. **Ease of translation to target** — back-end code generation is mechanical.
4. **Semantic clarity** — preserves the meaning of the original program exactly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Source Code to Three-Address Code

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is the most common KTU Part-B sub-question.** Practice this exact pattern until it is muscle memory.

### Given Source Statement (C-like)

$$\text{while} \; (a < b) \;\text{do} \; \{\; c = c + 1;\; b = b \;\ast\; 2;\; \}$$

### Step 1 — Lexical & Syntactic Pass (Conceptual Only)
The parser builds an AST representing the `while` loop, the condition, and the assignment statements.

### Step 2 — Apply ICG Algorithm to Emit Three-Address Code

We emit a sequence of TAC instructions, using:
- **Labels** $L_1, L_2, \dots$ for branch targets
- **Temporaries** $t_1, t_2, \dots$ for intermediate values
- **goto** for explicit control flow

The translation algorithm walks the AST and produces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{L1:}\;\; \text{if } a < b \;\text{ goto } L2 \\
&\text{goto } L3 \\
&\text{L2:}\;\; t_1 = c + 1 \\
&\quad\; c = t_1 \\
&\quad\; t_2 = b \,\star\, 2 \\
&\quad\; b = t_2 \\
&\quad\; \text{goto } L1 \\
&\text{L3:}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Analytical Interpretation of Each TAC Instruction

| **Line** | **Semantics** | **Why It Appears** |
|---|---|---|
| `if a < b goto L2` | Conditional branch — enters loop body | Translation of `while` condition |
| `goto L3` | Unconditional jump past body | Exits loop when condition fails |
| `t1 = c + 1` | Temporary holds the RHS of `c = c+1` | TAC rule: only 1 operator per instr |
| `c = t1` | Stores the result into `c` | Assignment to LHS variable |
| `t2 = b * 2` | Temporary holds `b * 2` | TAC rule again |
| `b = t2` | Stores back into `b` | Assignment |
| `goto L1` | Loop back-edge | Re-tests the condition |
| `L3:` | Exit label | Continues to next program statement |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Cost-Benefit Derivation — The $N \times M$ vs. $N + M$ Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a frequent 7-mark KTU question** under the heading *"Discuss the need for intermediate code generation."*

### Step 1 — Without ICG (Direct Translation)
For $N$ source languages and $M$ target machines, each pair needs its **own** compiler.

$$
T_{\text{no ICG}} = N \cdot M \;\text{ compiler implementations}
$$

### Step 2 — With ICG (Front-end + Back-end)
- **Front-end:** Source $\rightarrow$ IR. There is one front-end *per source language*, so $N$ implementations.
- **Back-end:** IR $\rightarrow$ Target. There is one back-end *per target machine*, so $M$ implementations.

$$
T_{\text{with ICG}} = N + M \;\text{ implementations}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Savings Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings Factor} &= \frac{T_{\text{no ICG}}}{T_{\text{with ICG}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{N \cdot M}{N + M} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{M} + \frac{1}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration
Take $N = 3$ source languages (C, Java, Python) and $M = 4$ target machines (x86, ARM, MIPS, RISC-V).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{no ICG}} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \;\text{compilers} \\
T_{\text{with ICG}} &= 3 + 4 = 7 \;\text{modules} \\
\text{Savings} &= 12 - 7 = 5 \;\text{modules avoided} \;(\approx 41.7\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $N$ and $M$ grow in real compiler suites (e.g., GCC supports 25+ languages and 30+ targets), the savings approach **$N + M \ll N \cdot M$**, justifying ICG as a *non-negotiable* architectural choice.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ICG Benefits Mapped to Course Outcomes (COs)

| **KTU 2024 CO** | **Benefit Addressed by ICG** | **Mapping Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| CO3 — Apply code generation strategies | Retargetability | One IR, multiple back-ends |
| CO4 — Design intermediate representations | Modularity & Clean Phases | Front-end/back-end separation |
| CO4 — Optimization | Machine-independent optimization | Optimizer operates on IR |
| CO5 — Analyze compiler performance | Cost savings $N \times M \to N + M$ | Quantitative engineering argument |

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Forms of Intermediate Representation

| **IR Form** | **Structure** | **Strengths** | **Weaknesses** | **Used By** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Postfix (RPN) | Stack-machine style | Very simple to generate | Hard to optimize | Early Pascal compilers |
| Three-Address Code | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Compact, optimizer-friendly | Still flat, no nesting | Most KTU textbook examples |
| AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) | Hierarchical tree | Lossless, structured | Hard to linearize for CFG | Front-end output |
| DAG | Shared subexpression graph | Detects CSE automatically | Construction is non-trivial | Local optimizations |
| SSA | TAC + $\phi$-functions | Powerful data-flow analysis | $\phi$-placement is complex | LLVM, modern GCC |
| Bytecode | Stack-based instructions | Portable, secure | Needs VM interpreter/runner | Java, .NET CIL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Phases of a Compiler with ICG Highlighted

```mermaid

    lexA --> lexOut[Tokens]

    semA --> typedAST[Typed AST]
    typedAST --> icgBox["ICG Module 3 - Intermediate Code Generator"]
    icgBox --> irOut["IR - Three Address Code"]
    irOut --> opt[Code Optimizer]
    opt --> irOpt["Optimized IR"]
    irOpt --> cg[Code Generator]
    cg --> tgtOut[Target Machine Code]

    style irOut fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```


## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of ICG Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Typed AST Input] --> procA[AST Traversal Module]
    procA --> procB[Symbol Table Lookup]

    tacOut --> opt1[Local Optimization]

    style tacGen fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Lifecycle of an IR Instruction Through the Compiler

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ASTNode : Source construct parsed
    ASTNode --> TACInstr : ICG phase emits TAC
    TACInstr --> Optimized : Machine-indep optimizer runs
    Optimized --> Assembly : Back-end code generator
    Assembly --> MachineCode : Assembler / linker
    MachineCode --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]**

### **Q1. Define Intermediate Code Generation. List any three properties of a good intermediate representation.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> Intermediate Code Generation (ICG) is the phase of a compiler that converts the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** produced by semantic analysis into a **machine-independent intermediate representation (IR)** such as three-address code, postfix notation, or syntax tree.

Three properties of a good IR:

1. **Ease of generation** — straightforward to emit from the AST.
2. **Ease of manipulation** — supports optimization passes like constant folding and dead-code elimination.
3. **Ease of translation to target** — back-end code generation becomes a simple pattern-matching task.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

### **Q2. Why is intermediate code considered "machine-independent"?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The intermediate code uses generic instructions (e.g., $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$) and abstract constructs such as temporaries and labels that are **not tied to any specific CPU's register set, instruction format, or addressing modes**. Since the IR is target-neutral, the *same* IR can be consumed by different back-ends to produce code for **x86, ARM, MIPS, RISC-V**, etc., without modification. This property is called **retargetability**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 contains questions that frequently combine ICG with subsequent topics like code generation or optimization. Below are **TWO complete alternative choices**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Option 1)*
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 3]**

#### **(a) Discuss the various reasons for the need of intermediate code generation in a compiler. Show how ICG reduces the number of translation modules from $N \times M$ to $N + M$.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
ICG produces a machine-independent intermediate representation (IR) between the front-end (semantic analysis) and the back-end (code generation). Common IR forms include three-address code (TAC), postfix notation, syntax trees, and SSA.

**Step 2 — Six Reasons for ICG:**

1. **Retargetability:** Front-end is written once; only the back-end changes per target.
2. **Reusability of Optimization:** Machine-independent optimizations (constant folding, dead-code elimination, CSE) work on the IR.
3. **Simpler Code Generation:** Back-end only translates a *regular, simple* IR — not complex source constructs.
4. **Modular Design:** Front-end and back-end teams work in parallel; phases are independently testable.
5. **Theoretical Elegance:** Data-flow analysis, control-flow graphs, SSA transformation are clean on a uniform IR.
6. **Portability:** The compiler can be ported to a new architecture by rewriting only the back-end.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Derivation:**

Let $N$ = number of source languages, $M$ = number of target machines.

Without ICG, each source–target pair needs a separate compiler:

$$
\text{Compilers needed (no ICG)} = N \cdot M
$$

With ICG:

$$
\text{Modules needed (with ICG)} = N \;(\text{front-ends}) + M \;(\text{back-ends})
$$

Reduction ratio:

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{N \cdot M}{N + M}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
For $N = 3$ (C, Java, Python) and $M = 4$ (x86, ARM, MIPS, RISC-V):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{No ICG} &= 12 \text{ compilers} \\
\text{With ICG} &= 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ modules} \\
\text{Savings} &= 5 \text{ modules} \;(\approx 41.7\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** ICG provides a clean, modular, retargetable compiler architecture, drastically reducing development effort as $N$ and $M$ grow.

---

#### **(b) Translate the following C code segment into three-address code (TAC), clearly showing labels, temporaries, and control flow.**
*(7 Marks)*

```text
if (x > y)
    z = x - y;
else
    z = y - x;
w = z * 2;
```

**Model Solution:**

Apply the standard ICG translation scheme for `if-then-else`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } x > y \;\text{ goto } L1 \\
&\text{goto } L2 \\
&\text{L1:}\;\; t_1 = x - y \\
&\quad\; z = t_1 \\
&\text{goto } L3 \\
&\text{L2:}\;\; t_2 = y - x \\
&\quad\; z = t_2 \\
&\text{L3:}\;\; t_3 = z \,\star\, 2 \\
&\quad\; w = t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of Each Instruction:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `if x > y goto L1` | Test the condition; branch to *then* if true |
| `goto L2` | Skip to *else* if condition false |
| `L1: t1 = x - y` | Compute RHS of `z = x - y` |
| `z = t1` | Assign result to `z` |
| `goto L3` | Jump past *else* block |
| `L2: t2 = y - x` | Compute RHS of *else* branch |
| `z = t2` | Assign result to `z` |
| `L3: t3 = z * 2` | Compute RHS of `w = z * 2` |
| `w = t3` | Final assignment |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to write `z = x - y` directly without using a temporary $t_1$. This **violates the three-address rule** (only one operator per instruction). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. **Always introduce a temporary for multi-step expressions.**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Option 2)*
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 3]**

#### **(a) Explain the role of intermediate code generation in enabling machine-independent optimization. List four machine-independent optimizations with one-line descriptions.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Role of ICG in Optimization:**

ICG produces a uniform, machine-independent IR on which the **optimizer** can perform transformations **without needing to know the target architecture**. Because the IR is:
- Simple (≤ 1 operation per instruction),
- Structured (explicit temporaries and control flow),
- And semantically lossless,

…the optimizer can safely apply algebraic transformations that are guaranteed to be correct on *every* target machine.

**Step 2 — Four Machine-Independent Optimizations:**

1. **Constant Folding:** Evaluate constant expressions at compile time. e.g., $x = 3 + 4 \Rightarrow x = 7$.
2. **Constant Propagation:** Replace a variable with its known constant value. e.g., if $x = 5$ and $y = x + 1$, then $y = 6$.
3. **Dead-Code Elimination:** Remove instructions whose results are never used.
4. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** If the same expression is computed twice, replace the second occurrence with the first computed value.

**Step 3 — Why ICG is the Right Place:**

These transformations are *easier* on IR because:
- Temporaries are explicit (easy to detect reuse).
- Control flow is explicit labels + gotos (easy CFG construction).
- No register-allocation interference yet.

---

#### **(b) Consider the following arithmetic expression. Generate its three-address code and identify the temporaries used:**
*(7 Marks)*

$$
\text{Expression:} \quad x = (a + b) \;\star\; (c - d) \;/\; e
$$

**Model Solution:**

Apply ICG with operator-precedence and three-address rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = a + b \\
&t_2 = c - d \\
&t_3 = t_1 \,\star\, t_2 \\
&t_4 = t_3 \;/\; e \\
&x = t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Justification:**

| Step | Instruction | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_1 = a + b$ | Parenthesized sub-expression $(a+b)$ → temporary |
| 2 | $t_2 = c - d$ | Parenthesized sub-expression $(c-d)$ → temporary |
| 3 | $t_3 = t_1 \,\star\, t_2$ | Multiplication of the two temps |
| 4 | $t_4 = t_3 \;/\; e$ | Division by $e$ |
| 5 | $x = t_4$ | Final assignment to LHS variable |

**Number of temporaries used:** $4 \;(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write $x = (a+b) \;\star\; (c-d) \;/\; e$ as a single TAC line — this breaks the three-address format.
> - **Always follow evaluation order** ($*$, $/$ are left-associative). Using wrong associativity loses 1 mark.
> - Mention the **number of temporaries** explicitly — examiners often allot 1 mark for this count.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ICG: Need of ICG**

- ✅ ICG converts the **AST / typed AST** into a **machine-independent IR** (most commonly TAC).
- ✅ Position: comes **after Semantic Analysis** and **before Code Optimization**.
- ✅ Three-address form: $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ — **at most one operator** per instruction.
- ✅ Need of ICG = **Retargetability + Reusability + Modularity + Machine-Independent Optimization + Portability + Simpler Code Generation**.
- ✅ Cost argument: $N \times M \to N + M$ compiler modules.
- ✅ ICG works with **labels, temporaries, and gotos** for control flow.
- ✅ Real-world IRs: **LLVM IR, GIMPLE (GCC), JVM Bytecode, .NET CIL**.
- ✅ Common IR types: **TAC, Postfix, AST, DAG, SSA, Bytecode**.
- ✅ Standard TAC for `if-else` and `while` patterns must be memorized verbatim.
- ✅ Always use **explicit temporaries** for sub-expressions — never compress them.
- ✅ Properties of good IR: **ease of generation, manipulation, translation, semantic clarity**.
- ✅ Common pitfall: forgetting `goto L3` after the *then* block to skip the *else* block.
- ✅ Quantitative savings scale as $N, M$ grow — central argument in viva answers.
- ✅ ICG is the **enabler** of machine-independent optimizations like constant folding, CSE, dead-code elimination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Representations of Intermediate Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Representations of Intermediate Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Intermediate Code** is an abstract, machine-independent representation of a source program generated by the front-end of a compiler, positioned strategically between the syntax analysis (parsing) phase and the code generation phase. It serves as a **bridge** that decouples language-specific front-ends from target-specific back-ends, enabling portability, retargetability, and the application of machine-independent optimizations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum for *PEITT631 - Compiler Design*, the syllabus explicitly identifies the following canonical representations of intermediate code:

1. **High-Level Intermediate Code** — Closest to the source language (e.g., AST).
2. **Low-Level Intermediate Code** — Closer to the target machine (e.g., Three-Address Code).
3. **Postfix Notation (Reverse Polish Notation)**.
4. **Syntax Trees (Abstract Syntax Tree — AST)**.
5. **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAG)**.
6. **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — implemented via *Quadruples*, *Triples*, and *Indirect Triples*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim flavour):** *Intermediate code is a representation of a program that is independent of the source language syntax but expresses the semantic operations in a form suitable for machine-independent optimization and easy translation to target code.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of intermediate code as **English used in a courtroom**, sitting between two languages:
- The *witness testifies* in their native tongue (source program in C/Java/Python).
- The *judge's verdict* is delivered in formal legal English (target machine code — say, x86 or ARM).
- A **court interpreter** translates the witness's statement into a neutral, precise legal transcript (**intermediate code**) before the verdict is issued.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in compilers:**
> - **Retargetability:** A single front-end (C parser) can target x86, ARM, RISC-V, and MIPS by *only* changing the back-end.
> - **Optimization Reuse:** Machine-independent optimizations (constant folding, dead code elimination) operate on the IR, not on every target language.
> - **Modular Design:** Cleanly separates *analysis* from *synthesis*.

## 1.3 Where It Fits in the Compiler Pipeline

The **Frontend** (lexical → syntax → semantic analysis) produces an **AST**, which is then *linearized* into an **Intermediate Representation (IR)**. The **Optimizer** rewrites this IR, and finally the **Backend (Code Generator)** emits target machine code.

> [!TIP]
> In **LLVM** (industry reference), this IR is called *LLVM IR*; in **GCC**, it is *GIMPLE / RTL*. KTU 2024 expects students to reason abstractly about IR without tying it to a specific toolchain.

---

## 1.4 Salient Properties (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> An ideal IR must satisfy:
> - **Machine Independence** — no registers, no memory addresses tied to a specific CPU.
> - **Ease of Generation** — straightforward to produce from the AST.
> - **Ease of Translation** — easy to lower to assembly.
> - **Ease of Optimization** — control flow is explicit; data flow is analyzable.
> - **Linear or Graphical form** — must support static analysis (linear scan) and transformations (graph rewriting).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Intermediate Representations

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies IRs along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories | KTU-Relevant Example |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **Level of Abstraction** | High-level (HIR), Mid-level (MIR), Low-level (LIR) | AST is HIR; TAC is MIR/LIR |
| **Structural Form** | Linear, Graphical, Hybrid | Postfix = Linear; AST/DAG = Graphical; TAC = Linear |
| **Naming Style** | Named vs. Positional | Quadruples = Named; Triples = Positional |

---

## 2.2 Representation #1 — Postfix Notation (Suffix / Reverse Polish)

### Operational Logic
- **Rule:** Place the operator **after** its operands.
- **Stack-based evaluation:** scan left to right, push operands onto a stack; on encountering an operator, pop the required number of operands, apply, and push the result.
- **No parentheses required** — operator precedence is encoded by the order.

### Conversion Algorithm (Infix → Postfix) — Shunting-Yard (Kernighan)
1. Initialize an empty operator stack.
2. Scan tokens left to right.
3. If token is an **operand** → append to output.
4. If token is an **operator** `op`:
   - While there is an operator `top` on stack with `precedence(top) >= precedence(op)` (and left-associative), pop `top` to output.
   - Push `op` onto stack.
5. If token is `(` → push onto stack.
6. If token is `)` → pop and append until `(` is encountered; discard both parentheses.
7. At end of input, pop all remaining operators to output.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Postfix

| Concept | Definition / Rule |
|---|---|
| Operand handling | Output immediately |
| Operator precedence (C convention) | `*`, `/`, `%` > `+`, `-` > `<<`, `>>` > `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` > `==`, `!=` > `&` > `^` > `\|` > `&&` > `\|\|` |
| Associativity | Most binary ops are **left-associative**; assignment is **right-associative** |
| Stack depth | Equals the max nesting of operands before an operator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Postfix is the **machine code of stack-based virtual machines** (JVM bytecode, .NET CIL, Forth). The conversion algorithm is a **classic 7-mark KTU question** every December/May cycle.

---

## 2.3 Representation #2 — Three-Address Code (TAC)

### Formal Definition
> A **Three-Address Code (TAC)** instruction is one in which **at most three addresses (operands)** appear — typically **two source operands** and **one destination operand**, with exactly **one operator** per instruction.

### Canonical General Form
$$
x = y \;\text{op}\; z
$$
where `x`, `y`, `z` are names (variables, temporaries, constants) and `op` is a binary operator.

### TAC Instruction Set (KTU-mandated list)

| Instruction Form | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `x = y op z` | Binary operation |
| `x = op y` | Unary operation (e.g., negation) |
| `x = y` | Copy |
| `goto L` | Unconditional jump to label `L` |
| `if x goto L` / `ifFalse x goto L` | Conditional jump |
| `if x relop y goto L` | Relational conditional |
| `param x` and `call p, n` | Procedure call (with `n` = #args) |
| `return y` | Return value |

### Why "Three-Address" — The Underlying Theorem
Any expression of arbitrary arity can be decomposed into a sequence of binary operations using **fresh temporaries** (T₁, T₂, ...). This is the **Sethi-Ullman labelling** principle (closely related). The number of temporaries needed equals the size of the DAG.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asked:** *Why not use one big expression?* — Because TAC exposes per-operation granularity, making **def-use chains** analyzable for optimization. This is the *core reason* TAC is the de-facto IR in GCC, LLVM, and academic compilers.

---

## 2.4 Representation #3 — Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) & Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

### Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
- A **hierarchical**, condensed parse tree in which:
  - Punctuation and noise tokens (parentheses, commas, `;`) are discarded.
  - Chains of single-child productions are flattened.
- Each **interior node = operator**; each **leaf = operand**.

### Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
- An AST in which **common subexpressions (CSE)** are *shared* via pointer reuse.
- A node `n` has **children** for operands; if the same subexpression appears twice, both parent nodes point to the **same** child node.
- Used to **identify and merge** redundant computation — the foundation of the *Common Subexpression Elimination* optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AST vs. Parse Tree:** AST is *abstracted* (no `ε`, no parens); Parse Tree is *concrete* (every grammar rule materialized). KTU repeatedly asks students to draw the AST — be precise with leaf labels and operator order.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — IR Storage Schemes for TAC

| Scheme | Tuple Structure | Naming | Optimization Friendliness | Space | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quadruples** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | Named (uses temporaries) | Best — easy reordering | Higher (one tuple per op) | ★★★ |
| **Triples** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | Positional — result = tuple index | Harder (changing order breaks refs) | Compact | ★★ |
| **Indirect Triples** | Two arrays: **instruction list** + **execution-order pointer list** | Positional but indirected | Easier than triples (reordering only updates pointer list) | Compact + flexible | ★★ |

> [!TIP]
> **Board trick question:** *"Why is optimization harder in triples than quadruples?"* — In triples, the result of a tuple `i` is referenced by index `i`; reordering or moving code invalidates these positional references. In quadruples, results are *named* (T₁, T₂, ...) and survive any reordering.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **LLVM IR (Low-Level Virtual Machine):** A typed, SSA-form, three-address representation used by Apple, Google, Intel, AMD, NVIDIA.
> - **JVM Bytecode:** Stack-based postfix — directly tied to Postfix IR theory.
> - **GCC GIMPLE:** Three-address, language-independent IR.
> - **Optimizing Compilers (HotSpot, V8, TurboFan):** All operate on a TAC-like IR.

Every modern compiler — from a student's `gcc -O2` to the *JavaScript JIT in your browser* — passes through an IR. **KTU tests this concept exhaustively in Module 3.**

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive** worked conversions required by KTU board examiners. Each algebraic step is explicit; nothing is left implicit.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Postfix Conversion of an Arithmetic Expression

**Source Expression:**
$$
E \;=\; (a + b) \;\cdot\; (c - d) \;+\; e \;\uparrow\; f
$$

**Operator Precedence (used):** `↑` (exponent, right-associative) > `*`, `/` > `+`, `-`.

### Step 1 — Token Stream
`(` `a` `+` `b` `)` `*` `(` `c` `-` `d` `)` `+` `e` `↑` `f`

### Step 2 — Shunting-Yard Trace

| Step | Token | Stack (top → right) | Output Buffer |
|------|-------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `a` | `(` | `a` |
| 3 | `+` | `(, +` | `a` |
| 4 | `b` | `(, +` | `a b` |
| 5 | `)` | empty (pop `+` to output, discard `(`) | `a b +` |
| 6 | `*` | `*` | `a b +` |
| 7 | `(` | `*, (` | `a b +` |
| 8 | `c` | `*, (` | `a b + c` |
| 9 | `-` | `*, (, -` | `a b + c` |
| 10 | `d` | `*, (, -` | `a b + c d` |
| 11 | `)` | `*` (pop `-`) | `a b + c d -` |
| 12 | `+` | `+` (pop `*` because `prec(*)=2` ≥ `prec(+)=1`) | `a b + c d - *` |
| 13 | `e` | `+` | `a b + c d - * e` |
| 14 | `↑` | `+, ↑` | `a b + c d - * e` |
| 15 | `f` | `+, ↑` | `a b + c d - * e f` |
| 16 | END | (pop all) | `a b + c d - * e f ↑ +` |

### Step 3 — Final Postfix

$$
\boxed{\,a\;b\;+\;c\;d\;-\;\ast\;e\;f\;\uparrow\;+\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack-depth check (KTU bonus):** The maximum stack size required to *evaluate* this postfix is **3** (push `a`, push `b`, pop+push result, push `c`, push `d`, pop+push, pop both+push product, push `e`, push `f`, pop+push `e↑f`, pop+push final).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — TAC, Quadruples, Triples for a Boolean + Arithmetic Mix

**Source Statement:**
$$
\texttt{if } (a > b) \;\&\&\; (c < d \;\|\; e == f) \texttt{ then } x = y + z;
$$

### Step 3.2.1 — TAC (Three-Address Code)

We must decompose every operator into a single binary TAC instruction. Introduce temporaries `T1`, `T2`, `T3`, `T4`, `T5`. Labels `L1`, `L2`, `L3` for control flow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{T1} = a > b \\
&\text{T2} = c < d \\
&\text{T3} = e == f \\
&\text{T4} = \text{T2} \;\|\; \text{T3} \\
&\text{T5} = \text{T1} \;\&\&\; \text{T4} \\
&\text{ifFalse T5 goto L1} \\
&\text{T6} = y + z \\
&x = \text{T6} \\
&\text{L1:}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why `ifFalse`?** KTU accepts both `ifFalse` and `if x goto L` — be consistent with your course notes. A common 7-mark question asks you to *re-target* the same TAC for *short-circuit vs. non-short-circuit* evaluation.

### Step 3.2.2 — Quadruple Representation

Each TAC line is a 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `>` | `a` | `b` | `T1` |
| (1) | `<` | `c` | `d` | `T2` |
| (2) | `==` | `e` | `f` | `T3` |
| (3) | `\|` | `T2` | `T3` | `T4` |
| (4) | `&&` | `T1` | `T4` | `T5` |
| (5) | `ifFalse` | `T5` | — | `L1` |
| (6) | `+` | `y` | `z` | `T6` |
| (7) | `=` | `T6` | — | `x` |

### Step 3.2.3 — Triple Representation

Positional — the result of instruction `i` is referenced by index `i`.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `>` | `a` | `b` |
| (1) | `<` | `c` | `d` |
| (2) | `==` | `e` | `f` |
| (3) | `\|` | (1) | (2) |
| (4) | `&&` | (0) | (3) |
| (5) | `ifFalse` | (4) | — |
| (6) | `+` | `y` | `z` |
| (7) | `=` | (6) | — |

### Step 3.2.4 — Indirect Triples

Two tables:

**Instruction table:**
| Idx | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `>` | `a` | `b` |
| 1 | `<` | `c` | `d` |
| 2 | `==` | `e` | `f` |
| 3 | `\|` | (1) | (2) |
| 4 | `&&` | (0) | (3) |
| 5 | `ifFalse` | (4) | — |
| 6 | `+` | `y` | `z` |
| 7 | `=` | (6) | — |

**Execution-order pointer list (initially identity):**

$$
(0) \rightarrow (1) \rightarrow (2) \rightarrow (3) \rightarrow (4) \rightarrow (5) \rightarrow (6) \rightarrow (7)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization example:** To *hoist* a later instruction forward, only the **pointer list** is reshuffled — the instruction table stays intact. KTU 2024 LOVES this point.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — AST and DAG with Common Subexpression

**Source Expression:**
$$
(a + b) \;\cdot\; (a + b) \;-\; c
$$

### Step 3.3.1 — Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

- Root: `-`
  - Left child: `*`
    - Left-left: `+`
      - Left-left-left: `a`
      - Left-left-right: `b`
    - Left-right: `+`
      - Left-right-left: `a`
      - Left-right-right: `b`
  - Right child: `c`

The subexpression `(a + b)` is **represented by two distinct `+` nodes**.

### Step 3.3.2 — Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

The subexpression `(a + b)` is **shared** — only **one** `+` node exists; both references of the multiplication point to it.

- Root: `-`
  - Left: `*`
    - Left operand: shared `+` node
      - Children: `a`, `b`
    - Right operand: **same** shared `+` node
  - Right: `c`

### Step 3.3.3 — Counting Nodes

| Form | Number of nodes | CSE detected? |
|---|---|---|
| AST | 7 (1 root `-`, 1 `*`, 2 `+`, 3 leaves) | No |
| DAG | 6 (1 `-`, 1 `*`, 1 `+`, 3 leaves) | **Yes** — by definition |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU tip:** When asked *"How many temporaries will TAC need?"*, the answer equals the number of *interior* nodes in the DAG. Here, **3** (`T1 = a + b`, `T2 = T1 * T1`, `T3 = T2 - c`).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision: When to Use What

| Source Construct | Recommended IR | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic expression evaluation | **TAC** | Linear, easy to optimize |
| Quick pattern-matching optimization | **DAG** | CSE is free |
| Stack-VM target (JVM, WASM) | **Postfix** | Native form |
| Program structure for high-level analysis | **AST** | Preserves hierarchy, scoping |
| Code generation (register allocation) | **Quadruples** | Named temporaries survive passes |
| Compact storage + reordering | **Indirect Triples** | Minimal move cost |
| Dense, no reordering | **Triples** | Smallest tuple |

---

```mermaid

    end
```

## 4.4 Postfix Evaluation — Stack State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S1: read a, push
    S1 --> S2: read b, push
    S2 --> S3: read +, pop b pop a, push (a+b)
    S3 --> S4: read c, push
    S4 --> S5: read d, push
    S5 --> S6: read -, pop d pop c, push (c-d)
    S6 --> S7: read *, pop (c-d) pop (a+b), push product
    S7 --> [*]: result on stack top
```
