# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> *What is an intermediate code? List any two benefits of using intermediate code in a compiler.*

**Model Answer (verbatim KTU key style):**
> Intermediate code is a machine-independent representation of the source program, generated after the semantic analysis phase and consumed by the code optimization and code generation phases.
>
> **Benefits:**
> (i) **Retargetability** — the same front-end can target multiple architectures by modifying only the back-end.
> (ii) **Machine-independent optimization** — optimizations like constant folding and dead code elimination are performed on the IR, not tied to any specific target.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
> *Differentiate between an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).*

**Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | AST | DAG |
> |---|---|---|
> | Node sharing | No — identical subexpressions are duplicated | Yes — common subexpressions share a single node |
> | Node count | Higher (no CSE) | Lower (CSE merged) |
> | Primary use | Semantic analysis, scoping | Common subexpression elimination |
> | Construction | From parse tree, dropping punctuation | From AST, merging equivalent nodes via hash table |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Postfix conversion trap:** Students forget that `↑` (exponent) is **right-associative** — the precedence-pop rule must invert for right-assoc ops. This is where **2–3 marks** are lost annually.
> 2. **Quadruples vs. Triples:** Students mix up the fields. Quadruples ALWAYS have a *named* result column; Triples NEVER do (result is the index).
> 3. **AST leaves:** Do NOT write grammar non-terminals on the leaves — only **tokens/identifiers/literals** belong on the leaves. Examiners deduct **1 mark per wrong leaf label**.
> 4. **DAG edges:** A common subexpression should show **two arrows** converging onto the shared node. Students often draw only one.
> 5. **Temporaries:** Use T₁, T₂, T₃, ... consistently. Reusing `T1` prematurely is a **deduction trigger**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Postfix + TAC

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**
> Consider the expression:
> $$
> x = (p \;\ast\; q) \;+\; (r \;-\; s)\;/\;(t \;+\; u\;\ast\; v)
> $$
> **(a)** Convert the expression to **postfix notation** using the Shunting-Yard algorithm. Show the stack state at every step. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Generate the **three-address code (TAC)** for the statement, then represent it as **quadruples**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Stack-trace table** (left = output, right = stack top at right):

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `p` | `(` | `p` |
| 3 | `*` | `(, *` | `p` |
| 4 | `q` | `(, *` | `p q` |
| 5 | `)` | (pop `*`) | `p q *` |
| 6 | `+` | `+` | `p q *` |
| 7 | `(` | `+, (` | `p q *` |
| 8 | `r` | `+, (` | `p q * r` |
| 9 | `-` | `+, (, -` | `p q * r` |
| 10 | `s` | `+, (, -` | `p q * r s` |
| 11 | `)` | `+` (pop `-`) | `p q * r s -` |
| 12 | `/` | `+, /` | `p q * r s -` |
| 13 | `(` | `+, /, (` | `p q * r s -` |
| 14 | `t` | `+, /, (` | `p q * r s - t` |
| 15 | `+` | `+, /, (, +` | `p q * r s - t` |
| 16 | `u` | `+, /, (, +` | `p q * r s - t u` |
| 17 | `*` | `+, /, (, +, *` | `p q * r s - t u` |
| 18 | `v` | `+, /, (, +, *` | `p q * r s - t u v` |
| 19 | `)` | `+, /` (pop `*`, pop `+`) | `p q * r s - t u v * +` |
| 20 | END | (pop `/`, pop `+`) | `p q * r s - t u v * + / +` |

$$
\boxed{\,p\;q\;\ast\;r\;s\;-\;t\;u\;v\;\ast\;+\;/\;+\,}
$$

**Max stack depth = 4** (steps 16–18) — *bonus half-mark in marking scheme.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Three-Address Code:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&T1 = p \ast q \\
&T2 = r - s \\
&T3 = u \ast v \\
&T4 = t + T3 \\
&T5 = T2 / T4 \\
&T6 = T1 + T5 \\
&x = T6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quadruples Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | `p` | `q` | `T1` |
| (1) | `-` | `r` | `s` | `T2` |
| (2) | `*` | `u` | `v` | `T3` |
| (3) | `+` | `t` | `T3` | `T4` |
| (4) | `/` | `T2` | `T4` | `T5` |
| (5) | `+` | `T1` | `T5` | `T6` |
| (6) | `=` | `T6` | — | `x` |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — DAG + AST + Storage Schemes

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**
> For the expression:
> $$
> (a \;\ast\; b) \;+\; (a \;\ast\; b)\;/\;(c - d) \;+\; (c - d)
> $$
> **(a)** Construct the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. Identify common subexpressions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Generate the **three-address code**, then represent it as **triples**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify subexpressions:**

- `E1 = a * b`  ← appears **twice** (CSE candidate)
- `E2 = c - d`  ← appears **twice** (CSE candidate)

**Step 2 — DAG construction (drawn top-down):**

- Root node: `+`
  - Left child: `+`
    - Left-left: `*` (E1)  → children: `a`, `b`
    - Left-right: `/`  → children: `*` (E1 — *shared*), `-` (E2 — *shared*)
  - Right child: `-` (E2) → children: `c`, `d`

**Mark CSE nodes**:
- The `*` node and the `-` node each have **two parents** — signature of CSE.

**Total interior nodes = 5** (1 `+` root, 1 `+`, 1 `/`, 1 `*`, 1 `-`); leaves = 4 (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Three-Address Code (4 temporaries):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&T1 = a \ast b \\
&T2 = c - d \\
&T3 = T1 / T2 \\
&T4 = T1 + T3 \\
&T5 = T4 + T2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Triples Table (result = index):**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | `a` | `b` |
| (1) | `-` | `c` | `d` |
| (2) | `/` | (0) | (1) |
| (3) | `+` | (0) | (2) |
| (4) | `+` | (3) | (1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is excellent pedagogy:** Notice that index (0) — `a*b` — is referenced *twice* (at (2) and (3)), and index (1) — `c-d` — is also referenced *twice* (at (2) and (4)). This **positional sharing** is exactly the DAG's information, encoded as index references.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (IR Representations):**

- ☐ **Intermediate code** = machine-independent, sits between front-end and back-end.
- ☐ Four canonical IRs to remember: **Postfix**, **AST**, **DAG**, **TAC**.
- ☐ **Postfix:** no parentheses; stack-based evaluation; Shunting-Yard for conversion. **Right-associative** operators (`↑`, `=`) invert the pop-condition.
- ☐ **AST:** hierarchical, condensed parse tree; interior nodes = operators, leaves = operands; **no** punctuation tokens.
- ☐ **DAG:** AST + **node sharing** for common subexpressions; foundation of *Common Subexpression Elimination*.
- ☐ **TAC:** general form `x = y op z`; each instruction has at most 1 operator; at most 3 addresses.
- ☐ **Quadruples:** `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` — **named** results, optimization-friendly.
- ☐ **Triples:** `(op, arg1, arg2)` — **positional** results, compact but reordering breaks references.
- ☐ **Indirect Triples:** two arrays — instruction table (immutable) + execution-order pointer list (reorderable). **Cheap optimization.**
- ☐ **Temporaries needed for TAC = number of interior nodes in the DAG.**
- ☐ **Max stack depth for postfix evaluation** = a frequent 1-mark bonus question.
- ☐ **JVM / CIL / WASM bytecodes** = real-world postfix IRs.
- ☐ **LLVM IR / GIMPLE** = real-world TACs.
- ☐ Remember the TAC instruction set: `x = y op z`, unary `x = op y`, copy, gotos, conditional jumps, `param`/`call`/`return`.
- ☐ For control flow: always use **labels** (`L1, L2, ...`) and `ifFalse` / `if x goto L`.
- ☐ When asked *"compare quadruples and triples"*, hit these four points: **naming, reordering, space, optimization**.

> **Final KTU Board Mantra:** *Draw clean. Label everything. Show the stack. Number every quadruple/triple. Use T₁, T₂, … consistently. Never confuse AST leaves with parse-tree non-terminals.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermediate Code for Assignment Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Code for Assignment Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Intermediate Code** is an abstract, machine-independent representation of a source program that sits between the high-level source language and the target machine code. For an **assignment statement**, it expresses the computation of the right-hand side (RHS) expression and the storage of the resulting value into the left-hand side (LHS) location, using a small, fixed set of primitive operations.

The most widely adopted form is **Three-Address Code (TAC)**, in which every instruction involves at most three operands — typically of the form:

$$x = y \ \ op \ \ z$$

where `x` is the result, `y` and `z` are operands (variables, constants, or temporaries), and `op` is a binary operator. Unary operations and copy assignments use the degenerate form `x = op y` and `x = y` respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The four canonical representations of three-address code are **(i) Quadruples, (ii) Triples, (iii) Indirect Triples, and (iv) Postfix Notation**. Students must be able to convert *any* assignment statement into all of these forms for the ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator"

Imagine a travel guide who speaks Malayalam, Hindi, and English, but neither you nor your destination friend speak each other's tongue. The guide listens to your Malayalam (high-level source), mentally translates it into a neutral, abstract idea (intermediate code), and then re-expresses it in English (target machine code). 

In this analogy:
- **Malayalam** = source language (`a = b + c * d`)
- **Neutral idea** = three-address code (`t1 = c * d; t2 = b + t1; a = t2`)
- **English** = x86 / ARM / RISC-V machine code

The "neutral idea" is platform-independent — the same TAC can be re-targeted to any CPU, much like the same notion can be spoken in any language.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is intermediate code necessary?** A direct front-end → back-end translation would require `(n × m)` translators for `n` source languages and `m` target machines. With an intermediate form, only `n + m` translators are needed. This is the **retargeting** advantage of compilers like LLVM and GCC.

### 1.3 Forms of Intermediate Representation (IR)

| IR Form | Structure | Key Property |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| **Postfix / Reverse Polish** | Operand stack form | No parentheses needed |
| **Syntax Tree** | Hierarchical tree | Visual & structural |
| **DAG** | Directed Acyclic Graph | Identifies common subexpressions |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Linear sequence of 3-operand instructions | Most common, machine-near |
| **Quadruples** | Record `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | Easy to optimize |
| **Triples** | Record `(op, arg1, arg2)` referencing other triples | Compact, no temporaries |
| **Indirect Triples** | List of pointers to triples | Easier reordering for optimization |

### 1.4 Assignment Statement — Categories in KTU Syllabus

An assignment statement can take any of the following shapes, all of which must translate cleanly into TAC:

1. **Simple assignment:** `a = b`
2. **Arithmetic assignment:** `a = b + c * d - e / f`
3. **Array-element assignment:** `a[i] = b + c` (address arithmetic)
4. **Pointer assignment:** `*p = q` or `p = &x`
5. **Boolean / conditional translation:** `a = b < c` (relational → boolean)
6. **Procedure-call assignment:** `a = foo(x, y)`

The translation algorithm must handle all of these by breaking the RHS into atomic three-address operations, introducing **synthesized temporaries** (`t1, t2, t3, …`) as needed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Translation Principle — SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation)

Intermediate code for an assignment statement is produced by an **L-attributed syntax-directed definition (SDD)** attached to a bottom-up parser. The relevant grammar is:

$$stmt \rightarrow id = E$$
$$E \rightarrow E + T \ \vert \ E - T \ \vert \ T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \ \vert \ T / F \ \vert \ F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \ \vert \ id \ \vert \ num$$

Each grammar symbol is augmented with a **synthesized attribute** `place` (or `addr`) that names a location holding the value of that subexpression. The semantic rules emit three-address instructions to compute the subexpression's value into a fresh temporary if it is not already in a named variable.

### 2.2 Core Semantic Rules (Step-by-Step Logic)

Let `newtemp()` return a fresh name `t1, t2, t3, …` each time, and `emit(...)` append a TAC instruction to the output stream.

**Rule 1 — Assignment statement:**
$$S \rightarrow id = E$$
**Emit:** `gen(id.place = E.place)`

**Logic:** The RHS expression's value, held in the location `E.place`, is assigned to the LHS identifier's location `id.place`. No new temporary is needed because the value already lives somewhere.

**Rule 2 — Addition / Subtraction (left-associative):**
$$E \rightarrow E_1 + T$$
- Let `E_1.place = E.place` (re-use the left operand's location if it is a temporary)
- Otherwise call `t = newtemp()`, emit `t = E_1.place + T.place`, set `E.place = t`

**Why this optimization?** It avoids generating `t1 = a; t2 = t1 + b; E.place = t2` when `a` is already a temporary — we simply *reuse* it. This is the **TAC optimization trick** KTU examiners frequently test.

**Rule 3 — Multiplication / Division:**
$$T \rightarrow T_1 * F$$
- Same re-use logic: if `T_1.place` is a temporary, set `T.place = T_1.place` and emit `T.place = T.place * F.place`
- Else: `t = newtemp(); emit(t = T_1.place * F.place); T.place = t`

**Rule 4 — Identifier / Number (terminals):**
$$F \rightarrow id \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.place = id.place$$
$$F \rightarrow num \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.place = num.lexval \text{ (the constant itself)}$$

**Rule 5 — Parenthesized expression:**
$$F \rightarrow (E) \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.place = E.place$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Address Calculations for Array Assignments

For a one-dimensional array `a` with elements of width `w` bytes, starting at base address `base_a`, the address of element `a[i]` is:

$$\text{addr}(a[i]) = \text{base}_a + i \times w$$

For a two-dimensional array declared as `a[low_1 .. high_1][low_2 .. high_2]` (row-major, as in C), the address of `a[i_1][i_2]` is:

$$\text{addr}(a[i_1][i_2]) = \text{base} + ((i_1 - \text{low}_1) \times n_2 + (i_2 - \text{low}_2)) \times w$$

where $n_2 = \text{high}_2 - \text{low}_2 + 1$ is the row length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This address-arithmetic formula is exactly what C compilers emit when generating pointer arithmetic for `&a[i]`, `*(a+i)`, or subscript expressions. It is the bridge between the symbolic source program and the raw memory layout the CPU actually uses.

### 2.4 Quick-Reference Comparison Table — IR Representations

| Property | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples | TAC (textual) |
|----------|------------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| **Record fields** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | pointer to triple | Full instruction text |
| **Number of temporaries introduced** | Explicit `t1, t2, …` | None — references other triple indices | None — references via pointer | Explicit `t1, t2, …` |
| **Compactness** | Less compact | More compact | Most compact | Least compact |
| **Optimization ease** | Easy (move instructions freely) | Hard (must update references) | Easy (only update pointer list) | Easy |
| **Typical KTU marks weight** | 7 marks (full problem) | 3–4 marks (conversion) | 2–3 marks (bonus) | 7 marks (full problem) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The intermediate code generator is the **heart of any production compiler**:

- **GCC** uses GIMPLE and later RTL (Register Transfer Language) as its IR.
- **LLVM** uses the LLVM IR, which is essentially typed three-address code.
- **JVM** uses bytecode — a stack-based IR with implicit three-address semantics.
- **.NET CLR** uses CIL (Common Intermediate Language).

Every optimization pass — constant folding, dead-code elimination, loop-invariant code motion, register allocation — operates on this IR, **not** on the original source. This is why mastering assignment-statement translation is foundational for compiler engineering careers in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Arithmetic Assignment

**Source statement:**
$$a = b + c \times d - e / f$$

**Step 1 — Build the syntax tree (and DAG) from the parsed expression.**

The expression is parsed bottom-up following the grammar, yielding the parse tree rooted at `=` whose RHS is a subtraction whose left child is an addition and right child is a division. The DAG merges common subexpressions if any (here none).

**Step 2 — Apply the semantic rules bottom-up (post-order).**

- `c × d` → introduce `t1`, emit `t1 = c * d`
- `b + t1` → introduce `t2`, emit `t2 = b + t1`
- `e / f` → introduce `t3`, emit `t3 = e / f`
- `t2 - t3` → introduce `t4`, emit `t4 = t2 - t3`
- `a = t4` → emit `a = t4`

**Step 3 — Final Three-Address Code (TAC).**

```
t1 = c * d
t2 = b + t1
t3 = e / f
t4 = t2 - t3
a = t4
```

**Step 4 — Quadruple Representation.**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `*` | `c` | `d` | `t1` |
| (1) | `+` | `b` | `t1` | `t2` |
| (2) | `/` | `e` | `f` | `t3` |
| (3) | `-` | `t2` | `t3` | `t4` |
| (4) | `=` | `t4` | `_` | `a` |

**Step 5 — Triple Representation.**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| (0) | `*` | `c` | `d` |
| (1) | `+` | `b` | `(0)` |
| (2) | `/` | `e` | `f` |
| (3) | `-` | `(1)` | `(2)` |
| (4) | `=` | `a` | `(3)` |

Notice that triples replace temporary names with the *index* of the producing triple — saving storage at the cost of harder code motion.

**Step 6 — Indirect Triple Representation.**

The *instruction list* is a sequence of pointers to the actual triples:

| # | pointer |
|---|---------|
| (0) | → (0) `* c d` |
| (1) | → (1) `+ b (0)` |
| (2) | → (2) `/ e f` |
| (3) | → (3) `- (1) (2)` |
| (4) | → (4) `= a (3)` |

To optimize, the compiler rearranges the *pointer list* without touching the underlying triples — a major advantage during code motion.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Array Assignment

**Source statement:**
$$x = a[i] + b$$

Assume `a` is an integer array with element width `w = 4` bytes, base address held in the symbol-table entry for `a` as a constant or pseudo-register `base_a`.

**Step 1 — Compute the address of `a[i]`.**

Per Section 2.3, the address is `base_a + i × w`. Translate as:

```
t1 = i * 4
t2 = base_a + t1
t3 = *t2        // dereference to load value of a[i]
t4 = t3 + b
x  = t4
```

**Step 2 — Quadruples for the array load.**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `*` | `i` | `4` | `t1` |
| (1) | `+` | `base_a` | `t1` | `t2` |
| (2) | `=[]` | `t2` | `_` | `t3` |
| (3) | `+` | `t3` | `b` | `t4` |
| (4) | `=` | `t4` | `_` | `x` |

> [!NOTE]
> The operator `=[]` denotes an **array-index load**. Some textbooks split it into `[]=` for store and `=[]` for load. Both are valid; KTU accepts either as long as semantics are clearly stated.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Store into Array Element

**Source statement:**
$$a[i] = b \times c - d$$

**Step 1 — Compute RHS value into a temporary.**

```
t1 = b * c
t2 = t1 - d
```

**Step 2 — Compute destination address.**

```
t3 = i * 4
t4 = base_a + t3
```

**Step 3 — Store the value.**

```
*t4 = t2
```

**Step 4 — Compact TAC listing.**

```
t1 = b * c
t2 = t1 - d
t3 = i * 4
t4 = base_a + t3
*t4 = t2
```

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Boolean / Conditional Assignment

**Source statement:**
$$x = (a < b) \ \text{AND}\ (c > d)$$

**Step 1 — Short-circuit translation using jumps.**

For a boolean assignment, the typical KTU-style approach produces jumps rather than storing 0/1. The translation:

```
t1 = a < b
if_false t1 goto L1
t2 = c > d
if_false t2 goto L1
t1 = 1
goto L2
L1: t1 = 0
L2: x = t1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often emit `t1 = a < b` as if it directly returned 0/1 stored in a single instruction. While logically valid, the **jump-based form** above is the canonical "three-address" representation for booleans and is what KTU's model answer scripts expect.

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Reuse-Temporary Optimization (L-value reuse)

**Source statement:**
$$a = b + c$$

**Naïve (non-optimized) TAC:**

```
t1 = b
t2 = t1 + c
a = t2
```

**Optimized TAC (using the L-attributed re-use rule from Section 2.2):**

```
t1 = b + c
a = t1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The optimization stems from the rule: if the left operand of `+` is already a temporary, *reuse it* as the destination. This is a **local TAC optimization** worth **2 bonus marks** in KTU valuation.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Conversion At a Glance

| Source Expression | TAC (with reuse) | # of Quadruples | # of Triples |
|------------------|------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| `a = b` | `a = b` | 1 | 1 |
| `a = b + c` | `t1 = b + c; a = t1` | 2 | 2 |
| `a = b + c × d` | `t1 = c * d; t2 = b + t1; a = t2` | 3 | 3 |
| `a = b + c × d - e / f` | `t1 = c * d; t2 = b + t1; t3 = e / f; t4 = t2 - t3; a = t4` | 5 | 5 |
| `a[i] = b` (int, w=4) | `t1 = i*4; t2 = base + t1; *t2 = b` | 3 | 3 |
| `x = a < b` | `if a < b goto L1; t1 = 0; goto L2; L1: t1 = 1; L2: x = t1` | 6 | 6 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compiler Pipeline — Where Intermediate Code Lives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Program] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer]
    LEX --> TOK[Token Stream]
    TOK --> PARSE[Syntax Analyzer]
    PARSE --> ST[Syntax Tree]
    ST --> SEM[Semantic Analyzer]
    SEM --> AST[Annotated AST]
    AST --> ICG[Intermediate Code Generator]
    ICG --> IR[TAC Quadruples Triples]
    IR --> OPT[Optimizer]
    OPT --> MIR[Optimized IR]
    MIR --> CG[Target Code Generator]
    CG --> MCODE[Machine Code]
```

### 4.2 Translation Flow for `a = b + c * d`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: S to id equals E] --> B[Parse RHS: E to E plus T]
    B --> C[Parse T to T star F]
    C --> D[F to id: c]
    D --> E[F to id: d]
    E --> F["Emit: t1 = c * d"]
    F --> G[Return t1 as T place]
    G --> H[F to id: b]
    H --> I["Emit: t2 = b + t1"]
    I --> J[Return t2 as E place]
    J --> K["Emit: a = t2"]
    K --> L[Done]
```

### 4.3 Quadruple Record Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0["Quad 0: op=star arg1=c arg2=d result=t1"] --> Q1
    Q1["Quad 1: op=plus arg1=b arg2=t1 result=t2"] --> Q2
    Q2["Quad 2: op=minus arg1=t2 arg2=t3 result=t4"] --> Q3
    Q3["Quad 3: op=assign arg1=t4 arg2=nil result=a"]
```

### 4.4 Triple Reference Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["Triple 0: star c d"] --> T1
    T1["Triple 1: plus b ref0"] --> T3
    T2["Triple 2: div e f"] --> T3
    T3["Triple 3: minus ref1 ref2"] --> T4
    T4["Triple 4: assign a ref3"]
```

### 4.5 Indirect Triple — Pointer List Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Pointer List index 0"] --> T0
    P1["Pointer List index 1"] --> T1
    P2["Pointer List index 2"] --> T2
    P3["Pointer List index 3"] --> T3
    P4["Pointer List index 4"] --> T4
    T0["Triple Pool: star c d"]
    T1["Triple Pool: plus b ref0"]
    T2["Triple Pool: div e f"]
    T3["Triple Pool: minus ref1 ref2"]
    T4["Triple Pool: assign a ref3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the pointer list matters:** During optimization, the compiler may want to move the execution order of operations. With **direct triples**, this forces renaming of all references. With **indirect triples**, only the pointer list is reordered — the underlying triples are untouched. This is why indirect triples are preferred in production compilers like early Pascal compilers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — 3 Mark Questions**

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is three-address code? Why is it called "three-address"? List its representations.**

**Model Answer:**

Three-Address Code (TAC) is an intermediate representation of a source program in which each instruction contains at most **three addresses** — two for the operands and one for the result. It is called "three-address" because every instruction has the general form `x = y op z`, involving three address fields.

> The four common representations of TAC are:
> 1. **Quadruples** — `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`
> 2. **Triples** — `(op, arg1, arg2)` with operand positions referencing other triples by index
> 3. **Indirect Triples** — list of pointers to a triple pool
> 4. **Postfix Notation** — stack-based operand order

TAC simplifies the compiler design by providing a uniform, machine-independent form that can be optimized before final code generation.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between quadruples and triples with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Quadruples | Triples |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| Fields per record | 4 (`op, arg1, arg2, result`) | 3 (`op, arg1, arg2`) |
| Temporary naming | Explicit (`t1, t2, …`) | Implicit (by reference) |
| Code movement ease | Easy | Difficult |
| Storage | Higher | Lower |

**Example for `x = a + b * c`:**

Quadruples:
| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| 0 | `*` | `b` | `c` | `t1` |
| 1 | `+` | `a` | `t1` | `t2` |
| 2 | `=` | `t2` | `_` | `x` |

Triples:
| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| 0 | `*` | `b` | `c` |
| 1 | `+` | `a` | `(0)` |
| 2 | `=` | `x` | `(1)` |

In quadruples, each temporary is named explicitly. In triples, the result of triple (0) is referenced as `(0)` in triple (1), eliminating the need for temporary names.

---

### **Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Translate the following assignment statement into three-address code, quadruples, and triples. Show all intermediate steps. Use temporary reuse optimization where applicable.**

$$a = (b + c) \times d - e / (f - g)$$

#### **Part (a) — Three-Address Code (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

Following the syntax-directed translation with L-attributed re-use:

**Step 1:** Evaluate `f - g` first (parenthesized sub-expression):
```
t1 = f - g
```

**Step 2:** Evaluate `e / (f - g)`:
```
t2 = e / t1
```

**Step 3:** Evaluate `b + c`:
```
t3 = b + c
```

**Step 4:** Evaluate `(b + c) * d`:
```
t4 = t3 * d
```

**Step 5:** Final subtraction and assignment:
```
t5 = t4 - t2
a  = t5
```

**Final TAC listing:**
```
t1 = f - g
t2 = e / t1
t3 = b + c
t4 = t3 * d
t5 = t4 - t2
a  = t5
```

#### **Part (b) — Quadruples and Triples (7 marks)**

**Quadruple Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `-` | `f` | `g` | `t1` |
| (1) | `/` | `e` | `t1` | `t2` |
| (2) | `+` | `b` | `c` | `t3` |
| (3) | `*` | `t3` | `d` | `t4` |
| (4) | `-` | `t4` | `t2` | `t5` |
| (5) | `=` | `t5` | `_` | `a` |

**Triple Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| (0) | `-` | `f` | `g` |
| (1) | `/` | `e` | `(0)` |
| (2) | `+` | `b` | `c` |
| (3) | `*` | `(2)` | `d` |
| (4) | `-` | `(3)` | `(1)` |
| (5) | `=` | `a` | `(4)` |

**Indirect Triple Pointer List:**

| # | Points to |
|---|-----------|
| (0) | Triple (0) |
| (1) | Triple (1) |
| (2) | Triple (2) |
| (3) | Triple (3) |
| (4) | Triple (4) |
| (5) | Triple (5) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**For the following source program fragment, generate the three-address code, then represent it as quadruples and triples. Also show the syntax tree.**

```c
x = a[i] + b * (c - d);
y = a[i] - c;
```

Assume array `a` has element width `w = 4` bytes and base address `base_a`.

#### **Part (a) — Syntax Tree and TAC for `x = a[i] + b * (c - d)` (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Syntax Tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["equals: x"] --> PLUS["plus"]
    ROOT --> AR1["arrayref: a index i"]
    PLUS --> STAR["star"]
    PLUS --> B["id: b"]
    STAR --> MINUS["minus"]
    MINUS --> C["id: c"]
    MINUS --> D["id: d"]
```

**Three-Address Code:**

```
t1 = i * 4
t2 = base_a + t1
t3 = *t2          // load a[i]
t4 = c - d
t5 = b * t4
t6 = t3 + t5
x  = t6
```

#### **Part (b) — TAC for `y = a[i] - c` + Quadruples and Triples for both statements (7 marks)**

**Three-Address Code for second statement (note: `a[i]` re-computed — KTU tests whether students realize the re-use opportunity):**

```
t7  = i * 4
t8  = base_a + t7
t9  = *t8
t10 = t9 - c
y   = t10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Insight (2 bonus marks):** A real compiler would *common-subexpression-eliminate* the two `a[i]` loads, computing `a[i]` once and re-using. KTU valuation awards bonus marks for mentioning this.

**Combined Quadruple Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `*` | `i` | `4` | `t1` |
| (1) | `+` | `base_a` | `t1` | `t2` |
| (2) | `=[]` | `t2` | `_` | `t3` |
| (3) | `-` | `c` | `d` | `t4` |
| (4) | `*` | `b` | `t4` | `t5` |
| (5) | `+` | `t3` | `t5` | `t6` |
| (6) | `=` | `t6` | `_` | `x` |
| (7) | `*` | `i` | `4` | `t7` |
| (8) | `+` | `base_a` | `t7` | `t8` |
| (9) | `=[]` | `t8` | `_` | `t9` |
| (10) | `-` | `t9` | `c` | `t10` |
| (11) | `=` | `t10` | `_` | `y` |

**Combined Triple Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| (0) | `*` | `i` | `4` |
| (1) | `+` | `base_a` | `(0)` |
| (2) | `=[]` | `(1)` | `_` |
| (3) | `-` | `c` | `d` |
| (4) | `*` | `b` | `(3)` |
| (5) | `+` | `(2)` | `(4)` |
| (6) | `=` | `x` | `(5)` |
| (7) | `*` | `i` | `4` |
| (8) | `+` | `base_a` | `(7)` |
| (9) | `=[]` | `(8)` | `_` |
| (10) | `-` | `(9)` | `c` |
| (11) | `=` | `y` | `(10)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting array address arithmetic:** When the source contains `a[i]`, many students emit a single TAC like `t1 = a[i]` without showing the `i * w` and `base + offset` steps. **This loses 2–3 marks.** Always expand array subscripts.
> 2. **Wrong operator precedence:** For `b + c * d`, the *multiplication* must be computed first. Students who emit `t1 = b + c; t2 = t1 * d` lose full marks.
> 3. **Triples: using variable names instead of indices:** In triple tables, the result of a prior triple is referenced as `(0)`, `(1)`, etc. — not by temporary name. Mixing both forms is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Skipping the parenthesized sub-expression:** For `a = b * (c - d)`, students sometimes emit `t1 = c - d; t2 = b * t1` but forget the parentheses force evaluation order. Always state *why* the sub-expression is evaluated first.
> 5. **Boolean assignment — wrong control flow:** Emitting `x = a < b` without jumps is acceptable *only* if the question allows it. Otherwise, use the canonical `if a < b goto L1; …` form.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is the most common IR; each instruction has the form `x = y op z` and uses at most three addresses.
- ✅ **Quadruples** store `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`; easy to optimize; introduce explicit temporaries.
- ✅ **Triples** store `(op, arg1, arg2)`; reference prior results by index `(0)`, `(1)`, …; compact but harder to move.
- ✅ **Indirect Triples** use a pointer list into a triple pool — best of both worlds for optimization.
- ✅ **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** with `place` (or `addr`) attribute produces TAC bottom-up.
- ✅ **Temporary Reuse Optimization:** If the left operand of a binary op is itself a temporary, *reuse* it as the destination — never emit redundant `t1 = x; t2 = t1 op y`.
- ✅ **Array Address Formula (1-D):** `addr(a[i]) = base_a + i × w`, where `w` is the element width in bytes (typically **4** for `int`).
- ✅ **Array Address Formula (2-D, row-major):** `addr(a[i][j]) = base + ((i - low_1) × n_2 + (j - low_2)) × w`.
- ✅ **Boolean Assignment Translation:** Use `if a op b goto L_true; t = 0; goto L_end; L_true: t = 1; L_end:` — short-circuit jumps are canonical.
- ✅ **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** If the same sub-expression (e.g., `a[i]`) appears multiple times, compute once and re-use. Mention this for bonus marks.
- ✅ **Order of evaluation** in TAC must respect operator precedence and parentheses — multiplication/division before addition/subtraction, left-to-right associativity.
- ✅ **KTU 14-mark problems** typically demand: (i) TAC, (ii) Quadruples, (iii) Triples — all three for the same statement.
- ✅ **KTU 3-mark questions** usually test definitions, comparisons, or single-form conversions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Sources of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principal Sources of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Optimization** is the final phase of a standard KTU-prescribed compiler design pipeline (Phases of Compiler) where the intermediate code (typically **Three-Address Code / TAC**) is transformed into a semantically equivalent but computationally superior form, with the goals of minimizing execution time, reducing memory footprint, and lowering power consumption — **without altering the program's observable output behavior**.

The **Principal Sources of Optimization** are the canonical, mutually-recognised transformation classes taught in the KTU 2024 *PEITT631 – Compiler Design* syllabus under **Module 4**. They are the "toolkit" from which the optimization phase selects techniques to enhance the target code.

These principal sources are broadly classified into two super-categories:

| Super-Category | Description | KTU Weightage Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine-Independent Optimizations** | Transformations applied on the **Intermediate Representation (IR)**. Portable across target architectures. | Highest in ESE questions |
| **Machine-Dependent Optimizations** | Transformations applied on the **target machine code**, exploiting register, pipeline, and cache features. | Moderate weightage |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line:**
> Imagine a car manufacturing plant. The raw car parts arrive, get assembled, and roll off the line. A quality engineer reviews the **assembly instructions (Intermediate Code)** and asks:
> - *"Can I weld two identical parts at the same station instead of repeating the welding step?"* → **Common Subexpression Elimination**.
> - *"Is the worker walking 50 meters to fetch a tool he uses in every cycle? Let me keep the tool beside him."* → **Code Motion / Loop-Invariant Code Motion**.
> - *"Multiplying by 2 is slower than shifting left by 1. Use the shift."* → **Strength Reduction**.
> - *"This worker has nothing to do in the last 5 minutes of his shift. Let me eliminate his station."* → **Dead Code Elimination**.

Every optimization in a compiler is essentially a smart factory-floor re-arrangement that **saves time and resources without changing the final product**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Basic Block (BB):** A maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves at the end, with no halt except possibly at the end.
> - **Flow Graph:** A directed graph $G = (N, E)$ whose nodes are basic blocks and whose edges represent control flow.
> - **Data-Flow Analysis:** A static analysis technique that gathers information about the possible set of values computed at various points in a program.
> - **Local Optimization:** Applied to a single basic block in isolation.
> - **Global Optimization:** Applied across multiple basic blocks using control-flow analysis.
> - **Peephole Optimization:** Examines a short sliding window (the "peephole") of target instructions and replaces suboptimal patterns.

## 1.4 The KTU 2024 Schematic Placement

$$
\text{Source Code} \rightarrow \text{Lexical} \rightarrow \text{Syntax} \rightarrow \text{Semantic} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Code Gen} \rightarrow \boxed{\textbf{Optimization Phase}} \rightarrow \text{Target Code Gen}
$$

The optimization phase sits **between Intermediate Code Generation and Target Code Generation**, making it the single most powerful lever for performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Principal Sources of Optimization

The KTU 2024 syllabus enumerates the following **seven principal sources**, organised as a hierarchy:

### A. Function-Preserving Transformations
These are the **safest** optimizations because they are guaranteed to preserve the program's semantics. They are typically machine-independent.

1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. **Copy Propagation**
3. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
4. **Constant Folding**
5. **Constant Propagation**
6. **Algebraic Simplification / Identity Elimination**

### B. Loop Optimizations
Specialized transformations targeting the hot regions (typically inner loops), which dominate execution time.

7. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**
8. **Induction Variable Elimination (IVE)**
9. **Strength Reduction (SR)**
10. **Loop Unrolling**
11. **Loop Jamming / Loop Fusion**

### C. Local & Global Optimizations
Defined by **scope of analysis**.

12. **Local Optimizations** — within a single basic block.
13. **Global Optimizations** — across basic blocks using data-flow analysis.

### D. Peephole Optimizations
Short, sliding-window transformations on the final target code.

14. Redundant `MOV` elimination, jump-chain shortening, multiplication-by-shift, etc.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
- **Why:** Repeated evaluation of the same expression wastes CPU cycles.
- **How:** The compiler identifies expressions $e_1$ and $e_2$ that compute the same value (using available-expressions analysis), then replaces $e_2$ with the temporary holding $e_1$.

### 2.2.2 Copy Propagation
- **Why:** After a copy `x = y`, subsequent uses of `x` can be replaced by `y` to expose further optimizations (especially DCE).
- **How:** For every use of `x` where `x`'s only definition reaching that use is the copy `x = y`, replace `x` with `y` and eliminate the copy if `x` has no other uses.

### 2.2.3 Dead Code Elimination (DCE)
- **Why:** Variables defined but never read consume register/memory resources.
- **How:** A variable is **dead** if no subsequent instruction uses its value. The live-variable analysis computes `use` and `def` sets; a definition is removed if its result is not in any `use` set.

### 2.2.4 Constant Folding & Constant Propagation
- **Why:** Arithmetic on compile-time constants can be evaluated at compile time, and known values can be substituted.
- **How:** The compiler evaluates expressions whose operands are all literals; subsequent uses of those literals are propagated forward.

### 2.2.5 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)
- **Why:** Code inside a loop that does not depend on the loop variable executes redundantly on every iteration.
- **How:** An expression $e$ is **loop-invariant** if all its operands are either constants or have all their definitions outside the loop. LICM hoists $e$ to a pre-header block.

### 2.2.6 Induction Variable Elimination (IVE)
- **Why:** A variable $i$ that changes by a constant amount on each loop iteration is an **induction variable**. Often, multiple such variables track the same progression.
- **How:** When two induction variables $i, j$ satisfy $j = c_1 \cdot i + c_2$, the compiler can eliminate $j$ entirely and substitute $c_1 \cdot i + c_2$ at every use.

### 2.2.7 Strength Reduction (SR)
- **Why:** Multiplication is costlier (in cycles) than addition; exponentiation is costlier than multiplication.
- **How:** Replace $i \cdot c$ with repeated addition; replace $i^2$ with $i \cdot i$ (often a hardware-supported single-cycle op).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Source / Technique | Mathematical / Logical Form | Triggering Condition | Effect on Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Common Subexpression Elimination | $t_1 = a + b;\; t_2 = a + b \;\Rightarrow\; t_2 = t_1$ | Available-Expr analysis gives $a+b$ available at $t_2$ | $\downarrow$ redundant ops |
| 2 | Copy Propagation | $x = y;\; \ldots\; z = x + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; z = y + 1$ | $x$ has only one reaching def `x=y` and $x \neq y$ | Enables DCE |
| 3 | Dead Code Elimination | Remove any def with no live use | Live-Variable: $v \notin \text{use}[B]$ | $\downarrow$ register pressure |
| 4 | Constant Folding | $x = 3 + 4 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 7$ | Both operands are literals | $\downarrow$ runtime arith |
| 5 | Constant Propagation | $x = 5;\; y = x + 2 \;\Rightarrow\; y = 7$ | $x$ has constant reaching def | Enables folding |
| 6 | Loop-Invariant Code Motion | Hoist $e$ out of loop if $\text{operands}(e)$ are loop-invariant | $e$ computed $k$ times $\rightarrow$ computed once | $\downarrow$ exec by factor $k$ |
| 7 | Induction Variable Elimination | $j = a \cdot i + b$ substituted everywhere | $j, i$ are IVs with linear relation | $\downarrow$ multiplications |
| 8 | Strength Reduction | $i \cdot c \;\Rightarrow\; t = t + c$ per iteration | Inside a loop, recurrence form | Multi-cycle $\rightarrow$ 1-cycle |
| 9 | Loop Unrolling | Repeat body $n$ times, adjust step | Known trip count or small $n$ | $\downarrow$ branch overhead |
| 10 | Loop Fusion | $(L_1; L_2)$ on same range $\rightarrow$ single loop | Adjacent loops, same index space | $\downarrow$ loop overhead |
| 11 | Peephole (redundant MOV) | $\text{MOV } R_1, R_2;\; \text{MOV } R_2, R_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NOP}$ | Sliding window of 2–5 instr. | $\downarrow$ instr. count |
| 12 | Algebraic Simplification | $x \cdot 1 = x,\; x + 0 = x,\; x^2 = x \cdot x$ | Pattern match on IR | $\downarrow$ op count |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Industry Compilers:** LLVM, GCC, MSVC, and Intel ICC perform **15+ optimization passes** drawn from the above sources. For example, LLVM's `-O2` flag activates LICM, CSE, SR, IVE, and DCE in a fixed pipeline.
> - **JIT Compilers:** The HotSpot JVM applies loop unrolling and LICM at runtime once a method becomes "hot".
> - **GPU / DSP compilers:** Strength reduction and loop unrolling are critical for SIMD/SIMT pipelines (NVIDIA NVCC, TI C6000).
> - **Embedded systems:** Aggressive DCE and constant folding reduce code size, fitting firmware in limited flash memory (typical KTU ECE/EEE project boards).

## 2.5 Safety / Correctness Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Preservation of Semantics):** Every principal source of optimization listed above is **function-preserving**, meaning for any input $I$, the optimized program $P'$ satisfies $P'(I) = P(I)$. This guarantee comes from the underlying data-flow analyses (reaching definitions, available expressions, live variables) being conservative over-approximations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Common Subexpression Elimination + Copy Propagation + DCE

### Source TAC (Input)
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_3 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_4 &= b \,[\, t_3 \,] \\
t_5 &= t_2 \cdot t_4 \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_6 &= t_2 \cdot 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the Repeated Expression
Expressions $t_1 = 4 \cdot i$ and $t_3 = 4 \cdot i$ are syntactically and operationally identical. From the **Available Expressions** analysis, the value $4 \cdot i$ is available at the point of $t_3$ (it was computed in $t_1$ with no redefinition of $i$ in between).

### Step 2 — Apply CSE
Replace $t_3 = 4 \cdot i$ with $t_3 = t_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_3 &= t_1 \\
t_4 &= b \,[\, t_3 \,] \\
t_5 &= t_2 \cdot t_4 \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_6 &= t_2 \cdot 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Copy Propagation
$t_3 = t_1$ is a pure copy. Substitute every use of $t_3$ with $t_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_4 &= b \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_5 &= t_2 \cdot t_4 \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_6 &= t_2 \cdot 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply DCE
The intermediate line $t_3 = t_1$ has been eliminated; all redundant defs are gone. (Further DCE may remove the second $t_2$ redefinition if its result is unused.)

**Net Result:** 7 instructions reduced to **6 instructions** with one fewer multiplication.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Strength Reduction on an Induction Variable

### Source Loop TAC
$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0 \\
t_1 &= i \cdot 4 \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
i &= i + 1 \\
\text{if } &i < n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the Induction Variable
$t_1 = i \cdot 4$ varies with $i$. The recurrence form of $i$ is $i_{k+1} = i_k + 1$.

### Step 2 — Derive the Linear Recurrence for $t_1$
At iteration $k$:
$$
t_1^{(k)} = i^{(k)} \cdot 4
$$
At iteration $k+1$:
$$
t_1^{(k+1)} = i^{(k+1)} \cdot 4 = (i^{(k)} + 1) \cdot 4 = i^{(k)} \cdot 4 + 4 = t_1^{(k)} + 4
$$

### Step 3 — Replace Multiplication with Addition (Strength Reduction)
Substitute the original $t_1 = i \cdot 4$ with a maintained recurrence variable updated by the constant 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0;\; t_1 = 0 \\
L_1:&\; t_2 = a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
& t_1 = t_1 + 4 \\
& i = i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Induction Variable Elimination (IVE)
Since $i$ is no longer used (only $t_1$ is), eliminate $i$ entirely by recognising that $i < n \iff t_1 < 4n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 0 \\
L_1:&\; t_2 = a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
& t_1 = t_1 + 4 \\
& \text{if } t_1 < 4n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Result:** A multiplication is replaced by an addition inside the hot loop — typical speed-up factor of **3× to 5×** on simple in-order pipelines.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

### Source Loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1:&\; t_1 = 4 \cdot i \\
& t_2 = a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
& t_3 = b + c \\
& t_4 = t_2 \cdot t_3 \\
& i = i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify Loop-Invariant Expression
$t_3 = b + c$ does not depend on $i$, the loop induction variable. Hence $t_3$ is **loop-invariant**.

### Step 2 — Hoist into the Pre-header
Introduce a new pre-header block $L_0$ and place the invariant computation there.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0:&\; t_3 = b + c \\
L_1:&\; t_1 = 4 \cdot i \\
& t_2 = a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
& t_4 = t_2 \cdot t_3 \\
& i = i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Result:** $b + c$ evaluated once instead of $n$ times — savings of $n - 1$ additions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Triggering Data-Flow Analyses

| Optimization Source | Required Data-Flow Analysis | Equation Form | Conservative? |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSE | Available Expressions | $\text{AVAIL}_{\text{out}}[B] = \text{AVAIL}_{\text{gen}}[B] \cup (\text{AVAIL}_{\text{in}}[B] - \text{AVAIL}_{\text{kill}}[B])$ | Yes |
| Copy Propagation | Reaching Definitions + Use-Def chains | Iterates over copies whose rhs is the only reaching def | Yes |
| DCE | Live-Variable Analysis | $\text{LIVE}_{\text{in}}[B] = \text{use}[B] \cup (\text{LIVE}_{\text{out}}[B] - \text{def}[B])$ | Yes |
| LICM | Reaching Definitions + Loop detection (Dominators) | $e$ is invariant iff $\forall \text{operand } o,\; \text{rdef}(o) \cap L = \emptyset$ | Yes |
| IVE / SR | Induction-Variable Detection (ω-algorithm) | $j = a \cdot i + b$ family | Yes |

## 3.5 Quantitative Performance Estimation

For a loop with **N** iterations and **k** invariant operations inside it:

$$
T_{\text{before}} = N \cdot (T_{\text{loop body}}) = N \cdot (k \cdot T_{\text{op}} + T_{\text{rest}})
$$

$$
T_{\text{after LICM}} = T_{\text{hoist}} + N \cdot ((k - k_{\text{inv}}) \cdot T_{\text{op}} + T_{\text{rest}})
$$

$$
\text{Speedup Factor } S = \frac{T_{\text{before}}}{T_{\text{after LICM}}} = \frac{N \cdot T_{\text{loop body}}}{T_{\text{hoist}} + N \cdot (T_{\text{loop body}} - k_{\text{inv}} \cdot T_{\text{op}})}
$$

For large $N$ and small $T_{\text{hoist}}$, the speedup approaches the asymptotic bound:

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{k_{\text{inv}} \cdot T_{\text{op}}}{T_{\text{loop body}}}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Principal Sources of Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRINCIPAL SOURCES OF OPTIMIZATION"]:::root
    A --> B1["Machine Independent"]:::cat
    A --> B2["Machine Dependent"]:::cat
    B1 --> C1["Function Preserving Transformations"]:::grp
    B1 --> C2["Loop Optimizations"]:::grp
    B1 --> C3["Local and Global Optimizations"]:::grp
    B2 --> C4["Peephole Optimizations"]:::grp
    B2 --> C5["Register and Pipeline Optimizations"]:::grp
    C1 --> D1["Common Subexpression Elimination"]:::leaf
    C1 --> D2["Copy Propagation"]:::leaf
    C1 --> D3["Dead Code Elimination"]:::leaf
    C1 --> D4["Constant Folding and Propagation"]:::leaf
    C1 --> D5["Algebraic Simplification"]:::leaf
    C2 --> E1["Loop Invariant Code Motion"]:::leaf
    C2 --> E2["Induction Variable Elimination"]:::leaf
    C2 --> E3["Strength Reduction"]:::leaf
    C2 --> E4["Loop Unrolling and Fusion"]:::leaf
    C3 --> F1["Local Basic Block Pass"]:::leaf
    C3 --> F2["Global Dataflow Pass"]:::leaf
    C4 --> G1["Redundant Instruction Removal"]:::leaf
    C4 --> G2["Jump Chain Compression"]:::leaf
    C5 --> H1["Register Allocation and Assignment"]:::leaf
    C5 --> H2["Instruction Scheduling and Pipelining"]:::leaf
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#3d6a9c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef grp fill:#6c8ebf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#b8d4f0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Optimization Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Program"]:::input
    IR1["Initial Three Address Code"]:::ir
    P1["Pass 1: Local Optimizer"]:::pass
    P2["Pass 2: Control Flow Analysis"]:::pass
    P3["Pass 3: Dataflow Analysis"]:::pass
    P4["Pass 4: Global Optimizer"]:::pass
    P5["Pass 5: Loop Optimizer"]:::pass
    P6["Pass 6: Peephole Optimizer"]:::pass
    IR2["Optimized TAC"]:::ir
    TGT["Target Machine Code"]:::out
    SRC --> IR1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> IR2 --> TGT
    P1 -. applies .-> DFE1["CSE, Constant Folding, DCE within BB"]:::note
    P3 -. applies .-> DFE2["Reaching Defs, Live Vars, Available Exprs"]:::note
    P4 -. applies .-> DFE3["Global CSE, Copy Prop, DCE across BB"]:::note
    P5 -. applies .-> DFE4["LICM, IVE, Strength Reduction, Unrolling"]:::note
    P6 -. applies .-> DFE5["Redundant MOV, Jump Compression"]:::note
    classDef input fill:#2d5a27,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ir fill:#5a4a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef pass fill:#a87f2a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#7a2a2a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef note fill:#dddddd,stroke:#666,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Flow Graph Illustrating LICM Opportunity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Preheader Block L0"]:::hdr
    H["Loop Header L1"]:::hdr
    B1["Body Block: t3 = b + c; t1 = 4i; t2 = a[t1]; t4 = t2*t3"]:::body
    B2["Increment: i = i + 1"]:::body
    C["Condition: if i less than n goto L1"]:::body
    E["Exit Block L2"]:::exit
    P --> H
    H --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C
    C -- true --> H
    C -- false --> E
    classDef hdr fill:#3a5f8f,color:#fff
    classDef body fill:#cfd8e8,color:#000
    classDef exit fill:#8a3a3a,color:#fff
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Block **B1** contains the loop-invariant expression `t3 = b + c`. The optimizer detects this and hoists the computation into a freshly-allocated **Preheader Block P**, reducing $n$ evaluations to $1$ evaluation.

## 4.4 Strength Reduction Recurrence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Iteration k: tk = ik * 4"]:::old
    B["Detect: ik+1 = ik + 1"]:::detect
    C["Derive: tk+1 = tk + 4"]:::derive
    D["Iteration k+1: tk+1 = tk + 4 (add instead of multiply)"]:::new
    A --> B --> C --> D
    classDef old fill:#a85a5a,color:#fff
    classDef detect fill:#a8a05a,color:#000
    classDef derive fill:#5aa85a,color:#fff
    classDef new fill:#5a6fa8,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Optimization Trade-off Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Optimization Decision"]:::root

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any four principal sources of machine-independent optimization. Briefly explain how constant folding differs from constant propagation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four principal sources of machine-independent optimization are:
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
3. **Copy Propagation**
4. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**

**Constant Folding** evaluates arithmetic expressions involving only literal constants at compile time (e.g., `x = 3 + 4` becomes `x = 7`).

**Constant Propagation** substitutes a variable that has a known constant value throughout its use-def chain (e.g., after `x = 5`, the use `y = x + 2` is rewritten as `y = 5 + 2`, which then enables constant folding in the next pass). *(1 mark for the distinction.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is strength reduction? Show one example applied to a loop.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Strength Reduction** is a loop optimization that replaces an expensive arithmetic operation with an equivalent cheaper one, typically by deriving a recurrence form. *(1 mark)*

The most common form replaces **multiplication by a loop-invariant constant** with **addition** of that constant at every iteration. *(1 mark)*

**Example:**
Original: inside a `for i = 1 to n` loop, the statement `a[i*4] = 0;` uses multiplication.
After strength reduction: maintain a running pointer `t = base;` and inside the loop write `t = t + 4; *(t) = 0;`. The compiler derives the recurrence `t_{k+1} = t_k + 4`, eliminating the multiply. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Common Subexpression Elimination**, **Copy Propagation**, and **Dead Code Elimination** with suitable intermediate code examples. Show how these three function-preserving transformations can be **chained together** to produce a final optimized code.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following three-address code segment, identify and apply **all possible principal sources of optimization**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 2 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_3 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_4 &= b \,[\, t_3 \,] \\
t_5 &= t_2 \cdot t_4 \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_6 &= t_2 + 0 \\
t_7 &= 6 \cdot 5 \\
t_8 &= t_7 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution

### Part A(a) — Function-Preserving Transformations

**Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) — 2 marks:**
Replaces redundant recomputation of an identical expression. Example:
Original: `t1 = a+b; t2 = a+b;` 
Optimized: `t1 = a+b; t2 = t1;` (avoids a second add).

**Copy Propagation — 2 marks:**
After `x = y`, substitutes all uses of `x` with `y` so the copy can be removed and DCE enabled.

**Dead Code Elimination (DCE) — 2 marks:**
Removes definitions whose values are never used. Identified using live-variable analysis.

**Chaining — 1 mark:**
The order in practice is **CSE → Copy Propagation → DCE** because CSE creates copies, copy propagation exposes dead code, and DCE cleans up the now-useless definitions.

### Part A(b) — Optimizing the Given TAC

**Step 1: Constant Folding**
`t7 = 6*5` → `t7 = 30` *(1 mark)*
`t8 = t7*1` → `t8 = t7` (algebraic) *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Algebraic Simplification**
`t6 = t2 + 0` → `t6 = t2` (identity) *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: CSE**
`t3 = 4*i` and `t1 = 2*i` are not identical (different constants), but if we look at `t2 = a[t1]` and the second `t2 = a[t1]`, the array subscript is identical, so CSE applies. *(1 mark)*

**Final Optimized TAC (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 2 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a \,[\, t_1 \,] \\
t_3 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_4 &= b \,[\, t_3 \,] \\
t_5 &= t_2 \cdot t_4 \\
t_6 &= t_2 \\
t_7 &= 30 \\
t_8 &= t_7
\end{aligned}
$$

After copy propagation and DCE, `t6`, `t7`, and `t8` can be further reduced or removed depending on whether downstream code uses them.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** and **Strength Reduction** techniques. Under what conditions is LICM safe to apply?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the loop below. Apply LICM and Strength Reduction to optimize it. State clearly which statements are moved and which are replaced.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 1 \\
L_1:&\; t_1 = 2 \cdot i \\
& t_2 = 5 \cdot j \\
& t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
& a \,[\, t_1 \,] = t_3 \\
& i &= i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Assume `j` and `n` are not modified inside the loop.)*

#### Model Solution

### Part B(a) — LICM and Strength Reduction

**LICM Explanation — 3 marks:**
Loop-Invariant Code Motion hoists a computation from inside a loop to a pre-header block if all its operands are either constants or have definitions strictly outside the loop. It is safe only when: (i) the expression is loop-invariant, (ii) the expression dominates all loop exits, and (iii) the expression has no side effects that could be triggered by exit predicates.

**Strength Reduction Explanation — 3 marks:**
Strength Reduction replaces an expensive operation (multiplication, exponentiation) inside a loop with a cheaper one (addition) by deriving a linear recurrence. For an induction variable $i$ with $i_{k+1} = i_k + c$, the expression $j = a \cdot i + b$ is replaced by $j_{k+1} = j_k + a \cdot c$.

**Conditions for LICM Safety — 1 mark:**
1. The expression is **loop-invariant** (all operands have reaching definitions outside $L$).
2. The expression **dominates all loop exits**.
3. The expression has **no side effects** that affect the exit branch.

### Part B(b) — Worked Optimization

**Step 1: Identify Loop-Invariant Expression** *(2 marks)*
`j` and `n` are not modified inside the loop. The expression `t2 = 5*j` depends only on `j` (a loop-invariant variable). Hence `t2` is loop-invariant.

**Step 2: Apply LICM** *(1 mark)*
Move `t2 = 5*j` to a pre-header block `L0`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0:&\; t_2 = 5 \cdot j \\
i &= 1 \\
L_1:&\; t_1 = 2 \cdot i \\
& t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
& a \,[\, t_1 \,] = t_3 \\
& i = i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Strength Reduction on $t_1$** *(2 marks)*
`t1 = 2*i` is an induction variable expression. Derive the recurrence:
At iter $k+1$: $t_1^{(k+1)} = 2 \cdot i^{(k+1)} = 2 \cdot (i^{(k)} + 1) = t_1^{(k)} + 2$.

Replace with a maintained running variable.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0:&\; t_2 = 5 \cdot j;\; t_1 = 2;\; i = 1 \\
L_1:&\; t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
& a \,[\, t_1 \,] = t_3 \\
& t_1 = t_1 + 2 \\
& i = i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Induction Variable Elimination** *(2 marks)*
Since $t_1 = 2i$ and the test $i \leq n$ is equivalent to $t_1 \leq 2n$, eliminate $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0:&\; t_2 = 5 \cdot j;\; t_1 = 2 \\
L_1:&\; t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
& a \,[\, t_1 \,] = t_3 \\
& t_1 = t_1 + 2 \\
& \text{if } t_1 \leq 2n \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Two multiplications (`2*i` and `5*j`) inside the hot loop are eliminated — replaced by an addition (`t1 = t1 + 2`) and a single hoisted multiply.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CSE with algebraic simplification.** CSE removes *identical re-evaluations*; algebraic simplification removes *redundancies within one expression* (e.g., `x*1` → `x`).
> 2. **Forgetting the "pre-header" requirement** for LICM. A student who simply says "move the code above the loop" without naming a *pre-header basic block* loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the recurrence derivation** in strength reduction. You must explicitly write the recurrence $t_{k+1} = t_k + c$ to earn full credit, not just state the rule.
> 4. **Not chaining the passes.** KTU examiners reward the demonstration of *how* one optimization *enables* the next (e.g., CSE produces copies → Copy Propagation → DCE).
> 5. **Ignoring safety conditions.** LICM applied to a non-loop-invariant expression is incorrect and will be penalized as a *correctness violation*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Principal Sources of Optimization:**
> - **Two super-categories:** Machine-Independent (on IR) and Machine-Dependent (on target code).
> - **Function-preserving transformations:** CSE, Copy Propagation, DCE, Constant Folding, Constant Propagation, Algebraic Simplification.
> - **Loop optimizations:** LICM, Strength Reduction, IVE, Loop Unrolling, Loop Fusion/Jamming.
> - **Local vs Global:** Local = within a single basic block. Global = across basic blocks using **data-flow analysis**.
> - **Peephole:** A small sliding window of target instructions examined for redundant patterns.
> - **Canonical order of optimization passes:** Local → Global CSE → Copy Propagation → DCE → LICM → IVE/SR → Peephole.
> - **Safety theorem:** All listed optimizations are *function-preserving* (semantic equivalence guaranteed by conservative data-flow analyses).
> - **Standard metric for loop savings:** LICM converts $N$ evaluations of an invariant into 1 evaluation, yielding a speedup of roughly $\frac{N}{1}$.
> - **Strength reduction recurrence formula:** $t_{k+1} = t_k + c$ replaces $t_{k+1} = (i_k + 1) \cdot c$.
> - **Most-tested pair in KTU ESE:** *CSE → Copy Propagation → DCE* chain (3-step question) and *LICM + Strength Reduction* on a sample loop.
> - **Memory aid (Mnemonic):** **"C³-D-LICM-SR"** = *Common-sub, Constant, Copy → DCE → LICM → Strength Reduction*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization of Basic Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization of Basic Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization of Basic Blocks** is a *local optimization* technique applied during the intermediate code generation phase of a compiler. It analyses a **basic block** — a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address statements in which flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves only at the end without any internal branches — and transforms it into an equivalent, but computationally more efficient sequence using a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** representation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic sits inside **Module 4: Code Optimization** and is a direct application of the principle that *redundant computation, repeated evaluations, and strength-heavy operators can be eliminated without altering the program's semantics*.

### Core Terminology Anchor

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|
| **Basic Block** | A straight-line sequence of three-address code with a single entry and single exit. |
| **Leader** | The first statement of a basic block (first instruction / target of branch / branch target / statement after branch). |
| **DAG** | Directed Acyclic Graph whose leaves represent identifiers/operands and whose interior nodes represent operators. |
| **Local Optimization** | Optimization confined to the boundary of one basic block. |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Intermediate representation where each instruction has at most three operands (result, op1, op2). |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Recipe Card" 🍳

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are a chef following a recipe card. The card is your *basic block* — every line is one three-address instruction. As you read the recipe carefully, you realize:
> - *"I already sliced the onion in step 2. In step 6, I don't need to slice it again — I can directly refer to the already-sliced onion."* ➜ This is **Common Subexpression Elimination**.
> - *"Instead of multiplying x by 8, I can just shift x left by 3 bits."* ➜ This is **Reduction in Strength**.
> - *"Step 11 says 'add 0 to y' — that's pointless."* ➜ This is **Use of Algebraic Identities**.
> - *"The variable z was assigned but never read afterwards."* ➜ This is **Dead Code Elimination**.

The compiler, in this metaphor, is the **chef + quality inspector** combined — it re-reads the recipe (basic block), redraws it as a DAG to spot redundancies, and rewrites it in a leaner form *without changing the dish (semantics)*.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiner's priority check: KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 weightage treats this topic as a **high-yield, sure-shot 14-marker**. The combination of **DAG construction + identification of local transformations** is the most repeated pattern in KTU University Exams.

**Physical / Engineering Metrics Treated as Bold Standards:**
- **Number of instructions reduced** — primary metric of optimization success.
- **Critical path depth** — number of nodes in longest chain in DAG (correlates with execution speed).
- **Space optimization** — number of reused intermediate nodes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DAG Construction Algorithm — Stepwise Logic

A Directed Acyclic Graph used for basic block optimization contains **three node categories**:

| Node Type | Symbol Convention | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Leaf Node** | Labelled with an identifier (possibly constant). | Operand of an instruction. |
| **Interior Node** | Labelled with an operator symbol. | An operation whose children are its operands. |
| **Marker / Chain** | Dashed edge with $i_k$ label. | Indicates that node was the *k-th definition* of identifier $i$. |

### DAG Construction Rules (3-Address Instruction Forms)

For every statement $x \:=\: y \; op \; z$ the compiler performs the following logical checks:

1. **Step 1 — Locate y node.** Find the node $N_y$ currently holding the *most recent* definition of $y$ in the DAG. If $y$ is a constant/identifier in the leaves, locate its leaf.
2. **Step 2 — Locate z node.** Same procedure for operand $z$, finding node $N_z$.
3. **Step 3 — Common Subexpression Check.** If there exists a node $M$ labelled $op$ whose left child is $N_y$ and right child is $N_z$, then reuse $M$. Set $x$ to point to $M$.
4. **Step 4 — New Node Creation (if Step 3 fails).** Otherwise, create a fresh interior node $N$ labelled $op$ with children $N_y$ and $N_z$. Set $x$ to point to $N$.
5. **Step 5 — Handle Unary / Assignment.** For $x \:=\: op \; y$ (unary) or $x \:=\: y$ (copy), the same lookup-reuse-or-create logic applies with one operand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Because the DAG is *acyclic*, every lookup is guaranteed to terminate. Because we always reference the *most recent* definition, we respect the sequential nature of basic block execution.

---

## 2.2 Local Optimization Transformations Identified from a DAG

Once the DAG is constructed, the following transformations are systematically applied:

| # | Transformation | DAG Mechanism | KTU Board Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Common Subexpression Elimination** | Multiple parents pointing to same interior node. | "Reuse the node" |
| 2 | **Dead / Redundant Store Elimination** | A node whose value is never used by any later instruction. | "Remove useless assignment" |
| 3 | **Use of Algebraic Identities** | Nodes like $x+0$, $x \cdot 1$, $x \cdot 0$, $x^2$ | "Apply identity rules" |
| 4 | **Reduction in Strength** | Replace $x \cdot 2^k$ with $x \ll k$; $x/2$ with $x \gg 1$. | "Replace with shift" |
| 5 | **Constant Folding** | All children of a node are constants — compute at compile time. | "Evaluate at compile time" |
| 6 | **Copy Propagation** | Variable $x$ copies $y$ → replace later uses of $x$ with $y$. | "Substitute definition" |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **master reference** the KTU examiner expects you to internalize before the ESE.

| Algebraic Identity | Replacement Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| $x + 0 = x$ | Replace `ADD x, 0` with `MOV x` | Embedded MCU arithmetic loops |
| $x \cdot 1 = x$ | Replace `MUL x, 1` with `MOV x` | DSP kernel pipelining |
| $x \cdot 0 = 0$ | Replace with constant 0 | Signal dead-branch handling |
| $x \cdot 2^{k}$ | Replace with $x \ll k$ | Cryptographic shift-cipher optimization |
| $x / 2$ | Replace with $x \gg 1$ | Image processing filters |
| $x^{2}$ | Replace with $x \cdot x$ only if no FPU | Floating-point pipeline stalls |
| $x - x = 0$ | Replace with constant 0 | Physics simulation solvers |
| $x \uparrow 0 = 1$ | Replace with constant 1 | Matrix identity initializers |

**Cost Function for Optimization Success** (useful for 7-mark derivations):

$$
\text{Speedup Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Instructions}_{\text{original}}}{\text{Instructions}_{\text{optimized}}}
$$

$$
\text{Code Size Reduction (\%)} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{removed}}}{N_{\text{original}}} \times 100
$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Compiler Backends (GCC `-O2`, LLVM `-O3`):** DAG-based local optimization is the very first pass executed before global passes.
> - **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V):** Reduction in strength (shift vs. multiply) directly reduces clock cycles and energy.
> - **GPU Shader Compilation (NVIDIA NVCC):** Constant folding of uniform variables yields massive parallel savings.
> - **High-Performance Computing (HPC) kernels:** Algebraic identity elimination on BLAS routines provides 5–15% runtime gain.

---

## 2.5 Relationship to the Broader Module 4 Framework

```
Module 4: Code Optimization
├── 4.1 Basic Blocks & Control Flow Graphs (CFG)
├── 4.2 DAG Representation of Basic Blocks      ← (THIS TOPIC)
├── 4.3 Local Optimizations derived from DAG
├── 4.4 Global Optimization (Data-flow analysis)
└── 4.5 Loop Optimizations
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example — DAG Construction from TAC

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact pattern** KTU examiners use. Memorize the layout.

### Given TAC (Three-Address Code)

```
t1  = a + b
t2  = a + b          ← (apparent common subexpression)
t3  = t1 - c
t4  = t2 - c
t5  = t1 * 2
t6  = t3 + t4
t7  = t5 + t6
d   = t7
```

---

### Step-by-Step DAG Construction (with explanations)

**Step 1 — Process `t1 = a + b`**

No existing node labelled `+`. Create new interior node $N_1$ with leaf children $a$ and $b$. Set `t1` to point to $N_1$.

$$
N_1 \;:\; \texttt{(+)} \;\leftarrow\; a, \, b
$$

**Step 2 — Process `t2 = a + b`**

Search: does a node labelled `+` with left child $a$ and right child $b$ already exist? **YES — it is $N_1$.** Reuse $N_1$. Set `t2` to point to $N_1$.

$$
\Rightarrow t_1 \equiv t_2 \quad \text{(common subexpression detected)}
$$

**Step 3 — Process `t3 = t1 - c`**

Look up the node for `t1` → $N_1$. Look up leaf for `c` → leaf $c$. No existing `-` node with these children. Create $N_2$.

$$
N_2 \;:\; \texttt{(-)} \;\leftarrow\; N_1, \, c
$$

**Step 4 — Process `t4 = t2 - c`**

`t2` points to $N_1$ (same as `t1`). Children of `-` candidate: $N_1, c$. An existing $-$ node with these children exists → **it is $N_2$**. Reuse $N_2$.

$$
\Rightarrow t_3 \equiv t_4 \quad \text{(second common subexpression detected)}
$$

**Step 5 — Process `t5 = t1 * 2`**

`t1` → $N_1$. Look for `*` node with children $N_1$, constant `2`. None exists. Create $N_3$.

$$
N_3 \;:\; \texttt{(*)} \;\leftarrow\; N_1, \, 2
$$

> **Apply Reduction in Strength:** $t_5 = t_1 \cdot 2 \;\equiv\; t_1 \ll 1$. So node $N_3$ will later emit a left-shift.

**Step 6 — Process `t6 = t3 + t4`**

Both `t3` and `t4` point to $N_2$. Look for `+` node with children $N_2, N_2$. None exists. Create $N_4$.

$$
N_4 \;:\; \texttt{(+)} \;\leftarrow\; N_2, \, N_2
$$

> **Apply Algebraic Identity:** $x + x = 2x$. Therefore $t_6 = 2 \cdot t_3 \;\equiv\; t_3 \ll 1$.

**Step 7 — Process `t7 = t5 + t6`**

`t5` → $N_3$. `t6` → $N_4$. No existing `+` node with these children. Create $N_5$.

$$
N_5 \;:\; \texttt{(+)} \;\leftarrow\; N_3, \, N_4
$$

**Step 8 — Process `d = t7`**

`t7` → $N_5$. This is a copy; mark node $N_5$ as defining `d`.

---

### 3.2 The Resulting DAG (Analytical Matrix)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes:} \quad & N_1: (+) \;\leftarrow\; a,\; b \\
& N_2: (-) \;\leftarrow\; N_1,\; c \\
& N_3: (\ll 1) \;\leftarrow\; N_1 \quad \text{(after strength reduction)} \\
& N_4: (\ll 1) \;\leftarrow\; N_2 \quad \text{(after algebraic identity)} \\
& N_5: (+) \;\leftarrow\; N_3,\; N_4 \quad \text{(computes } d\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Statistics table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original TAC statements} &= 8 \\
\text{DAG interior nodes (unique ops)} &= 5 \\
\text{Instructions eliminated (redundant)} &= 3 \\
\text{Code Size Reduction (\%)} &= \frac{3}{8} \times 100 = 37.5\% \\
\text{Speedup Ratio} &= \frac{8}{5} = 1.6 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Optimized Three-Address Code (Reverse Order from DAG)

The DAG is processed in **reverse topological order**, emitting each interior node as an instruction, applying every transformation we discovered:

```
t1  = a + b
t3  = t1 - c
t5  = t1 << 1        ← strength reduction
t6  = t3 << 1        ← algebraic identity (x + x → 2x)
t7  = t5 + t6
d   = t7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare: original block had **8 instructions**, optimized block has **6 instructions**, and `t2` and `t4` were eliminated as **common subexpressions**. Every line above is *semantically identical* to the original — verified by induction on DAG construction.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Proof of Equivalence (for 14-mark completeness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** *The set of values computed at the leaves reaching the DAG root in the optimized code equals the value at `d` in the original code.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original:}\quad & t_1 = a+b,\; t_2 = a+b,\; t_3 = t_1-c,\; t_4 = t_2-c,\; t_5 = 2t_1,\; t_6 = t_3+t_4 = 2t_3,\; t_7 = t_5+t_6,\; d = t_7 \\
\text{Optimized:}\quad & t_1' = a+b,\; t_3' = t_1'-c,\; t_5' = 2t_1',\; t_6' = 2t_3',\; t_7' = t_5'+t_6',\; d' = t_7' \\
\text{Since } t_2 &= t_1 \text{ and } t_4 = t_3 \text{ by construction: } \\
t_7 &= (2t_1) + (2t_3) = 2(a+b) + 2(t_1-c) \\
t_7' &= (2t_1') + (2t_3') = 2(a+b) + 2(t_1'-c) \\
\therefore \quad d &= d' \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Optimization Pipeline (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Program"] --> B["Lexical and Syntax Analysis"]
    B --> C["Three Address Code TAC Generation"]
    C --> D["Identify Basic Block Leaders"]
    D --> E["Partition Code into Basic Blocks"]
    E --> F["Construct Directed Acyclic Graph DAG"]
    F --> G["Apply Local Transformations"]
    G --> H["Emit Optimized TAC"]
    H --> I["Final Machine Code"]

    style A fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#c7ceea,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 The DAG Architecture for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    leafA["a"] --> nodeA1["PLUS a b N1"]
    leafB["b"] --> nodeA1
    leafC["c"] --> nodeS1["MINUS N1 c N2"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeS1
    nodeA1 --> nodeM1["SHL1 N1 N3"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeM2["SHL1 N2 N4"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeP1["PLUS N3 N4 N5 result d"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeP1

    style leafA fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style leafB fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style leafC fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeA1 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeS1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeM1 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeM2 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeP1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        I1["Three Address Code TAC"]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS["Analysis Stage"]
        A1["Leader Detection Module"]
        A2["Block Partitioner"]
        A3["DAG Builder Engine"]
    end

    subgraph TRANSFORM["Transformation Stage"]
        T1["Common Subexpression Eliminator"]
        T2["Strength Reducer"]
        T3["Algebraic Identity Engine"]
        T4["Constant Folder"]
        T5["Dead Code Remover"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        O1["Optimized TAC Stream"]
    end

    I1 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> O1

    style I1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T1 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T4 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T5 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style O1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Reuse vs. Create a DAG Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Encounter statement x equals y op z"}
    Q1 -- Yes, both operands exist --> Q2{"Existing node with operator op and same children"}
    Q1 -- Unary or copy --> Q3{"Existing node matches operator and child"}
    Q2 -- YES --> R1["REUSE existing node and attach x to it"]
    Q2 -- NO --> R2["CREATE new interior node labelled op"]
    Q3 -- YES --> R1
    Q3 -- NO --> R2
    R1 --> X1["Continue to next TAC line"]
    R2 --> X2["Add children references and continue"]

    style Q1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#b5ead7,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Define a basic block. List the conditions under which a three-address statement qualifies as a leader of a basic block.**
