**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address statements in which the flow of control enters only at the beginning and exits only at the end, without any internal jumps or branch targets. **(1 mark)**

A statement qualifies as a **leader** if any one of the following holds: **(2 marks)**
1. It is the **first statement** of the program/intermediate code.
2. It is the **target** of a conditional or unconditional branch (goto).
3. It **immediately follows** a conditional/unconditional branch (branch successors).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]`

**What is the role of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in the optimization of basic blocks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** provides a graphical, acyclic representation of a basic block where leaves represent operands (identifiers/constants) and interior nodes represent operations. **(1 mark)**

Its roles are:
1. **Detection of common subexpressions:** Two instructions computing the same value point to the same interior node, enabling automatic elimination of redundant computation. **(1 mark)**
2. **Identification of dead/dead-store code:** A node whose value is not used as an operand by any subsequent node can be removed, since its result is never consumed. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Regular]`

**(a)** Construct the DAG for the following basic block and identify all **common subexpressions**, **algebraic identities**, and **strength reduction** opportunities.

```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = e - t2
t4 = a + b
t5 = t1 * 4
t6 = t2 + t3
t7 = t4 * 4
t8 = t5 - t6
t9 = t7 + t8
x  = t9
```

**(b)** Rewrite the optimized three-address code in proper topological order from the DAG. Compute the percentage reduction in instructions.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — DAG Construction (7 marks)

We process each line carefully:

**Step 1 — `t1 = a + b`**
No existing `+` node. Create $N_1$ with leaves $a, b$. `t1` → $N_1$.

**Step 2 — `t2 = c + d`**
No existing `+` node with $c, d$. Create $N_2$ with leaves $c, d$. `t2` → $N_2$.

**Step 3 — `t3 = e - t2`**
`t2` → $N_2$. No existing `-` node with child $e, N_2$. Create $N_3$. `t3` → $N_3$.

**Step 4 — `t4 = a + b`**
Search: existing `+` node with children $a, b$? **YES — $N_1$.** Reuse. `t4` → $N_1$.

> ✅ **Common subexpression identified:** $t_1 \equiv t_4$

**Step 5 — `t5 = t1 * 4`**
`t1` → $N_1$. No `*` node with $N_1, 4$. Create $N_4$ as `*`. `t5` → $N_4$.

> ✅ **Strength reduction:** $t_5 = t_1 \cdot 4 \;\equiv\; t_1 \ll 2$

**Step 6 — `t6 = t2 + t3`**
`t2` → $N_2$. `t3` → $N_3$. No existing `+` with $N_2, N_3$. Create $N_5$. `t6` → $N_5$.

**Step 7 — `t7 = t4 * 4`**
`t4` → $N_1$. Search: existing `*` node with $N_1, 4$? **YES — $N_4$.** Reuse. `t7` → $N_4$.

> ✅ **Common subexpression identified:** $t_5 \equiv t_7$

**Step 8 — `t8 = t5 - t6`**
`t5` → $N_4$. `t6` → $N_5$. No existing `-`. Create $N_6$. `t8` → $N_6$.

**Step 9 — `t9 = t7 + t8`**
`t7` → $N_4$. `t8` → $N_6$. No existing `+`. Create $N_7$. `t9` → $N_7$.

**Step 10 — `x = t9`**
Copy: `x` → $N_7$.

**Summary Table of Detected Transformations (full marks for the table):**

| Statement | Transformation | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `t4 = a + b` | Reuse $N_1$ | Common Subexpression Elimination |
| `t5 = t1 * 4` | Replace with `t1 << 2` | Reduction in Strength |
| `t7 = t4 * 4` | Reuse $N_4$ (same as `t5`) | Common Subexpression Elimination |

#### Part (b) — Optimized Code (7 marks)

Emit in **reverse topological order**, applying all transformations:

```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = e - t2
t5 = t1 << 2        ← strength reduction of *4
t6 = t2 + t3
t8 = t5 - t6        ← (re-using node pattern)
t9 = t7 + t8
x  = t9
```

Wait — `t7` is identical to `t5`, so substitute:

```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = e - t2
t5 = t1 << 2
t6 = t2 + t3
t8 = t5 - t6
t9 = t5 + t8        ← substituted t7 with t5
x  = t9
```

**Final optimized block: 8 instructions.**

**Metrics Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original instructions} &= 10 \\
\text{Optimized instructions} &= 8 \\
\text{Eliminated (redundant)} &= 2 \\
\text{Code Size Reduction (\%)} &= \frac{2}{10} \times 100 = 20\% \\
\text{Speedup Ratio} &= \frac{10}{8} = 1.25 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain with a suitable example the concept of **Reduction in Strength** and **Use of Algebraic Identities** as local optimization techniques applied to basic blocks.

**(b)** Given the following three-address code, identify the leaders, partition into basic blocks, and apply DAG-based optimization to eliminate redundant computations:

```
1.  i = 1
2.  j = 1
3.  t1 = 10 * i
4.  t2 = t1 + j
5.  t3 = 2 * t2
6.  if t3 > 100 goto 10
7.  i = i + 1
8.  if i <= 10 goto 3
9.  j = j + 1
10. print j
```

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)

**Reduction in Strength (3.5 marks):**
Reduction in strength replaces an expensive arithmetic operation with a computationally cheaper equivalent.

> The canonical form is: $x \cdot 2^{k} \equiv x \ll k$.

For example:

```
y = x * 256       ← expensive multiplication
y = x << 8        ← single bit shift (4–8x faster on most ISAs)
```

Similarly, $x / 2 \equiv x \gg 1$, and $x^2$ can be replaced by $x \cdot x$ when no floating-point unit exists. This is particularly crucial in **embedded systems** and **DSP kernels** where integer multipliers consume significant silicon area and power.

**Use of Algebraic Identities (3.5 marks):**

Algebraic identities are mathematical truths that allow the compiler to simplify expressions at compile time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x + 0 &= x, \quad x \cdot 1 = x, \quad x \cdot 0 = 0 \\
x - x &= 0, \quad x \uparrow 0 = 1 \\
2 \cdot x &= x \ll 1 \quad \text{(special case of strength reduction)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:**

```
p = q * 1         ← replaced with:  p = q
r = s + 0         ← replaced with:  r = s
t = u - u         ← replaced with:  t = 0
v = 2 * w         ← replaced with:  v = w << 1
```

These transformations are *semantics-preserving* — the optimized program produces the same outputs for every legal input.

#### Part (b) — Leader Identification, Partitioning & DAG Optimization (7 marks)

**Leader Identification (2 marks):**

Per the rules:
- Rule 1: Statement 1 (`i = 1`) is the **first** statement → **Leader** ✓
- Rule 2: Statement 3 (`t1 = 10 * i`) is target of `goto 3` → **Leader** ✓
- Rule 2: Statement 10 (`print j`) is target of `goto 10` → **Leader** ✓
- Rule 3: Statement 7 (`i = i + 1`) immediately follows a conditional `goto 10` → **Leader** ✓
- Statement 9 follows `goto 3` block → not a leader itself, but statement 7 already covered.

**Basic Block Partition (2 marks):**

| Block | Statements | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **B1** | 1, 2 | Initialization |
| **B2** | 3, 4, 5, 6 | Loop header + computation + branch |
| **B3** | 7, 8 | Loop increment + branch |
| **B4** | 9 | Nested increment |
| **B5** | 10 | Print statement |

**DAG-based Optimization of Block B2 (3 marks):**

Inside B2, on **successive loop iterations**, `i` changes but the structure remains:

```
t1 = 10 * i    →   t1 = i << 3 + i << 1  (if 10i → strength reduction possible only via constant folding)
                   Better: t1 = 10*i remains as scaled add: t1 = (i<<3) + (i<<1)
t2 = t1 + j
t3 = 2 * t2    →   t3 = t2 << 1  (strength reduction applied)
```

> Since loop optimization is global, the **DAG local pass** simply detects that `t3 = 2*t2` can be replaced by `t3 = t2 << 1`, reducing one multiplication per iteration — saving **N clock cycles per loop execution** where $N$ is the iteration count.

**Result:** Block B2 emits a faster sequence, and when combined with loop-invariant code motion (Module 4 advanced topic), `t1 = 10 * i` can be partially strength-reduced.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "re-use node" with "re-use variable name":** Marks are deducted if you say "$t_2 = t_1$" without showing the DAG node pointer logic. Always state *which interior node* is being reused.
> 2. **Skipping the strength-reduction step:** Many students stop at common subexpression elimination and miss the 3-mark bonus available for algebraic identities.
> 3. **Wrong reverse-topological order in part (b):** The optimized TAC must be emitted in correct order so that every operand is defined before use. Random ordering = 2 marks deducted.
> 4. **Forgetting the metrics calculation:** Even if your DAG is perfect, the question asks for % reduction. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Treating loops as a single basic block:** A loop with a backward branch is *multiple* basic blocks; partition first, then optimize each block.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU Board revision checklist — high-density, exam-ready:**

- ✅ A **basic block** has exactly **one entry and one exit**; identified via **leaders**.
- ✅ **Leader rule summary:** first statement / target of `goto` / successor of a `goto`.
- ✅ **DAG leaves** = identifiers or constants; **DAG interior nodes** = operators.
- ✅ **Three DAG node labels:** leaf, interior, marker (chain edge $i_k$).
- ✅ **Five mandatory local optimizations:** CSE, dead-store elim, algebraic identity, strength reduction, constant folding.
- ✅ **Strength reduction formula:** $x \cdot 2^{k} \;\equiv\; x \ll k$.
- ✅ **Reduction order of cost:** shift $<$ add $<$ mul $<$ div.
- ✅ **Optimization is semantics-preserving** — never change program meaning.
- ✅ **Reverse-topological emission** is the rule for generating optimized TAC from a DAG.
- ✅ **Always compute** $\text{Speedup Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{\text{original}}}{N_{\text{optimized}}}$ for full marks.
- ✅ **Local vs Global** — local = within one basic block; global = across blocks (Module 4 advanced).
- ✅ **DAG construction is iterative**, one TAC statement at a time — *no batch processing*.
- ✅ **Constant folding example:** `t = 3 + 5` → `t = 8` at compile time.
- ✅ **Copy propagation example:** `x = y; ... z = x * 2` → `z = y * 2`.
- ✅ **KTU expected length:** Part A = 1 page, Part B = 3–4 pages with diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Generation: Issues in the Design of a Code Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Generation: Issues in the Design of a Code Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Code Generation — Issues in the Design of a Code Generator

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Code Generation** is the final phase of a compiler that translates the intermediate representation (IR) of the source program — typically three-address code (TAC), quadruples, or RTL — into equivalent **target machine code** (assembly or object code). The component that performs this translation is called the **Code Generator**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEITT631, Module 4), the **Issues in the Design of a Code Generator** refer to the strategic, structural, and optimization-oriented decisions that a compiler designer must make to produce **correct, efficient, and compact** target code. These issues govern *how* the intermediate code is mapped onto the underlying machine architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *"The code generation problem — issues in the design of a code generator — target language — basic blocks and flow graphs — optimization of basic blocks — peephole optimization — register allocation and assignment — a simple code generator — the concept of a code generator."*

### 1.2 Primary Issues in Designing a Code Generator

The following are the **canonical issues** identified in Aho, Sethi, Ullman (Dragon Book) and prescribed in the KTU syllabus:

| # | Issue | Core Concern |
|---|-------|-------------|
| **I1** | **Input to the Code Generator** | What IR form (TAC, quadruples, stack code) is fed? |
| **I2** | **Target Program** | What is the output (absolute/relocatable/assembly)? |
| **I3** | **Instruction Selection** | How to map IR operations to target machine instructions? |
| **I4** | **Register Allocation & Assignment** | Which operands go into which registers? |
| **I5** | **Evaluation Order** | What order do we generate code for operations? |
| **I6** | **Storage Management (Activation Records)** | How are local variables, temporaries, and parameters mapped to memory? |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Translator" 🌍

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a **UN-style simultaneous translator** (the code generator) standing between a **speaker in English** (the IR/three-address code) and a **Japanese audience** (the target CPU — say, x86 or ARM). The translator must not only translate each sentence correctly, but also:
> - Choose the **right words** (instruction selection),
> - Use **memorized vocabulary** rather than flipping a dictionary each time (register allocation),
> - Translate in an **order** that makes sense in Japanese grammar (evaluation order), and
> - Know whether to give a **formal written summary or a verbose speech** (target program form).

A bad translator (poor code generator) produces *correct but slow* code; a great one produces *correct AND blazing fast* code. **Designing a code generator is the art of being that great translator.**

### 1.4 Quality Metrics of Generated Code (Measurable Targets)

- **Speed (Execution Time)** — primary metric
- **Code Size** — critical in embedded/IoT targets
- **Power Consumption** — important for mobile/ARM targets
- **Memory Footprint** — minimal stack/heap usage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Design Goals (KTU Standard):** The generated code must be **Correct**, **Efficient**, and **Compact**, while leveraging all hardware features (registers, addressing modes, pipelining, etc.) of the target machine.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Design Issue

#### **Issue I1 — Input to the Code Generator**

The code generator typically receives:
- **Intermediate Representation:** Three-Address Code (TAC), Quadruples, Indirect Triples, or SSA form.
- **Symbol Table:** Contains type information, scope, and memory locations of variables.
- **Literal Table:** Stores constants and string literals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TAC?** It exposes each sub-expression independently, making register allocation and instruction selection tractable. Example TAC: `t1 := a + b`, `t2 := t1 * c`.

#### **Issue I2 — Target Program Form**

Three output choices exist:

| Form | Description | Trade-off |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Absolute Machine Code** | Fixed addresses; executable directly | Fast load, but no relocations |
| **Relocatable Object Code** | Allows sub-program linking (`.o` files) | Standard for compilers |
| **Assembly Code** | Symbolic; needs an assembler | Easier to produce, slower compile |

#### **Issue I3 — Instruction Selection**

The mapping from IR statements to machine instructions. A naive approach: generate a fixed template for each IR opcode (e.g., `ADD → ADD reg, reg`). A smarter approach uses **tree-pattern matching** or **dynamic programming** (Aho-Johnson algorithm) to find optimal instruction sequences.

**Factors influencing selection:**
- **Addressing modes** available (direct, indirect, indexed, base+offset)
- **Cost model**: each instruction has an associated cost (cycles / bytes)
- **Uniform vs. Non-uniform cost models**

#### **Issue I4 — Register Allocation & Assignment**

This is often the **most complex** issue. We must decide:

> **Register Allocation:** *Which* set of variables should reside in registers?
> **Register Assignment:** *Which specific register* (R0, R1, …, Rn) holds each variable?

**Strategy:** Use **graph coloring** (Chaitin's algorithm) where:
- Nodes = variables (temporaries)
- Edge between u and v if u and v are *live simultaneously* (interference).
- Colors = available registers.
- A graph that requires *k* colors needs *k* registers; if *k > available registers*, **spill** some variables to memory.

#### **Issue I5 — Evaluation Order**

The order in which computations are emitted affects:
- **Register pressure** (fewer temporaries alive at once = better)
- **Pipeline utilization** on modern CPUs

**Heuristic:** Use *bottom-up* evaluation, computing an operand only when both its sources are available, minimizing stack/register usage.

#### **Issue I6 — Storage / Activation Record Management**

A **stack frame (activation record)** is allocated per function call containing:

| Region | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Returned Value** | Slot for function's return value |
| **Control Link** | Pointer to caller's frame (dynamic chain) |
| **Access Link** | Pointer to enclosing scope (static chain) |
| **Saved Machine Status** | Return address, saved registers |
| **Parameters** | Actual arguments passed in |
| **Local Variables** | Function's locals & temporaries |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Cost of instruction *i* | $C(i)$ | Execution cycles or bytes |
| Total program cost | $P_{cost} = \sum_{i \in \text{instructions}} C(i)$ | Sum of all instruction costs |
| Register requirement (graph coloring) | $\chi(G)$ | Chromatic number of interference graph $G$ |
| Spill condition | $\chi(G) > \vert R \vert$ | Where $\vert R \vert$ = number of available registers |
| Live range of variable *v* | $[d^{\text{def}}(v),\ d^{\text{use}}(v)]$ | From definition to last use |
| Set of successors in CFG | $succ(B)$ | Blocks immediately following $B$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention (KTU):** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside tables — never a literal pipe `|`, to avoid breaking markdown.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Compilers (GCC ARM, IAR):** Code generation issues determine binary size and battery life.
- **JIT Compilers (HotSpot JVM, V8):** Dynamic code generation uses the same principles for runtime performance.
- **GPU/Shader Compilers (NVIDIA NVCC):** Massive register allocation problems drive optimization research.
- **DSP Compilers:** Custom instruction selection for TI C6000 / SHARC architectures.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Generating Code from TAC

**Source TAC:**

```
t1 := a - b
t2 := a - c
t3 := t1 * t2
d := t3
```

Assume registers: `R0`, `R1`, `R2`; variables `a, b, c, d` are in memory locations. We apply **bottom-up code generation**:

```
Load  R0, a        ; R0 := a
Sub   R0, R0, b    ; R0 := R0 - b   (this is t1)
Load  R1, a        ; R1 := a
Sub   R1, R1, c    ; R1 := R1 - c   (this is t2)
Mul   R0, R0, R1   ; R0 := R0 * R1   (this is t3)
Store d, R0        ; d := R0
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$T_1:\ a - b \implies \text{requires 1 load + 1 sub}$$

$$T_2:\ a - c \implies \text{requires 1 load + 1 sub (in a different register, since } t_1 \text{ is still live)}$$

$$T_3:\ t_1 \times t_2 \implies \text{requires 1 mul}$$

**Total instruction count** = 5 instructions (excluding final store). If we had **reuse** by allowing `a` to remain in a register, we could eliminate redundant loads → **peephole optimization** opportunity.

### 3.2 Cost Optimization Derivation

Let $S$ be a TAC sequence with $n$ statements, each mapped to $k_i$ instructions. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Naive Cost: } C_{\text{naive}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i \cdot c_{\text{avg}} \\
\text{Optimized Cost: } C_{\text{opt}} &= C_{\text{naive}} - \sum_{\text{eliminated redundancies}} c_{\text{redundant}} \\
\text{Savings: } \Delta C &= C_{\text{naive}} - C_{\text{opt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical demonstration:** Consider the redundant load case above. If each load costs $4$ cycles, eliminating 1 load saves $\Delta C = 4$ cycles. If executed $10^6$ times in a loop, total savings $= 4 \times 10^6$ cycles $= 4\ \text{ms}$ on a 1 GHz processor.

### 3.3 Register Allocation via Graph Coloring

**Procedure (Chaitin's Heuristic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Build interference graph } G = (V, E). \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Repeatedly remove (push onto stack) a node } v \in V \text{ with degree } < k, \\
                & \quad \text{where } k = \vert R \vert. \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{If all nodes removed } \Rightarrow G \text{ is } k\text{-colorable.} \\
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{Else, pick a "spill candidate" node, mark for memory storage, repeat.} \\
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{Pop nodes from stack; assign lowest available color (register).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why it works (sketch):** A node of degree $< k$ can always be colored when neighbors are already colored, because at least one of the $k$ colors remains free. Removing such nodes is safe (a node removed early can always be re-added later when its neighbors have been colored).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Code Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IR["Intermediate Representation (TAC / Quadruples)"] --> ST["Symbol Table Lookup"]
    ST --> IS["Instruction Selection Module"]
    IS --> RA["Register Allocator"]
    RA --> AS["Register Assigner"]
    AS --> EO["Evaluation Order Scheduler"]
    EO --> SM["Storage / Activation Record Manager"]
    SM --> TM["Target Machine Code (Assembly / Object)"]
    subgraph Optimization_Pass
        OPT["Peephole Optimizer"] -.-> TM
    end
    classDef io fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef proc fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef out fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    class IR,ST io
    class IS,RA,AS,EO,SM,OPT proc
    class TM out
```

### 4.2 Activation Record (Stack Frame) Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Returned Value"] --> B["Control Link (Dynamic Chain)"]
    B --> C["Access Link (Static Chain)"]
    C --> D["Saved Machine Status"]
    D --> E["Parameters (Actual Arguments)"]
    E --> F["Local Variables / Temporaries"]
    classDef frameRegion fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#334155,stroke-width:1px,color:#0F172A
    class A,B,C,D,E,F frameRegion
```

### 4.3 Register Allocation Pipeline (Graph Coloring View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["Liveness Analysis"] --> N2["Build Interference Graph"]
    N2 --> N3{"All nodes degree < k?"}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4["Push node, repeat"]
    N4 --> N2
    N3 -- No --> N5["Mark Spill Candidate"]
    N5 --> N2
    N2 --> N6["All nodes removed?"]
    N6 --> N7["Pop & Assign Colors"]
    N7 --> N8["Register-Mapped Code"]
    classDef decide fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713F12
    classDef step fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,stroke-width:2px,color:#312E81
    classDef result fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    class N3,N6 decide
    class N1,N2,N4,N5,N7 step
    class N8 result
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three major issues in the design of a code generator.*

**Model Answer:**
The three major issues in the design of a code generator are:
1. **Instruction Selection** — mapping intermediate operations to target machine instructions efficiently.
2. **Register Allocation and Assignment** — deciding which variables live in CPU registers and which specific register each occupies.
3. **Evaluation Order** — determining the sequence in which computations are emitted to minimize temporaries and maximize resource utilization.

*(Additional valid issues: storage management, target program form, handling of procedure calls.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the target program in the context of code generation? Name its three possible forms.*

**Model Answer:**
The **target program** is the output of the code generator — the actual machine-executable representation of the source program.
The three possible forms are:
1. **Absolute machine code** — relocatable addresses fixed at compile time, directly executable.
2. **Relocatable object code (`.o` files)** — allows separate linking of subprograms.
3. **Assembly language code** — symbolic form requiring a separate assembler pass.

---

### 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Question]**

*(a)* Explain in detail the major issues in the design of a code generator. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Given the three-address code below, generate efficient target code using a simple code-generation algorithm. Assume two registers are available. *(7 marks)*

```
t1 := a + b
t2 := c + d
t3 := e - t2
t4 := t1 * t3
x  := t4
```

##### 🔍 Model Solution — Part (a)

The design of a code generator is non-trivial because the generator must produce **correct, efficient, and compact** code. The following are the canonical issues:

**1. Input to the Code Generator:**
The input is typically Three-Address Code (TAC) along with the symbol table. TAC is preferred because each statement involves at most three operands, simplifying instruction selection. Other IR forms include quadruples, triples, and indirect triples.

**2. Target Program:**
The output may be absolute machine code (fast loading but inflexible), relocatable object code (allows modular linking), or assembly code (easier to produce but slower overall compilation).

**3. Instruction Selection:**
Mapping IR operations to machine instructions. The cost of an instruction depends on its addressing modes and execution cycles. The problem reduces to covering an IR tree with the minimum-cost set of machine instruction templates (a tiling problem solvable by dynamic programming, as in the Aho-Johnson algorithm).

**4. Register Allocation and Assignment:**
Since registers are the fastest memory, we want maximum usage. Register **allocation** decides *which* variables live in registers, while **assignment** maps them to specific registers. The classical solution models this as **graph coloring**: an interference graph is built (edges between variables live at the same time), and the goal is to color it with $k$ colors where $k$ equals available registers. If coloring fails, variables are **spilled** to memory.

**5. Evaluation Order:**
The order in which the code generator emits instructions for expressions affects register usage. Generating code **bottom-up** (postorder) ensures an operand is computed only when both its operands are available, minimizing the number of simultaneously live temporaries.

**6. Storage Management:**
Activation records (stack frames) must be managed for procedure calls. The code generator emits instructions to set up the frame, save/restore registers, pass parameters, and access local variables — typically using a frame pointer register (e.g., `$fp`).

##### 🔍 Model Solution — Part (b)

Given 2 registers `R0`, `R1` and TAC:

```
t1 := a + b
t2 := c + d
t3 := e - t2
t4 := t1 * t3
x  := t4
```

Apply **next-use / register-descriptor** algorithm (bottom-up):

**Generated code:**

```
MOV  R0, a          ; R0 := a
ADD  R0, R0, b      ; R0 := a + b   => t1 lives in R0
MOV  R1, c          ; R1 := c
ADD  R1, R1, d      ; R1 := c + d   => t2 lives in R1
SUB  R1, e, R1      ; R1 := e - t2  => t3 overwrites t2 (not needed anymore)
MUL  R0, R0, R1     ; R0 := t1 * t3 => t4 in R0
MOV  x, R0          ; x := R0
```

**Instruction count = 6.** Without register reuse, we would need explicit `STORE` between each temporary — yielding 10 instructions. Hence **4 cycles saved per execution**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Resit Paper]**

*(a)* Discuss register allocation and assignment. Explain how graph coloring is used for register allocation. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the typical structure of an activation record. List the contents stored in each region. *(7 marks)*

##### 🔍 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Register Allocation** decides *which* variables should reside in CPU registers at any point in execution. **Register Assignment** decides *which physical register* (e.g., `R0`, `R1`) should hold each variable.

**Why it matters:** Registers are 10–100× faster than memory. Reducing memory accesses by even 10% can yield 20–30% speedup in register-bound code.

**Graph Coloring Approach (Chaitin's Algorithm):**

- **Step 1 — Liveness Analysis:** Determine, for every program point, the set of variables whose values may be needed in the future. Two variables are *live simultaneously* if they appear together in any liveness set.
- **Step 2 — Build Interference Graph:** Each variable is a node. Add an edge between nodes u and v if u and v are simultaneously live. The edge means they cannot occupy the same register.
- **Step 3 — Coloring:** Color the graph using $k$ colors, where $k$ = number of available registers. If $k$-coloring exists, each color class corresponds to one register. Otherwise, some variables must be **spilled** to memory (load/store inserted).
- **Optimality:** Finding minimum $k$ is NP-complete in general, but heuristics (Kempe's chain, simplification, spill heuristics) work well in practice.

**Example:** With three temporaries `t1, t2, t3` all mutually live, the interference graph is a triangle ($\chi = 3$). Two registers suffice only if we can *break* one interference by reordering or spilling.

##### 🔍 Model Solution — Part (b)

An **Activation Record** (stack frame) is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack for each active procedure call. Its structure is as follows:

| Region (Top → Bottom of Frame) | Contents |
|-------------------------------|----------|
| **Returned Value** | Slot for the function's return value (passed by caller or callee). |
| **Control Link (Dynamic Chain)** | Pointer to activation record of the caller; used to restore frame on return. |
| **Access Link (Static Chain)** | Pointer to the activation record of the enclosing scope (for non-local access in nested procedures). |
| **Saved Machine Status** | Return address, saved contents of machine registers, condition codes. |
| **Parameters** | Actual arguments passed to the procedure (some passed in registers per calling convention). |
| **Local Variables & Temporaries** | Locals declared inside the procedure and compiler-generated temporaries. |

```
+-------------------+
| Returned Value    |  ← (optional)
+-------------------+
| Control Link      |
+-------------------+
| Access Link       |
+-------------------+
| Saved Machine Stat|
+-------------------+
| Parameters        |
+-------------------+
| Local Variables   |
+-------------------+
| Temporaries       |
+-------------------+
        ↑
     Frame Pointer (FP)
```

The **Frame Pointer (FP)** typically points to a fixed offset within this record, while the **Stack Pointer (SP)** moves dynamically as the frame is allocated/deallocated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "allocation" with "assignment"** — many students lose marks by treating these as synonyms. Remember: *Allocation = which set*; *Assignment = which specific register*.
> 2. **Forgetting to spill** — when describing graph coloring, students often skip the case where $k$-coloring fails. Always mention **spilling** as the fallback.
> 3. **Mixing up "live" vs "in use"** — a variable is live from its **definition to its last use**, not just while it's in a register.
> 4. **Skipping the access link** in activation records — this is a frequent omission; examiners often award a sub-part mark specifically for it.
> 5. **Confusing target program forms** — absolute code is NOT relocatable; assembly code requires an extra pass.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Six Core Issues:** Input IR, Target program, Instruction selection, Register allocation, Evaluation order, Storage management.
- 🧠 **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is the canonical IR input; each statement has ≤ 3 operands.
- 🖥️ **Target forms:** Absolute code, Relocatable object code, Assembly code.
- 🌳 **Instruction Selection** = tiling IR tree with minimal-cost machine instruction patterns (Aho-Johnson DP algorithm).
- 🎨 **Register Allocation** = Graph coloring problem → Nodes = variables, Edges = interference, Colors = registers.
- 💧 **Spill** to memory when $k$-coloring is impossible.
- 📚 **Activation Record** contains: return value, control link, access link, saved status, parameters, locals.
- ⬇️ **Bottom-up evaluation order** minimizes simultaneously live temporaries.
- ⚡ **Optimization passes:** peephole, redundant load elimination, copy propagation, strength reduction.
- 🛠️ **Real-world impact:** Embedded binaries (size), GPU kernels (registers), JIT engines (HotSpot, V8).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** Always frame answers with a definition first, then a diagram, then a worked example. Examiners reward **structured, illustrated responses** generously. Use bullet points and tables to maximize clarity within limited time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Simple Code Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT631 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** A Simple Code Generator ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Code Generator:** The final phase of a compiler's backend (synthesis phase) that translates the **intermediate representation (IR)** — typically **three-address code (TAC)**, **quadruples**, or **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** — into the **target machine code** (assembly/machine instructions), while optimizing the use of registers, memory, and execution time. The **Simple Code Generator** (as described in the standard compiler design text by Aho, Sethi, and Ullman) is a foundational, register-aware algorithm that maps each three-address instruction into a sequence of target machine instructions using a fixed strategy for **register allocation** and **addressing modes**. ### 1.2 The Three Primary Inputs to a Code Generator | # | Input | Description | |---|-------|-------------| | 1 | **Intermediate Representation (IR)** | TAC, quadruples, triples, or SSA form produced by the front-end. | | 2 | **Symbol Table** | Contains type, scope, and runtime address of every user-defined name. | | 3 | **Activation Record Map** | Maps local variables, temporaries, and parameters to memory offsets inside the stack frame. | > [!NOTE] > The output may be **absolute machine code**, **relocatable machine code**, or **assembly code**. A simple code generator typically emits **assembly code**, which is later passed to an assembler. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bilingual Project Manager" Imagine you are a **project manager** in a company where the **engineering team** writes its plans in a structured, high-level format (think: *flowcharts + tables* — this is your **Three-Address Code**). Your job, as the **Code Generator**, is to: 1. **Read** each line of the high-level plan. 2. **Decide** which "tool" (register, memory slot, or immediate value) each engineer (the CPU) should pick up. 3. **Translate** the plan into the **shop-floor language** the engineers actually understand (assembly instructions like `MOV`, `ADD`, `MOVEM`). If two engineers need the same tool at the same time, you must **share carefully** (this is **register allocation**). If a tool is needed later but is currently free, you must remember to **save it** in a labelled drawer (this is **next-use information** and **register descriptors**). --- ### 1.4 Major Issues in the Design of a Code Generator (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU syllabus explicitly requires knowledge of the following design issues. Master each one. 1. **Input to the Code Generator** — TAC, symbol table, machine description. 2. **Target Program** — Absolute, relocatable, or assembly. 3. **Memory Management** — Static allocation, stack allocation, heap allocation. 4. **Instruction Selection** — Choosing the most efficient machine instruction sequence. 5. **Register Allocation** — Deciding which variables reside in the limited register set. 6. **Evaluation Order** — Ordering of TAC instructions affects register usage. 7. **Peephole Optimization** — Local post-pass cleanup of generated code. --- ### 1.5 Target Machine Model (RISC-style) The "simple machine" assumed for pedagogical purposes has the following properties (used throughout KTU questions): | Property | Specification | |----------|---------------| | **Registers** | **0 to 6** (typically 7 general-purpose: R0, R1, …, R6); R0 often reserved. | | **Two-address instructions** | `op src, dst` | | **Memory model** | Byte-addressable; words hold 4 bytes typically. | | **Instruction formats** | Register-register, register-memory, immediate. | Example instruction set (subset): | Instruction | Meaning | |-------------|---------| | `MOV Ri, Rj` | Copy contents of Rj into Ri | | `ADD Ri, Rj, Rk` | Ri := Rj + Rk | | `SUB Ri, Rj, Rk` | Ri := Rj − Rk | | `MUL Ri, Rj, Rk` | Ri := Rj × Rk | | `DIV Ri, Rj, Rk` | Ri := Rj ÷ Rk | | `LD Ri, addr` | Load Ri from memory address | | `ST Ri, addr` | Store Ri into memory address | | `BR label` | Unconditional branch | | `BRZ Ri, label` | Branch to label if Ri == 0 | | `BRN Ri, label` | Branch to label if Ri < 0 | --- ### 1.6 Data Structures Maintained by the Simple Code Generator | Data Structure | What It Tracks | |----------------|----------------| | **Register Descriptor** | For each register, *what variable name* (or names) is currently held inside it. | | **Address Descriptor** | For each program variable, *all locations* (registers and/or memory addresses) where its current value can be found. | | **Next-Use Information** | For each temporary, the next instruction (line number) that uses it; used to decide whether to *spill* a register. | > [!NOTE] > A variable's value in a register is **valid** only as long as no later instruction overwrites that register with another value. The **next-use** rule tells us whether the value will be needed soon — if yes, we **keep it in a register**; if no, we may safely **spill** it to memory. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Simple Code Generation Algorithm — Operational Logic The algorithm processes three-address instructions **one by one**, in a fixed sequence (basic-block order). For each instruction of the form: $$ x = y \ \text{op}\ z $$ the algorithm invokes the function `getreg(x = y \ \text{op}\ z)` to select a register `R` for `x`. Then: 1. Issue `LD R, y'` (where `y'` is the location holding `y`'s current value) **if `y` is not already in `R`**. 2. Issue the operation: `op R, z'` (where `z'` is the location of `z`). 3. Update **register descriptor** for `R` → now holds `x`. 4. Update **address descriptor** for `x` → includes `R`. 5. If `y` and/or `z` no longer in `R`, remove them from `R`'s descriptor and (if their last live location is now memory only) update their address descriptors. ### 2.2 The `getreg()` Function — Three-Case Decision Tree `getreg(I)` where `I : x = y \ \text{op}\ z` selects a register using the following **greedy heuristic**: | Case | Condition | Action | |------|-----------|--------| | **Case 1** | The value of `y` is already in some register `R`, **AND** `R` holds only `y` (no other variable), **AND** `y` is not used in the future, **AND** the operation does not need a second register operand (`op` is a unary/binary operator using `y` once). | **Return `R`.** | | **Case 2** | An **empty register** is available. | **Return the empty register.** | | **Case 3** | No register is empty. | **Spill** the register whose contents are **used farthest in the future** (or never used again). Generate `ST R, M` to write its current value back to memory, then return it. | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **next-use information** is computed via a **backward scan** over the basic block. For each instruction `i`, set `next-use(i) = i`. For each variable `v` appearing in instruction `i`, walk backward: when `v` is defined at line `j < i`, set `next-use(v)` at line `j` to `i`. Otherwise, propagate `next-use` backward until the definition of `v` is reached. ### 2.3 Handling Special Instruction Forms | TAC Form | Code Generation Strategy | |----------|--------------------------| | `x = y` | `getreg` returns `R`. If `y` is in some register `R1`, generate `MOV R, R1`; else `LD R, y'`. | | `x = op y` (unary) | Choose register `R`. Load `y` into `R`. Execute `op R`. | | `x = y[i]` or `x = i[y]` (array) | Compute address using base + offset; generate indexed load. | | `x[i] = y` (array store) | Generate indexed store. | | `x = *p` (pointer deref) | Generate indirect load: `LD R, p'`, then use as address. | | `*p = x` | Generate indirect store. | | `goto L` | Generate `BR L`. | | `if x oprel y goto L` | Use `CMP x', y'` (or `SUB`), then `BRZ/BRN R, L`. | | `param x` and `call p, n` | Generate argument-marshalling and `CALL p`. | | `return y` | Generate `MOV Rv, y'` (move to return-value register) and `BR end`. | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept / Equation | Definition / Formula | |---|--------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Three-Address Instruction (TAC)** | $x = y\ \text{op}\ z$ — at most one operator per instruction. | | 2 | **Next-Use Distance** | $d(v) = \text{next instruction index using } v - \text{current index}$. | | 3 | **Register Pressure** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{\text{simultaneous live values}}}{N_{\text{registers}}}$. | | 4 | **Spill Cost** | Cost of saving a register = `1` `ST` instruction if not already in memory, else `0`. | | 5 | **Memory Address of Variable** | $\text{addr}(x) = \text{base\_of\_frame} + \text{offset}(x)$ | | 6 | **Activation Record Size** | $\vert AR \vert = \sum_{i} \text{size}(slot_i) + \text{control\_link} + \text{access\_link} + \text{return\_addr}$ | | 7 | **Live Range of a Variable** | $[d(v),\ u(v)]$ where `d` is definition point and `u` is last use. | | 8 | **Spilling Decision** | If $d(v) > \text{threshold}$, **spill** $v$'s register. | | 9 | **Peephole Cost Reduction** | Redundant `MOV` elimination: `MOV R1, R0; MOV R0, R1` → no-op. | | 10 | **Total Instructions Bound** | $\leq 2 \times N_{\text{TAC}} + \epsilon$ for the simple algorithm. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** When computing next-use, remember it is a **per-block** computation. Variables live across block boundaries must be treated as "live on exit," which forces the register to be **spilled at block end**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This algorithm — though pedagogical — underpins the **first pass** of every real-world compiler backend: - **GCC** and **LLVM** begin register allocation with a similar "local" model before applying **graph coloring**. - **JIT compilers** (HotSpot, V8) use next-use principles to spill registers to the **spill slot** in the stack frame. - **Embedded compilers** (for ARM Cortex-M, AVR) are heavily register-constrained, so this strategy is still relevant. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Generating Code for a TAC Sequence **Given TAC (three-address code):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a - b \\ t_2 &= c + d \\ t_3 &= e \times t_2 \\ t_4 &= t_1 + t_3 \\ x &= t_4 \end{aligned} $$ Assume the machine has **2 registers** (R0, R1) and `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e` are initially in memory. Compute next-use and emit assembly. --- #### Step A: Compute Next-Use by Backward Scan | Instruction | $t_1$ | $t_2$ | $t_3$ | $t_4$ | |-------------|-------|-------|-------|-------| | `x = t4` | — | — | — | next-use = **none** | | `t4 = t1 + t3` | next-use at L4 | — | next-use at L4 | — | | `t3 = e * t2` | — | next-use at L3 | — | — | | `t2 = c + d` | — | — | — | — | | `t1 = a - b` | — | — | — | — | So next-use table: | Variable | Next-use | |----------|----------| | $t_1$ | line 3 (used by $t_4$) | | $t_2$ | line 2 (used by $t_3$) | | $t_3$ | line 3 (used by $t_4$) | | $t_4$ | line 1 (used by `x`) | | `a, b, c, d, e` | none | --- #### Step B: Apply `getreg` Greedy Heuristic (only 2 registers, both initially empty) | Line | TAC | `getreg` choice | Reasoning | |------|-----|-----------------|-----------| | L1 | `t1 = a - b` | **R0** (empty) | Case 2 | | L2 | `t2 = c + d` | **R1** (empty) | Case 2 | | L3 | `t3 = e * t2` | Need register. `R1` holds `t2`; `t2` next-use = none after L2 used → Case 1 → **R1** | | L4 | `t4 = t1 + t3` | Need register. `R0` holds `t1` (next-use at L3, **just used**) → Case 1 → **R0** | | L5 | `x = t4` | Need register for `t4`. `R0` holds `t1` (dead), `R1` holds `t3` (dead). Pick R1 → **R1** | > [!NOTE] > The "next-use is none after the line we're about to emit" trick allows reusing a register even though it was just used. --- #### Step C: Emit Assembly $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LD } R0, a \quad &&;\ \text{load } a \text{ into R0} \\ &\text{SUB } R0, b \quad &&;\ R0 \leftarrow R0 - b \\ &\text{LD } R1, c \quad &&;\ \text{load } c \text{ into R1} \\ &\text{ADD } R1, d \quad &&;\ R1 \leftarrow R1 + d \\ &\text{MUL } R1, e \quad &&;\ R1 \leftarrow R1 \times e \\ &\text{ADD } R0, R1 \quad &&;\ R0 \leftarrow R0 + R1 \\ &\text{MOV } R1, R0 \quad &&;\ R1 \leftarrow R0 \quad (\text{transfer for } x) \\ &\text{ST } R1, x \quad &&;\ \text{store } x \end{aligned} $$ Total: **8 instructions**, achieving minimum for this sequence. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Conditional Jump Code Generation **TAC:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{if } x < y \text{ goto } L_1 \\ &z = x + 1 \\ &w = y \times z \\ &L_1\text{: } \quad v = w - x \end{aligned} $$ **Generated code (assuming `x` in R0, `y` in R1 at entry):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{CMP } R0, R1 \quad &&;\ \text{or equivalently } SUB\ R, R1 \text{ where } R \text{ is temp} \\ &\text{BRN } R, L_1 \quad &&;\ \text{branch if negative} \\ &\text{ADD } R0, \#1 \quad &&;\ R0 \leftarrow x + 1 \\ &\text{MUL } R1, R0 \quad &&;\ R1 \leftarrow y \times z \\ &L_1\text{: } \quad \text{SUB } R1, R0 \quad &&;\ v = w - x \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test this exact translation. Always show **both** the comparison generation and the branch. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Procedure Call Translation **Source TAC:** $$ \begin{aligned} &p = a + b \\ &q = p \times c \\ &\text{param } q \\ &\text{call } \text{sum}, 1 \\ &x = \text{return\_value} \end{aligned} $$ **Generated code:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LD } R0, a \quad &&;\ \text{load } a \\ &\text{ADD } R0, b \quad &&;\ R0 \leftarrow a + b \quad (p) \\ &\text{MUL } R0, c \quad &&;\ R0 \leftarrow p \times c \quad (q) \\ &\text{ST } R0, param0 \quad &&;\ \text{marshal argument } \\ &\text{CALL sum} \quad &&;\ \text{call } sum(x) \\ &\text{MOV } R0, \text{RV} \quad &&;\ \text{load return value into R0} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** If the calling sequence has more live values than registers, you **must** save them with `PUSH` / `POP` (or `ST` to the stack frame) **before** the `CALL`, because the callee may overwrite registers per the calling convention. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Spill vs. Keep | Scenario | Live After Instruction? | Next-Use Distance | Decision | |----------|------------------------|-------------------|----------| | Register holds `y`; `y` used at next line | Yes | 1 | **Keep** | | Register holds `y`; `y` used at line +5 | Yes | 5 | Spill only if no other choice | | Register holds `y`; `y` not used again | No | $\infty$ | **Spill** (free choice) | | Register holds a variable **live across block** | Yes on exit | $\infty$ within block | **Spill at block end** | | Register holds a value **not yet in memory** | Yes | any | **Spill to memory** (must save) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Position of the Code Generator in a Compiler ```mermaid flowchart LR Source["Source Program"] --> Lex["Lexical Analyzer"] Lex --> Parser["Syntax Analyzer + Semantic"] Parser --> IRGen["Intermediate Code Generator"] IRGen --> IR["Three-Address Code / IR"] Parser --> ST["Symbol Table"] ST --> CodeGen["Code Generator"] IR --> CodeGen ST --> Opt["Machine-Independent Optimizer"] Opt --> CodeGen CodeGen --> Asm["Assembly / Machine Code"] Asm --> Out["Target Program"] classDef def fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef hl fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; class Lex,Parser,IRGen,Opt,Asm def; class CodeGen,IR,ST hl; ``` --- ### 4.2 Flowchart — The `getreg()` Decision Process ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Function call: getreg x = y op z"]) --> Q1{"Is y already in
some register R
AND R is not occupied
by another name
AND y not used later?"} Q1 -- Yes --> RetR(["Return R (Case 1)"]) Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is there an
empty register?"} Q2 -- Yes --> PickEmpty["Pick any empty register R"] PickEmpty --> RetEmpty(["Return R (Case 2)"]) Q2 -- No --> Q3["Pick register R whose
value is used farthest
in the future
or never used again"] Q3 --> Spill["If R holds a value not
in memory, emit ST R, M"] Spill --> RetSpill(["Return R (Case 3)"]) classDef startend fill:#385723,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef decision fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef process fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; class Start,RetR,RetEmpty,RetSpill startend; class Q1,Q2 decision; class Q3,Spill,PickEmpty process; ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Structures Maintained During Code Generation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SG["SIMPLE CODE GENERATOR"] direction TB In["Input: TAC instruction
x = y op z"] --> GR["getreg() Selection"] GR --> RegDesc["Register Descriptor
(which var in each R0..R6)"] In --> AddrDesc["Address Descriptor
(for each var: list of
locations holding it)"] AddrDesc --> GR In --> NextUse["Next-Use Table
(precomputed backward scan)"] NextUse --> GR RegDesc --> Emitter["Instruction Emitter"] AddrDesc --> Emitter Emitter --> Update["Update both descriptors
after each instruction"] Update --> RegDesc Update --> AddrDesc Emitter --> Out["Output: Assembly instructions"] end classDef io fill:#385723,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef proc fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef data fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; class In,Out io; class GR,Emitter,Update proc; class RegDesc,AddrDesc,NextUse data; ``` --- ### 4.4 Flowchart — Algorithm for Next-Use Computation (Backward Scan) ```mermaid flowchart TD Init(["Set next-use of all temporaries = NONE"]) --> Scan["Scan instructions
i = N down to 1"] Scan --> ForVar["For each name v in instruction i:"] ForVar --> FoundDef{"Does v appear
as a definition
in instruction i?"} FoundDef -- Yes --> Attach["Attach next-use i to v
at instruction i"] FoundDef -- No --> Propagate["Propagate next-use of v
to its previous definition"] Attach --> Next["Move to next name"] Propagate --> Next Next --> MoreI{"More names
in instruction i?"} MoreI -- Yes --> ForVar MoreI -- No --> MoreN{"More instructions
to scan?"} MoreN -- Yes --> Scan MoreN -- No --> Done(["Next-use table ready"]) classDef startend fill:#385723,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef process fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef decision fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; class Init,Done startend; class Scan,ForVar,Attach,Propagate,Next process; class FoundDef,MoreI,MoreN decision; ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List the principal issues in the design of a code generator. Mention any three.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The principal issues in the design of a code generator are: 1. **Input to the code generator** — The intermediate representation (three-address code, quadruples), symbol table, and the target machine description are the inputs. 2. **Target program form** — Whether the output is absolute machine code, relocatable machine code, or assembly code. 3. **Instruction selection** — Choosing the most efficient instruction sequence to implement each TAC statement. 4. **Register allocation** — Mapping the unlimited set of temporaries onto the limited physical registers of the target machine. 5. **Evaluation order** — Ordering the TAC instructions to minimize register usage. 6. **Peephole optimization** — Local transformations on the emitted code to remove redundant loads/stores and jumps. *(Any three well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is meant by *next-use information* of a variable in a basic block? How is it used during code generation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Next-use information** of a temporary variable `t` is the index of the *next* three-address instruction in the same basic block in which `t` is used as an operand. If `t` is not used again in the block, its next-use is said to be `NONE`. **Use during code generation:** It is computed by a **backward scan** of the basic block before code emission. During code generation, when a register must be freed (because all registers are occupied), the algorithm chooses to **spill** the register whose value is used **farthest in the future** (or not used at all). This minimizes the number of redundant memory accesses (`ST`/`LD`) and hence reduces the total instruction count. --- ### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Internal Choice:** A student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the function `getreg()` used in the simple code generation algorithm. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following three-address code sequence. The target machine has **two registers** (R0, R1). All variables `a, b, c, d` initially reside in memory. Generate the assembly code using the simple code generation algorithm. Show the next-use information clearly. **(7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= c + d \\ t_3 &= t_1 - t_2 \\ x &= t_3 \times a \end{aligned} $$ --- **Model Solution:** **(a) `getreg()` Flowchart (7 marks):** The function `getreg(I)` selects a register for instruction `I : x = y op z` according to the following priority: | Priority | Condition | Action | |----------|-----------|--------| | 1 | `y` is already in some register `R`, that register holds *only* `y`, and `y` has no next-use | Return `R` | | 2 | An empty register exists | Return an empty register | | 3 | No empty register exists | Spill the register whose contents are used **farthest in the future**, or never used again. If its value is not in memory, emit `ST R, M` first. | **Decision logic:** 1. **Examine the operand `y`.** If `y` is in a register `R` and `y` is not used later → reuse `R`. 2. **Otherwise search for an empty register.** If found → use it (load operands as needed). 3. **If no empty register is available**, pick the register whose value will not be used for the longest time in the remaining instructions. This minimizes the number of spills. 4. If the chosen register holds a value not yet in memory, emit a store first. **(b) Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute next-use via backward scan:** | Line | TAC | next-use updates (going backward) | |------|-----|------------------------------------| | L4 | `x = t3 * a` | $t_3$ used at L4 → next-use = L4 | | L3 | `t3 = t1 - t2` | $t_1$ used at L3 → next-use = L3; $t_2$ used at L3 → next-use = L3 | | L2 | `t2 = c + d` | `c, d` next-use = NONE (only at L2) | | L1 | `t1 = a + b` | `a` used at L1 and L4 → next-use at L1 = L4; `b` next-use = NONE | **Next-Use Summary Table:** | Variable | a | b | c | d | t1 | t2 | t3 | |----------|---|---|---|---|----|----|----| | **Next-Use** | L4 | — | — | — | L3 | L3 | L4 | **Step 2 — Code Emission (both registers empty initially):** | Line | TAC | getreg Decision | Emitted Code | |------|-----|------------------|---------------| | L1 | `t1 = a + b` | R0 empty → **R0** | `LD R0, a`
`ADD R0, b` | | L2 | `t2 = c + d` | R1 empty → **R1** | `LD R1, c`
`ADD R1, d` | | L3 | `t3 = t1 - t2` | Need reg for `t3`. R0 holds `t1` (next-use = L3, used now) → Case 1 → **R0**. R1 still holds `t2` (next-use = L3, used now). | `SUB R0, R1` | | L4 | `x = t3 * a` | Need reg for result. R1 holds `t2` (no next-use, dead) → **R1**. R0 holds `t3` (no next-use after this). | `MOV R1, R0`
`MUL R1, a`
`ST R1, x` | **Final Assembly Code (10 instructions):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LD } R0, a \\ &\text{ADD } R0, b \\ &\text{LD } R1, c \\ &\text{ADD } R1, d \\ &\text{SUB } R0, R1 \\ &\text{MOV } R1, R0 \\ &\text{MUL } R1, a \\ &\text{ST } R1, x \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > Note the clever `MOV R1, R0` before the multiply — because the multiply instruction requires both operands in registers, we first transfer `t3` (in R0) into R1 so that the result `x` ends up in R1, then we don't need a second move. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the concepts of **register descriptor** and **address descriptor** used in a simple code generator. Show how they are updated after generating code for the instruction `x = y + z`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Generate code for the following TAC, assuming 3 registers are available and using the simple code generator algorithm. Variables `a, b, c, d, e` are initially in memory. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= b + c \\ t_2 &= t_1 \times d \\ t_3 &= a - t_2 \\ t_4 &= t_3 + e \\ x &= t_4 \end{aligned} $$ --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Register & Address Descriptors (7 marks):** **Register Descriptor** is, for each register, the set of **variable names** whose current value is held in that register. Initially, all descriptors are empty. **Address Descriptor** is, for each program variable, the set of **locations** (memory address and/or register names) where its current value can be found. Initially, every variable is in its assigned memory location. **Example:** For instruction `x = y + z`, the steps are: 1. Call `getreg(x = y + z)` → returns register `R`. 2. If `y` is not already in `R`, generate `LD R, y'` (load `y` from its current location). 3. Generate `ADD R, z'` (add `z` from its current location). 4. **Update Register Descriptor of R** → `R` now holds `x`. Remove any previous name from `R`'s descriptor. 5. **Update Address Descriptor of `x`** → add `R` to the set of locations for `x`. Remove other registers from `x`'s descriptor (since `x`'s *new* value is only in `R`). 6. **Update Address Descriptors of `y` and `z`** → if `y`'s or `z`'s previous location was a register that has been overwritten, remove that register from their address descriptors. **(b) Solution (7 marks):** **Next-Use Table (backward scan):** | Variable | t1 | t2 | t3 | t4 | |----------|----|----|----|----| | **Next-Use** | L2 | L3 | L4 | L5 | All memory variables (`a, b, c, d, e`) have next-use = NONE except `e` (L4) and `a` (L3). **Code Generation (3 registers: R0, R1, R2):** | Line | TAC | getreg | Action | Emitted Code | |------|-----|--------|--------|---------------| | L1 | `t1 = b + c` | R0 empty → **R0** | Load b, add c | `LD R0, b`
`ADD R0, c` | | L2 | `t2 = t1 * d` | R0 holds `t1` (next-use = L2) → **R0** | Load d into R1, multiply | `LD R1, d`
`MUL R0, R1` | | L3 | `t3 = a - t2` | R1 empty → **R1** | Load a into R2, subtract R0 (t2) | `LD R2, a`
`SUB R2, R0` | | L4 | `t4 = t3 + e` | R2 holds `t3` (next-use = L4) → **R2** | Load e into R1, add | `LD R1, e`
`ADD R2, R1` | | L5 | `x = t4` | Need reg for result. R0 holds `t2` (dead), R1 holds `e` (dead). Pick **R0**. | Move R2 → R0, store | `MOV R0, R2`
`ST R0, x` | **Final Assembly Code (12 instructions):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LD } R0, b \\ &\text{ADD } R0, c \\ &\text{LD } R1, d \\ &\text{MUL } R0, R1 \\ &\text{LD } R2, a \\ &\text{SUB } R2, R0 \\ &\text{LD } R1, e \\ &\text{ADD } R2, R1 \\ &\text{MOV } R0, R2 \\ &\text{ST } R0, x \end{aligned} $$ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners deduct marks for:** > > 1. **Forgetting to show the next-use table** — Without it, your register-allocation decisions appear unmotivated. Always draw it. > 2. **Wrong direction of next-use scan** — It is a **backward** scan from the last instruction to the first, not forward. > 3. **Spilling a value that is needed soon** — If a register holds `t` and `t` is used in the very next instruction, **never** spill it. > 4. **Ignoring descriptors** — If your answer does not mention *updating* the register and address descriptors, you lose at least 2 marks in any 7-mark sub-part. > 5. **Misplacing the `MOV`** — When both operands are needed in registers and only one register is free, you must use `MOV` to copy one operand into the free register *before* the operation. > 6. **Treating blocks independently** — Variables live across blocks must be spilled at block end with explicit `ST` instructions. Examiners often include a multi-block TAC to test this. > 7. **No addressing-mode optimization** — Using `LD` even when a value is already in a register costs marks; always check both descriptors before issuing a load. --- ### 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density summary — read this 5 minutes before the exam.** - ✅ The **code generator** is the final phase of the compiler; it converts **three-address code** to **target machine code**. - ✅ Three principal inputs: **IR**, **symbol table**, **machine description**. - ✅ Three principal outputs: **absolute**, **relocatable**, or **assembly code**. - ✅ Six design issues: input, target form, memory management, instruction selection, **register allocation**, evaluation order, peephole optimization. - ✅ **Register Descriptor** = set of names held by each register. - ✅ **Address Descriptor** = set of locations holding the current value of each variable. - ✅ **Next-use information** is computed by a **backward scan** over the basic block. - ✅ `getreg()` returns a register via three cases: - **Case 1:** `y` already in a register holding only `y` and `y` has no next-use. - **Case 2:** An empty register is available. - **Case 3:** Spill the register whose contents are used farthest in the future. - ✅ For instruction `x = y op z`: emit `LD R, y'` (if needed) → `op R, z'` → update descriptors. - ✅ Conditional branches: generate a `CMP`/`SUB` followed by `BRZ`/`BRN`. - ✅ Procedure calls: marshal parameters with `ST`, issue `CALL`, retrieve return value from the return-value register. - ✅ Peephole optimizations include: redundant `MOV` elimination, jump-to-jump optimization, strength reduction, constant folding at the assembly level. - ✅ Memory layout uses **activation records** with base + offset addressing; static, stack, and heap allocation each have distinct lifetimes. - ✅ Cross-block liveness forces register spill at block boundaries with `ST` instructions.
